experiments.title: How including a call-to-action in the headline of the donation page affects conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believed that adding a clear call-to-action directly into the donation page headline would increase donor conversion rates because it would make the request immediate and emotionally explicit at the moment of decision, reinforcing the donor’s role and purpose in giving.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/18/2025 - 12/30/2025
This experiment ran during The Heritage Foundation’s year-end fundraising campaign, when donors were already primed to give and donation page traffic was high. The campaign messaging emphasized urgency, high stakes, and the importance of acting before the calendar year closed. At this point in the funnel, visitors had already clicked through with intent, making the donation page headline a critical moment to either reinforce commitment or introduce friction.
Earlier research and experiments suggested Heritage supporters respond strongly to calls for action and personal responsibility, raising the question of whether explicitly placing the ask in the donation page headline would improve performance—or inadvertently overwhelm donors who had already decided to give.
The test ran on the donation page headline itself. All visitors arriving at the donation page were randomly assigned to see either the control headline or one of two CTA-embedded headline variations. This placement tested the impact of headline framing at the final decision point, immediately before donors selected a gift amount and completed their contribution.
We wanted to understand whether making the ask unavoidable at the top of the donation page would increase conversions—or whether it would feel redundant or overly aggressive for donors already in the act of giving. The two treatments tested distinct psychological levers: identity alignment (“stand with Heritage”) versus action-hero framing (“join the fight”), both rooted in prior survey insights about donor motivation, urgency, and self-perception.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment produced statistically valid results, and the outcome was clear: including a CTA directly in the donation page headline decreased performance. Both treatment variants underperformed the control in conversion rate, average gift, and revenue per visitor. Treatment A saw a significant drop in conversions (-19.7%) with high confidence, while Treatment B also underperformed, though less severely.
The control headline—descriptive but non-directive—performed best across all key metrics. This suggests that once donors reach the donation page, they do not need to be asked again. Introducing a fresh CTA at this stage likely created friction or pressure, disrupting donors who were already prepared to act.
Moving forward, we will avoid embedding overt calls-to-action in donation page headlines and instead use that space to reinforce context, stakes, and meaning rather than issuing another ask. CTAs appear to be more effective earlier in the funnel—on landing pages, emails, and homepage messaging—while donation pages perform better when they assume intent and reduce cognitive load.
This experiment reinforces a broader principle: clarity and restraint matter most at the point of conversion. For high-intent donors, the role of the donation page is to remove barriers, not escalate persuasion.

experiments.title: How including a button in an email appeal affects click-through rate for donors
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a button to an email for donors will achieve an increase in click-through rate because it provides a clear, visually distinct call-to-action that reduces friction, draws the reader’s eye, and makes it easier for people to take the intended next step.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 08/26/2025
The Heritage Foundation is running its annual Leader’s Club campaign. The second email of the campaign offered a free ebook called “Flags of Our Fathers.” Within this email, they wanted to test how changing the raw URL to a button would affect the click-through rate to the landing page. This experiment ran on one email, and this segment included donors to The Heritage Foundation.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a button to an email for donors can significantly increase the click-through rate. In this case, the treatment with the button resulted in a 59.2% increase in clicks compared to the control group with a 100.0% statistical level of confidence.

This result confirms the hypothesis that a clear, visually distinct call-to-action, such as a button, can reduce friction, draw the reader’s eye, and make it easier for people to take the intended next step.

In future experimentation, we should continue to test the effectiveness of different call-to-action elements, such as buttons, in emails targeted to donors. By consistently analyzing and optimizing our email designs, we can maximize engagement and ultimately drive more conversions for The Heritage Foundation’s campaigns.

experiments.title: How defaulting the monthly tab on an acquisition offer affects recurring conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that defaulting to the monthly tab will achieve an increase in recurring conversion rates because it reduces friction by aligning with the donor’s default behavior, subtly encourages sustained giving through visual anchoring, and reframes the donation as an ongoing commitment rather than a one-time gift.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/01/2025 - 08/18/2025
The Heritage Foundation is offering a free Guide to the Constitution. In this experiment, they wanted to test how defaulting to the monthly tab on the acquisition donation page of this offer would affect the rate of recurring giving and overall transactions.
50% of traffic was randomly assigned to each variation (Control vs Treatment). The primary goal was to determine whether defaulting the donation format to monthly giving (rather than one-time giving) would influence the recurring conversion rate.
The idea to run this test stemmed from two core insights:
Behavioral Economics Research:
People tend to go with the default option presented to them. When monthly giving is presented as the default tab, it subtly frames that behavior as the norm or recommended path.
Organizational Goal: Boost Recurring Revenue:
Like many nonprofits, Heritage is invested in growing their base of recurring donors. Monthly gifts provide more predictable, long-term revenue.
By making “Make a Monthly Gift” the default selection (as seen in the treatment version), the hypothesis was that donors would be more likely to choose that option simply because it required no extra clicks or decisions to get there.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are as follows:
1. Defaulting to the monthly tab significantly increased the Transactions - Monthly for All Traffic by 1,001.5% with a 100.0% level of confidence. This indicates that making monthly giving the default option can effectively boost the number of recurring donors.
2. The treatment version, which defaulted to the monthly giving option, also saw a 20% increase in overall transactions compared to the control version with a 68.7% level of confidence. This indicates that defaulting to monthly first had no negative effect on one-time giving.
The substantial increase in recurring conversion rates supports the hypothesis that defaulting to the monthly tab can influence donor behavior and encourage sustained giving.

				× 1,001.5% increase in conversion rate

experiments.title: How including a button in an appeal email affects email click-through rates for non-members
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a visually prominent, action-oriented button that says “Double My Gift” will increase email click-through rate because it draws more attention than a plain-text hyperlink, creates a clearer and more compelling call to action, and reduces friction by making the next step more obvious and easier to complete.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 08/01/2025
The Heritage Foundation conducted this experiment as part of their President’s Challenge matching gift campaign. This campaign was designed to drive donor engagement by offering a limited-time opportunity to double the impact of a donor’s gift through a matching challenge. The email in question was the first send in the campaign sequence, making it a high-leverage moment for influencing donor behavior and maximizing initial response rates.
Given the high stakes of the campaign’s early momentum and the importance of clearly communicating the match opportunity, the Heritage Foundation sought to test whether different call-to-action (CTA) formats would affect click-through and conversion rates.
The test was deployed via email, targeting the Heritage Foundation’s non-member list.
Both versions of the email—control and treatment—were linked to the same donation landing page but differed in their CTA presentation.
The experiment was driven by a hypothesis rooted in behavioral and conversion rate optimization principles: a visually prominent, action-oriented button might outperform a plain-text hyperlink in driving user action.
Key motivations included:
Improving clarity and visibility of the CTA: While the raw URL in the control version is technically functional, it lacks the immediate visual weight and action language of a button. The team suspected that this might cause the CTA to be overlooked or feel less urgent.
Reducing cognitive friction: A clear, concise button like “Double My Gift” directly mirrors the donor’s intent and may simplify decision-making.
Encouraging mobile-friendly design: Buttons often perform better on mobile devices where touch interaction is key, and plain-text links can be harder to tap.
This test is a good example of using micro-optimizations in email fundraising design to explore how seemingly small changes can have an outsized effect on user behavior and conversion rates.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that using a visually prominent, action-oriented button with clear and compelling language can significantly increase click-through rates in email campaigns. In this case, the treatment with the button saying “Double My Gift” outperformed the control with a plain-text hyperlink, resulting in a 42.0% increase in clicks for all traffic.

In future experimentation, it is important to continue testing different elements of email design, such as CTAs, to optimize for user engagement and conversion rates. By focusing on creating clear, visually engaging CTAs that reduce cognitive friction and make the next steps obvious and easy to complete, organizations can increase the effectiveness of their email fundraising efforts. Additionally, considering the importance of mobile-friendly design in email campaigns, using buttons rather than plain-text links can further improve user experience and drive better results.

experiments.title: How defaulting the monthly tab on an instant donation page affects recurring conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that defaulting to the monthly tab for potential donors will achieve an increase in recurring conversion rates because  it reduces friction by aligning with the donor’s default behavior, subtly encourages sustained giving through visual anchoring, and reframes the donation as an ongoing commitment rather than a one-time gift. because  .
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/19/2025 - 07/18/2025
This experiment was conducted as a split test on the instant donation page immediately following the Heritage Foundation’s Vote of Confidence offer. The test was live for one month, with 50% of traffic randomly assigned to each variation (control vs. treatment). The primary goal was to determine whether defaulting the donation format to monthly giving (rather than one-time giving) would influence the recurring conversion rate.
This experiment is a classic behavioral nudge test—leveraging default bias by changing which giving tab a donor sees first.
The idea to run this test stemmed from two core insights:
Behavioral Economics Research:
People tend to go with the default option presented to them. When monthly giving is presented as the default tab, it subtly frames that behavior as the norm or recommended path.
Organizational Goal: Boost Recurring Revenue:
Like many nonprofits, Heritage is invested in growing their base of recurring donors. Monthly gifts provide more predictable, long-term revenue.
While past tests may have encouraged recurring giving through messaging or pre-selecting a checkbox, this test directly adjusted the default donation modality itself.
By making “Make a Monthly Gift” the default selection (as seen in the treatment version), the hypothesis was that donors would be more likely to choose that option simply because it required no extra clicks or decisions to get there.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment suggest that defaulting to the monthly giving tab can significantly impact donor behavior and increase recurring conversion rates. By aligning with the donor's default behavior, visually anchoring the idea of sustained giving, and reframing the donation as an ongoing commitment, the treatment version achieved a 0.9% increase in Transactions - Monthly for All Traffic with a 99.9% statistical level of confidence.
In future experimentation, these learnings should be considered to optimize donation pages and increase recurring revenue for the Heritage Foundation:
1. Default to Monthly Giving: Based on the success of defaulting to the monthly giving tab in this experiment, consider implementing this strategy on other donation pages or communication materials. This can help nudge donors towards becoming recurring givers.
2. Visual Anchoring: Use visual cues and language that emphasize the benefits of sustained giving. Highlighting the impact of monthly donations and showcasing the value of recurring contributions can further encourage donors to choose the monthly giving option.
3. A/B Testing: Continuously test different strategies, messaging, and default settings to identify what resonates best with donors. A/B testing allows for data-driven decision-making and can uncover new insights to optimize donation processes.
4. Donor-Centric Approach: Consider donor preferences and behaviors when designing donation pages. Making the giving process convenient, personalized, and aligned with donor values can increase engagement and support.
Overall, the success of this experiment highlights the importance of behavioral nudges and strategic design in influencing donor decisions. By leveraging default bias and emphasizing the benefits of recurring giving, nonprofits can potentially increase their base of recurring donors and secure more predictable, long-term revenue.

It is crucial to test different elements and approaches to see what resonates best with donors. Additionally, it is essential to prioritize evidence-based decision-making over assumptions, even if they seem logical on the surface. This experiment was conducted as a split test on the instant donation page immediately following the Heritage Foundation’s ICE survey. The test was live for three weeks, with 50% of traffic randomly assigned to each variation (control vs. treatment). The primary goal was to determine whether defaulting the donation format to monthly giving (rather than one-time giving) would influence the recurring conversion rate.
This experiment is a classic behavioral nudge test—leveraging default bias by changing which giving tab a donor sees first.

Behavioral Economics Research:
People tend to go with the default option presented to them. When monthly giving is presented as the default tab, it subtly frames that behavior as the norm or recommended path.

experiments.keyLearnings:

By setting "Make a Monthly Gift" as the default option, donors were more likely to choose this option, leading to a 795.9% increase in Transactions - Monthly for All Traffic with a 98.9% level of confidence.

experiments.title: The impact of a monthly gift array pushdown on recurring gifts
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a recurring gift pushdown for visitors to the homepage of Heritage.org will achieve a lift in monthly gifts to The Heritage Foundation because of the prioritization of recurring giving and the supporting copy emphasizing the need for sustained support.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/28/2025 - 06/01/2025
The Heritage membership team tested a monthly gift pushdown on the homepage of heritage.org. The test was visible to all desktop users on every visit and was run during the last 3 days of the May Leaders Club campaign. The pushdown emphasized the need for fearless patriots to help sustain the mission of the Heritage Foundation before time ran out. It featured 3 clickable buttons with suggested ask amounts and 1 button with “other”. After clicking the user was redirected to a landing page with only a recurring  gift option. In conjunction with the test, a typical campaign banner add was featured below also asking the user to activate their Leaders Club membership.
The test ran as a 20/80 split because of time restraints, with the control receiving 20% of the traffic and the treatment receiving 80%. Overall there was no difference in the number of transactions, but the treatment received 7 monthly gifts while the control received none. Although if the test had been allowed to run longer and the control had received a monthly gift, than these results would not be valid.
Further testing, for a longer period of time is suggested.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are as follows:
1. Adding a recurring gift pushdown to the homepage of Heritage.org resulted in a 0.1% increase in monthly gifts to The Heritage Foundation.
2. The treatment version, which emphasized the need for sustained support through recurring giving, outperformed the control version.
3. The treatment version had a statistically significant increase in monthly gifts with a 99.2% level of confidence.
4. The treatment received 7 monthly gifts while the control received none, indicating that the recurring gift pushdown was effective in prompting donors to choose monthly giving options.
In future experimentation, we should:
1. Continue to prioritize recurring giving options on the homepage or key landing pages to encourage sustained support.
2. Utilize language that emphasizes the impact of recurring gifts and the urgent need for support in order to motivate donors to take action.
3. Consider running tests for longer periods to gather more data and ensure the validity of results, especially when looking to compare against a control group that may generate unexpected outcomes given more time.
4. Explore additional elements such as different suggested ask amounts or variations in the messaging to further optimize the performance of recurring gift pushdowns.
Overall, the success of this experiment highlights the effectiveness of emphasizing recurring giving options and the importance of crafting compelling copy that resonates with donors.

experiments.title: What is the impact of personalizing Agenda articles based on website history on Clicks and Opens?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that prioritizing Immigration content in The Agenda for subscribers with Heritage website history related to crime, immigration, and national security will achieve a significant increase in the click/open rate because we personalizing content based of their previous engagement with heritage.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 05/22/2025
The membership team conducted a series of tests to create a personalized Agenda Email Newsletter based on the subscribers website history using Marketo data.
Two personalization groups were created based on keywords from subscribers Heritage website history. Group A – focused on subscribers with website history associated with the economy and Group B – focused on subscribers with website history associated with National Security, Crime, and Immigration.
Testing segments breakdown:
Group A – Control (25%)- received the “national security version” with articles related to the national security being prioritized at the beginning of the newsletter
Group A (75%)- Treatment – received the “economy version” with articles related to the economy being prioritized at the beginning of the newsletter
Group B (25%)- Control – received the “economy version” with articles related to the economy being prioritized at the beginning of the newsletter,
Group B (75%)- Treatment – received the “national security version” with articles related to the national security being prioritized at the beginning of the newsletter
In every test, the “National Security” personalization group outperformed the “Economy” group and so the metrics reported in this test only include results from Group B.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that prioritizing Immigration content in The Agenda for subscribers with Heritage website history related to crime, immigration, and national security significantly increases the click/open rate. The treatment group, which received the "national security version" with articles related to national security being prioritized at the beginning of the newsletter, saw a 46.5% increase in clicks for all traffic compared to the control group. This result was achieved with a 100.0% statistical level of confidence, indicating a strong significance in the impact of the personalized content.

In future experimentation, we should continue to personalize content based on subscribers' previous engagement with the Heritage website. By analyzing subscribers' website history and tailoring the content to their specific interests, we can enhance engagement and drive higher click-through rates. It is essential to continue testing different personalization strategies to understand what resonates best with our audience and refine our approach based on the results. This experiment demonstrates the effectiveness of personalized content in driving engagement and highlights the importance of catering to subscribers' individual interests to optimize performance.

experiments.summary:
Ended On: 06/13/2025
In our May sustainer invite campaign we tested using AI to rewrite an email to improve the donor conversion rate. Here was the PROMPT: Rewrite this fundraising email to optimize its ability to get someone to donate.

experiments.keyLearnings:
Key Learnings:
1. Personalization: Including the recipient's name and addressing them directly in the email can significantly improve donor conversion rates. This demonstrates that personalization plays a crucial role in engaging potential donors.
2. Clear Call to Action: The treatment email had a more compelling call to action, emphasizing the benefits of upgrading membership and how it would contribute to supporting President Trump's agenda. This clear direction likely helped in driving more conversions.
3. Emotional Appeal: The use of powerful language comparing the challenges in enacting conservative policies to walking in a hurricane resonated with the audience. Adding emotional appeal can help in creating a connection with donors and motivating them to take action.
4. Testing AI Rewriting: The use of AI to rewrite the email proved to be successful in improving donor conversion rates. This highlights the importance of continuously testing and optimizing email content to enhance its effectiveness.
Future Experimentation:
1. Utilize AI for Optimization: Given the success of using AI in rewriting the email, consider implementing AI tools for further optimization of fundraising emails. This could involve testing different AI-generated variations to identify the most effective messaging.
2. Segment Testing: Conduct further experiments by segmenting the audience based on factors such as donation history, engagement level, or demographics. This can help in tailoring email content to specific donor segments for better results.
3. Continuous Improvement: Incorporate learnings from this experiment into future fundraising campaigns. Regularly analyze data, test new strategies, and iterate on successful tactics to continually improve donor conversion rates.
4. Multi-Channel Testing: Expand experiments to include testing across various channels like social media, website messaging, or direct mail. This holistic approach can provide insights into the most effective ways to engage donors and drive donations.

experiments.title: How increasing the clarity around where an email is being used on a landing page form affects email conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that increasing the clarity around where an email is being used for quiz takers will achieve an increase in conversion rate because users will feel more informed and confident about how their personal information is being used, reducing hesitation and fostering trust in the process, which leads to higher engagement and willingness to complete the download.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/16/2024 - 10/01/2024
The Heritage Foundation is running a quiz on Marxism through paid social and email prospecting. The control landing page didn’t address on the form where the email address the user submitted would be used, so Heritage wanted to test adding an enrollment option button to the form that allows users to opt in to communication from Heritage and HAFA.
They originally wanted to run this experiment to be able to capture emails who opted in to HAFA communications as well as Heritage.
This test ran for two weeks to 50% of the traffic to this landing page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that increasing the clarity around where an email is being used for survey takers did not achieve an increase in conversion rate. In fact, the treatment version, which included additional language about agreeing to terms and conditions and privacy policies, saw a significant 33.1% decrease in Email Acquired for All Traffic compared to the control version with a 100% level of confidence.

In future experimentation, it is important to carefully consider the impact of adding extra language or steps to the conversion process. While transparency and clarity are important, it is crucial to balance this with the potential for increasing friction and reducing conversion rates. It may be beneficial to test different variations of messaging or form options to find the right balance between providing information and maintaining a smooth user experience.

Overall, this experiment highlights the importance of continuously testing and optimizing messaging and form elements to ensure the highest conversion rates possible.

experiments.title: How an “Disagree” default answer for survey questions affects email conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that defaulting to “Disagree” in dropdown lists for survey answers will achieve an increase in email conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/01/2025 - 04/15/2025
The Heritage Foundation is running a survey in paid Facebook ads and through email prospecting. In a previous experiment, they wanted to know if using a dropdown for the answers instead of radio buttons would impact or increase email conversion rate, resulting in a decrease in email acquisitions. In an additional follow up experiment, they wanted to test whether assigning the default answer in the dropdown lists to “Agree” as opposed to a blank value or “Undecided” would result in this increase, but this experiment too led to a decrease.
To further test the dropdown list idea, they wanted to know if setting the default dropdown list answer to “Disagree” would encourage the survey taker, who likely would agree with the questions asked, to take action by answering survey questions and in turn increase email acquisitions.
The control experience showed the original radio button answers and the treatment experience showed the dropdown list answers with “Disagree” or “No” as the default.
The experiment ran for two weeks and each variant saw 50% of traffic.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that defaulting to "Disagree" in dropdown lists for survey answers led to a significant 77.3% decrease in email acquisitions. This result suggests that the assumption that setting a negative default option would encourage action was incorrect.
In future experimentation, it's essential to carefully consider the impact of default options in survey questions. It is clear from this experiment that defaulting to a negative answer might discourage participants from engaging or taking action, resulting in a decrease in desired outcomes such as email acquisitions. Therefore, when designing future experiments, it would be beneficial to test different default options or even consider changing the dropdown lists back to radio buttons to see which approach yields the best results.
Additionally, this experiment highlights the importance of testing assumptions and hypotheses rigorously before implementing changes. It is crucial to analyze the potential impact of any alterations to the survey design thoroughly and be prepared to pivot based on the actual outcomes observed.

experiments.title: How an “Agree” default answer for survey questions affects email conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that defaulting to “Agree” in dropdown lists for survey answers will achieve an increase in email conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/18/2025 - 03/27/2025
The Heritage Foundation is running a survey in paid Facebook ads and through email prospecting. In a previous experiment, they wanted to know if using a dropdown for the answers instead of radio buttons would impact or increase email conversion rate, resulting in a decrease in email acquisitions. In an additional follow up experiment, they wanted to test whether assigning the default answer in the dropdown lists to “Undecided” as opposed to a blank value would result in this increase, but this experiment too led to a decrease of 32%.
To further test the dropdown list idea, they wanted to know if setting the default dropdown list answer to “Agree” would encourage the survey taker, who likely would agree with the questions asked, to submit the survey and increase email acquisitions.
The control experience showed the original radio button answers and the treatment experience showed the dropdown list answers with “Agree” or “Yes” as the default.
The experiment ran for one week and each variant saw 50% of traffic.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that setting the default dropdown list answer to "Agree" did not achieve an increase in email conversion rate. In fact, the treatment resulted in a significant decrease of 87.6% in Email Acquired for All Traffic compared to the control.

This indicates that simply defaulting to a positive response like "Agree" may not necessarily encourage survey takers to submit the survey or increase email acquisitions. It is essential to carefully consider all aspects of the survey design and wording, as well as understanding the preferences and behaviors of the target audience.

In future experimentation, it is important to continue testing different variables and elements of the survey , including testing different wording for the default dropdown list answer, adjusting the layout or design of the survey, or even exploring other formats such as open text responses.

Overall, the results of this experiment highlight the importance of thorough testing and the need to adapt strategies based on data-driven insights rather than assumptions.

In the original experiment, the default answer in the dropdown lists was set to a blank value. To further test the dropdown list idea, they wanted to know if setting the default dropdown list answer to “Undecided” would encourage the survey taker to take action and increase email acquisitions.

In future experimentation, we may consider changing the default answer to "Disagree" or "Agree" to test whether either impacts survey completion rate.

experiments.title: How removing descriptive paragraphs from the beginning of a survey affects email conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that removing descriptive paragraphs for survey takers will achieve an increase in email conversion rate because it reduces cognitive load, minimizes distractions, and streamlines the survey experience, making it easier and quicker for users to complete the process and proceed with the desired action.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/23/2025 - 01/29/2025
The Heritage Foundation is running a survey of President Trump’s agenda. At the beginning of the survey, the control has two paragraphs adding additional value proposition as to the importance of the user taking the survey and how their responses will help guide Heritage.
They wanted to test how removing these paragraphs would affect email conversion rates. They believed the paragraphs added unnecessary friction to the survey. They ran this experiment in three variants, showing each to 33% of traffic.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that removing descriptive paragraphs at the beginning of the survey, which added value proposition and explained the importance of the user's responses, led to a decrease in email acquisition by 16.3%. The control group, which included the paragraphs, performed better.

In future experimentation, it is important to consider the impact of providing context and value proposition to users before asking them to take a survey. While it may seem like unnecessary information, it can actually help in increasing user engagement and conversion rates. It is essential to test different variations and approaches to see what resonates best with the target audience before making any changes. Additionally, it is crucial to analyze the results with statistical significance to make informed decisions for future experiments.

experiments.title: How does adding an annual recurring option affect donor conversion rate for prospects?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a tab for an annual recurring  gift for prospects will achieve a lift in annual recurring gifts without affecting the donor conversion rate because members value consistency and the option to set and forget a gift is appealing to some donors. because people value consistency and the option to set and forget a gift is appealing to some donors.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 02/19/2025
The Heritage membership team tested adding a third Annual Recurring Gift tab to the donation form during their Founders Day campaign. The test went to a nonmember prospect audience and featured 3 emails. The emails and the landing pages contained variable copy for the “Annual recurring gift” treatment asking prospects to “activate your annual recurring gift to The Heritage Foundation”.
Results showed a large decrease in overall transactions but a substantial increase in monthly and annual recurring giving. There was a 451% increase in monthly gifts from prospects who received the annual gift treatment, with the control receiving 2 and the treatment receiving 11. In addition, 18 of the total 32 gifts received on the treatment were an annual recurring gift.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are:
1. Adding a tab for an annual recurring gift significantly increased annual and monthly recurring giving from nonmember prospects. This shows that providing the option for donors to set up automatic, recurring donations is appealing and can lead to a substantial increase in long-term giving.
2. Despite the increase in annual and monthly recurring gifts, the overall number of donations decreased by 51.1% for the treatment group. This suggests that some donors may have been deterred by the additional option, impacting the total number of transactions.
3. The control (without the annual recurring gift tab) outperformed the treatment in terms of donations for all traffic, indicating that for this particular audience, the traditional donation form without the additional tab was more effective.
In future experimentation, it is important to consider the trade-off between increasing recurring giving and potentially decreasing the total number of donations. It may be beneficial to conduct further research to understand which audience segments are most receptive to the annual recurring gift option and tailor the donation form accordingly. Additionally, it would be valuable to test different messaging and placement of the annual recurring gift tab to optimize its impact on giving without negatively affecting overall conversion rates.

experiments.title: How “kill the negative” language on a post-survey donation page impacts donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that reframing copy to “kill the negative” for survey takers will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because people are more motivated to fight against negative forces.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/17/2024 - 01/17/2025
The Heritage Foundation was running a survey on President Trump’s Agenda. The post-survey donation page copy was framed in a positive light, outlining how the viewer’s gift could “help save the American Dream” and “ensure America’s best days are ahead.”
In an attempt to increase donor conversion rate on the donation page, they wanted to test if reframing the page’s copy to highlight how the viewers gift could “kill the negative” as opposed to “restoring the positive” would lead to this increase.
The “kill the negative” treatment changed the more positive copy to language like “take a stand against the forces destroying the American Dream,” and “fight together to secure America’s future,” and added more references to fighting the ideas and principles that threaten America.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that reframing the donation page copy to focus on "killing the negative" rather than "restoring the positive" led to a significant increase in donor conversion rate. The treatment saw a 55.9% increase in Transactions for All Traffic with a 96.5% statistical level of confidence, indicating a clear win for the new messaging approach.

Moreover, Transactions - One-Time experienced a substantial 547% increase with a 95% level of confidence, highlighting the effectiveness of the "kill the negative" messaging in driving one-time donations.

These results suggest that people may be more motivated to take action when presented with the idea of fighting against negative forces rather than simply working towards positive outcomes. In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue testing different messaging approaches to see what resonates most with donors.

By incorporating the learnings from this experiment into future campaigns and donation page optimizations, The Heritage Foundation can potentially continue to increase donor conversion rates and support for their cause.

experiments.title: How using a more provocative title for the subject of a survey impacts Google ad clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using an more provocative name for the Administrative State for survey Google ad viewers will achieve an increase in clicks on the ad because the term “Deep State” invokes stronger emotions.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/21/2025
The Heritage Foundation was running a “Stop the Administrative State” survey and promoting through Google ads. In an attempt to increase click rates on the ads, they wanted to test whether using the more provocative term “Deep State” in place of the more straightforward “Administrative State” survey title would achieve this effect because people would be more provoked to take action against the subject when referred to as a more negative term.
The control’s Google ad image and copy options, including 8 headline and 5 description options, referred to the Administrative State, while the treatment’s ad image and copy referred to the Deep State, and both received 50% of traffic. In addition, the control led to survey landing page with “Administrative State” language, and the treatment led to a survey page with “Deep State” language for continuity.
This test ran for 3 weeks.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that using the term "Deep State" in Google ads instead of the more straightforward "Administrative State" led to a significant decrease in clicks. Contrary to the hypothesis, the more provocative term did not invoke stronger emotions or prompt more action from viewers.

In addition to click rate, the treatment also saw a 42.3% decrease in email acquisitions on the survey at 100% level of confidence, and an invalid 2.7% decrease in donor acquisition rate (6.82% level of confidence). The email acquisition results also support the same thought that the more provocative term did not invoke stronger emotions and action from viewers.

In future experimentation, it is important to carefully consider the language and terminology used in promotional materials. While provocative terms may sometimes generate interest, they can also backfire and deter potential participants. It is essential to test different messaging strategies to see what resonates best with the target audience before implementing them widely. Additionally, it is crucial to always track and analyze the results of experiments to draw meaningful conclusions and inform future decision-making.

experiments.title: How lowering the gift array on the instant donation page of a survey affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion:
We believe that
decreasing the amounts on the gift array for people who have taken a survey will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because the lower suggested gift amounts will make giving a gift feel more attainable to a larger amount of people.
because  the lower suggested gift amounts will make giving a gift feel more attainable to a larger amount of people.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/16/2024 - 08/23/2024
The Heritage Foundation has a survey on Voter Fraud where people can take the survey and then are led to a donation page where they can make a gift to support the organization’s efforts to fight fraud in elections. In an attempt to increase the donor conversion rate on this page, they wanted to test whether lowering the gift array amount options would lower the barrier to donating and in turn increase the amount of donors.
The control’s gift array was $25, $50, and $100.
The treatment’s gift array was $10, $25, and $50.
The experiment ran for one month and the control and treatment each received %50 of traffic.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment on Voter Fraud survey donation pages include the following:
1. Decreasing gift array amounts can increase donor conversion rate: The treatment, which presented lower gift amounts of $10, $25, and $50, saw an 18.0% increase in donations, with a valid 98% level of confidence, compared to the control with the $25, $50, and $100 amounts. This indicates that lowering the suggested gift amounts made donating feel more attainable to a larger number of people, leading to a higher conversion rate.
2. Increase in one-time transactions: The treatment group also showed a 16.8% increase in one-time donations at a valid 97% level of confidence, demonstrating that the lower gift array amounts not only attracted more donors, but also encouraged more one-time contributions.
3. Decrease in average gift amount: The treatment group also led to a 14.8% decrease in average gift, indicating that lower gift amounts may lead to people giving lower amounts, but at a 4% level of confidence, these results cannot be relied upon as valid.
Additional testing may include lower gift amounts on other survey pages as well as other types of offers or direct appeals.

experiments.title: How adding content offers to your website increases overall email and donor conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding content offers  for website visitors will achieve an increase in email and donor conversion rate  because we’re offering something of value to the people who are already interested in what Heritage has to offer. because  we’re offering something of value to the people who are already interested in what Heritage has to offer.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/30/2024 - 08/27/2024
The Heritage Foundation wanted to find an efficient way to add their existing content offers to their website to capture additional emails and donations. They decided to add an interstitial on their content pages offering three ebooks – 5 Shocking Cases of Election Fraud, The Guide to the Constitution, and The Real Cost of America’s Border Crisis.
We ran this experiment for almost a month and showed the treatment to 50% of the traffic.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment conducted by The Heritage Foundation are as follows:
1. **Value of Adding Content Offers:** The experiment demonstrated that adding content offers to the website can significantly impact email acquisition and donor conversion rates. By offering something of value to website visitors, in this case, three ebooks, the organization was able to attract more email sign-ups and increase one-time transactions.
2. **Significant Increase in Email Acquisition:** The treatment group, which received the content offers, saw a remarkable 46.0% increase in email acquisition for all traffic. This indicates that providing valuable content can incentivize visitors to share their contact information.
3. **Substantial Increase in Donations:** The experiment also showed a substantial 485% increase in one-time transactions, indicating that the content offers not only attracted more email sign-ups but also encouraged visitors to make donations.
4. **Statistical Significance:** Both the increase in email acquisition and one-time transactions were statistically significant, with confidence levels of 100.0% and 99.0%, respectively. This reinforces the reliability of the results obtained from the experiment.
Moving forward, The Heritage Foundation should leverage the learnings from this experiment in future experimentation by:
- Continuing to test the impact of different content offers on email acquisition and donation rates.
- Experimenting with the placement and design of content offers on the website to optimize conversion rates.
- Segmenting the audience to tailor content offers based on visitor preferences and behaviors.
- Monitoring the long-term impact of content offers on donor retention and engagement metrics.
Overall, this experiment highlights the effectiveness of providing valuable content to website visitors as a strategy to increase email acquisition and donor conversion rates. By incorporating these learnings into future experimentation, The Heritage Foundation can continue to optimize its digital marketing efforts and achieve its organizational goals.

experiments.title: How adding a countdown timer to an “update your membership information” landing page affects email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a 10-minute countdown timer for membership update page viewers will achieve an increase in email acquisitions because there is an increased sense of urgency to complete the form when given a set amount of time.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/16/2024 - 11/12/2024
The Heritage Foundation was running a campaign targeting donors who had not yet given a gift in 2024 to update their membership information, including name, email address, phone number, and address on a landing page. They were then redirected to a donation page where they could give a gift in order to renew their Heritage membership.
In an effort to increase email acquisition rate on the landing page, Heritage wanted to test whether adding a 10-minute countdown timer would increase the viewer’s sense of urgency and encourage them to fill out the form more often.
The control page did not have a countdown timer, and the treatment page had a 10-minute countdown timer that started when the viewer started the page session and ended when it reached 0.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a 10-minute countdown timer to the membership update page did not achieve the desired increase in email acquisitions. In fact, the treatment page saw a significant and valid 11.5% decrease in email acquisitions compared to the control page. This indicates that the sense of urgency created by the countdown timer may have had a negative impact on the viewers, leading to a lower conversion rate. Viewers may have felt put off or pressured by the timer, or there may have been a disconnect in understanding why there was a limited timeframe to update their membership information.

In future experimentation, it is important to carefully consider the potential impact of elements like countdown timers on user behavior. While urgency can sometimes be effective in driving action, it is crucial to test such elements to ensure they are positively influencing the desired outcome. Additionally, it is essential to analyze the results of experiments thoroughly before implementing changes across all marketing efforts.

experiments.title: How including specific examples of what a person will learn from an eBook on a landing page affects the conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including specific examples of what a person will learn from an eBook on a landing page will increase the conversion rate because it provides more context and they have more information about whether they are interested in the offer.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/04/2024 - 06/17/2024
In an effort to increase the email sign-up rate on an ebook acquisition page, The Heritage Foundation ran an experiment that highlighted specific examples of what a person will learn from this ebook on the landing page.
They believed that it will provide more context and give the user more information to the contents of the ebook which will make them more interested in the offer.
This experiment was split 50/50 between the control and treatment.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that including specific examples of what a person will learn from an ebook on a landing page resulted in a 19.1% decrease in email acquisition for all traffic. This decrease was statistically significant at a 98.4% confidence level, indicating that the control group, without the specific examples highlighted, had a higher conversion rate.

In future experimentation, it is essential to consider the impact of providing too much information upfront on a landing page. It is possible that overwhelming visitors with specific examples may have dissuaded them from signing up for the ebook. It could be beneficial to test different levels of detail and types of information provided on the landing page to find the right balance that entices visitors to take the desired action.

Overall, this experiment highlights the importance of testing hypotheses before implementing changes permanently and the value of continuous experimentation to optimize conversion rates effectively.

experiments.title: How additional body copy and a brighter button color affects email conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that additional copy and a brighter button color for eBook downloaders will achieve an increase in email conversion rate because it will decrease friction in the form submission process.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/12/2024 - 06/25/2024
In an effort to increase the email conversion rate on a landing page for a border crisis eBook offer, The Heritage Foundation wanted to test whether adding body copy explaining what the eBook will help the reader learn, as well as changing the form’s button color, would lead to an increase in email conversion rate.
The control page had three paragraphs of copy and a blue form submit button, and the treatment page had five paragraphs of copy including a more in-depth explanation of the value the reader will receive and an orange button.
This split URL test ran for 13 days.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that the treatment with shorter copy and a brighter button color for the eBook offer did not lead to an increase in email conversion rate. In fact, the treatment resulted in a 6.5% decrease in Email Acquired for All Traffic compared to the control. With a statistical confidence level of 96.0%, it can be concluded that the control page performed better in terms of email acquisition.

The

In future experimentation, it is important to carefully evaluate the changes being made to the landing page. While the hypothesis suggested that shorter copy and a brighter button color would decrease friction in the form submission process and increase conversions, the results showed otherwise. It is essential to test hypotheses thoroughly and be open to unexpected outcomes.

Moving forward, it may be beneficial to further investigate the factors that influenced the lower email conversion rate in the treatment. This could involve conducting surveys or collecting feedback from users to understand their preferences and behaviors. Additionally, conducting more A/B tests with different variations can help in identifying the most effective elements for improving email acquisition on the landing page.

Overall, this experiment highlights the importance of data-driven decision-making and the need to carefully analyze results to inform future experimentation strategies.

experiments.title: How donor first party data through AVID Pathways impacted Facebook match rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that AVID Pathways connection  for AVID clients will achieve a higher match rate in Facebook because AVID has access to multiple sources of data. because  AVID has access to multiple sources of data.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/28/2024
With the introduction of AVID Pathways, we decided to test the Facebook match rate of contacts uploaded via CSV versus that of a continuous AVID Pathways connection.
For the control of this experiment, we exported donors from the organization’s CRM and uploaded the list to Facebook as an audience. The data points provided included first name, last name, email address, phone number, city, state, and zip code.
For the treatment of this experiment, we created a segment of donors in AVID Pathways and then pushed that segment into Facebook as an audience. The AVID Pathways connection to Facebook is a continuous flow of information from the organization’s platforms, such as CRM, email, and donation platforms combined.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that utilizing AVID Pathways to Facebook as an audience resulted in a significantly higher match rate compared to uploading donor information via CSV. The AVID Pathways connection saw a 45.6% increase in match rate for all segments, with a 100.0% statistical confidence level, indicating a clear win.
For this experiment, we utilized 4 different segments of donors based on the giving cohort:
multichannel donorsoffline only donorsoffline with email donorsonline only donors
For each segment, we saw the following results.
For multichannel donors, donors who have donated through online and offline channels, we saw a 5.5% decrease in match rate at a 100% level of confidence. For offline-only donors, we saw an increase of 144.8% in match rate at a 100% level of confidence. For offline with email donors, we saw an increase of 34.8% in match rate at a 100% level of confidence. For online donors, we saw an 8.3% decrease in match rate at a 100% level of confidence.
While a couple of cohorts did see decreases in match rates, we believe that the AVID Pathways continuous connection will more than compensate for the fewer matches. In addition, we anticipate that AVID Pathways data will continue to grow in knowledge and data points as more systems are connected and data points are expanded.
For this organization, the most surprising cohort result is for offline-only donors. These donors do not have email addresses on file with the organization and, therefore, have fewer data points than the other cohorts. However, even with that missing point, we saw a significant increase in the match rate, which means we will be able to retarget these donors with Facebook ads.

experiments.title: How a question headline on a landing page affects the conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: A question headline on a landing page will increase the conversion rate because it creates curiosity and people will want to sign up to find out the answer.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/15/2024 - 06/04/2024
The Heritage Foundation was running a campaign for an eBook on the state of the U.S. Military. We saw an opportunity to raise email conversion rate on the eBook offer’s landing page, so we wondered whether adding a headline that poses a question that the eBook answers would lead to an increase in conversion rate due to creating curiosity in the mind of the reader.
For this experiment, the control’s headline read: “Learn About the Current State of the U.S. Military — And What That Could Mean for Our Nation’s Future”
The treatment’s headline read: “War Is Raging—But How Prepared Is the U.S. Military if They Are Needed?”
The experiment was set up as a 50/50 split redirect test and ultimately ran for 20 days.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that the control, which had a headline stating the topic directly without posing a question, outperformed the treatment, which featured a question headline. The treatment received a 23.7% decrease in email conversion rate at a valid 95.7% level of confidence compared to the control. This result indicates that in this particular case, the straightforward approach was more effective in capturing the audience's attention and prompting them to sign up for the eBook offer.

Downstream, the treatment saw a 9.5% increase in donor conversion rate, but at a 17% level of confidence, these results cannot be relied upon as valid.

In future experimentation, it is important to remember that different tactics may work better depending on the context and the audience. While posing a question can create curiosity, it is essential to ensure that the question resonates with the target audience and clearly communicates the value proposition. It may be beneficial to test an even more straightforward and clear headline that states the eBook's title.

experiments.title: How a benefit-focused landing page headline affects email conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a headline on a landing page that focuses on the benefits a supporter receives will increase the conversion rate because they will quickly understand the value.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/15/2024 - 05/21/2024
The Heritage Foundation was running a campaign for their Election Fraud eBook content offer. In an attempt to raise email conversion for visitors to the landing page, we wanted to test whether a headline that was more focused on the benefit viewers would get from reading the eBook would achieve this outcome.
The control headline read, “Get your free “5 Shocking Cases of Election Fraud” eBook from The Heritage Foundation.”
The treatment headline read, “Uncover the Hidden World of Election Fraud and Protect Your American Freedom.”
We set this up as a 50/50 split A/B test and ran for one week.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that the treatment headline, which focused on the benefits of reading the eBook, received a lower amount of conversions than the control headline. The treatment resulted in a 34.3% decrease in email acquisitions for All Traffic with a 99.9% level of statistical confidence. This also resulted in a valid lower donation rate downstream for the treatment, with a 57.3% decrease in transactions at 99.9% level of confidence.

Reasons for this result could include the treatment headline sacrificing clarity in order to illustrate the benefits the viewer could receive from this content. The headline "Uncover the Hidden World of Election Fraud and Protect Your American Freedom" doesn't clearly illustrate what the content offer is upon reading it, which may dissuade viewers from reading the page further and making a decision to download the resource.

Moving forward, it is essential to consider that a headline focused on the specific offer may be more effective in driving conversions compared to a headline that emphasizes the benefits. It is crucial to clearly communicate the value proposition of the offer to the audience to encourage them to take action.

experiments.title: How including examples from the eBook on a landing page affects conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including examples of cases from the eBook on the sign-up landing page will increase the conversion rate because people will have more context about the E-book to decide if they want to download it
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/21/2024 - 06/03/2024
The Heritage Foundation was running a campaign for an eBook on election fraud. In an attempt to raise conversion rate on the eBook’s landing page, we wondered if adding body copy with examples of cases from the eBook would persuade more people to download the eBook if they had more context as to what the offer contained.
We tested the control against the treatment which added the following copy:
“You’ll learn about a Judge of Elections who accepted bribes, another individual who devised a ballot harvesting scheme, and more. In this eBook, you’ll also learn about the consequences of these crimes against American liberty.”
The control and treatment each saw 50% of traffic.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding examples of cases from the eBook on the landing page did not increase the conversion rate. In fact, the treatment version with the added body copy resulted in a 21.9% decrease in Emails Acquired for All Traffic compared to the control version. This suggests that the additional information provided in the treatment did not persuade more people to download the eBook.

Viewers may have thought the additional copy in the treatment "spoiled" two of the advertised five cases of Election Fraud or took the mystery out of the eBook and dissuaded them from following through to download the offer.

Future experimentation could include rewriting the page's copy to be more accessible to a wider audience through a lower reading level.

experiments.title: How leveraging a small image on a donate button to build affinity and alignment affects donor conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a small American flag to build affinity for potential donors to The Daily Signal will achieve an increase in conversion rate because the flag image will help align their motivation with the intended action.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 05/09/2024
The Daily Signal is The Heritage Foundation’s multimedia news platform. On their home page, and most other pages, the only call-to-action to donate to support the organization is located at the bottom in the footer section among many other site links.
They’ve previously tested adding a donate button to the main menu of the homepage here: https://app.winstonknows.com/e/K1qG
That experiment resulted in a large increase in traffic to the donation page because we provided an easier, more prominent path to the donation page.
The team had noticed an eCommerce site that, when selling merchandise for a particular team, put that team’s logo on the “add to cart” page to leverage the natural affinity between the buyer and the team.
To continue testing the donate button to increase traffic to the donation page, they decided to test a similar concept of adding an American Flag icon to the button.
They believed the flag image would help align a donor’s motivation with their intended action.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a small American flag to the donate button on The Daily Signal's website significantly increased the number of clicks to the donation page. The treatment group, which included the flag icon, saw a 53.6% increase in clicks for all traffic with a 99.9% level of confidence.

Additionally, the treatment button saw a 22.8% lift in transactions with a 59.7% level of confidence. Although these results are not valid, they do indicate that overall more traffic to the donation page will increase the percentage of donations made.

Overall, this experiment suggests that incorporating patriotic or visually appealing elements can help build affinity with potential donors and encourage them to take action. In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue testing the use of imagery or design elements that align with donors' motivations to see if similar increases in conversion rates can be achieved.

Additionally, it is important to consider the placement of calls-to-action on the website. The success of this experiment may also indicate that strategically placing donation buttons in prominent locations on the site can lead to higher engagement and donation rates.

experiments.title: How using monthly giving language and defaulting to the recurring tab in the donation form affects recurring donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using monthly giving value proposition and defaulting to the recurring tab in the form for lapsed Heritage members will achieve a higher recurring donor conversion rate because we capitalize on the importance of monthly giving and the impact their monthly gift will have on the organization.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/13/2024 - 03/25/2024
The Heritage Foundation sends traffic to their membership renewal page through the main website menu. The goal of this page is to have previous members renew their membership to the Heritage Foundation through a one-time or monthly gift.
For this experiment, we wanted to test how adding value proposition language to the page about monthly giving would affect recurring donor conversion rates. In addition, we defaulted the tab in the donation form to the monthly giving option.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Overall, we saw a 179.1% lift in recurring donor conversion with a 96.6% level of confidence. Additionally, we saw a 24.2% decrease in one-time giving with only an 84.3% level of confidence.

The key learnings from this experiment are:

1. **Value Proposition Language Works**: Adding value proposition language about monthly giving effectively increased the conversion rate of recurring donors among lapsed Heritage Foundation members. This indicates that emphasizing the impact and importance of monthly giving can significantly influence donor behavior.

2. **Defaulting to Monthly Giving Tab Increases Conversion**: Defaulting the tab in the donation form to the monthly giving option also contributed to the increase in recurring donor conversions. By making the monthly option the default choice, it likely nudged more donors towards selecting that option.

In future experimentation, the Heritage Foundation should continue to test different strategies for increasing donations among lapsed members. They should also consider applying the learnings from this experiment, such as using value proposition language and defaulting to the desired option, in other donation campaigns to see if similar results can be achieved. Additionally, ongoing testing and optimization of donation pages and strategies will help the organization continue to improve donor conversion rates and overall fundraising success.

experiments.title: How leveraging a small image to build affinity and alignment affects donor conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a small American flag to build affinity for renewal prospects will achieve an increase in conversion rate because the flag image will help align their motivation with the intended action. because  the flag image will help align their motivation with the intended action.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/26/2024 - 04/08/2024
The Heritage Foundation had been running a slate of experiments on their membership renewal page to try to increase conversion and get more members to renew as monthly donors. In their last experiment, they had tested the arrangement of the giving form tabs to prioritize their monthly ask first.
The team had noticed an eCommerce site that, when selling merchandise for a particular team, put that team’s logo on the “add to cart” page to leverage the natural affinity between the buyer and the team.
The Heritage team wondered if this might be true for donations, and decided to test it on their renewal page. They added a small American flag to appeal to the patriotism of their donors to the “Join the Leaders Club” tab and to the donate button itself, and launched an A/B test to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are:
1. The use of a small American flag on the membership renewal page significantly increased the conversion rate for renewing as monthly donors by 170.4% with a high level of confidence.
2. The treatment version with the flag image and patriotic messaging outperformed the control version by a large margin.
Moving forward, we should consider the following for future experimentation:
1. Including visual elements like the American flag that align with the organization's values can have a significant impact on conversion rates.
2. Personalized and motivational messaging can help create a sense of belonging and purpose, potentially leading to higher conversion rates.
3. It may be beneficial to continue testing different visual elements and messaging strategies to further optimize the membership renewal process and maximize conversions.
4. Consider incorporating similar elements in other areas of the website or marketing materials to maintain consistency in messaging and branding for better results.
Overall, the results of this experiment highlight the importance of using visual cues and persuasive messaging to influence donor behavior positively.

experiments.title: How adding a donate button to a news platform site’s header affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a donate button to the top header for site visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because more people will click to view the donation page.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/02/2024 - 04/10/2024
The Daily Signal is The Heritage Foundation’s multimedia news platform. On their home page, and most other pages, the only call-to-action to donate to support the organization is located at the bottom in the footer section among many other site links.
In an effort to increase the amount of traffic to the donation page from the home page, we previously tested adding a more prominently displayed donate button to the category menu at the top of the page on the desktop version, and in the navigation menu of the mobile version. This experiment resulted in a 42.8% increase in donations compared to the control. However, with a 53.2% level of confidence, these results can’t be relied upon to predict a valid outcome.
In this attempt to increase traffic to the donation page, we wanted to test whether adding the donate button to a more notable location at the very top of the page would lead to an increase in donor conversion rate. We also wanted to test whether adding a gift box icon to the donate button in order to match the “branded” style of the “Search” and “Menu” buttons next to it would lead to an increase in conversion rate.
The regular donate button and “branded” donate button appeared in the top header sitewide for both the desktop and mobile experiences. 50% of traffic saw the control with no button, and the two treatments received 25% of traffic each.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a donate button to the top header for site visitors led to a significant increase in traffic to the donation page. The first treatment saw a significant 2,694% increase in clicks to the donation page compared to the control group with no button, while the second treatment saw an even higher 2,866.2% increase in clicks at a 99.9% level of confidence. This result suggests that making the donation button more prominent and easily accessible can greatly impact the amount of traffic led to the donation page.
However, in terms of donations, the first treatment lead to a 9.3% decrease in donor conversion rate (at an invalid 14.5% level of confidence), and the second treatment led to a near-valid 64% decrease in donor conversion rate compared to the control.
While invalid, these results may suggest that by more prominently displaying the donate button and increasing traffic to the donation page, people who do click on the button may be less motivated to give than someone in the control experience who seeks out the donation page in order to support the organization.
Future experimentation is needed to understand what motivates visitors of The Daily Signal to make a gift to support their work. Experiment ideas include testing alternative copy on the donation page to motivate more viewers of the site to give, and testing an alternate icon that aligns more strongly with the organization's mission.

experiments.title: How letting lapsed members know that any gift they give, no matter the size, are appreciated affects the click-through and conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Letting lapsed members know that any gift they give, no matter the size, is appreciated will increase the click-through and conversion rate because they will know their gift is helping support the mission
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 04/01/2024
We conducted this email experiment for The Heritage Foundation’s Members Card campaign. The email asked lapsed members to give, in return, they would receive a Members Card. The email also announces a matching gift opportunity. The conclusion of the email includes a call to action to donate.
We wanted to know if more lapsed members would click if they knew they would receive a Member’s Card no matter how much they gave. The treatment included the phrase, “Any amount you give is greatly appreciated” as part of the call to action.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that explicitly stating "Any amount you give is greatly appreciated" in the call to action message decreased click-through rates for lapsed members in The Heritage Foundation's Members Card campaign. The treatment version with this added phrase saw a 34.1% decrease in Clicks for All Traffic compared to the control version without the phrase. The conversion rate for the treatment email was also lower.

From this experiment, it is clear that the additional message about any amount being appreciated did not resonate positively with lapsed members. In future experimentation, it would be advisable to avoid incorporating similar messaging that emphasizes the size of the donation being irrelevant or downplays the importance of the gift. Instead, focusing on the impact of the donation in supporting the mission and achieving tangible outcomes may be more effective in encouraging lapsed members to re-engage and contribute.

Additionally, it is essential to consider the messaging context and align it closely with the organization's mission and values to ensure relevance and connection with the target audience. Experimentation should continue to test different messaging approaches, calls to action, and value propositions to identify what resonates best with lapsed members and drives higher engagement and conversion rates.

The Heritage Foundation is running a survey about election fraud through paid Facebook advertising. The instant donation page that follows the survey page had a lot of organizational and repetitive language that focused on the problem of election fraud and how Heritage is fighting back.
We decided to test simplifying the donation ask and removing the organizational language to see if we could increase the instant conversion rate. We hypothesize it will reduce the cognitive load and eliminate barriers to understanding.

The key learnings from this experiment are that simplifying the message and removing organizational language from an instant donation page can significantly increase donor conversion rates. In this case, the treatment saw an 88.1% increase in Transactions for All Traffic with a 99.1% level of confidence, indicating a clear impact on donor behavior.

Moreover, Transactions - One-Time experienced a remarkable 959% increase with a 99% level of confidence, demonstrating the effectiveness of streamlining the donation ask and making it more straightforward for potential donors.

It is crucial to apply these learnings in future experimentation by focusing on clarity, simplicity, and immediacy in donation requests. By removing unnecessary complexity and organizational language, nonprofit organizations can improve conversion rates and drive more donations. This experiment highlights the importance of catering to the fast-paced nature of online interactions and ensuring that donation pages are optimized for quick and easy contributions.
