experiments.title: How placing an additional call to action higher in the copy of a fundraising email for members affects the click-through rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Adding another call to action sooner in a fundraising email for current members will increase the click-through and conversion rate because they already support this organization and understand how their gift will be beneficial.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/28/2024
We conducted this email experiment for The Heritage Foundation’s Members Card campaign. The email asked members to give, in return, they would receive a Members Card. The email also announces a matching gift opportunity. The conclusion of the email includes a call to action to donate.
We wanted to know if members would click if a call to action was placed earlier in the body of the email. Since members are familiar with the mission, values, and issues of the Heritage Foundation they wouldn’t need as much information to make an additional donation.
The treatment included a one-sentence call-to-action after the second paragraph of the email.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Key learnings from this experiment include:

1. Adding a call to action sooner in a fundraising email for current members can significantly increase click-through and conversion rates.

2. Providing a clear and concise call to action early in the email can effectively prompt members to take action.

3. Current members of The Heritage Foundation are more likely to respond to donation requests since they already understand the organization's mission and values.

Note: The treatment email also received more conversations, though not at a significant statistical difference.

In future experimentation, it is important to continue testing the placement and wording of calls to action in emails to optimize engagement and donation rates. Utilizing the knowledge that current members are more likely to donate, future emails can be structured to emphasize the impact of their contributions and make it easy for them to support the organization. Additionally, incorporating matching gift opportunities and other incentives can further motivate members to donate.

experiments.title: How mentioning a match offer sooner in a fundraising email to non-members affects the click-through and conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Adding the match gift offer sooner in a fundraising email to members will increase the click-through rate because the incentive to give is given greater emphasis.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/28/2024
We conducted this email experiment for The Heritage Foundation’s Members Card campaign.  The email asked non-members to give, in return, they would receive a Members Card. The email also announces a matching gift opportunity. The conclusion of the email includes a call to action to donate.
We wanted to know if non-members would click if the matching gift offer was placed earlier in the body of the email. The treatment included a one-sentence call-to-action that mentioned the matching gift opportunity after the second paragraph of the email.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding the matching gift offer sooner in a fundraising email did not increase the click-through rate for The Heritage Foundation's Members Card campaign. The treatment saw a 7.6% decrease in clicks compared to the control group. This suggests that the incentive to give may not be as effective when emphasized early in the email.
Note: The conversion rate also decreased from the treatment email.
In future experimentation, it is important to carefully consider the placement of incentives in fundraising emails. The timing and presentation of matching gift offers and other incentives can have a significant impact on donor response rates. It may be beneficial to test different approaches to determine the most effective strategy for motivating recipients to donate. Additionally, it's essential to continue testing and analyzing different variables to optimize email fundraising campaigns for greater success.

experiments.title: How adding 3 links to a fundraising email for non-members affects the click-through rate.
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that having three calls to action throughout an email for non-members will achieve a higher click-through rate and lead to an increase in donations because it provides more opportunity to take action.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/01/2024 - 03/01/2024
We conducted this email experiment for The Heritage Foundation’s Founder’s Day campaign. In the email, the President thanked non-members for their generosity and the issues America is facing. The conclusion of the email includes a call to action to donate.
We wanted to know if having three hyperlinked calls to action placed at the beginning, middle, and end of the email would encourage non-members to click more than having only one call to action at the end.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that having three calls to action throughout an email for non-members resulted in a significantly higher click-through rate compared to having only one call to action at the end. The treatment group, which had three hyperlinked calls to action placed at the beginning, middle, and end of the email, saw a 100.2% increase in clicks for all traffic with a 100.0% statistical level of confidence.

Note: While the click-through rate increased the donation conversion rate was the same for both the control and treatment email.

In future experimentation, this learning should be applied by incorporating multiple calls to action strategically throughout emails or communication pieces. By providing more opportunities for recipients to take action, it increases the likelihood of engagement and conversion. Additionally, testing different placements and wording for these calls to action could further optimize the approach to maximize results. This experiment underscores the importance of experimentation and testing in marketing strategies to identify the most effective tactics for achieving goals.

experiments.title: How highlighting that only a small minority people donate after taking a survey affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that highlighting that only a small minority people donate for survey takers will achieve an increase in conversion rate because  it will appeal to the ideal donor, who are attracted to the idea of being in a small minority of people who were willing to do the “right thing” rather than the “popular thing”. because   it will appeal to the ideal donor, who are attracted to the idea of being in a small minority of people who were willing to do the “right thing” rather than the “popular thing”.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/19/2024 - 01/31/2024
The Heritage Foundation had been running a Voter Fraud survey for several months. The survey was incredibly cost-effecient at getting emails, but the donor conversion rate had been less than one percent.
They had seen other tests that highlighted the fact that “less than 1%” of people who take the survey are willing to take the next step and donate. This language had increased conversion because it appealed to the ideal donor, who was attracted to the idea of being in a small minority of people who were willing to do the “right thing” rather than the “popular thing”.
They inserted this language in two places, and split the traffic to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are:
1. Highlighting that only a small minority of people donate for survey takers can significantly increase the conversion rate. The treatment group, which included this language, experienced a 105.4% increase in Transactions for All Traffic compared to the control group.
2. The use of language that appeals to the ideal donor, emphasizing the importance of doing the "right thing" rather than the "popular thing," can be an effective strategy to attract donations. This was evidenced by the significant increase of 91.7% in Transactions - One-Time for the treatment group.
This is an application of social identity theory. This theory suggests that people tend to categorize themselves and others into various social groups. Supporting the underdog can be related to group identity and solidarity with a particular group.
Based on these learnings, it is recommended to continue using the language that highlights the small minority of people who donate in future experimentation. This messaging appears to resonate with the target audience and create a sense of exclusivity and righteousness in supporting the cause. However, it would also be beneficial to continuously test and optimize this messaging to ensure its continued effectiveness and relevance.

experiments.title: How leading a membership ask email with the problem for non-members affects click-through rate and donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that leading an email with the problem will increase email click-through rate because it will give the reader more context as to why they should renew their membership with Heritage.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/16/2024 - 01/16/2024
The Heritage Foundation is running thier annual Kickoff Campaign to acquire members. You can become a member by giving a gift of any size, one-time or recurring. This experiment was ran on the first email of the campaign for non-members to Heritage asking them to activate their membership. For this test, we wanted to see how non-members would react to leading the email with a question that poses the problem Heritage is trying to solve rather than leading the email with a direct ask to become a member. In doing so, we believe users will be more likely to click through to the donation page and activate their membership because the leading question allows them to relate to the issue and want to be apart of the change.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that leading an email with a problem statement did not increase email click-through rates for new members. In fact, the treatment group experienced a 39% decrease in clicks compared to the control group. In addition, the treatment group saw a 61% decrease in donations with a 99.9% level of confidence.

Based on this result, it is clear that the approach of posing a problem as the lead in the email is not effective in motivating non-members to click through and make a donation. In future experimentation, it would be advisable to consider alternative approaches to engage non-members and encourage them to become members. This could involve highlighting the benefits of membership, showcasing successful outcomes of past campaigns, or offering exclusive content or promotions for members. Additionally, it would be beneficial to conduct further research to understand the reasons behind the negative response to the problem statement approach, such as through qualitative interviews or surveys.

experiments.title: How a simplified message that focuses only on impact in the final email of a high urgency campaign affects conversions for lapsed members
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that simplifying the message and only focusing on the impact in the final email of the campaign will produce a higher conversion rate because the treatment email presents a more straightforward and concise message, which can be easier for recipients to understand quickly.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 02/06/2024
The Heritage Foundation is running thier annual Kickoff Campaign to acquire members. You can become a member by giving a gift of any size, one-time or recurring. This experiment was ran for lapsed members to Heritage asking them to reactivate their membership. For this test, we wanted to see how a concise message that only focuses on their impact in the final email of the campaign would affect conversion rates. We believe a clear and simple call-to-action may lead to a better response as it reduces cognitive load for the reader.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that simplifying the message and focusing only on the impact in the final email of the campaign resulted in a higher conversion rate. The treatment email, which presented a more straightforward and concise message, saw a 37.4% increase in clicks compared to the control email with a 96.4% level of confidence. Additionally, we saw a 15.4% decrease in donor conversion with only a 43% level of confidence which tells us that the change in email copy did not affect the motivation to give on the donation page.
Based on this learning, future experimentation should prioritize clear and simple call-to-action messages that highlight the impact of the organization. By reducing cognitive load for the readers and making it easier for them to understand the value of their membership, we can improve the conversion rates and encourage more individuals to reactivate their memberships.
Additionally, it is important to continue conducting A/B testing to validate and refine these findings. By testing different variations of messages, designs, and strategies, we can further optimize our campaigns and ensure that we are effectively engaging our audience.

experiments.title: How removing an image in an MMS message affects opt-out rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that removing an image from an MMS message for lapsed donors will achieve a decrease in opt-out rate because it looks less like marketing and more like a text a human would send.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/20/2024
The Heritage Foundation was running SMS and MMS ads as part of their kickoff campaign. They had leveraged texting because of the personal nature of the communication, two-way conversation ability, and high deliverability, and wanted to test a P2P MMS platform However, they wondered if their personal message was undermined by the image they sent alongside the message, since that’s not something that people usually send along with 1:1 communications.
They split their list in half and sent half of the list an MMS without an image to see if it increased conversion rate. This ended up converting the message to an SMS message, which should be noted.
However, they also wanted to monitor other metrics that might be affected by this, including opt-out rate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that removing an image from an MMS message for lapsed donors resulted in a decrease in opt-out rate.

Based on this experiment, it can be concluded that lapsed donors may be more likely to disengage with a message stream that includes an image. The personal nature of the communication may be undermined by the image, as it might not be seen as a genuine message from a human.

When donors do not see a communication as "marketing", then you have the opportunity to transcend a transactional relationship and open a legitimate communication channel.

experiments.title: How does calling out the loss of exclusive membership benefits for members affect conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that calling out what they will lose by not renewing their membership for existing members will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because the fear of losing something can be a more powerful motivator than the prospect of gaining something of equal value.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/31/2024
The Heritage Foundation is running thier annual Kickoff Campaign to acquire members. You can become a member by giving a gift of any size, one-time or recurring. This experiment was sent to existing members in 2023 to Heritage asking them to renew their membership. For this test, we wanted to see how existing members would react to calling out what they will lose by not renewing their membership in 2024.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that calling out what existing members will lose by not renewing their membership can be a powerful motivator for donations. The treatment that mentioned the potential loss saw a 41.9% increase in donations compared to the control group with a 99.4% level of confidence.
This suggests that emphasizing the negative consequences of not renewing membership can be more effective than highlighting the positive benefits or incentives of renewing. It taps into the fear of missing out and the desire to avoid losing something valuable.
To apply this learning in future experimentation, it would be beneficial to test different variations of messaging that focus on the potential loss. This could include highlighting specific benefits or privileges that members would no longer have access to, such as exclusive content, events, or discounts. It may also be effective to use personalized messaging that reminds individuals of the specific contributions they have made and the impact they have had as a member.
Overall, the key takeaway is to leverage the fear of loss as a motivator in membership renewal campaigns. By highlighting what individuals will miss out on if they don't renew, nonprofits like The Heritage Foundation can increase the likelihood of conversions and maintain their supporter base.

experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that leading an email with a question that poses the problem did not increase email click-through rate for existing members to renew their membership with The Heritage Foundation. In fact, the treatment group showed a 55.8% decrease in clicks compared to the control group. In addition, the treatment group saw a 23.5% decrease in donor conversion with a 97.6% level of confidence.

These findings suggest that existing members may not respond well to a problem-focused approach when it comes to renewing their membership. It is possible that they already have a strong understanding of the problem and are more interested in the direct ask to renew their support.

In future experimentation, it may be more effective to lead the email with a direct ask to renew membership rather than focusing on the problem. Additionally, it could be beneficial to test other strategies such as emphasizing the impact of their support or offering a specific benefit or incentive for renewing. These approaches may help to engage existing members and encourage them to click through and renew their membership.

experiments.title: How versioning ad copy to lapsed donors affects conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that versioning copy to acknowledge support for past donors will achieve an increase in conversion rate because it will personalize the ask and acknowledge their previous behavior.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 12/18/2023
The Heritage Foundation was showing ads to lapsed donors to reactivate them to active donors. However, these ads were originally developed for acquisition. They wanted to test the impact of adding “acknowledgement” language of the donors’ past giving history to the ad copy, with the thought that it might personalize the copy and make the donor realize that they were being spoken to directly.
They launched a test against the control language to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding "acknowledgement" language to the ad copy for lapsed donors did not lead to an increase in conversion rate. In fact, it resulted in a significant decrease in donations compared to the control group.

Based on this outcome, it is clear that personalizing the ask and acknowledging past support did not resonate with lapsed donors in this particular campaign. Therefore, it is important for future experimentation to focus on other strategies or messaging approaches to reactivate lapsed donors.

It is also important to consider the statistical significance of the results. With a 97.1% level of confidence, we can be highly confident that the decrease in donations is not due to random chance. This strengthens the validity of the findings and emphasizes the importance of using the control group as a benchmark for comparison.

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to explore alternative tactics for re-engaging lapsed donors. This could involve testing different messaging techniques, creative approaches, or targeting strategies. It is also essential to continue tracking and analyzing key metrics such as conversion rate, donation amount, and donor retention to assess the effectiveness of any changes made.

experiments.title: How an eBook acquisition offer dear reader affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using an eBook acquisition offer dear reader for news article readers will achieve an increase in instant donation rate because readers will be more motivated to donate after receiving a valuable ebook for free.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/07/2023 - 12/12/2023
The Heritage Foundation regularly uses dear reader ads to promote direct appeals or acquisition offers on The Daily Signal news articles.
During their year-end campaign, Heritage wanted to know whether using an acquisition offer, specifically their Guide to the Constitution eBook, in a dear reader instead of a year-end direct appeal would lead to an increase in donations.
The CYE (calendar year end) dear reader contained a countdown timer with the amount of time left to donate in the campaign, directly asked the viewer to “Donate Today”, and led directly to the year end donation page. The Guide to the Constitution dear reader contained an eBook image mockup and asked the viewer to “GET YOUR ACCESS NOW”, leading to a landing page where readers could request the eBook and then to an offer-specific donation page.
The dear readers appeared on every Daily Signal article sitewide.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that using an eBook acquisition offer in a dear reader instead of a direct appeal for donations can significantly increase the instant donation rate.
We ran this experiment for six days at the beginning of Heritage's year end campaign until it validated with a 97.5% level of confidence in favor of the treatment. The treatment group, which received the eBook offer, saw a 148.2% increase in donations compared to the control group, which received the CYE direct appeal messaging.
This suggests that providing readers with a valuable incentive, such as a free eBook, can enhance their motivation to donate. Offering something of value in return for their donation can make the action feel more rewarding and increase their likelihood of taking immediate action.
The results of this experiment should be used in future experimentation by incorporating acquisition offers, such as eBooks, in dear reader ads. This strategy can help to optimize donation rates and improve the effectiveness of direct appeals. Further testing might include adding a fundraising campaign's direct appeal language to an existing content offer during the campaign to determine whether the language provides an extra, timely incentive to give.

The Heritage Foundation is offering an ebook about the Second Amendment. In order to try and increase the email sign-up rate on the landing page, they wanted to test adding a quote from a legal fellow about the importance of your second amendment rights.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a quote from a legal fellow about the importance of second amendment rights to the landing page for a second amendment ebook download had a positive impact on the email sign-up rate. The treatment group, which saw the landing page with the quote, had an 11.8% increase in Emails Acquired compared to the control group. This result is statistically significant with a confidence level of 95.8%.
Based on this learning, it is recommended to include quotes or statements that emphasize the value and importance of the ebook content in future landing pages. This can help potential downloaders understand the significance of the ebook, which may encourage them to sign up for the email list.
Additionally, it is important to continue conducting A/B experiments to further optimize the email sign-up rate on landing pages. By testing different elements, such as quotes, headlines, and calls-to-action, the organization can continue to improve the conversion rate and acquire a larger audience for future campaigns.

experiments.title: How a donation interruptor including a monthly upgrade ask affects recurring donation rates for previous donors
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that presenting a monthly upgrade ask for one-time, returning donors during the donation process will achieve higher recurring donation rates by increasing its visibility at the decision-making point and prominently communicating its value.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/15/2023 - 03/25/2023
In an effort to increase recurring donor acquisition for a public policy organization during a recent high-urgency campaign, we wanted to measure the effect of interrupting the donation process with a pop-up asking the donor to become a monthly supporter instead of making only a one-time gift.
This pop-up was set up as an A/B test that would only fire for 50% of donors who were in the process of completing a one-time gift under $100, reiterating the value of becoming a monthly donor, both in terms of impact and exclusive benefits, and asking them to a) make a recurring monthly donation of $20 or b) proceed with their one-time gift.
This is a follow-up experiment to a previous donation interruptor test in order to test whether less body copy, and thus a better mobile viewing experience, would lead to an increase in recurring donations.
This A/B test targeted only the member donation page (previous donors of $25 or more).
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that interrupting the donation process with a pop-up asking donors to become monthly supporters instead of making a one-time donation result in a decrease in donations for all traffic.
After running this experiment for 9 days, we saw a 64.6% increase in monthly giving with a 81.6% level of confidence. As a result, this experiment is invalid.
We also saw a 20.9% decrease in overall donations with a 99.9% level of confidence.
We believe that multiple factors contributed to producing this result:
Forcing donors to make another choice after submitting their one-time gift may produce friction. Asking donors to commit to a recurring gift in this case may be too strong of an ask and be perceived as pushy or invasive.Asking donors to join a Leadership Club may produce confusion at what membership entails, how it is different from "renewing" Heritage membership, and seem like too much of a commitment as opposed to a one-time gift to receive a Heritage 2023 member card.Results may be inaccurate due to the experiment recording all page viewers as part of the sample, even before they've seen either the control or donation interruptor experience. The sample size should be adjusted to apply only to those who have clicked the donation form submit button, excluding viewers who land on the page but navigate away before seeing either experience. This will isolate the experiment data only to those who donate or attempt to donate.
Based on these results and the data concerns, we will explore additional testing with added technical feature to record results only when the user clicks on the submit button and sees either experience at that step.
experiments.fluxMetrics:

experiments.title: How does conversational, donor-focused language affect donation rates on an instant donation page.
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using conversational, donor-focused language on an instant donation page will achieve higher donation rates by putting the donor at the center of the solution and making the appeal more relatable.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/07/2023 - 04/14/2023
In an effort to improve the performance of an instant donation page for a content offer that was seeing healthy sign-up rates, we wanted to know how a more conversational tone and donor-focused language would affect donation rates.
We noticed that the instant donation page, as it existed, spoke a lot about the difference the organization was going to make—but we believed there was an opportunity to frame it in such a way that would put the donor at the center of the solution with more conversational language that would forge a stronger connection with the reader.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that using conversational and donor-focused language on an instant donation page can significantly increase donation rates. The treatment showed a 54.5% increase in donations for all types of traffic with a 97.9% level of confidence. Desktop visitors had a 1184% increase, returning visitors had a 2138% increase, referral visitors had a 71% increase, and 18-24 visitors had a 7421% increase in donations, all with a 100% level of confidence.
In future experimentation, it is important to continue using conversational and donor-focused language to engage potential donors and establish a connection between them and the organization. It may also be beneficial to segment target audiences and tailor messaging to specific age groups or referral sources to further optimize donation rates. Regular experiments and testing will help identify the most effective language and strategies for donation pages.

0% increase in traffic

experiments.title: How a donation interruptor including a monthly upgrade ask affects recurring donation rates
experiments.researchQuestion:
We believe that
presenting a monthly upgrade ask for one-time donors during the donation process will achieve higher recurring donation rates by increasing its visibility at the decision-making point and prominently communicating its value.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/16/2023 - 01/30/2023
In an effort to increase recurring donor acquisition for a public policy organization during a recent high-urgency campaign, we wanted to measure the effect of interrupting the donation process with a pop-up asking the donor to become a monthly supporter instead of making only a one-time gift.
This pop-up was set up as an A/B test that would only fire for 50% of donors who were in the process of completing a one-time gift under $100, reiterating the value of becoming a monthly donor, both in terms of impact and exclusive benefits, and asking them to a) make a recurring monthly donation of $20 or b) proceed with their one-time gift.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running this experiment for approximately 2 weeks, we observed a  93% increase in monthly giving with a 99.2% level of confidence.
We believe that several motivating factors came into play in producing this result:
First, the interrupter pop-up dramatically increased the visibility of the recurring-giving option. Second, it clearly and thoroughly laid out the value of becoming a recurring donor in terms of impact and exclusive benefitsThird, by creating a mental interruption, it may have given donors an opportunity to reset the mental process of completing their donation (transaction forms being as prevalent as they are in the digital space may cue a certain degree of auto-pilot during the transaction process) and allowed them the time to consider their options.And finally, the pop-up treatment positioned monthly giving as an opportunity rather than an ask, giving agency to the donor and casting them in the role of someone who is able to do (and get) more than they would by making a one-time gift.
And while at first glance it appears that the treatment produced a 12.6% decrease in donations and a 16.4% decrease in revenue, upon further examination it appears unlikely that these dips were caused by the pop-up since viewers technically "enter" the experiment upon page-load and the pop-up does not appear until they have already made the decision to give. Additionally, language in the headline of the pop-up indicates to the donor that their donation has not yet been processed to reduce instances of closing the browser window before the donation process is complete.
This leads us to believe that any decrease in donations and/or revenue is more likely a result of the visitor deciding to give when viewing the page, rather than a result of the pop-up itself.
Based on these results, we will explore additional opportunities to globalize this learning.

0% increase in traffic
				× 93.6% increase in conversion rate

experiments.title: How a more detailed landing page with longer copy affects sign-up rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding more detail to the copy for landing page visitors will achieve higher sign-up rates because it will create greater urgency around the issue the asset addresses and better explain what the prospect will gain from accepting the offer. because it will create greater urgency around the issue the asset addresses and better explain what the prospect will gain from accepting the offer.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/04/2023 - 02/01/2023
For this experiment for a public policy organization, we wanted to know how longer value proposition copy affected sign-up rates. Because sign-up rates were so low, we redesigned several elements of the landing page in an effort to convey more value and urgency around the offer—an interactive map that scores the integrity of elections in each state.
We crafted the headline to include the word free and show what someone would learn from the offer. In the body copy, we included some specific examples of alleged voter fraud to increase urgency around the problem and thus the need for the resource. And in the bullets, we removed the “and more” bullet and instead included all 12 metrics the scorecard used to assess election integrity.
We ran this experiment for 30 days with 50% of traffic seeing the control and 50% of traffic seeing the treatment
experiments.keyLearnings:
At the conclusion of this test, we observed a 12.7% decrease in sign-up rates with a 99.9% level of confidence, proving our hypothesis that longer value-proposition copy would increase sign-up rates.

Upon review of these results, we believe that the length of the copy gave the appearance of a "wall" of text the visitor would have to read, creating enough friction for the prospect to click away. The longer copy may have done more to halt the momentum of the prospect than it did encourage them to sign up.

Because the landing page visitor has already expressed interest in both the offer and the problem it addresses, we hypothesize that it may be more effective to reduce the amount of copy and create a more frictionless path to acquisition once the prospect lands on the sign-up page.

As such, we plan to follow up this experiment with a new treatment that removes the first 3 paragraphs of copy from the control (much of which is restated from the Facebook ads through which the prospect has clicked—and get straight to the point by immediately telling the reader what they will gain by accessing the scorecard, making it easier and quicker for them to sign up.

0% increase in traffic

experiments.title: How centering an ask around a specific initiative affects donation rates for house file traffic during a Giving Tuesday campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that centering an ask around a specific initiative will increase donation rates during a Giving Tuesday campaign by adding exclusivity and tangibility to the appeal.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 11/30/2022
In an effort to help a public policy client differentiate their message on a day when we know that donors will be inundated with a flood of appeals, we wanted to know how focusing the messaging around a specific initiative would affect donation rates during a one-day Giving Tuesday campaign.
We ran this test for one day, splitting house file traffic between a control that focused on high-level value language and a treatment that focused on supporting a specific initiative.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running this experiment for one day, with one email appeal sent in the morning and one in the afternoon, the treatment focusing its messaging around a specific initiative produced a 275% statistically valid lift in donation rates with a 99.9% level of confidence.
We believe that one of the main contributing factors to this result is that focusing the appeal on a specific initiative helps to increase the perceived value of a donor's gift by adding exclusivity, authority, tangibility, and specificity to the appeal.
The initiative-specific appeal offered a tangible solution to an issue recipients care about by letting them know exactly what their gift would support, and the concrete steps our client would be taking on their behalf to influence the outcome donors desired.
It also leveraged exclusivity by offering a solution that is unique to this organization and authority by making it clear that they would be working with a broad coalition of other influential organizations to get the job done.
Specificity adds texture to the appeal while building trust by not just saying what the organization planned to do but also how they planned to do it.
Finally, we believe all of these elements combined to differentiate the message of the appeal on a day when donors received a large concentration of emails which were typically focused on very high-level messaging containing nearly identical language ... ultimately making the treatment appeal stand out from the crowd, hold their prospects' attention, and motivate them to support the initiative at hand.

experiments.title: How a program-specific value proposition impacted donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a Daily Signal focused page increase donor conversion for visitors coming from a news-focused email?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/02/2017 - 08/16/2017
During The Heritage Foundation Board Match fundraising campaign, we wanted to incorporate one of their more popular programs (The Daily Signal) into a fundraising appeal email. The goal was to show the broad impact of the organization by talking about the various aspects of the organization.
Historically, we had utilized the same landing page throughout the campaign for each email. This is something that we had tested into, finding that switching landing pages didn’t often result in any significant lifts so we determined it was not worth the extra time and energy.
However, in this situation, we felt that the brand recognition and popularity of the program may be worth creating its own page. We decided to test that hypothesis by creating a new landing page that was branded as The Daily Signal and focused its value proposition on that one aspect of Heritage.
experiments.keyLearnings: The landing page that reinforced The Daily Signal brand and focused the value proposition solely on that aspect of the organization increased donations by 272%. This emphasizes the importance of creating a cohesive message all the way through the donation process. It also all verifies a trend that we had been noticing in the last several month, that The Daily Signal is a viable program for raising funds with.

experiments.title: How the amount of value proposition on a page affects email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: How does the amount of value proposition on a page affect email acquisition rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/02/2016 - 12/09/2016
The Heritage Foundation was promoting the Index of Military Strength, a publication that gave insight into the U.S. government’s military spending and readiness. They put together a landing page to promote this publication, but had the feeling that their copy was too short and didn’t properly “sell” the offer.
They created a second page that listed out many additional details about the Index, expanding the value proposition. Though the copy was much longer and it pushed the email acquisition form down the page and below the fold, they hypothesized that it would result in increased signups.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The added value proposition increased email acquisition rate by 25.6%, which meant many more emails were acquired for the same amount of ad spend. This reiterated the principle that, although a free offer doesn't have a price, visitors must be sold on an offer all the way up to the point that they accept it by submitting the form.

This prompted more testing to add more evidentials into the page that supported the additional claims of value.

experiments.title: How one-click giving for dual ask gifts impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: How will simplifying the giving process impact revenue and conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/16/2015 - 12/27/2015
The Heritage Foundation has a unique process where, after every online gift, the donor is immediately prompted to make a second gift to their sister organization, Heritage Action for America. All the donor has to do for this second gift is enter their donation amount since we already have their address and credit card information from the initial form. This secondary ask generally has a 5% to 8% conversion rate for donors making that second gift.
We had the opportunity to test a new feature in their giving system that allowed for one-click giving. Existing donors would only have to enter their zip code and credit card number to make a donation.  We decided to test this functionality out in their 2015 calendar year end campaign and monitor the impact on all aspects of the donation process.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Interestingly enough, the one-click giving process did not have any statistically significant impact on the initial donor conversion. Our hypothesis is that the decreased friction that came from the simplified form was balanced out but the confusion the process caused. While it was a simpler process; it was different than what the donors were use to.

However, while the immediate conversion was not impacted, the follow on donation opportunity saw a 90.4% decrease in conversion with the one-click giving. Since the donors did not have to enter their address or credit card for the first form, they would have to enter it for the second form (due to system limitations). This added significant friction to the second step in the process that was enough to significantly impact the gifts made to Heritage Action.

experiments.researchQuestion: Will the more active “challenge” language increase the amount of traffic being driven compared to the more passive “announcement” language?
experiments.summary:

As part of the Board Challenge campaign, we ran Facebook advertising targeted at known donors and audiences who matched the profile of a Heritage supporter. The landing page had a quote from a well-known public figure so we were able to use them in the advertisement to help drive traffic. We wanted to discover what messaging approach would allow us to drive the most traffic.
experiments.keyLearnings: The "challenge" language captured the interest of those that saw the advertisement. This resulted in a 45% increase to visitors to the landing page.

experiments.title: How a direct ask about planned giving affects clickthrough rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Which approach will attract more clicks?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/06/2015 - 04/30/2015
The Heritage Legacy Society sent a prospecting email to ask Heritage Foundation members if they planned to include the organization in their estate planning. They didn’t know the best way to approach this through email, so they tested two distinct approaches: a direct ask, supported by facts about tax planning, against a story-based approach with a personal testimony from a Heritage Legacy Society member.
experiments.keyLearnings: This told us that we don't necessarily need to "soften" the ask with a story. The story might introduce friction as an estate commitment is a personal decision, not one based on third-party influence.

experiments.title: How third-party validation affects email acquisition for a free digital offer
experiments.researchQuestion: Will increasing clarity and adding social proof increase email acquisition?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/29/2016 - 07/01/2016
The Heritage Foundation was giving away a digital version of their popular 475-page Guide to the Constitution in exchange for an email address. They had a landing page that was converting 58% of visitors. But this meant that 42% of visitors weren’t downloading the digital Guide, so their team wanted to try four tweaks to increase conversion.
One confusing part of the offer was that the digital version of the Guide was in the form of a microsite, so they were really offering “access” rather than a digital download. This caused potential confusion to the user, so they marketed the offer as a digital download (since there was an available PDF).
Then, they launched an A/B test to see if the new page increased conversion.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The new treatment converted 65.4% of visitors to accept the offer -- an increase of 11.2%! This shows that the new landing page tweaks did in fact increase clarity and add some social proof that helped convince the visitor to take action.

While this is a great lift and results in more emails for the same spend, it still means that 35% of our traffic is not converting—which means more testing and optimization is needed!

experiments.title: How emphasizing recurring giving impacts overall revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: Which donation page treatment will yield the greatest number of donors and revenue?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/29/2016 - 12/07/2016
In the latest in the series of optimization experiments on the main Heritage donation forms, we implemented a new donation form and value proposition that emphasized the value of recurring giving. In addition to the expanded and more visual value proposition, the new form also laid out a cohesive product-level value proposition on why recurring giving is so important.
With this new treatment, we ran a test across several of the most highly trafficked donation pages to see which would result in more gifts.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment had interesting results. The new treatment resulted in a 54% decrease in the number of donors acquired. However, since the goal of the treatment was to acquire recurring donors given their increased value, we need to analyze the impact on that segment. In the case of recurring donors, the new form does see a 87% increase in acquisition.

Assuming that the average recurring donor will last 12 months, we then factored these two results and discovered that after 12 months, the new treatment would effectively break even on revenue compared to the original control. With a 54% drop in donors, continuing with the treatment would seem to be a pretty risky bet on long-term revenue potential. Based upon this analysis, we decided to end the test and continue with the current control.

experiments.title: How reinforcing the value proposition and adding a call-to-action affects donor conversion.
experiments.researchQuestion: Will adding a call-to-action and reinforcing the value proposition improve donor conversion and revenue?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/13/2016 - 06/13/2016
In an effort to optimize the main donation page for The Heritage Foundation, we took a look at the copy that was currently there to find ways to improve and increase the force of the value proposition. While the existing copy was strong and provided solid evidentials, we noticed it was missing an important element in the copy, an ask. Our hypothesis was that we would be able to increase donor conversion and overall revenue by adding copy, to what was already there, that included a call-to-action and reinforcement of the value proposition.
experiments.keyLearnings:
We had arrived at the original copy through a series of prior experiments. As a result, we were relatively confident that it adequately communicated the value proposition of The Heritage Foundation, but by simply adding an additional paragraph that reinforced that message and asking them to make a gift, we were able to not only get more donors to give, but give even more generously. The treatment increased donor conversion by 49%.
Additionally, we saw an increase in average gift by 62.9%. While the average gift lift had not yet statistically validated by the time the test was shut off, the approaching validity seems to indicate that we would be experiencing a combined lift to revenue of 143.5%.
This experiment reinforced the importance of increasing the force of the value proposition by reminding people of what their gift will do and always having a call-to-action in the copy.

experiments.title: How the strength of the value proposition affects pay-per-click donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a stronger value proposition increase donor conversion from search engine marketing?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/01/2016 - 05/03/2016
The Heritage Foundation had run pay-per-click campaigns on brand terms (like “Heritage Foundation”, “Heritage Foundation membership”) for years. These campaigns ran at a positive ROI and gave users who expressed intent through search a clear path to make their gifts.
Because users had high motivation that was expressed through their search terms, a very transactional landing page had tested well. But Heritage wanted to see if a more emotional appeal might improve conversion — one that told a narrative story and utilized the four pillars of a successful value proposition: appeal, exclusivity, clarity, and credibility. The page also used photography, different background, and stacked member benefits right before it asked for the conversion.
They launched an A/B test to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment page with the more thorough value proposition and member benefits produced a 44.1% lift in donor conversion. This tells us that we can actually affect donor motivation -- even though someone is looking to make a gift, they aren't always entirely convinced. The strength of the value proposition must capture them where they are and assure them even more in their decision.

We are never done "selling" the donation until they submit their gift. That's why landing pages must always leverage each of the four pillars of the value proposition to maximize the force with which it is delivered.

experiments.title: How testing a new offer in a “Dear Reader” ad affected clicks and donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that offering a relevant, time-sensitive resource for website visitors will achieve increase clicks and donations.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/11/2020 - 10/24/2020
As we prepared to launch a new offer to the housefile and in acquisition, we were faced with a dilemma. The new eBook offer was relevant and time-sensitive, but we didn’t want to kick out an offer that was already performing strongly and miss out on additional conversions by losing a month of run time. So instead of replacing the current Dear Reader eBook offer outright, we decided to make the new offer compete against the old offer for the spot.
experiments.keyLearnings:
What we found is that the new offer had lower performance than the current offer across the board. We saw a 32.8% decrease in conversion rate for the new eBook offer, which you can see broken down by segment below, resulting in fewer engagements in both clicks and donations. These results helped us reach a conclusion to keep the existing offer in place rather than rolling out the new eBook offer in this high-traffic Dear Reader spot. New is not always better! For future new offers, we will plan to run additional A/B tests to determine which offer will lead to maximum results instead of giving away valuable real estate to an offer with unknown performance and risk losing potential revenue and have to make up for lost time later. This experimental approach with initially rolling out to only 50% of site visitors will help us focus less on if the offer is fresh and instead care more about if the offer works. 

Observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment:
Direct Traffic visitors had a 52.4% decrease in cta click with a 100% level of confidence.
 	Email visitors had a 29.7% decrease in cta click with a 100% level of confidence.
 	Organic Search visitors had a 61.9% decrease in cta click with a 100% level of confidence.
 	Social visitors had a 49.1% decrease in cta click with a 100% level of confidence.
 	All Traffic visitors had a 32.8% decrease in cta click with a 100% level of confidence.
 	Desktop visitors had a 23.0% decrease in cta click with a 100% level of confidence.
 	Mobile visitors had a 40.4% decrease in cta click with a 100% level of confidence.
 	Tablet visitors had a 34.5% decrease in cta click with a 100% level of confidence.
 	New visitors had a 46.6% decrease in cta click with a 100% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How increasing the force of the value proposition affects revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a stronger value proposition increase donations and revenue, in spite of a different design?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/09/2016 - 03/15/2016
The Heritage Foundation was sending lots of traffic to their “Conservative Declaration”, a document that asked conservatives to unite under shared principles. Immediately after signing, the visitors were presented with an offer to become a member of The Heritage Foundation by making a donation. The entire experience took place within a single design.
Heritage had another membership page that had been performing extremely well. However, design was a key part of this page, and it didn’t match the design of the Conservative Declaration. Knowing that congruency is a key part of conversion, they decided to test it anyways to see if increasing the force of the value proposition outweighed design consistency.
experiments.keyLearnings: The treatment produced a 460% increase in revenue, despite breaking from the design of the original offer. This is a powerful learning that shows that the message is more important than the container. This also points the way to a future test to see if revenue can be further increased by working the new value proposition into the existing design and increasing congruency.

experiments.title: How a DailySignal value proposition impacted conversion on the serial ask
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a DailySignal ask for Heritage donors will achieve an increase in overall serial ask conversion.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/31/2020 - 11/01/2020
In an effort to boost overall revenue for Heritage, regardless of the designation of the funds, we wanted to test out the serial ask language for donors to the main donation form, the /join form, and the /renew donation form. Given the crossover for DailySignal readers and Heritage donors, we decided to test out a value proposition that would utilize those efforts as the reason to give a second gift. We then tested the two value propositions against each other and validated the experiment based upon the overall number of serial ask donors compared to the number of people who saw the message (THF donors).
experiments.keyLearnings: After running for a full three months, we saw a 27% decrease in overall conversion to the serial ask. With this in mind, we decided to end this experiment and moved on to testing other value propositions related to HAFA to try to improve the serial ask conversion.

experiments.title: How testing a new messaging approach affects conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Which messaging approach will be most effective at converting visitors to members and increasing revenue?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/23/2015 - 10/30/2015
As part of their membership campaign, The Heritage Foundation wanted to determine which messaging approach would be most appealing to their donors. At the start of the campaign, we created three landing page treatments based upon two new hypotheses of what the members would respond best to.
Our current control version focused on the current situation in American politics and framed it around the negative aspects of the “establishment.” Alternatively, we wanted to test a more positive tone using the same framework. The third treatment was a far more radical redesign using a more modern design that pointed to historical accomplishments and touted the benefits of membership.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Both variants resulted in a statistically significant lift

with the more radical redesign achieving a 87.2% lift over the control. This would lead us to hypothesize that the constituency is more likely to respond to a positive messaging approach. They may have frustrations with the current state of American politics but this is not what motivates them to give. They are responding to a message of hope and positive change.

				gift).

0% increase in traffic
				× 100.0% decrease in conversion rate

experiments.title: How more contextualized ad media affects click-through-rates on a Facebook ad
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using more contextualized imagery on a Facebook ad will increase click-through rates by adding clarity and appeal to an offer in order to increase its perceived value.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 10/18/2022
After turning off some underperforming Facebook ads, we wanted to introduce a new treatment to test against our previous champion. For this treatment, we wanted to know if more contextualized ad media would lead to higher click-through rates than a simple 3D render of the ebook associated with the offer. To test this concept, we created new ad media which placed the eBook (dealing with critical race theory) over an image of children in a classroom while also adding some value prop copy that aimed to demonstrate WHAT the viewer would learn by signing up for the ebook.
For this experiment, we changed only the ad creative and ran the test for 30 days.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running this experiment for 30 days, we observed a 27.5% DROP in click-through rates, with a 100% level of confidence. When analyzing this experiment, we identified a potential reason for these results:

Decreasing the size of the ebook in the treatment image, along with placing it over a busy background may have created friction for the viewer by making it unclear what they should do.

This may also have reduced the visual impact of showing what is being offered (the ebook), prompting viewers who may have otherwise been enticed by this offer to keep scrolling, not realizing a free ebook was being offered

The image may also have failed to make the intended impact because it is dark in tone, with heavy shadows and much of the subject matter obscured by the ebook, potentially making it difficult for the viewer to quickly discern what the image is trying to convey.

Overall, we believe that these factors combine to make the treatment ad rather "invisible" to the viewer, failing to do enough to catch the reader's attention, get them to stop scrolling, and click through to the offer.

The Flux Metrics analyze the three primary metrics that affect revenue (traffic, conversion rate, and average
				gift).
				This experiment produced the following results:
27.5% decrease in traffic
				× 0% increase in conversion rate
				× 0% increase in average gift

experiments.title: How previewing a pledge affects click-through rates on a Facebook ad
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that previewing a pledge on a Facebook ad will increase click-through rates by engaging the viewer and prompting them to click/tap to make their selection.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 10/18/2022
After turning off some underperforming Facebook ads, we wanted to introduce a new treatment to test against our previous champion. For this treatment, we chose a concept that has shown success in the past: previewing a choice from the offered asset in order to entice the viewer to engage and click through to the asset itself. For this experiment, we changed only the ad creative and ran this test for 30 days.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running this experiment for 30 days, we observed a 22.7% drop in click-through-rates with a 99.9% level of confidence. When analyzing these results, we identified a couple of factors that may have contributed to this decline.
First, while such a treatment has worked well for survey offers in the past, we feel that this approach may not be as effective for a pledge because we are not "asking" them to complete any action, i.e. when previewing a survey question, the preview "asks" the viewer a question ... but in the context of a pledge, the viewer simply sees a statement with a checked box beside it. This might make it unclear to the viewer that they are being asked to engage, create friction and fail to do enough to get them to stop scrolling.
Similarly, because the treatment presents a statement with a box already checked, it may simply cue viewers to agree and move on. In the control, action is encouraged by agitating urgency around the issue, likely doing more to encourage the viewer to stop, consider the situation, and want to learn more/take some action.
Lastly, we also noticed that in an attempt to strengthen the call to action, we added more copy and reduced the font size—"sign the pledge" appears much larger and eye-catching on the control than it does on the treatment, which could, again, making it clear to the viewer that there is an action to take.

22.7% decrease in traffic

experiments.title: How radically redesigning an email affected donation revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a radical redesign of the email that increases the force of the value proposition and introduces images increase response and revenue?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/15/2017 - 11/20/2017
The treatment produced a 123% increase in donations as well as an increase in revenue, which produced a big difference in return on investment. This showed the power of the increased value proposition, with the additional impact of the imagery to help the recipient visualize the offer.
This is a promising result that should be tested on more lists to ensure that there isn’t an anomaly with the first list choice.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The Heritage Foundation created a popular offer for email acquisition with their Digital Guide to the Constitution. After a visitor downloaded the Digital Guide, they were offered membership to Heritage, along with an offer to get the print copy of the Guide to the Constitution for a gift of $100. This offer flow had been quite effective at converting prospects into donors using a text-based approach that had tested well with the house file. But since acquisition mailings reached an audience of people who were not as familiar with Heritage, they created a treatment to test that increased the force of the value proposition and introduced images of the offer.

The control, which introduced the free offer with a casual greeting, focused on the content contained in the Guide. The treatment created an enemy: “Right now, liberals are waging an all-out war against our nation’s founding principles…”. Instead of a resource, it offered a “gift”, and highlighted the hard work that went into its creation, before revealing that it was created for free. The treatment email also included an image of the offer to provide clarity.

Then, Heritage ran an A/B test to determine a winner.

				gift).

0% increase in traffic
				× 123.1% increase in conversion rate
				× 0% increase in average gift

experiments.title: How the amount of value proposition on a page affects email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: How does the amount of value proposition on a page affect email acquisition rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/02/2016 - 12/09/2016
The Heritage Foundation was promoting the Index of Military Strength, a publication that gave insight into the U.S. government’s military spending and readiness. They put together a landing page to promote this publication, but had the feeling that their copy was too short and didn’t properly “sell” the offer.
They created a second page that listed out many additional details about the Index, expanding the value proposition. Though the copy was much longer and it pushed the email acquisition form down the page and below the fold, they hypothesized that it would result in increased signups.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The added value proposition increased email acquisition rate by 25.6%, which meant many more emails were acquired for the same amount of ad spend. This reiterated the principle that, although a free offer doesn't have a price, visitors must be sold on an offer all the way up to the point that they accept it by submitting the form.

This prompted more testing to add more evidentials into the page that supported the additional claims of value.

experiments.title: Discovering the Daily Signal’s value proposition with an inline donation ask
experiments.researchQuestion: Which value proposition will drive the most donations for The Daily Signal?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/18/2016 - 09/26/2016
The Daily Signal was originally launched several years back to serve as an unbiased news source. Since its launch, it has seen a significant boost in traffic with millions of people visiting each month. This site is an initiative that is run by The Heritage Foundation.
A decision was made recently to discover ways to start monetizing the traffic while keeping the actually content free for visitors. It was decided that we could position a donation ask at the end of each article. The question then became “what aspect of The Daily Signal would visitors be most willing to sponsor?” To determine this, we launched an experiment utilizing three different value proposition statements.
experiments.keyLearnings: After a month and a half of running this experiment, we determined that the "investigative journalism" and "conservative policy analysis" aspects of The Daily Signal were more appealing to the readers than the other options. This value proposition lifted donor conversion by 275%. Our hypothesis is that this statement was the one that best tapped into the unique aspects of the website. While the other value proposition statements may have been appealing to visitors, they could have been said about any number of other websites.

				gift).

0% increase in traffic
				× 275.5% increase in conversion rate
				× 0% increase in average gift

experiments.title: How the placement of an inline offer impacts conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: How does the placement of the donation advertisement impact donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/25/2017 - 06/05/2017
Moving the donation ask to the bottom of the article resulted in a 48% increase to the number of visitors going to the donation page and a 91% increase to overall donations. Our hypothesis is that allowing the visitor to experience the content before making the ask increased the clarity of the value proposition. This is what would have led to the increased volume of traffic and conversion.
experiments.keyLearnings:
On The Daily Signal website, we had been testing an inline direct ask for a donation. Given the high volume of traffic to the website, Heritage was seeing considerable revenue come from this offer. However, we began to be concerned about the visitor experience since the offer was interrupting the flow of the article.

We decided to test the impact of moving the donation ask to the bottom of the article and simply including an show inline sentence that would link to the bottom. Our belief was that even if the treatment saw no significant difference, it would be a win since it would improve the readers’ experience.

experiments.title: How a simpler, more direct value proposition affects clickthrough rate on a Facebook ad
experiments.researchQuestion: How does a shorter, more direct value proposition affect clickthrough rate on a Facebook ad?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/12/2017 - 04/10/2017
The shorter ad with the direct question produced a 122.1% increase in clickthrough rate, which showed that the elements of this ad increased the clarity of the value proposition. Longer copy can be more effective, but only if if clarifies the value proposition. In addition, the direct question to provoke the reader to respond by taking the intended action.
This success prompted further testing to isolate the variables of length and the direct question to understand how each of these played into the success of the ad.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The Heritage Foundation was promoting a quiz around the “Gadsden Flag” that led to a membership offer with the flag as a premium. They had created two versions of a Facebook ad, as they had seen some research that longer, more engaging copy could increase clickthrough and conversion rate. The control, in this case, had much longer copy that talked about the history of the flag and made an offer to the reader to test their knowledge. The treatment copy was much shorter, and asked a direct question to the reader: “How much do you know about the Gadsden Flag?”

They split the traffic to determine which ad attracted more clicks.

experiments.title: How the tactic used in presenting the value proposition impacted The Daily Signal’s donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Which tactic in presenting the value proposition will lead to the greatest increase in donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/21/2016 - 12/21/2016
The experiment produced significant lifts in both click through rate and donation rate. The table above lists the individual conversion rates from reader to donor. The click through rates followed similar trends. This results in two key learnings:
By increasing the process-level value of donating (fully laying out the reasons to give), both treatments were able to outperformed the control.
The “Dear Reader” version had the highest impact on clickthrough rate (256% lift) and donor conversion rates (642% lift).
experiments.keyLearnings:
Through a series of previous experiments, we had discovered a value proposition for The Daily Signal that seemed to resonate with donor. This value proposition focused on the type of journalism provided by TDS on a daily basis. With this in mind, we wanted to see if we could find a better means to deliver this message.

We created two new treatment options:
The first treatment took the form of a “Wikipedia-style” delivery that appeared in the middle of every article. It devoted more space to cohesively lay out the reasons to support The Daily Signal.
 	The second treatment took the form of an inline-survey to readers. The idea was to get the readers to say whether they valued the content. If they did, they would then be shown the donation value proposition as the next step in the process. If they selected they did not like it, they would be shown a comment box to submit the reasons why.

experiments.title: How the minimum premium gift impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Can we increase overall revenue by increasing the minimum gift required for a donation premium?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/10/2016 - 06/23/2016
On one of their donor acquisition campaign, The Heritage Foundation was testing the use of a premium to increase conversion and average gift. Previous experiments had proven that the premium increased the likelihood of a donor to convert but we wanted to see if we could also increase to overall revenue coming from the campaign. We decided to try testing the premium at a $125 minimum gift level. The control treatment had the minimum gift set at $100.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The $125 minimum gift was able to lift the average gift by 46% but it decreased the overall conversion by 58%. This resulted in a 39% decrease to overall revenue for the treatment. In this situation, the $100 minimum gift amount was the clear winner between the two.

However, even if this test had managed a slight increase to revenue but still decreased conversion rate significantly, the control would still have been the winner. The purpose of this campaign was new donor acquisition. It would take a significant increase in revenue to justify any loss in the number of donors acquired.

				gift).

0% increase in traffic

experiments.title: How emphasizing recurring giving impacts overall revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: Which donation page treatment will yield the greatest number of donors and revenue?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/29/2016 - 12/07/2016
In the latest in the series of optimization experiments on the main Heritage donation forms, we implemented a new donation form and value proposition that emphasized the value of recurring giving. In addition to the expanded and more visual value proposition, the new form also laid out a cohesive product-level value proposition on why recurring giving is so important.
With this new treatment, we ran a test across several of the most highly trafficked donation pages to see which would result in more gifts.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment had interesting results. The new treatment resulted in a 54% decrease in the number of donors acquired. However, since the goal of the treatment was to acquire recurring donors given their increased value, we need to analyze the impact on that segment. In the case of recurring donors, the new form does see a 87% increase in acquisition.

Assuming that the average recurring donor will last 12 months, we then factored these two results and discovered that after 12 months, the new treatment would effectively break even on revenue compared to the original control. With a 54% drop in donors, continuing with the treatment would seem to be a pretty risky bet on long-term revenue potential. Based upon this analysis, we decided to end the test and continue with the current control.

experiments.title: How third-party validation affects email acquisition for a free digital offer
experiments.researchQuestion: Will increasing clarity and adding social proof increase email acquisition?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/29/2016 - 07/01/2016
The Heritage Foundation was giving away a digital version of their popular 475-page Guide to the Constitution in exchange for an email address. They had a landing page that was converting 58% of visitors. But this meant that 42% of visitors weren’t downloading the digital Guide, so their team wanted to try four tweaks to increase conversion.
One confusing part of the offer was that the digital version of the Guide was in the form of a microsite, so they were really offering “access” rather than a digital download. This caused potential confusion to the user, so they marketed the offer as a digital download (since there was an available PDF).
Then, they launched an A/B test to see if the new page increased conversion.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The new treatment converted 65.4% of visitors to accept the offer -- an increase of 11.2%! This shows that the new landing page tweaks did in fact increase clarity and add some social proof that helped convince the visitor to take action.

While this is a great lift and results in more emails for the same spend, it still means that 35% of our traffic is not converting—which means more testing and optimization is needed!

experiments.title: How a more personal subject line affects open rate
experiments.researchQuestion: How does a more personal subject line impact open rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/10/2014 - 12/18/2014
With a little more than two days left before the end of the year, Heritage Foundation email subscribers had received six different messages from Jim DeMint– the president of the organization– asking for their support with a year-end gift. Best practices suggest that the email signer should be the most publicly know leader of the organization, and should rarely (if ever) change to ensure donors hear a consistent voice. Heritage tested that theory by sending an email from someone else in the organization.
experiments.keyLearnings: This test proved that a more personalized message, even if not from the primary organizational sender, can have a significant impact on open rate -- and therefore the donor’s likelihood to give.

experiments.title: How a direct ask about planned giving affects clickthrough rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Which approach will attract more clicks?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/06/2015 - 04/30/2015
The Heritage Legacy Society sent a prospecting email to ask Heritage Foundation members if they planned to include the organization in their estate planning. They didn’t know the best way to approach this through email, so they tested two distinct approaches: a direct ask, supported by facts about tax planning, against a story-based approach with a personal testimony from a Heritage Legacy Society member.
experiments.keyLearnings: This told us that we don't necessarily need to "soften" the ask with a story. The story might introduce friction as an estate commitment is a personal decision, not one based on third-party influence.

experiments.title: How a softer call to action affects conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a softer call to action generate more donations?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/14/2016 - 03/18/2016
The Heritage Foundation was promoting their Membership Card to non-members giving them the opportunity to become a member.
Anticipating that the direct ask to “become a member” would receive fewer (but more motivated) clicks from the email, they wanted to test whether the added step and call-to-action to “confirm the name on your card” would generate more clicks, and result in more donations.
Since they weren’t making a direct ask, they created a different offer flow for this treatment. They created a two-step form for the treatment that started with a simple “confirm your name” step (pre-populated with the visitor’s name), and then took them to complete their donation. Because the visitor was indicating their intent to become a member by completing the first step, they removed much of the sales copy on the donation page in order to remove friction from the process.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The softer call to action generated a 54% increase in clicks, but a 51.6% decrease in donations. This revealed a key insight: all traffic is not the same. Visitors come with different motivations, and the traffic to the treatment flow was not at all "sold".

This prompted a second test to see if reintroducing the value proposition copy on the follow-on donation form would affect the results.
