experiments.title: How a program-specific value proposition impacted donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a Daily Signal focused page increase donor conversion for visitors coming from a news-focused email?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/02/2017 - 08/16/2017
During The Heritage Foundation Board Match fundraising campaign, we wanted to incorporate one of their more popular programs (The Daily Signal) into a fundraising appeal email. The goal was to show the broad impact of the organization by talking about the various aspects of the organization.
Historically, we had utilized the same landing page throughout the campaign for each email. This is something that we had tested into, finding that switching landing pages didn’t often result in any significant lifts so we determined it was not worth the extra time and energy.
However, in this situation, we felt that the brand recognition and popularity of the program may be worth creating its own page. We decided to test that hypothesis by creating a new landing page that was branded as The Daily Signal and focused its value proposition on that one aspect of Heritage.
experiments.keyLearnings: The landing page that reinforced The Daily Signal brand and focused the value proposition solely on that aspect of the organization increased donations by 272%. This emphasizes the importance of creating a cohesive message all the way through the donation process. It also all verifies a trend that we had been noticing in the last several month, that The Daily Signal is a viable program for raising funds with.

experiments.title: How differing calls-to-action affect donor conversion rate across different channels
experiments.researchQuestion: Which call-to-action will result in more donations—”confirm”, or “activate”?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/23/2018 - 03/28/2018
The Heritage Foundation had tested different messaging as they sought to acquire donors with their Membership Card offer. They had tested calls-to-action with their house file, and found that “confirm your membership” led to more donations than “activate your membership”.
However, as they began to roll out acquisition with rented lists, they knew that this audience was more distant from Heritage than those who received their publications, so they wanted to re-test the CTA to make sure the result stayed the same.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Both emails had a very similar clickthrough rate. But not all clicks are created equal! The CTA of “activate” produced a 79% increase in donations over the CTA of “confirm”, which was the exact opposite of how nonmembers on the house file performed. This showed that even though they weren’t members, people on the house file felt that “confirm” was more applicable to their membership status. They felt more like members—the opposite of a rented list, who felt that “activate” was the most relevant verb to their membership status.

This shows the importance of replicating tests across different channels and audiences, as they don’t all act the same way.

experiments.title: How relating an appeal to donor affinities impacted conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a message that focuses on Donald Trump lift response rates for The Heritage Foundation email file?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/04/2016 - 08/05/2016
During the 2016 Heritage Board Challenge moneybomb, we wanted to validate a finding from a recent donor study. In this study, we found that Heritage donors had a high affinity for Donald Trump. Given Heritage’s impact on the future administration, we wanted to use this as a reason to give a gift. We decided to test this against the standard “challenge” style messaging.
experiments.keyLearnings: The Donald Trump message resulted in a 346% increase in response rate to the email. This experiment demonstrates the importance in aligning the messaging in the appeals to the donors’ affinities. However, we do need to be careful to validate this in the future, especially related to Trump, as affinities can change over time.

experiments.title: How testing a new offer in a “Dear Reader” ad affected clicks and donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that offering a relevant, time-sensitive resource for website visitors will achieve increase clicks and donations.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/11/2020 - 10/24/2020
As we prepared to launch a new offer to the housefile and in acquisition, we were faced with a dilemma. The new eBook offer was relevant and time-sensitive, but we didn’t want to kick out an offer that was already performing strongly and miss out on additional conversions by losing a month of run time. So instead of replacing the current Dear Reader eBook offer outright, we decided to make the new offer compete against the old offer for the spot.
experiments.keyLearnings:
What we found is that the new offer had lower performance than the current offer across the board. We saw a 32.8% decrease in conversion rate for the new eBook offer, which you can see broken down by segment below, resulting in fewer engagements in both clicks and donations. These results helped us reach a conclusion to keep the existing offer in place rather than rolling out the new eBook offer in this high-traffic Dear Reader spot. New is not always better! For future new offers, we will plan to run additional A/B tests to determine which offer will lead to maximum results instead of giving away valuable real estate to an offer with unknown performance and risk losing potential revenue and have to make up for lost time later. This experimental approach with initially rolling out to only 50% of site visitors will help us focus less on if the offer is fresh and instead care more about if the offer works. 

Observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment:
Direct Traffic visitors had a 52.4% decrease in cta click with a 100% level of confidence.
 	Email visitors had a 29.7% decrease in cta click with a 100% level of confidence.
 	Organic Search visitors had a 61.9% decrease in cta click with a 100% level of confidence.
 	Social visitors had a 49.1% decrease in cta click with a 100% level of confidence.
 	All Traffic visitors had a 32.8% decrease in cta click with a 100% level of confidence.
 	Desktop visitors had a 23.0% decrease in cta click with a 100% level of confidence.
 	Mobile visitors had a 40.4% decrease in cta click with a 100% level of confidence.
 	Tablet visitors had a 34.5% decrease in cta click with a 100% level of confidence.
 	New visitors had a 46.6% decrease in cta click with a 100% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How the minimum premium gift impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Can we increase overall revenue by increasing the minimum gift required for a donation premium?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/10/2016 - 06/23/2016
On one of their donor acquisition campaign, The Heritage Foundation was testing the use of a premium to increase conversion and average gift. Previous experiments had proven that the premium increased the likelihood of a donor to convert but we wanted to see if we could also increase to overall revenue coming from the campaign. We decided to try testing the premium at a $125 minimum gift level. The control treatment had the minimum gift set at $100.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The $125 minimum gift was able to lift the average gift by 46% but it decreased the overall conversion by 58%. This resulted in a 39% decrease to overall revenue for the treatment. In this situation, the $100 minimum gift amount was the clear winner between the two.

However, even if this test had managed a slight increase to revenue but still decreased conversion rate significantly, the control would still have been the winner. The purpose of this campaign was new donor acquisition. It would take a significant increase in revenue to justify any loss in the number of donors acquired.

				gift).
				This experiment produced the following results:
0% increase in traffic
				× 58.4% decrease in conversion rate
				× 0% increase in average gift

experiments.title: How a softer call to action affects conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a softer call to action generate more donations?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/14/2016 - 03/18/2016
The Heritage Foundation was promoting their Membership Card to non-members giving them the opportunity to become a member.
Anticipating that the direct ask to “become a member” would receive fewer (but more motivated) clicks from the email, they wanted to test whether the added step and call-to-action to “confirm the name on your card” would generate more clicks, and result in more donations.
Since they weren’t making a direct ask, they created a different offer flow for this treatment. They created a two-step form for the treatment that started with a simple “confirm your name” step (pre-populated with the visitor’s name), and then took them to complete their donation. Because the visitor was indicating their intent to become a member by completing the first step, they removed much of the sales copy on the donation page in order to remove friction from the process.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The softer call to action generated a 54% increase in clicks, but a 51.6% decrease in donations. This revealed a key insight: all traffic is not the same. Visitors come with different motivations, and the traffic to the treatment flow was not at all "sold".

This prompted a second test to see if reintroducing the value proposition copy on the follow-on donation form would affect the results.

The Flux Metrics analyze the three primary metrics that affect revenue (traffic, conversion rate, and average
				gift).
				This experiment produced the following results:
0% increase in traffic
				× 51.6% decrease in conversion rate
				× 0% increase in average gift

experiments.title: How changing the landing page design affects revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: Which landing page design produces the most donor conversions and revenue?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/09/2015 - 06/23/2015
The Heritage Foundation had been using the same landing page design for their search engine marketing (SEM) donor campaigns for the past three years. During those three years, we had run multiple experiments on the main organizational donation page which had gradually evolved into something that looked completely different.
We were in the process of moving the SEM page to the same domain as the main donation form and realized that it would be a good time to validate that the old design was still effective for the SEM audience segment.  We created an experiment by duplicating the page content but displaying it on the two different page designs.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The new design treatment caused an 85.1% drop in revenue by decreasing conversion rate by 67.6% and decreasing average gift by 54.1%. 

The simplified design used in the treatment was developed for a page with minimal copy. When we added the long value proposition text that had been developed for this specific landing page, it became difficult to read.

Landing pages must be designed so that they are easily scannable by the visitor. In this situation, the sub-headlines didn't stand out enough and the lack of page border made the page seem longer than it was.  All of these elements caused enough friction in the mind of the donor to have a negative impact on both conversion rate and average gift.
experiments.fluxMetrics:
The Flux Metrics analyze the three primary metrics that affect revenue (traffic, conversion rate, and average
				gift).
				This experiment produced the following results:
0% increase in traffic
				× 67.6% decrease in conversion rate
				× 54.1% decrease in average gift
									85.1% decrease in revenue

experiments.title: How a more personal subject line affects open rate
experiments.researchQuestion: How does a more personal subject line impact open rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/10/2014 - 12/18/2014
With a little more than two days left before the end of the year, Heritage Foundation email subscribers had received six different messages from Jim DeMint– the president of the organization– asking for their support with a year-end gift. Best practices suggest that the email signer should be the most publicly know leader of the organization, and should rarely (if ever) change to ensure donors hear a consistent voice. Heritage tested that theory by sending an email from someone else in the organization.
experiments.keyLearnings: This test proved that a more personalized message, even if not from the primary organizational sender, can have a significant impact on open rate -- and therefore the donor’s likelihood to give.

				× 0% increase in average gift

experiments.title: How adding an additional newsletter opt-in checkbox affects form completion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will adding an extra form field checkbox affect email acquisition rate on a petition?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/12/2017 - 09/19/2017
The treatment, with the additional checkbox at the end of the form, produced a 9.6% decrease in email acquisition rate. The team at Heritage decided, after analyzing the results, that this decrease was acceptable, since 63% of people who signed the treatment page also opted into the Morning Bell—which resulted in several hundred new subscribers with increased value.
experiments.keyLearnings: The Heritage Foundation was running an open letter to grow its email file and motivate the Senate to take action on judicial appointments. While this offer was running, they performed an analysis that found that subscribers to their Morning Bell were more likely to become donors. They decided to add a checkbox to the form to allow signers of the open letter to opt in to the Morning Bell. They hypothesized that it would decrease conversion, but the data showed that as long as the decrease was slight, the increased value of the added subscribers would more than make up for it. They launched an A/B test to determine a winner.

experiments.title: How does shortening copy length on an instant donation page affect instant conversion rates?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a more specific copy will increase conversion rates for potential donors on an instant donation page—even if it results in a longer copy.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/02/2022 - 07/24/2022
The Heritage Foundation, a public policy client, was launching a new acquisition offer, and we wanted to take a look at how copy length impacted conversion rates on an instant donation page.
We created 3 treatments for this test:
A longer version that spent more words agitating the problem, stating what could happen if it’s not addressed, and outlining a solution that the potential donor could support
A short treatment that retained urgency but sacrificed specificity surrounding both the problem and the solution to move more quickly through to the CTA
A medium-length treatment that was almost identical to the short treatment, but added an additional paragraph basically restating the paragraph that precedes it with a “we’re looking for …” call to action.
Because this was a new offer and not a pre-existing offer we were attempting to improve, this experiment presented us with an opportunity to reinforce previous learnings which have shown that specificity provides clarity and has a positive impact on conversion rates.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment:
Desktop visitors had a 38.7% decrease in donations with an 86% level of confidence.
 	Returning visitors had a 34.4% decrease in donations with a 91% level of confidence.
 	65+ visitors had a 3.8% increase in donations with a 96% level of confidence.
 	Male visitors had a 213.5% increase in donations with an 86% level of confidence.
 	Housefile Email visitors had a 100.0% decrease in donations with a 100% level of confidence.
 	55-64 visitors had a 6.3% increase in donations with a 96% level of confidence.
 	Paid Search visitors had a 100.0% increase in donations with a 100% level of confidence.
 	25-34 visitors had a 100.0% decrease in donations with a 94% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How emphasizing exclusivity in a Facebook ad graphic impacted clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that increasing appeal by highlighting exclusivity  for Facebook ad viewers will achieve an increase in click rate.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 08/19/2022
We were running an eBook cover image for a new offer from The Heritage Foundation and wondered if we could make the offer more appealing by highlighting the exclusivity of the resource. This research report is very unique as it is only available from this organization and no one else, but this was not communicated anywhere in the ad. We created a treatment graphic that placed emphasis on the fact that this offer was exclusive and we reiterated the CTA “Learn More” directly on the graphic to help it stand out in the newsfeed.
experiments.keyLearnings: What we found after running both versions of the ad was that the treatment produced a 21.6% lift in click rate with 98.4% level of confidence so the test results are valid. We believe this experiment saw positive results because the enhanced appeal of making the offer more exclusive made it more desirable because ad viewers were now aware they could not get a resource like this from any other organization or get access to this information anywhere else. We will continue testing this concept on other exclusive offers from The Heritage Foundation and see if we can achieve similar results in other acquisition spaces—such as email prospecting—using this learning.

The Flux Metrics analyze the three primary metrics that affect revenue (traffic, conversion rate, and average
				gift).
				This experiment produced the following results:
21.6% increase in traffic
				× 0% increase in conversion rate
				× 0% increase in average gift

experiments.title: How adding a security box around payment fields affects instant conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a security box around payment fields on an instant donation page will achieve higher instant conversion rates on a content acquisition offer.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/22/2022 - 08/20/2022
The donation form used by a public policy client, while secure, did not use any visual cues to reinforce this to prospective donors. While our client knew that the form was indeed secure, we hypothesized that the lacking visual cues could potentially be causing friction for donors who were concerned about submitting their payment if the form was not secure. And even if this was not causing friction in the minds of potential donors, we thought that making the form’s security obvious with visual cues could be a motivating factor for people who were ‘on the fence’, by demonstrating that the security of their sensitive data was important to this organization.
We ran this A/B test over a period of one month with traffic split 50/50.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running this experiment for one month, we were able to validate a 49% lift in instant conversion rates with a 97.5% level of confidence.
These results validated our hypothesis that visually reinforcing the security of payment fields on a donation form would reduce friction, reinforce legitimacy, and ultimately lead to an increase in the number of people who completed the donation process.
The positive results seen across many key visitor segments lead us to believe that using visual cues to reinforce the security of a potential donor's transaction plays an important role in reassuring donors that their data is safe, which ultimately influences their willingness to give. When reviewing this experiment, we also observed that the colored security box worked to break the donation process into chunks, potentially reducing friction during the donation process.
Because we were unable to validate these results for mobile users, we may want to test this secure donation form treatment for mobile visitors before rolling it out to all forms.

experiments.title: How clarity of the value proposition affects conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a simpler copy approach and stacked design increase name conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/11/2015 - 05/12/2015
The Heritage Foundation launched a petition against Common Core Education Standards. Despite reaching an ideal audience and achieving a low cost-per-email, the page still had room for improvement in terms of name acquisition. They hypothesized that stopping “Common Core” might be too specific of an ask, if they weren’t familiar with the policy fight itself. They introduced new copy that focused on the underlying principle of the argument — that the Federal Government shouldn’t control public education. Additionally, they placed the petition itself under the copy to allow the reader to fully digest the value proposition before completing it.
experiments.keyLearnings: We were concerned that the target audience wasn't as familiar with the specific term "Common Core" and would respond more favorably to the principles underlying the relationship between the federal government and education. This tells us that Common Core might actually be the best "opposition keyword", at least with this highly-educated group.

The Flux Metrics analyze the three primary metrics that affect revenue (traffic, conversion rate, and average
				gift).
				This experiment produced the following results:
0% increase in traffic
				× 37.5% decrease in conversion rate
				× 0% increase in average gift

experiments.title: How does adding cues to the donation form affect donation rates?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding conversational cues for donation page visitors will achieve higher conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/01/2019 - 12/14/2019
Heritage wanted to test a way to improve donation rates on their main donation page.
For this experiment, they decided to try putting numbered cues/instructions above each step of the donation form to see if this helped ‘pull’ the donor along the donation process and reduce friction potentially created by the amount of fields necessitated by transaction donation forms.
This experiment was run for 41 days with a 50/50 split in traffic.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment validated the hypothesis by yielding a 19% lift in donation rates with a 99% level of confidence. This experiment also produced a 126% lift in revenue with a 95% level of confidence.

Breaking up the donation form fields with conversational cues helps to break the donation form into more digestible chunks, helping to 'pull' the donor through the process while reducing friction and thus the chance for a donor to reconsider their contribution. 

Due to the positive results of this experiment, it would be worth testing this on instant donation pages and campaign donation pages to futher validate this learning.

experiments.title: How asking an open-loop question on an ad image affects click through rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that asking an open loop question on the ad image for Facebook users will achieve increase donation rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/25/2022 - 06/06/2022
The Heritage Foundation wanted to improve CTR on their Big Tech Survey campaign. The original ad had long, issue-focused copy and a statement on the ad image telling the audience to take their survey to fight back against big tech.
in an attempt to earn more clicks, Heritage tested two ad treatments: one with the same image/statement but shorter body copy and a second with a new image and an open loop question to encourage people to click to find out what they knew.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running the test for 30 days, Heritage observed that treatment #2 (with open loop question on ad image) produced a dramatic lift of 304% with a 100% LoC. This indicates that by opening a loop of curiosity on the most prominent part of the ad, Heritage was able to inspire more people to click through, presumably because they wanted to test their knowledge — confirming the hypothesis that creating curiosity would result in a lift in clicks.
This test also supports the general supposition that making changes to the ad's most prominent element - the ad image - may have a greater impact than making similar changes to the body copy, which is less prominent.
Next, Heritage may choose to test a single element of this concept — either the ad image or the value prop language — to see which element has a bigger impact when changed. But for now, we can conclude that:
Making changes to the ad image has a larger impact on performance than changing the body copyOpening a loop of curiosity is likely to encourage more people to click through

experiments.title: How removing a progress thermometer to a donation page affects donation rates during a high urgency campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that removing a progress thermometer on a campaign donation page will result in lower conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/19/2022 - 08/08/2022
In previous high-urgency campaigns, we’ve had success increasing conversion rates by showing a progress thermometer on the top of the donation page once we were nearing our goal. Knowing this, we wanted to see if we could replicate these results during a recent high-urgency campaign for a public policy client. We hoped to increase conversion rates by inspiring prospects to be one of the donors that helped “fill the bar” and help us meet our goal.
This concept attempts to employ the principles of urgency (we only have X to go), social proof (many others found this cause worthy to support), and unity (belonging to a group) in an effort to inspire donors to act.
We initially planned to show the progress thermometer to 100% of traffic, but then we decided to test this concept again and instead split traffic 50/50 between the control (including the progress thermometer) and the treatment (without the progress thermometer), hypothesizing that removing the thermometer would reduce conversion rates.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Despite previous success using the progress thermometer concept during high urgency campaigns, in this instance removing the thermometer produced a valid lift of 17.1% at a 99% level of confidence.
Interestingly, while removing the progress thermometer increased donations, it also resulted in an 8.7% decrease in revenue. And while we were unable to validate this decrease (75.9% level of confidence), when we combine this finding with the fact that the average gift given by donors increased by 24.8% with the thermometer, it appears that while removing the thermometer increased the amount of gifts received, including the thermometer improved the quality of gifts and ultimately raised more money—presumably by motivating donors to give more so that the goal could be reached and because the amount of money already raised indicated that this was a worthy cause to support.
Because of the mixed results produced by this experiment, it may be worth seeking additional opportunities to test this hypothesis again, focusing on revenue, in an effort to validate the portion of our findings that indicated that including the progress thermometer increased overall revenue during the time this experiment was run.

experiments.title: How does shortening copy length on an instant donation page affect instant conversion rates?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that more specific copy will increase conversion rates for potential donors on an instant donation page—even if it results in longer copy.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/02/2022 - 07/24/2022
The Heritage Foundation, a public policy organization, was launching a new acquisition offer, and we wanted to take a look at how copy length impacted conversion rates on an instant donation page.
We created 3 treatments for this test:
A longer version that spent more words agitating the problem, stating what could happen if it’s not addressed, and outlining a solution that the potential donor could support
A short treatment that retained urgency but sacrificed specificity surrounding both the problem and the solution to move more quickly through to the CTA
A medium-length treatment that was almost identical to the short treatment, but added an additional paragraph basically restating the paragraph that precedes it with a “we’re looking for …” call to action.
Because this was a new offer and not a pre-existing offer we were attempting to improve, this experiment presented us with an opportunity to reinforce previous learnings which have shown that specificity provides clarity and has a positive impact on conversion rates.
experiments.keyLearnings:
In this experiment, we observed that shortening copy length at the expense of specificity produced a decrease in instant conversion rates, with the short treatment producing an 18.6% decrease in donations and the medium-length treatment (with the appended "we're looking for" paragraph) producing a 42.3% decrease in donations.

We were unable to validate this experiment but still noted some interesting learnings based on the data we acquired.

First, while not validated, this experiment suggests that copy that is clear and specific has a positive impact on donation rates, even if it is longer. The long-form treatment uses brief paragraphs, varying sentence lengths, and crisp language, written in a persuasive rather than conversational tone that follows a definable copywriting framework, P-A-S to succinctly organize the content of the appeal.

Interestingly, in terms of donation rates, the medium-length treatment performed worst of all. We believe that this is because the appended statement did little in the way of providing new information or clarity, simply restating the paragraph which preceeded it. This suggests that reading the same information stated two ways may create friction for the potential donor and cause them to abandon.

This experiment suggests that copy length, in and of itself, neither motivates nor demotivates donors to give ... but that, rather, it is the content of the copy that most matters. It also suggests that conversion-driven copy should contain only the words necessary to convincingly convey the value prop, use crisp, persuasive language in an easily digestible framework, and be careful not to contain redundancies which may cause friction for potential donors.

experiments.title: How strengthening the value proposition with a radical redesign to the copy on an instant donation page affects instant conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that strengthening the value proposition on an instant donation page will achieve higher instant conversion rates for a paid acquisition campaign.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/12/2022 - 08/14/2022
We had been running a content offer for a free ebook on CRT produced by one of our public policy clients. At the time of this experiment, the offer had produced a healthy email acquisition rate of 46.7% but suffered from a low instant conversion rate (ICR) of just 1.8%.
After reviewing the instant donation page, we quickly identified an opportunity to inspire more people to give by improving the offer’s value proposition—why a potential donor should give to our client, instead of someone else, or not at all—by improving its appeal, clarity, exclusivity, and credibility.
Since other key metrics were trending positive, we felt that we were reaching the right people with the right offer and that improving the value proposition around an issue our audience cared about presented a significant opportunity to improve the performance of this campaign.
Improving the value proposition for this offer meant we would need to change a number of variables in the way the story was being presented through the copy on the instant donation page.
To improve appeal, we replaced passive, informative language with copy that focused first on the urgency of the problem, then the specific impact the prospect’s donation would have—and the specific things they would help the organization do to fight back. 
To improve clarity, we broke the copy into smaller, concisely written paragraphs that focused on continuing the narrative forward in a logical linear fashion, reducing potential friction caused by the meandering nature of the control. We also put the call to action front and center in a headline to make it clear to the prospective donor exactly why they were there.
To improve exclusivity, we replaced generalized impact language with persuasive bullet points that expressed not only what the organization was doing to solve the problem, but also how, and the impact these actions would have
To improve credibility, we used our last bullet point to present the model legislation that our client wrote and which was already being used by lawmakers in several states to ban CRT from schools—something we felt clearly set them apart from other organizations
experiments.keyLearnings:
The results of our experiment were encouraging: our focus on improving the value proposition produced a valid 25% lift in donations with a 95.2% level of confidence for all traffic and a directional lift of 58.3% directional lift in donations (91.7% LoC) for paid social traffic.
The treatment also produced a 21.3% lift in revenue (87% LoC) for all traffic and a 113% directional lift in revenue for paid social traffic.
And while we are encouraged by these results, importantly, it turns out that we changed an additional variable by personalizing the confirmation message at the top of the page with the prospective donor's first name. We will still want to verify these results by running a subsequent experiment to isolate the impact of first name personalization vs. strengthening of the value prop.

experiments.title: How a “one click” donation experience impacted giving from current donors
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that removing friction for returning donors will achieve an increase in conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/09/2020 - 12/21/2020
During The Heritage Foundation’s year-end campaign, we wanted to streamline the process to capture gifts from active members. To reduce friction from filling out multiple form fields, and simplify the gift processing option for existing donors, we presented a “one click” experience similar to what one might experience with other online transactions as a repeat visitor, like Amazon or Paypal. Instead of the donor having to re-enter payment details, we presented them with a message indicating the last 4 digits of the most recent card they used on a past donation while still giving them the option to change or update their payment method. We also created a second treatment that allowed the donor to “swipe” the button to make a gift using their past payment details.
experiments.keyLearnings:
By acknowledging our pre-existing relationship with the donor and reducing friction for mobile visitors we saw a 122.1% increase in donations with a 100% level of confidence. Since we know the year-end giving donation page receives highly motivated traffic during the month of December, we would want to re-test this experience during other campaigns as well to compare results but given the increase in both conversion rate and revenue, we will move forward with this approach for future appeals to current members.
Notable changes to other metrics due to the experiment:
Revenue experienced a 242.9% increase with a 98% level of confidence.New visitors had a 95.7% increase in donations with a 99% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How Revamping Ad Creative with Photography and Value Prop Language Affected the Click Rate on Facebook Ads
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using photography and adding value prop language to ads for Facebook users will achieve a higher CTR and lower CPC.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/25/2022 - 05/25/2022
Traditionally, The Heritage Foundation has used a digital render of their Ebook offers for creative on Facebook ads. But when we recently launched a new content offer, we noticed that the cost per click was higher than anticipated, signifying that it was time for a revamp to our ad creative.
We hypothesized that this may be because, over time, the renders began to all look alike to viewers, causing them to either a) think they had already engaged with the offer, or b) tune the offer out altogether.
To help the new content offer stand out, we refreshed the campaign with treatments which made use of photography rather than renders while incorporating value prop language directly on the ad creative to catch viewers’ attention and hopefully pique their interest.
We created two treatments for this experiment, with the control and two new treatments receiving roughly the same traffic.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running this experiment for 23 days, both new ad treatments using photography and value prop language in the ad creative produced a lift in click-through rates, with the winning treatment producing a 43.6% lift in click rates with a 99.9% level of confidence.
This lift may be attributable to using eye-catching graphics and value prop language in the ad graphics, doing a better job of grabbing people's attention, 'stopping the scroll', and giving them a compelling reason to click.
The winning treatment may be more compelling because the image creates curiosity in the viewer who is likely to ask, "who's Bob?"
These results have inspired us to move away from using only a 3D render of the Ebook for Facebook ads. We plan to test further into the kinds of images and copy that inspire people to take notice and click through.

experiments.title: How a radical redesign on an ebook offer impacted email signups
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a radical redesign  for landing page visitors will achieve an increase in email acquisition.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/29/2022 - 04/16/2022
When reviewing landing page conversion across several offers, we found an eBook that was underperforming. We compared this landing page to higher-performing offers and found a handful of differences that could be optimized for higher conversion. We built a treatment that added social proof – “Downloaded over 2,500 times!” and revised the headline to offer more clarity about the desired action: “Get your FREE copy of Knowing Critical Race Theory When You See It and Fighting It When You Can today!” We also revised the opening paragraph to indicate more relevancy and urgency from “For years” to “Today/right now” and removed the distracting call to action of “And please share it with family and friends” from the last paragraph.
experiments.keyLearnings: We ran the experiment for about 2 weeks and observed a 9.9% decrease in conversion rate with 98.3% level of confidence so the test results are valid. Two different segments saw conflicting results which are important to note: 25-34 aged visitors had a 57.1% decrease in email acquisition with a 100% level of confidence while 55-64 aged visitors had a 22.1% increase in email acquisition with a 93% level of confidence. It is important to keep in mind for future tests that even though the treatment reduced conversion overall, we did see a lift on a key target demographic but for a much smaller sample size. Since the control had low performance and the treatment worsened it, we believe there is a larger issue with the offer copy on this landing page that needs to be re-evaluated and revised for future testing. We will look at ways to enhance the value of the offer and give further clarity on the contents. Because we are seeing the majority of traffic come from the 65+ age range, we will incorporate language that addresses not only children but also grandchildren in the treatment copy on the next experiment.

experiments.title: How layering a donation ask impacts overall revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: We hypothesized that by asking the user to make one decision at a time and layering the two donation forms, we could reduce mental friction and decision friction on the page and increase conversion rate and revenue.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/06/2014 - 10/10/2014
The Heritage Foundation and its sister organization Heritage Action For America wanted to run a joint campaign to raise money for both organizations and highlight the individual strengths of each organization. This campaign concept mirrored a direct mail concept that included two separate reply envelopes, one for each organization.
Since the organizations are independent from one another, they needed to have two donation forms. Initially, the campaign put both of these donation forms on the same page, right next to each other. We developed a treatment that layered these two asks, one after the other, with the ask for Heritage Action first.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Layering the two asks meant accepting a trade-off: the site visitor would not be allowed to choose to give to either organization. So, it was not a surprise when overall conversion dipped 12.5%. However, the layered asks resulted in more people choosing to give to both organizations, which had a significant impact on average gift. Overall, layering the two asks resulted in a revenue increase of 98.8% -- nearly doubling the previous revenue per visitor.
This provided a great learning for future campaigns -- and was immediately put into use on the main Heritage donation form. After a donor makes a gift on that form, they are immediately offered the opportunity to give to Heritage Action as well. This has resulted in an additional, consistent revenue stream for Heritage Action.

				gift).
				This experiment produced the following results:
0% increase in traffic
				× 12.5% decrease in conversion rate
				× 127.3% increase in average gift
									98.8% increase in revenue

experiments.title: How a tabbed donation form impacts recurring donor acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a tabbed form for visitors to /renew and the main donation page will achieve increased recurring donor acquisition.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/13/2019 - 01/09/2020
During year end, we wanted to find ways to increase membership to the Leaders Club for The Heritage Foundation. One tactic that had worked with a similar organization was utilizing a tabbed donation form. This allowed us to adjust the suggested gift array lower for recurring donors and changed the order of the mental conversation to ask whether they wanted to give recurring prior to asking how much they wanted to give.
experiments.keyLearnings:
We observed a 1,029% increase to recurring donor acquisition with the treatment. However, after a deeper analysis, we discovered that many of these donor didn't realize they had been making a recurring gift. This is due to the recurring option being pre-selected for all visitors.
Since we had no way of knowing what gift type they were trying to give, we decided to cancel all recurring gifts and rerun the experiment in the new year without selecting the recurring option for them.

experiments.title: How donation page copy impacted donor conversion in a high urgency campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that focusing value proposition on the need for existing members will achieve an increase in conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/13/2021 - 04/03/2021
When reviewing the donation page for a high urgency renewal campaign, we wanted to test reducing brand references and making the copy more donor-focused. Since visitors to this page were returning members, our hypothesis was they already knew or at least had some familiarity with who The Heritage Foundation is and what they do. For the treatment, we reframed the copy to show instead what the membership gift would accomplish and highlight the need, rather than emphasizing what Heritage was already doing.
experiments.keyLearnings:
One element that was removed from the treatment copy was social proof, which we know is a key factor in donor motivation. The control shares that The Heritage Foundation shares over 500,000 supporters. For future testing, we will look for opportunities to keep elements of social proof while reducing brand mentions to see if there is any notable effect. The control copy also had congruency in the headline and the final ask on the page, making it abundantly clear what the desired action on the page was while the treatment headline leaves some ambiguity. "Defend conservative change in America" - but how? There are multiple ways to do so. In future headline tests, we can draw the connection between renewing membership and defending conservative change in America so the reader is not left guessing. We'd also like to re-test this concept at a later date considering it is an annual campaign and donors may already be motivated to give during this season. Donation page visitors could be inclined to renew their membership regardless of reading through the value proposition on the page, especially if they have been a long-time donor.
Notable changes to other metrics due to the experiment:
Revenue experienced a 7.2% decrease with a 88% level of confidence.
Observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment:
Desktop visitors had a 7.3% decrease in donations with a 94% level of confidence.New visitors had a 9.0% decrease in donations with a 97% level of confidence.Housefile Email visitors had a 6.3% decrease in donations with a 94% level of confidence.18-24 visitors had a 45.7% increase in donations with an 89% level of confidence. (We'd like to do some extra digging into this directional lift for this younger age segment!)45-54 visitors had a 29.7% decrease in donations with a 98% level of confidence.65+ visitors had a 12.6% decrease in donations with a 93% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How naming the recurring gift option impacts recurring giving
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that emphasizing the recurring gift option for high urgency campaign visitors will achieve increased recurring donor acquisition.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/01/2021 - 03/15/2021
For the Heritage Kickoff campaign, we wanted to try to re-validate an experiment that had been run in the prior year. We had found that there was no statistically significant impact to using the tabbed donation form. However, we had the hypothesis that if we called out the recurring gift option and gave a reason for giving it, we would be able to increase recurring givers.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running for the length of the campaign, we discovered the emphasizing the Leaders Club option via a tabbed form call out increased recurring gifts by 38% without any negative impact on overall conversion rate. This is something to be utilized in future campaigns and retested on the primary donation pages.

experiments.title: How asking for less impacted overall revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that asking for less than their previous highest contribution for existing donors will achieve increase overall response and revenue.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/30/2021 - 12/02/2021
As part of their Giving Tuesday campaign, The Heritage Foundation wanted to test a relatively new concept known as the downgraded ask. For this experiment, they created a chart of standards based upon the donors’ previous highest contribution where they would ask for a certain number of donors to give a specific amount. For the treatment version of this ask, they asked for a level below what the donor had previously given and added language acknowledging that they were being asked for less.
This was then tested across both emails that were sent out on Giving Tuesday and results were determined based upon all online giving (excluding recurring gifts) during Giving Tuesday and the day following.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The "downgrade" treatment resulted in a 34% increase in overall revenue with a 95% level of confidence. However, it did not reach the required sample size to statistically validate this learning so it should be viewed as a directional lift.
When digging into the data, we analyzed a few distinct segments to better understand how the decreased giving language impacted donors based upon how much they have given in the past. Here is what we observed:
Highest gift between $1 - $99: We saw a 10% increase in conversion rate but an 8% decrease in average gift. The net result was a 1% increase in overall revenue which was not statistically significant.Highest gift between $100 - $999: The treatment segment saw an increase in both conversion rate (10%) and average gift (52%) which resulted in a 67.3% statistically significant increase in revenue (both LoC and Sample Size validated). This means we that, despite asking for less than they have given in the past, the segment actually gave more!Highest gift greater than $1K: This segment did not have enough gifts to be able to observe a trend.
This means that for the lower-dollar donors, the tactic was able to increase overall response rate but at the cost of average gift. In the future, for this segment, we would want to consider this tactic only for those we're trying to reactivate (like lapsed donors) rather than the whole file.
For the $100-$999 donors, this is absolutely a tactic that should be repeated in future campaign. We would need additional testing to validate the $1K+ segment in the future.

experiments.title: How conversion optimization for likely donors impacted results
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that optimizing for clicks or emails for a modeled donor audience will achieve increased results.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/01/2021 - 10/31/2021
Heritage was looking for ways to expand beyond their existing donor audience to find new supporters. Wiland was able to create Facebook audiences of likely donors based upon Heritage’s existing donors. Since these were donor modeled audiences, they wanted to understand what optimization method would work best at finding new donors. We decided to test optimizing for clicks vs. emails vs. donors for a variety of email acquisition offers.
experiments.keyLearnings: After spending $1,000 on each optimization method, we found that optimizing for purchases was a statistically significantly better option for improving donor acquisition. This is in spite of the fact that the audience was already a primed donor audience. This tells us that Facebook's proprietary algorithm is able to overlay additional information to help further refine the audience selection which results in improved cost per donor and donor conversion metrics.

experiments.title: How call-to-action visibility affects email clickthrough rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will adding a second button to the top of the email increase click through rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/01/2016 - 05/13/2016
The treatment with the second button increased click through rate 40.4%. The new button at the top of the email received some clicks, but the CTR on the unchanged links lower in the email increased as well (19%, 11%, and 27% respectively). This confirmed our hypothesis that more readers would stay engaged with the email and ultimately click if there was a call to action visible immediately upon opening.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The Heritage Foundation was partnering with Townhall Media to generate email signups through an eBook giveaway. Townhall generated new signups by sending sponsored emails to their list.
Early results showed acceptable open and conversion rates, but lower than expected clickthrough rates.
We hypothesized that the email openers were abandoning the email because the value proposition and call to action were not immediately visible upon opening. By adding a second call to action button at the top of the email we anticipated a higher number of readers would remain engaged with the email and ultimately click through.

experiments.title: How urgency impacted donor conversion on The Daily Signal donation ad
experiments.researchQuestion: Will including language and a countdown clock about the approaching year end increase donor conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/21/2016 - 01/01/2017
The Heritage Foundation had recently discovered a tactic that was able to successfully increase the donor conversion rate of readers of The Daily Signal. This website is a Heritage-owned property that receives over 2 million visitors per month so even minor improvements can lead to significant gains.
In the days leading up to December 31st, we wanted to see if we could utilize the upcoming deadline to increase the urgency of the gift. For this test, we modified the existing value proposition statement to allude to the December 31st deadline for tax-deductibility and then added a countdown clock to visually reinforce this date.
We then launched the experiment with 10 days remaining in the year.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment had two interesting observations. The first, and most important, is that we were able to lift donor conversion rate by 61% by reinforcing the urgency of the December 31st deadline. The second observation came from an analysis of the number of visitors that clicked on the initial ad.
What we found is the year end messaging resulted in a 45.6% decrease in click through rate (statistically significant). That means we were able to achieve a 61% lift in donations from roughly half the number of visitors. The key to this learning is that, while the urgency-based message may not be as appealing to a larger audience, it increases the motivation of those that do click on the link.

experiments.title: How a subject line impacted email opens for members in a high urgency campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a subject line utilizing mystery and personal connection for returning members will achieve an increased open rate.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/31/2021
For an annual membership campaign, we wanted to look at ways to optimize the email envelope for The Heritage Foundation appeals. We had a set of historically used subject lines and wanted to test using a different subject line to drive email engagement. We set up the control as “Here’s the next big challenge” and developed a treatment subject line using only the word “Reconnecting”. The sender and body copy remained the same for each version.
experiments.keyLearnings:
What we saw was the treatment produced a 30.1% increase in open rate for the member segment with 100% level of confidence. Our hypothesis was the treatment would lift open rate since the language utilizes the element of mystery for the receiver to make them ask - What does the sender want to reconnect with me about?
Beyond the email open rate, we made further observations that are noteworthy. Clickthrough rate on the treatment saw a 25.1% lift with100.0% level of confidence. Additionally, the treatment produced a 93.6% increase in donor conversion with 100.0% level of confidence.
One thing that is important to keep in mind, however, is this email was randomly split so there may be some imbalance in returning member behavior that could have led to these results on donor conversion. Before leaning too heavily or making conclusions on the lift in donor conversion, we would want to do additional analysis on the two segments to verify it was a true and fair split based on time on file and previous giving history.

experiments.title: How a custom Facebook conversion event to target recurring purchases impacted recurring donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Would a custom “recurring purchase” event type increase a campaign’s recurring donor conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/26/2020 - 09/25/2020
As a part of experimenting with Facebook’s custom conversion events, we wanted to experiment with whether the custom-defined conversion event which specifically identified and send payment details back to Facebook’s algorithm to differentiate between a single gift and a monthly recurring gift purchase type would impact the recurring donor conversion rate.
For this campaign, we partnered with Wiland to identify a custom audience of lookalike donors who are most likely (per the Wiland model) to become a recurring donor for The Heritage Foundation.
The only variable in this experiment was (a) the standard, out of the box, “purchase” event from Facebook vs. (b) the custom-defined “recurring purchase” event that we created and installed in Facebook.
experiments.keyLearnings:
With a 99.5% level of confidence, we observed a significant decrease of -63.9% in recurring donor conversion rate. We observed a -62% decrease in revenue (essentially, a proportionate decrease relative to the lesser quantity of donors coming through the treatment experience) with a 100% level of confidence, as well.
It should be noted that we turned this experiment off only about 72% of the way through the minimum number of transactions we would like to see in a donor conversion study of this type (and even less—about 35%—of a revenue lift study), but the volume was still high enough and the spread great enough between the control and treatment experiences that we believed it would be best to not finish the study with the target minimum number of gifts we would normally like to see.
Further experimentation may be required with the purchase event programming and audience targeting combinations, but this certainly seems to point towards a decrease in donor conversion for recurring donor-focused campaigns.
experiments.fluxMetrics:

experiments.title: How the value proposition of an ad impacted the motivation of donor traffic
experiments.researchQuestion: Will the “alternative to mainstream media” value proposition increase the number of donors from the site?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/14/2017 - 03/20/2017
The new “Alternative to Mainstream Media” value proposition was able to significantly increase donor conversion rate by 165%. Interestingly, the click through rate for the ad increased by only 12% (which was not statistically significant). This would imply that the ad was not necessarily more appealing but, instead, it produced a higher motivation for those that it did attract which made them more likely to give.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The “Dear Reader” call out on the DailySignal.com (TDS) website had become a significant source of donors for The Heritage Foundation. The message in the treatment highlighted the nonprofit nature of the work and the benefit it provided to the reader.
The organization had recently come under attack from some of the more mainstream media outlets due to TDS’s level of access within the White House. Using this attack, we decided to test out a new value proposition that depicted The Daily Signal is an alternative to the mainstream media. We also added urgency by mentioning the potential loss of access due to the attacks.

experiments.title: How reducing decision friction impacted email clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: Will reducing decision friction by minimizing the number of links included in the email increase click rate?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 04/22/2020
As part of a weekly cultivation series to a segment of their housefile, The Heritage Foundation was sending an invitation that included several links to participate in upcoming webinars taking place on various dates and covering various topics. They wanted to test if sending a single link to an individual webinar would increase email engagement. Our hypothesis was that eliminating the additional webinar options and focusing the call to action on one specific event would optimize for more clicks and lead to additional traffic to the website.
experiments.keyLearnings: We saw a 31.5% increase in email clicks by removing the additional webinar links in the email. This indicates that we can drive more traffic to our website from email marketing when using a single call to action with a specific focus rather than presenting additional links to various content. To increase email engagement and website traffic, The Heritage Foundation should continue using this approach for future webinar invitations.

As part of the Board Challenge campaign, we ran Facebook advertising targeted at known donors and audiences who matched the profile of a Heritage supporter. The landing page had a quote from a well-known public figure so we were able to use them in the advertisement to help drive traffic. We wanted to discover what messaging approach would allow us to drive the most traffic.
experiments.keyLearnings: The “challenge” language captured the interest of those that saw the advertisement. This resulted in a 45% increase to visitors to the landing page.

experiments.title: How the image of a membership card increased donor conversion rates through email prospecting
experiments.researchQuestion: Will the membership card as a part of the email creative improve donor conversion rates in the email prospecting channel?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 04/12/2019
As a part of the Membership Card campaign for The Heritage Foundation in 2019, we experimented with using an image of the membership card that was being offered (as a part of activating your membership) within the email prospecting channel. We wanted to see if the card would increase donor conversion rates.
experiments.keyLearnings:
With a 96.9% level of confidence, we successfully validated that the treatment improved donor conversion rates by 92.4% when compared to the control within the email prospecting channel.
There are actually two variables that were changed in the treatment, which were:
Adding the membership card image, andParagraph 5 and the call to action in paragraph 6 were expanded slightly.
Because of these two variables changing, we cannot definitively say that it was the membership card image that increased results singularly, however, we know that the treatment did dramatically improve results over the previous control collectively. With that said, we intend to use this treatment in the future.
Further testing with adding an image of the offer in the email prospecting channel may be warranted to singularly isolate that variable moving forward.

The Heritage Foundation had a major donor step forward and agree to cover all of the administrative and fundraising costs for the organization for a period of time. The goal behind this action was to allow 100% of all other donations to be directed toward the mission. It was our hypothesis that this type of language would increase the perceived impact of the donors’ gifts which would increase their likelihood to donate.
experiments.keyLearnings: By including the language about 100% of the donation going toward the mission, we were able to increase conversion rate by 16.6%. This language gave the visitors the perception that their gift was having a greater impact which acted as an incentive to the giving process.

experiments.title: How promoting a video, then retargeting video viewers with an offer decreased results

rows.name: Video View » Purchase Optimized Content Offer

experiments.researchQuestion: Would targeting prospecting audiences with a related video to a content offer increase donor acquisition campaign results?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/13/2019 - 11/10/2019
Facebook agency partners consistently tell agencies, who then tell the clients they serve, that the best way to promote an offer is to launch with a video, optimize your campaign for video views, then retarget video viewers with an offer.
The Heritage Foundation decided that they would like to test this process once and for all and get to the bottom of whether or not this approach does indeed increase results.
experiments.keyLearnings:
With a 99.9% level of confidence, we observed a decrease in donor conversion rate of -98.5% when targeting prospecting audiences with a video, followed by a content offer promotion.
With that said, here are some positive observations from the treatment experience:
Our audience "reach" (the number of unique people that saw our ad at least once) increased by 198%.Our impressions increased by 332%.
However, these are the other decreases for the treatment experience that we witnessed during this experiment:
Clicks decreased by 67%.The number of emails we captured decreased by 86%.The number of donors we created decreased by 98%.Revenue decreased by 95%
These results were produced on a slightly less (-6%) investment relative to what was spent on the control experience.
Where everything falls apart for this experience type is in the cost per result or outcome measurements. Here is what we observed:
Our cost per email acquired increased nearly 6x over the control experience.Our cost to acquire a donor increased by slightly more than 13x over the control experience.Our ROI on this type of campaign decreased by 95.5% when compared to results achieved in the control experience.
Key Learnings:
For nonprofit organizations who need to squeeze the best returns from every dollar invested, it would appear that video promotion + offer promotion to video viewers is absolutely NOT a viable path to grow your file and maximize returns to the organization on ad spend.If your organization is attempting to grow awareness (and perhaps purchase intent or favorability on a particular issue), video optimized campaigns certainly appear to be effective in terms of generating reach, however brand lift experimentation is advised to be sure of the exact value from that type of investment.Purchase optimized content offers still appear to be the best option when it comes to producing a tangible return on investment (emails, donors, instant revenue).

				× 98.5% decrease in conversion rate

experiments.title: How optimizing remarketing ads for video views decreased donor conversion rate

Timeframe: 10/14/2019 - 11/30/2019
In an attempt to better understand the best way to reach newly acquired subscribers with remarketing messages to convert them into first time donors, The Heritage Foundation wanted to experiment with the optimization events to determine which approach was most effective.
experiments.keyLearnings:
With a 99.9% level of confidence, using the "video view" optimization event for a video-based remarketing ad decreased donor conversion rate by 87.8%.
It would appear that using the "purchase" conversion event in Facebook remarketing campaigns is the most effective approach, considering we attempted to experiment with "landing pageviews" earlier in the year for a similar campaign type.

experiments.title: How asking non-donors to be one of a few donors to give a specific amount impacted donor conversion rate during a matching gift campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: How does communicating a specific quantity of non-donors we’re asking to make a first membership-qualifying gift impact donor conversion or revenue?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 08/04/2020
As a part of The Heritage Foundation’s matching gift campaign (called “The Board Challenge”), we wanted to experiment with the ask amount and approach. Typically, the organization has used “make your best gift today” kind of language within their control copy.
In an attempt to set a smaller, more reasonable target, we wanted to experiment with driving non-donors to be “one of just X donors we need today to make a gift of Y.“
The idea behind this is to (a) set a relevant ask amount for the non-donors, but also (b) allow them to see that they can help achieve a “small quantity” goal at that amount, as well.
experiments.keyLearnings:
With a 99.9% level of confidence, we observed a +44.3% increase in donor conversion rate from non-donors with this approach.
We also observed that 36% of the non-donors within the treatment segment gave more than the amount we asked them to, with another 58% giving exactly what we asked for (totaling 94%)!
Conclusion: Without a doubt, this was a verified technique to activate first-time donors with a membership-qualifying gift amount. This should be implemented in every campaign moving forward (for this segment).

experiments.title: How soliciting from a cultivation personality to a list they are not cultivating impacted donor conversion rate during a matching gift campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: Will soliciting from the portion of the list not being actively cultivated by the cultivation-only personality increase donor conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 08/04/2020
The Heritage Foundation’s Director of Membership has been cultivating a portion of the email house file for 9-months now, never having ever solicited the list directly. During this campaign, we wanted to see how the list he was cultivating would respond when he solicited. Furthermore, we also wanted to experiment with how using him as a solicitor would work for the portion of the list he was not cultivating, as well.
experiments.keyLearnings:
With a 99.9% level of confidence, we observed a +30.5% increase in donor conversion rate when using this personality (who had never solicited the audience before) as a solicitor for the list he was not actively cultivating.
We also observed a validated lift in revenue of +25.3% (LoC: 93.4%), as well.
Conclusion: Using a person who has never solicited an audience before within a campaign seems to show an increase in donor conversion rate, considering we also observed a +41% increase in donor conversion rate (99.9% LoC) for this solicitor when asking his cultivation list to give during the campaign, as well.
In other words, if you have a personality, in a ranking position that has never solicited an audience before, it may make sense to experiment with them as a sender within a campaign, as shown here.

experiments.title: How soliciting donor opinion during the donation process impact conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will allowing donors to give their opinion increase donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/29/2018 - 06/05/2018
The Heritage Foundation had run an experiment a few years back that indicated soliciting the opinion of existing donors during the donation process increased their likelihood to give a gift. These results were surprising because we have often found adding unnecessary friction like this can negatively impact conversion. We were never able to implement this experiment due to a donation form transition so we decided to retest the concept with the new donation form technology.
experiments.keyLearnings:
When we focused on users that were on a desktop or tablet computer, which makes up 88% of the traffic, we saw a 34% increase to donor conversion by soliciting the donor's opinion. However, when we focused on the mobile traffic, we saw a 56% decrease in donor conversion (92% LoC). These two wildly different results emphasize the importance of looking into the user experience of our donors at individual segment levels.

One key learning we can pull from this experiment is how donor engagement can improve the overall giving experience. When they were in a position that the additional friction from the question was not detrimental (in this case, on a desktop computer where it is easier to fill out a form), soliciting their opinion made them feel like they had a voice within the organization which increased their likelihood to give a gift.

* We used Google Analytics to validate the results of this experiment and it should be noted that the mobile segment of traffic was too small to statistically validate the 56% decrease. It should be treated as a directional indicator.

experiments.title: How soliciting from a cultivation personality to their cultivation list impacted donor conversion rate during a matching gift campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: Will soliciting from a cultivation-only personality increase donor conversion rate with the list they are actively cultivating?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 08/04/2020
The Heritage Foundation’s Director of Membership has been cultivating a portion of the email house file for 9-months now, never having ever solicited the list directly. As a part of their matching gift campaign (called “The Board Challenge”), we decided to experiment with how the audience he was cultivating would respond when solicited by him for the first time during this campaign.
experiments.keyLearnings:
With a 99.9% level of confidence, we observed a +41.9% increase in donor conversion rate when using the long-time cultivating personality to solicit the list that he was cultivating for the first time during this matching gift campaign.
We also observed a validated increase in revenue of +34.4% (LoC: 95.7%), as well.
Conclusion: If you have a personality that has been cultivating a list, consider using them as one of the solicitors within a direct fundraising campaign effort. Further, it is advisable to open cultivation up to the entire file for this personality moving forward.

experiments.title: How soliciting the donor’s opinion affects the gifts acquired
experiments.researchQuestion: How does a “gift designation” field impact donor conversion and total revenue?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/10/2015 - 09/16/2015
The addition of the “gift designation” field ended up increasing the donations acquired by 20.8%. The addition of the field certainly caused additional friction for the donor but the value communicated by this field was more than enough to overcome it.
The key to success in this experiment was the change in approach to the standard gift designation field found with many other donation forms. Instead of asking where the funds should be direct, which is a very organizationally-centric question, we asked the donors how Heritage should be spending its time. This empowered the donors to be able to state their opinion at a high level instead of a worrying about the tactical elements that it takes to implement the aspects.
Additionally, a lift in the number of gifts was achieved in both steps of the process. The donation form makes an initial ask for the Heritage Foundation and then a subsequent ask for their sister organization, Heritage Action for America. The “gift designation” field produced a 12% lift to conversion on the initial form and an 82% lift to the subsequent form which is what resulted in 20.8% increase to the gifts acquired
experiments.keyLearnings:
The Heritage Foundation had the hypothesis that if a donor were able to direct their donation to a specific areas, they would be more likely to give a gift. Historically, Heritage has shied away from using gift designations on their donation form due to the friction it can cause for the donor and the problems associated with restricted funds.

In order to test out this hypothesis, we create a spin on the standard gift designation question. Instead of asking the donor where their funds should be directed, we asked them where they thought Heritage should focus its efforts. We then deployed this experiment on the member renewal page.
experiments.fluxMetrics:

				gift).
				This experiment produced the following results:
0% increase in traffic
				× 21.7% increase in conversion rate
				× 0% increase in average gift

experiments.title: How tailoring your ask to the COVID-19 (Coronavirus) crisis impacted donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Would tailoring an appeal based upon the COVID-19 crisis improve donor conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 04/01/2020
During The Heritage Foundation’s annual Membership Card Campaign, the team decided to experiment with an upcoming membership renewal campaign email by tailoring the content and the call to action specifically to the COVID-19 crisis tethered to their core value proposition for membership.
We wanted to see how mentioning the COVID-19 crisis would impact renewal and giving.
experiments.keyLearnings:
With a 99.9% level of confidence, we observed that the donor conversion rate in the experiment was decreased by -81.1%.
When it comes to a membership renewal campaign, it certainly appears that tying your core value proposition to the COVID-19 crisis does not motivate donors to give (or members to renew their support).
In Heritage's case, certainly providing policy research and recommendations to lawmakers as they developed government policies in response to the crisis did not work.
It is worth noting that Heritage also used a different sender for this experiment, and the clickthrough rate was impacted (as shown in the number of sessions), which may have had an impact to the results, as well.
It is left to be seen and further testing may be required to test direct fundraising appeals for this core value proposition in light of the crisis, but it would appear that acknowledging the COVID-19 (or Coronavirus) at all in this campaign was not effective. Subsequently, the control messaging was used for the remainder of the series of emails in the campaign.

experiments.title: How running Facebook ads to Membership Card campaign targeting impacted donor conversion rates from active members
experiments.researchQuestion: Does adding a third channel in Facebook advertising (alongside email and direct mail channels) improve response rates or revenue?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/01/2019 - 03/31/2019
We wanted to continue experimentation with running Facebook ads alongside focused campaign messaging used this time to promote their Membership Card campaign.
experiments.keyLearnings:
With a 99.9% level of confidence, we were able to increase revenue by 7.0% from the active member segment.
This increase was largely driven by a +7.95% increase in average gift, as the donor conversion rate decreased by -0.74% versus the control segment.  The increase in average gift is consistent with previous experimentation with adding ads as a lift/support channel to campaign efforts.
Finally, the incremental expense to serve ads to this audience generated a 4.98 ROI from the revenue lift in the segment alone.
It is highly advised to run ads to active members throughout campaigns moving forward.
Note: Offline gifts were not included as a part of this assessment. This included online transactions only, and was run through the first half of the final day of the campaign. Additional updates will be forthcoming.
