									161.1% increase in revenue

experiments.title: How the placement of an inline offer impacts conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: How does the placement of the donation advertisement impact donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/25/2017 - 06/05/2017
On The Daily Signal website, we had been testing an inline direct ask for a donation. Given the high volume of traffic to the website, Heritage was seeing considerable revenue come from this offer. However, we began to be concerned about the visitor experience since the offer was interrupting the flow of the article.
We decided to test the impact of moving the donation ask to the bottom of the article and simply including an show inline sentence that would link to the bottom. Our belief was that even if the treatment saw no significant difference, it would be a win since it would improve the readers’ experience.
experiments.keyLearnings: Moving the donation ask to the bottom of the article resulted in a 48% increase to the number of visitors going to the donation page and a 91% increase to overall donations. Our hypothesis is that allowing the visitor to experience the content before making the ask increased the clarity of the value proposition. This is what would have led to the increased volume of traffic and conversion.

experiments.title: How a simpler, more direct value proposition affects clickthrough rate on a Facebook ad
experiments.researchQuestion: How does a shorter, more direct value proposition affect clickthrough rate on a Facebook ad?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/12/2017 - 04/10/2017
The Heritage Foundation was promoting a quiz around the “Gadsden Flag” that led to a membership offer with the flag as a premium. They had created two versions of a Facebook ad, as they had seen some research that longer, more engaging copy could increase clickthrough and conversion rate. The control, in this case, had much longer copy that talked about the history of the flag and made an offer to the reader to test their knowledge. The treatment copy was much shorter, and asked a direct question to the reader: “How much do you know about the Gadsden Flag?”
They split the traffic to determine which ad attracted more clicks.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The shorter ad with the direct question produced a 122.1% increase in clickthrough rate, which showed that the elements of this ad increased the clarity of the value proposition. Longer copy can be more effective, but only if if clarifies the value proposition. In addition, the direct question to provoke the reader to respond by taking the intended action.
This success prompted further testing to isolate the variables of length and the direct question to understand how each of these played into the success of the ad.

experiments.title: How a program-specific value proposition impacted donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a Daily Signal focused page increase donor conversion for visitors coming from a news-focused email?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/02/2017 - 08/16/2017
During The Heritage Foundation Board Match fundraising campaign, we wanted to incorporate one of their more popular programs (The Daily Signal) into a fundraising appeal email. The goal was to show the broad impact of the organization by talking about the various aspects of the organization.
Historically, we had utilized the same landing page throughout the campaign for each email. This is something that we had tested into, finding that switching landing pages didn’t often result in any significant lifts so we determined it was not worth the extra time and energy.
However, in this situation, we felt that the brand recognition and popularity of the program may be worth creating its own page. We decided to test that hypothesis by creating a new landing page that was branded as The Daily Signal and focused its value proposition on that one aspect of Heritage.
experiments.keyLearnings: The landing page that reinforced The Daily Signal brand and focused the value proposition solely on that aspect of the organization increased donations by 272%. This emphasizes the importance of creating a cohesive message all the way through the donation process. It also all verifies a trend that we had been noticing in the last several month, that The Daily Signal is a viable program for raising funds with.

experiments.title: How the tactic used in presenting the value proposition impacted The Daily Signal’s donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Which tactic in presenting the value proposition will lead to the greatest increase in donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/21/2016 - 12/21/2016
Through a series of previous experiments, we had discovered a value proposition for The Daily Signal that seemed to resonate with donor. This value proposition focused on the type of journalism provided by TDS on a daily basis. With this in mind, we wanted to see if we could find a better means to deliver this message.
We created two new treatment options:
The first treatment took the form of a “Wikipedia-style” delivery that appeared in the middle of every article. It devoted more space to cohesively lay out the reasons to support The Daily Signal.
The second treatment took the form of an inline-survey to readers. The idea was to get the readers to say whether they valued the content. If they did, they would then be shown the donation value proposition as the next step in the process. If they selected they did not like it, they would be shown a comment box to submit the reasons why.
With such a high level of traffic, we were able to launch these three options at the same time over a period of a month.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The experiment produced significant lifts in both click through rate and donation rate. The table above lists the individual conversion rates from reader to donor. The click through rates followed similar trends. This results in two key learnings:
By increasing the process-level value of donating (fully laying out the reasons to give), both treatments were able to outperformed the control.
The “Dear Reader” version had the highest impact on clickthrough rate (256% lift) and donor conversion rates (642% lift).

				gift).

experiments.title: How radically redesigning an email affected donation revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a radical redesign of the email that increases the force of the value proposition and introduces images increase response and revenue?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/15/2017 - 11/20/2017
The Heritage Foundation created a popular offer for email acquisition with their Digital Guide to the Constitution. After a visitor downloaded the Digital Guide, they were offered membership to Heritage, along with an offer to get the print copy of the Guide to the Constitution for a gift of $100. This offer flow had been quite effective at converting prospects into donors using a text-based approach that had tested well with the house file. But since acquisition mailings reached an audience of people who were not as familiar with Heritage, they created a treatment to test that increased the force of the value proposition and introduced images of the offer.
The control, which introduced the free offer with a casual greeting, focused on the content contained in the Guide. The treatment created an enemy: “Right now, liberals are waging an all-out war against our nation’s founding principles…”. Instead of a resource, it offered a “gift”, and highlighted the hard work that went into its creation, before revealing that it was created for free. The treatment email also included an image of the offer to provide clarity.
Then, Heritage ran an A/B test to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment produced a 123% increase in donations as well as an increase in revenue, which produced a big difference in return on investment. This showed the power of the increased value proposition, with the additional impact of the imagery to help the recipient visualize the offer.
This is a promising result that should be tested on more lists to ensure that there isn’t an anomaly with the first list choice.

				gift).

experiments.title: How providing a midpoint call-to-action impacts overall conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will removing the midpoint call-to-action increase overall conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/15/2017 - 01/02/2018
We discovered that removing the midpoint call-to-action reduced the number of people clicking on the ad by 24% which then led to a 40% decrease in overall donations. It would appear that the midpoint CTA does more than just jump people to the bottom of the page. It gives them a hint at the offer they will soon see which made them not only more likely to click on the ad but more likely to donate too.
experiments.keyLearnings:
For The Daily Signal, their Dear Reader inline donation ask had become a significant source of revenue for the organization. During the last calendar year end campaign, we did some testing around both the messaging and ways to increase urgency. Over the last 12 months, we had also learned that moving the ask to the bottom of the article was able to significantly increase donations. However, when we moved it to the bottom, we left a small, midpoint call-to-action that was a short headline and sentence that linked to the full offer at the bottom of the article.
During this year end campaign, we wanted to see if we could extrapolate what we learned from the positioning experiment. We wanted to remove the midpoint CTA entirely which would give readers an uninterrupted reading experience before the ask was made. Our hypothesis was that this would lead to an increased likelihood of someone engaging with the ad at the end.

experiments.title: How the “villian” of an appeal impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: What “villian” will provide the best donor conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/09/2017 - 12/26/2017
To take advantage of the current political headlines, The Heritage Foundation was running and appeal focused on passing the tax reform legislation. Historically, appeals had generally focused on the liberal members of Congress as the “villians” with copy focusing on their efforts to block conservative legislation. However, in this situation, the bigger problem was with certain Republican congressmen that were waffling on the legislation. The Left was still going to try to block the legislation but since they were in a minority, it wouldn’t really have any impact if all Republicans voted along party lines.
Given this situation, we decided to test the standard value proposition. We wanted to see if people would be more motivated by “getting the Republican Establishment in line” or “battling the Left.”
experiments.keyLearnings: For this appeal, we discovered that “the Liberals” were the most motivating reason to make a gift. Despite the reality of the situation, it would appear most donors were not motivated to give in order to combat people that they otherwise would have aligned themselves with. This is definitely a learning that would need to be further validated with other situations but a good lesson to keep in mind when crafting appeals.
experiments.fluxMetrics:
The Flux Metrics analyze the three primary metrics that affect revenue (traffic, conversion rate, and average
				gift).

experiments.title: How the minimum premium gift impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Can we increase overall revenue by increasing the minimum gift required for a donation premium?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/10/2016 - 06/23/2016
On one of their donor acquisition campaign, The Heritage Foundation was testing the use of a premium to increase conversion and average gift. Previous experiments had proven that the premium increased the likelihood of a donor to convert but we wanted to see if we could also increase to overall revenue coming from the campaign. We decided to try testing the premium at a $125 minimum gift level. The control treatment had the minimum gift set at $100.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The $125 minimum gift was able to lift the average gift by 46% but it decreased the overall conversion by 58%. This resulted in a 39% decrease to overall revenue for the treatment. In this situation, the $100 minimum gift amount was the clear winner between the two.
However, even if this test had managed a slight increase to revenue but still decreased conversion rate significantly, the control would still have been the winner. The purpose of this campaign was new donor acquisition. It would take a significant increase in revenue to justify any loss in the number of donors acquired.

				gift).

				× 58.4% decrease in conversion rate
				× 46.0% increase in average gift
									39.3% decrease in revenue

experiments.title: How employing active language affects clickthrough rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Does active language incentivize more people to click through?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/24/2014 - 08/14/2014
The Heritage Foundation has an annual fundraising campaign backed by a challenge grant from their board of Trustees. In 2014, Steve Forbes wrote an introductory paragraph on the landing page about why the board was matching gifts. Heritage wanted to tease the quote in the ad, but wanted to test the active language of Forbes “issuing a challenge” versus the more passive language of “announcing a challenge grant”.
experiments.keyLearnings: By using active language, Heritage's new ad attracted more interest from people who saw it. Nonprofit organizations commonly use challenge grants during fundraising campaigns -- but "issuing a challenge" from the board is a much stronger way to approach the same action.

experiments.title: How the tone of advertising impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will the more action-oriented “Drain the Swamp” message increase the motivation of visitors clicking on the callout enough to influence donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/24/2017 - 10/03/2017
The Heritage Foundation had learned from previous experiments that a homepage callout section that focused on new member acquisition as able to drive a high volume of donor motivated traffic. In previous tests on this callout, we had tested the entire funnel and found that a membership offer was the highest performing with regards to new donors but that the “Drain the Swamp” message resulted in the most clicks.
We decided to test out a “Drain the Swamp” message on the homepage callout against the standard “Join Heritage” language. Both of these would include a final call to action of “Become a member” and would land on the same donation landing page that focused on membership as the offer.
experiments.keyLearnings: We found that the “Drain the Swamp” message was able to increase overall donor conversion by 116%. When we analyze the differences between the two versions, we can see that the “Drain the Swamp” message is highly action-oriented compared to the “Join Heritage” message that focused more on the past accomplishments and what Heritage is rather than what it will do. This leads to the hypothesis that people support Heritage based upon what it will do, not on what it has done in the past.

				gift).

0% increase in traffic
				× 115.9% increase in conversion rate
				× 0% increase in average gift

experiments.title: How adding a “micro-yes” decision impacted conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will creating an additional micro-yes prior to a direct ask increase overall donations?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/06/2017 - 11/22/2017
The Daily Signal had been running a series of experiments known as the “Dear Reader” for the past 10 months. This experiment added a direct ask to every Daily Signal article and had resulted in significant revenue for the organization. Over the course of the 10 months, we had tested everything from value proposition to placement.
One learning we had recently observed was a boost in our advertising click through rate from Facebook when we incorporated exceptionally long copy. When doing that, Facebook automatically added a “Read More…” link in the ads. One of our hypotheses for this boost in performance was that having this link created an additional micro-yes decision that required people to mentally commit that they wanted to read more; this caused additional buy-in prior to the call to action being made.
We decided to test this hypothesis out on the Daily Signal Dear Reader treatment. We would hide the content related to the direct ask behind a “Read More” button. Our belief is this additional micro-yes would create more buy-in prior to the donation ask.
experiments.keyLearnings: The Read More button resulted in a 54% decrease in donations. This drop in donations was primarily the result of a drop in clicks on the donation button in the ad; the number of people visiting the donation page dropped by 64%. In this situation, the additional decision created more friction than mental buy-in which negatively impacted the results.
experiments.fluxMetrics:
The Flux Metrics analyze the three primary metrics that affect revenue (traffic, conversion rate, and average
				gift).
				This experiment produced the following results:
0% increase in traffic
				× 54.2% decrease in conversion rate
				× 0% increase in average gift

experiments.title: How a more complex value proposition affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will including value proposition copy about a sister organization increase donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/26/2017 - 06/26/2017
Heritage Action was raising funds to spur Congress to action on several initiatives. They had recently reviewed donor research that revealed that their sister organization, The Heritage Foundation, was a big factor in their donors’ decision to give. Though this campaign was raising money for Heritage Action, they hypothesized that including some copy to integrate the value proposition of The Heritage Foundation into the copy might increase donors’ likelihood to give.
They created a treatment page that attempted to bolster the value proposition for Heritage Action using this copy and launched an A/B test to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment with the additional Heritage Foundation value proposition copy reduced donor conversion by 12.3%. This showed that in this instance, trying to increase the force of the value proposition by adding additional copy reduced the likelihood that donors would give. This could be for multiple reasons:
The Heritage-focused copy was a distraction to donors who were already motivated to give to Heritage Action.
The Heritage-focused copy was in the wrong place.
The Heritage-focused copy wasn’t necessary and added friction that caused more donors to stop reading and leave the page.
The research that backed this experiment was valid, which means that this concept must be tested even further.

				× 12.3% decrease in conversion rate
				× 29.3% increase in average gift
									13.4% increase in revenue

experiments.title: Discovering the Daily Signal’s value proposition with an inline donation ask
experiments.researchQuestion: Which value proposition will drive the most donations for The Daily Signal?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/18/2016 - 09/26/2016
The Daily Signal was originally launched several years back to serve as an unbiased news source. Since its launch, it has seen a significant boost in traffic with millions of people visiting each month. This site is an initiative that is run by The Heritage Foundation.
A decision was made recently to discover ways to start monetizing the traffic while keeping the actually content free for visitors. It was decided that we could position a donation ask at the end of each article. The question then became “what aspect of The Daily Signal would visitors be most willing to sponsor?” To determine this, we launched an experiment utilizing three different value proposition statements.
experiments.keyLearnings: After a month and a half of running this experiment, we determined that the “investigative journalism” and “conservative policy analysis” aspects of The Daily Signal were more appealing to the readers than the other options. This value proposition lifted donor conversion by 275%. Our hypothesis is that this statement was the one that best tapped into the unique aspects of the website. While the other value proposition statements may have been appealing to visitors, they could have been said about any number of other websites.

				× 75.3% increase in average gift
									558.5% increase in revenue

experiments.title: How emphasizing recurring giving impacts overall revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: Which donation page treatment will yield the greatest number of donors and revenue?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/29/2016 - 12/07/2016
In the latest in the series of optimization experiments on the main Heritage donation forms, we implemented a new donation form and value proposition that emphasized the value of recurring giving. In addition to the expanded and more visual value proposition, the new form also laid out a cohesive product-level value proposition on why recurring giving is so important.
With this new treatment, we ran a test across several of the most highly trafficked donation pages to see which would result in more gifts.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment had interesting results. The new treatment resulted in a 54% decrease in the number of donors acquired. However, since the goal of the treatment was to acquire recurring donors given their increased value, we need to analyze the impact on that segment. In the case of recurring donors, the new form does see a 87% increase in acquisition.
Assuming that the average recurring donor will last 12 months, we then factored these two results and discovered that after 12 months, the new treatment would effectively break even on revenue compared to the original control. With a 54% drop in donors, continuing with the treatment would seem to be a pretty risky bet on long-term revenue potential. Based upon this analysis, we decided to end the test and continue with the current control.

				gift).

									11.5% increase in revenue

experiments.title: How reinforcing the value proposition and adding a call-to-action affects donor conversion.
experiments.researchQuestion: Will adding a call-to-action and reinforcing the value proposition improve donor conversion and revenue?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/13/2016 - 06/13/2016
In an effort to optimize the main donation page for The Heritage Foundation, we took a look at the copy that was currently there to find ways to improve and increase the force of the value proposition. While the existing copy was strong and provided solid evidentials, we noticed it was missing an important element in the copy, an ask. Our hypothesis was that we would be able to increase donor conversion and overall revenue by adding copy, to what was already there, that included a call-to-action and reinforcement of the value proposition.
experiments.keyLearnings:
We had arrived at the original copy through a series of prior experiments. As a result, we were relatively confident that it adequately communicated the value proposition of The Heritage Foundation, but by simply adding an additional paragraph that reinforced that message and asking them to make a gift, we were able to not only get more donors to give, but give even more generously. The treatment increased donor conversion by 49%.
Additionally, we saw an increase in average gift by 62.9%. While the average gift lift had not yet statistically validated by the time the test was shut off, the approaching validity seems to indicate that we would be experiencing a combined lift to revenue of 143.5%.
This experiment reinforced the importance of increasing the force of the value proposition by reminding people of what their gift will do and always having a call-to-action in the copy.

									143.5% increase in revenue

experiments.title: How the amount of value proposition on a page affects email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: How does the amount of value proposition on a page affect email acquisition rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/02/2016 - 12/09/2016
The Heritage Foundation was promoting the Index of Military Strength, a publication that gave insight into the U.S. government’s military spending and readiness. They put together a landing page to promote this publication, but had the feeling that their copy was too short and didn’t properly “sell” the offer.
They created a second page that listed out many additional details about the Index, expanding the value proposition. Though the copy was much longer and it pushed the email acquisition form down the page and below the fold, they hypothesized that it would result in increased signups.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The added value proposition increased email acquisition rate by 25.6%, which meant many more emails were acquired for the same amount of ad spend. This reiterated the principle that, although a free offer doesn’t have a price, visitors must be sold on an offer all the way up to the point that they accept it by submitting the form.
This prompted more testing to add more evidentials into the page that supported the additional claims of value.

experiments.title: How a softer call to action affects conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a softer call to action generate more donations?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/14/2016 - 03/18/2016
The Heritage Foundation was promoting their Membership Card to non-members giving them the opportunity to become a member.
Anticipating that the direct ask to “become a member” would receive fewer (but more motivated) clicks from the email, they wanted to test whether the added step and call-to-action to “confirm the name on your card” would generate more clicks, and result in more donations.
Since they weren’t making a direct ask, they created a different offer flow for this treatment. They created a two-step form for the treatment that started with a simple “confirm your name” step (pre-populated with the visitor’s name), and then took them to complete their donation. Because the visitor was indicating their intent to become a member by completing the first step, they removed much of the sales copy on the donation page in order to remove friction from the process.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The softer call to action generated a 54% increase in clicks, but a 51.6% decrease in donations. This revealed a key insight: all traffic is not the same. Visitors come with different motivations, and the traffic to the treatment flow was not at all “sold”.
This prompted a second test to see if reintroducing the value proposition copy on the follow-on donation form would affect the results.

				gift).
				This experiment produced the following results:
0% increase in traffic
				× 51.6% decrease in conversion rate
				× 0% increase in average gift

This prompted a second test to see if reintroducing the value proposition copy on the follow-on donation form would affect the results.

experiments.title: How increasing the force of the value proposition affects revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a stronger value proposition increase donations and revenue, in spite of a different design?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/09/2016 - 03/15/2016
The Heritage Foundation was sending lots of traffic to their “Conservative Declaration”, a document that asked conservatives to unite under shared principles. Immediately after signing, the visitors were presented with an offer to become a member of The Heritage Foundation by making a donation. The entire experience took place within a single design.
Heritage had another membership page that had been performing extremely well. However, design was a key part of this page, and it didn’t match the design of the Conservative Declaration. Knowing that congruency is a key part of conversion, they decided to test it anyways to see if increasing the force of the value proposition outweighed design consistency.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment produced a 460% increase in revenue, despite breaking from the design of the original offer. This is a powerful learning that shows that the message is more important than the container.
This also points the way to a future test to see if revenue can be further increased by working the new value proposition into the existing design and increasing congruency.

experiments.title: How a direct ask about planned giving affects clickthrough rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Which approach will attract more clicks?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/06/2015 - 04/30/2015
The Heritage Legacy Society sent a prospecting email to ask Heritage Foundation members if they planned to include the organization in their estate planning. They didn’t know the best way to approach this through email, so they tested two distinct approaches: a direct ask, supported by facts about tax planning, against a story-based approach with a personal testimony from a Heritage Legacy Society member.
experiments.keyLearnings: This told us that we don’t necessarily need to “soften” the ask with a story. The story might introduce friction as an estate commitment is a personal decision, not one based on third-party influence.

experiments.title: How testing a new messaging approach affects conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Which messaging approach will be most effective at converting visitors to members and increasing revenue?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/23/2015 - 10/30/2015
As part of their membership campaign, The Heritage Foundation wanted to determine which messaging approach would be most appealing to their donors. At the start of the campaign, we created three landing page treatments based upon two new hypotheses of what the members would respond best to.
Our current control version focused on the current situation in American politics and framed it around the negative aspects of the “establishment.” Alternatively, we wanted to test a more positive tone using the same framework. The third treatment was a far more radical redesign using a more modern design that pointed to historical accomplishments and touted the benefits of membership.
experiments.keyLearnings: Both variants resulted in a statistically significant lift with the more radical redesign achieving a 87.2% lift over the control. This would lead us to hypothesize that the constituency is more likely to respond to a positive messaging approach. They may have frustrations with the current state of American politics but this is not what motivates them to give. They are responding to a message of hope and positive change.

experiments.title: How a more personal subject line affects open rate
experiments.researchQuestion: How does a more personal subject line impact open rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/03/2017 - 01/05/2017
With a little more than two days left before the end of the year, Heritage Foundation email subscribers had received six different messages from Jim DeMint– the president of the organization– asking for their support with a year-end gift. Best practices suggest that the email signer should be the most publicly know leader of the organization, and should rarely (if ever) change to ensure donors hear a consistent voice. Heritage tested that theory by sending an email from someone else in the organization.
experiments.keyLearnings: This test proved that a more personalized message, even if not from the primary organizational sender, can have a significant impact on open rate — and therefore the donor’s likelihood to give.

experiments.title: How a two-step form reduced affects cognitive friction and conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a two-step form allow for the capture of more information compared to a single page form that acquired all of the information at one time?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/10/2015 - 09/09/2015
For The Heritage Foundation’s Presidents’ Club Meeting registration, we wanted to find a way to increase the number of attendees to the event. Historically, the forms had all been a single page with all of the data capture fields required before someone could register. These fields included selecting each session they would like to attend and entering all of their information into the form.
We had the hypothesis that if we moved the process to a two-step process, it would create less friction in the visitor’s minds since the forms would be shorter. This two step process would also allow us to look up the donor’s information (based upon their email address or ID) and use this to pre-populate the second form.  We were able to not only fill in their address information but also pre-select the sessions that their membership level qualified for.  We wanted to see if this process would lead to an increased number of completed registrations.
experiments.keyLearnings: The two step form led to a 99% increase in the number of donors completing their registration information. This increase is the result of more than just the two step form; the ability to pre-fill the donor’s information into the second form would have significantly reduced the friction associated with the process. This reduction in friction made the form easier to understand end simpler to fill out.

experiments.title: How focusing on the product-level value proposition affects revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: Which value proposition is most appealing to visitors from the Daily Signal?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/17/2015 - 08/05/2015
The Heritage Foundation operates a political news organization called the Daily Signal. Part of the site has links back to the Heritage Foundation properties include a donation link found at the bottom of the page.
Since the donors coming from the site may not be as familiar with The Heritage Foundation, we wanted to discover what value proposition would be most appealing to the donors. We decided to create two treatments, one that targeted the primary value proposition of giving to The Heritage Foundation and the other that focused on a product-level value proposition of giving to the Daily Signal.
experiments.keyLearnings: By focusing the messaging on the value of the Daily Signal, we were able to better align with the visitors’ motivations. We were also able to keep visual congruence by changing the header of the page to reflect the branding of the Daily Signal. All of these adjustments led to a lift in revenue by 172.8%.

experiments.title: How changing the landing page design affects revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: Which landing page design produces the most donor conversions and revenue?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/09/2015 - 06/23/2015
The Heritage Foundation had been using the same landing page design for their search engine marketing (SEM) donor campaigns for the past three years. During those three years, we had run multiple experiments on the main organizational donation page which had gradually evolved into something that looked completely different.
We were in the process of moving the SEM page to the same domain as the main donation form and realized that it would be a good time to validate that the old design was still effective for the SEM audience segment.  We created an experiment by duplicating the page content but displaying it on the two different page designs.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The new design treatment caused an 85.1% drop in revenue by decreasing conversion rate by 67.6% and decreasing average gift by 54.1%. The simplified design used in the treatment was developed for a page with minimal copy. When we added the long value proposition text that had been developed for this specific landing page, it became difficult to read.
Landing pages must be designed so that they are easily scannable by the visitor. In this situation, the sub-headlines didn’t stand out enough and the lack of page border made the page seem longer than it was.  All of these elements caused enough friction in the mind of the donor to have a negative impact on both conversion rate and average gift.

experiments.title: How visually increasing urgency in an email affects response rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Adding an animated countdown clock to the email will increase the urgency in the mind of the donor which will increase the response rate of the campaign.

Timeframe: 12/30/2014 - 12/31/2014
December 31st is the biggest giving day of the year for many nonprofits. As the end of the year approaches, organizations often look for ways to increase urgency to inspire additional giving. DTS added some animation to their emails as a way to visually remind supporters of the approaching deadline.
experiments.keyLearnings: Since the countdown clock increased both conversion rate and average gift, this tactic will be used in future campaigns that contain an inherit deadline like calendar year end or fiscal year end. Interestingly, the countdown clock in the email did not actually increase the number of clicks to the landing page but significantly increase the number of donors that subsequently gave a gift.

experiments.title: How point-of-donation upgrading affects  conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Do members to the Heritage foundation give more in order to receive greater benefits?  How does communicating the available benefits of the next level affect conversion and average gift amount?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/11/2015 - 01/23/2015
For existing members to the Heritage Foundation, the donation form is prepopulated with the donor’s prior giving amount as a suggested gift.  However, in the case of the control, it is not clearly presented to the member what level their donation qualifies them for and what benefits are provided.  As a way to upgrade these donors, this experiment contemplated the value of communicating the membership level and associated benefits for a donation at the next highest level of membership.
experiments.keyLearnings: The results of this experiment are very interesting.  On one hand, including the upgrade language increases average gift amount by 12.2%.  But on the other hand, the upgrade language decreased average gift by 12.6%.  This suggests that though the tactic may prove to be effective to get a donor to upgrade their gift amount, it may also turn others off.  More testing needs to be conducted to understand how changes in the language might be able to mitigate the drop off in conversion while still positively affecting average gift amount.

experiments.title: How a shorter email affects donations
experiments.researchQuestion: The shorter email will drive more visitors to the website which will then allow for more conversions.

Timeframe: 12/18/2014 - 12/19/2014
Once the messaging approach for the second email in the year end campaign was proven with a previous test, Heritage wanted to determine the best way to roll this email out to the entire file.  They developed a test to determine if giving the constituents more information or less in the email would drive additional traffic and, concurrently, additional giving.
experiments.keyLearnings: The shorter email drove 83% more traffic to the website.  Often times, emails that drive incremental traffic produce lower response rates due to the traffic being less qualified.  In this instance, the overall response rate was 128% higher than the longer email meaning we drove more traffic that was more highly qualified.  This is a messaging approach to be used in the future.

									139.6% increase in revenue

experiments.title: How the call-to-action in an email affects click rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Which messaging approach for the year end campaign is the most appealing to the Heritage Foundation audience?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/15/2014 - 12/17/2014
At the start of their end of year campaign, Heritage crafted four different emails that would offer insights into what motivated their donors visit the campaign landing page.  The goal was that by testing these different approaches, we would be able to gain insights as to what messaging approach was most appealing to the visitors to help craft the rest of the campaign messages.
experiments.keyLearnings: The shorter email with a softer call-to-action was able to drive incremental amounts of traffic with additional gifts (although the response rate did not have a level of confidence above 95%).

experiments.title: How adding membership level indicators affects membership renewal
experiments.researchQuestion: Does adding language on the donation form that informs donors what member level they qualify for and then telling them the amount needed to move to the next level increase the average revenue per visitor compared to the standard donation form?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/07/2015 - 01/08/2015
The Heritage Foundation has 12 different levels of membership that come with incremental benefits as the donors give me. The standard donation form uses member language but does not detail what level their gift qualifies them for. During their annual membership renewal campaign, we wanted to find a way to let donors know their membership level based upon their giving amount while finding ways to upgrade them.
We devised a donation form that would display what level their current gift qualifies them form and what it would take to upgrade to the next level.  We made sure to include what benefits would come with the next level in membership.  Before rolling it out, we wanted to validate that it would improve the results of the campaign.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The membership language treatment was able to lift the average gift of the donor by 18.6% but it negatively affected the conversion rate by 34.5%. This negative effect on conversion was enough to adversely effect the overall revenue of the form.
Upon digging deeper into the data, we discovered that the language would lift the average gift for donors above $50 but would cause lower level donors, those below $25, to not give at all. We may test membership language for future campaigns but limit the language so that only the larger donors see the language.

				gift).

0% increase in traffic
				× 34.5% decrease in conversion rate
				× 18.6% increase in average gift
									22.3% decrease in revenue

experiments.title: How replacing 3rd party credibility via social validation affects revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: Showing recent donors will add to the credibility of the previously stated value proposition and will foster a herd mentality within the donor’s mind. This will increase conversion rate compared to other 3rd party credibility indicators
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/16/2014 - 12/26/2014
As part of most campaign landing pages, Heritage has included 3rd party credibility indicators (such as external quotes and media appearances) as a way to reduce anxiety. However, we thought it might be possible to make it more relevant if the 3rd party credibility came from actual donors instead of well known sources. As such, we developed an embeddable widget that would scroll through recent donors.
experiments.keyLearnings: Seeing recent donors did not carry as much weight as quotes and appearances in well known sources. In addition to using the 3rd party credibility indicators near the donation form, we will also include these facts in the copy and throughout the page design where appropriate.

experiments.title: How testing the call-to-action on thank you page affects conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Which call-to-action will produce the greatest amount of revenue in the long term?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/19/2014 - 12/08/2014
With the number of donors each month, the thank you page on the primary Heritage donation form offered an opportunity for testing. In an effort to further capitalize on the donor’s motivation, we created two follow-on calls-to-action to either become a recurring monthly donor or to give a gift to their sister organization, Heritage Action.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The follow-on call-to-action of giving to Heritage Action produced significantly more gifts and additional revenue beyond what would be provided by the monthly donors. The revenue given in the Heritage Action donation even out performed the recurring donor revenue when the recurring donor gifts were multiplied to account for the recurring nature of the gift. The donors saw Heritage Action as another way to make their gift go further which carried more value in their mind.
This follow-on call-to-action will be rolled out to other donation forms maintained by Heritage and it will be utilized in future campaigns for join fundraising opportunities.-

experiments.title: How shortening an email affects clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: By placing the content on the landing page and using the email to tease and get the click, can we drive additional traffic to the page which will increase the potential number of donors?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/10/2014 - 12/12/2014
Shorter copy with a soft call-to-action drove 481.4% more visitors to the site. However, both treatments had identical response rates which suggests the need to optimize future long-copy landing pages.
experiments.keyLearnings: Nonprofits always ask us: how long should our emails be? There’s no absolute right answer for every organization — but it’s something that every nonprofit should be testing. Since the goal of the email is to get the click, DTS decided to test long copy (with a donation-focused call to action in the email) against an shorter email (that made the reader click to find out more).

experiments.title: How employing active language affects clickthrough rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Does active language incentivize more people to click through?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/24/2014 - 08/14/2014
The Heritage Foundation has an annual fundraising campaign backed by a challenge grant from their board of Trustees. In 2014, Steve Forbes wrote an introductory paragraph on the landing page about why the board was matching gifts. Heritage wanted to tease the quote in the ad, but wanted to test the active language of Forbes “issuing a challenge” versus the more passive language of “announcing a challenge grant”.
experiments.keyLearnings: By using active language, Heritage’s new ad attracted more interest from people who saw it. Nonprofit organizations commonly use challenge grants during fundraising campaigns — but “issuing a challenge” from the board is a much stronger way to approach the same action.

experiments.title: How third-party validation affects email acquisition for a free digital offer
experiments.researchQuestion: Will increasing clarity and adding social proof increase email acquisition?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/29/2016 - 07/01/2016
The Heritage Foundation was giving away a digital version of their popular 475-page Guide to the Constitution in exchange for an email address. They had a landing page that was converting 58% of visitors. But this meant that 42% of visitors weren’t downloading the digital Guide, so their team wanted to try four tweaks to increase conversion.
One confusing part of the offer was that the digital version of the Guide was in the form of a microsite, so they were really offering “access” rather than a digital download. This caused potential confusion to the user, so they marketed the offer as a digital download (since there was an available PDF).
They rewrote the headline to increase the clarity of the offer and who it was from.
They added multiple images to the sidebar to show that the Guide could be read on any device
They added user reviews (pulled from Amazon) to increase social proof.
They changed the button copy from “get access now” to “get the Guide now”
Then, they launched an A/B test to see if the new page increased conversion.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The new treatment converted 65.4% of visitors to accept the offer — an increase of 11.2%! This shows that the new landing page tweaks did in fact increase clarity and add some social proof that helped convince the visitor to take action.
While this is a great lift and results in more emails for the same spend, it still means that 35% of our traffic is not converting—which means more testing and optimization is needed!

The Flux Metrics analyze the three primary metrics that affect revenue (traffic, conversion rate, and average
				gift).
				This experiment produced the following results:
0% increase in traffic
				× 11.2% increase in conversion rate
				× 0% increase in average gift

experiments.title: How call-to-action visibility affects email clickthrough rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will adding a second button to the top of the email increase click through rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/01/2016 - 05/13/2016
The Heritage Foundation was partnering with Townhall Media to generate email signups through an eBook giveaway. Townhall generated new signups by sending sponsored emails to their list.
Early results showed acceptable open and conversion rates, but lower than expected clickthrough rates.
We hypothesized that the email openers were abandoning the email because the value proposition and call to action were not immediately visible upon opening. By adding a second call to action button at the top of the email we anticipated a higher number of readers would remain engaged with the email and ultimately click through.
experiments.keyLearnings: The treatment with the second button increased click through rate 40.4%. The new button at the top of the email received some clicks, but the CTR on the unchanged links lower in the email increased as well (19%, 11%, and 27% respectively). This confirmed our hypothesis that more readers would stay engaged with the email and ultimately click if there was a call to action visible immediately upon opening.

experiments.title: How the strength of the value proposition affects pay-per-click donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a stronger value proposition increase donor conversion from search engine marketing?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/01/2016 - 05/03/2016
The Heritage Foundation had run pay-per-click campaigns on brand terms (like “Heritage Foundation”, “Heritage Foundation membership”) for years. These campaigns ran at a positive ROI and gave users who expressed intent through search a clear path to make their gifts.
Because users had high motivation that was expressed through their search terms, a very transactional landing page had tested well. But Heritage wanted to see if a more emotional appeal might improve conversion — one that told a narrative story and utilized the four pillars of a successful value proposition: appeal, exclusivity, clarity, and credibility. The page also used photography, different background, and stacked member benefits right before it asked for the conversion.
They launched an A/B test to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment page with the more thorough value proposition and member benefits produced a 44.1% lift in donor conversion. This tells us that we can actually affect donor motivation — even though someone is looking to make a gift, they aren’t always entirely convinced. The strength of the value proposition must capture them where they are and assure them even more in their decision.
We are never done “selling” the donation until they submit their gift. That’s why landing pages must always leverage each of the four pillars of the value proposition to maximize the force with which it is delivered.

				gift).

experiments.title: The impact on one-click giving for dual ask gifts
experiments.researchQuestion: How will simplifying the giving process impact revenue and conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/16/2015 - 12/27/2015
The Heritage Foundation has a unique process where, after every online gift, the donor is immediately prompted to make a second gift to their sister organization, Heritage Action for America. All the donor has to do for this second gift is enter their donation amount since we already have their address and credit card information from the initial form. This secondary ask generally has a 5% to 8% conversion rate for donors making that second gift.
We had the opportunity to test a new feature in their giving system that allowed for one-click giving. Existing donors would only have to enter their zip code and credit card number to make a donation.  We decided to test this functionality out in their 2015 calendar year end campaign and monitor the impact on all aspects of the donation process.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Interestingly enough, the one-click giving process did not have any statistically significant impact on the initial donor conversion. Our hypothesis is that the decreased friction that came from the simplified form was balanced out but the confusion the process caused. While it was a simpler process; it was different than what the donors were use to.
However, while the immediate conversion was not impacted, the follow on donation opportunity saw a 90.4% decrease in conversion with the one-click giving. Since the donors did not have to enter their address or credit card for the first form, they would have to enter it for the second form (due to system limitations). This added significant friction to the second step in the process that was enough to significantly impact the gifts made to Heritage Action.
