experiments.title: How a dynamic goal thermometer on a high urgency donation page affects revenue and conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that replacing a single, static $500,000 campaign thermometer with a dynamic thermometer that resets in $5,000 increments will achieve an increase in revenue and conversion rates because smaller, frequently achievable goals make progress feel more attainable, reinforce donor impact, and create a stronger sense of momentum and urgency during the year-end giving period.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 12/18/2025
This experiment took place during The Heritage Foundation’s annual calendar year-end fundraising campaign, one of their most important giving periods of the year. The campaign had a total goal of $500,000, and the primary donation experience included a progress thermometer meant to signal momentum and urgency to donors as they considered giving.
Because year-end traffic is high and donors are especially motivated by deadlines and progress cues, this was an ideal moment to test whether an alternative framing of the goal and progress indicator could increase motivation and perceived impact.
The test was run directly on the campaign donation page.
Control: The standard donation page displayed a traditional progress thermometer showing the total amount raised toward the fixed $500,000 campaign goal.
Treatment: The donation page used a dynamic thermometer that “reset” once a smaller goal was reached, increasing by $5,000 increments each time the goal was met. This created the perception of frequent wins and ongoing momentum rather than a single, distant finish line.
Both versions were otherwise consistent, ensuring the thermometer behavior was the primary variable being tested.
The motivation for this experiment came from a behavioral insight: large, distant goals can feel abstract and discouraging, especially late in a campaign when progress may slow. In contrast, smaller, rapidly achievable goals can increase donor confidence, urgency, and the sense that their gift meaningfully contributes to immediate success.
We wanted to test whether breaking a large year-end goal into visible, recurring milestones would:
Make progress feel more attainable
Increase perceived donor impact
Sustain momentum throughout the campaign rather than only near the final deadline
This experiment was designed to evaluate whether a dynamic, milestone-based progress indicator could outperform a traditional static thermometer during a high-stakes year-end fundraising push.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The most significant takeaway from this experiment was that the dynamic, milestone-based thermometer meaningfully increased revenue, even without a corresponding increase in transactions. The treatment generated a 30% lift in revenue, with an 89.7% level of confidence, indicating a strong and practically meaningful impact on total dollars raised.
At the same time, transactions decreased by 10.8%, though this result came with only 48% confidence, suggesting the decline is not statistically reliable and may be due to natural variance rather than a true negative effect. Taken together, these results indicate that the revenue lift was driven primarily by higher average gift amounts, not by more donors completing gifts.
This suggests that the dynamic thermometer helped donors feel more confident making larger contributions by reinforcing momentum and the sense that their gift could meaningfully “complete” a near-term goal.
Moving forward, this experiment reinforces the value of breaking large fundraising goals into visible, attainable milestones, especially during high-pressure moments like year-end campaigns. Rather than relying solely on a single, long-term progress bar, future donation experiences can leverage smaller, frequently achieved goals to sustain momentum and encourage generosity.
Specifically, we will:
Continue testing dynamic or segmented goal framing in high-volume fundraising momentsPair milestone-based thermometers with strategic ask array testing to better understand how goal framing influences gift sizeBe cautious about interpreting transaction volume changes when confidence is low, focusing instead on revenue-per-visitor and average gift as primary success metrics
Overall, this test demonstrates that optimizing how progress is framed can materially impact fundraising outcomes—even when surface-level conversion metrics appear flat or mixed.

experiments.title: How adding clarity of the gift type to the submit button affects transactions (ICE Survey)
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding clarity for the gift type on the submit button for donors will achieve an increase in transactions because the donor more clearly understands the commitment of the gift.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/08/2025 - 09/04/2025
This experiment tested whether clarifying the gift type on the ICE Survey donation form’s submit button would increase completed transactions. The submit button previously did not specify whether the donor was making a one-time or recurring gift, which could introduce uncertainty at the point of decision. To address this, we ran an A/B test for just under 30 days, introducing a variation that clearly stated the gift type on the button (for example, “Make My Monthly Gift” or “Make My One-Time Gift”) to reduce friction and give donors more confidence in their commitment.
The goal was to see if clearer messaging on the submit button would lead to more completed transactions. This aligns with behavioral research showing that reducing uncertainty at critical decision points can increase conversion rates. The test measured the impact on completed transactions as the primary success metric, with button click-throughs as a secondary metric to understand engagement before completion.
If the treatment variation outperformed the control, it would demonstrate the value of transparent communication at the point of conversion. Insights from this test could inform updates to other donation forms and campaigns, ultimately helping drive overall donation growth. By clarifying donor expectations, we aim to create a smoother giving experience and encourage more supporters to complete their gifts.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment variation that added clarity about the gift type to the submit button resulted in a -63.7% relative difference in transactions compared to the control, indicating a substantial decrease in performance. The experiment reached a 93.1% confidence level, providing strong statistical evidence that the treatment negatively impacted conversions.

This suggests that adding gift type language to the submit button created unintended friction or confusion for donors, reducing their likelihood of completing the transaction. 

Future tests should focus on maintaining simplicity and momentum at the conversion point, while addressing commitment clarity at earlier, lower-friction stages of the donor journey.

experiments.title: How adding clarity of the gift type to the submit button affects transactions (Membercard)
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding clarity for the gift type on the submit button for donors will achieve an increase in transactions because the donor more clearly understands the commitment of the gift.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/20/2025 - 09/04/2025
The Heritage Foundation is urging its donors to refresh their membership cards as part of an effort to improve transaction completion.This experiment tested the hypothesis that adding clarity about the selected gift type (one-time or monthly) to the submit button on a donation page would increase completed transactions. The goal was to reduce any hesitation or uncertainty at the final step of the giving process by confirming to donors exactly what type of gift they were about to make.
We conducted an A/B test on the donation page from August 20, 2025 to September 4, 2025. The control version displayed the existing generic submit button “Make My Gift”, while the treatment version dynamically updated the button text to reflect the donor’s selected gift type (e.g. “Make My Monthly Gift” or “Make My One-Time Gift”). All other elements of the page remained the same. The primary success metric was completed transactions, with secondary metrics including form starts and abandonment rate.
This two-week A/B test provided insight into how small, high-visibility text changes at the point of conversion can influence donor behavior.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment were that adding gift type clarity to the submit button did not increase transactions as hypothesized; instead, it resulted in a -30.3% relative decrease in conversion rate with an 81.1% confidence level, indicating a directional but not statistically significant negative effect. 

Donors may have perceived the added detail as complexity or an unexpected change in their intended gift, creating last-minute doubt or second-guessing. Instead of offering reassurance, the extra wording may have disrupted their momentum toward completing the donation.

Future testing could explore lighter, more subtle ways to reinforce gift type clarity earlier in the flow rather than at the final submit button. This could preserve donor confidence without disrupting the decision moment at the point of conversion.

experiments.title: How removing checkboxes from a declaration affects email conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that removing the checkboxes for declaration signers will achieve an increase in email acquisition because we are removing friction from the form.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/05/2025 - 09/04/2025
This experiment tested the hypothesis that removing the “vote of confidence” checkboxes from the Vote of Confidence form would reduce friction and increase email acquisition. The checkboxes were originally included to allow signers to show public support, but they added an extra action to the form. We believed that eliminating these checkboxes while keeping their descriptions as a bulleted list, would create a smoother experience that encourages more people to complete the form and provide their email addresses.
We conducted a split URL test, sending half of the traffic to the original form (with checkboxes) and half to a new version of the form (with the checklist shown as a bulleted list). This setup ensured completely isolated experiences, minimizing cross-contamination between variants. Aside from the removal of the checkboxes, all other design, copy, and flow elements remained consistent. We tracked email acquisition as the primary success metric, while also monitoring revenue, and transaction rates as secondary indicators.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The removal of the checkboxes resulted in a -3.7% relative decrease in email acquisition, with an 88.6% level of confidence, indicating a directional but not statistically significant effect.

Contrary to our hypothesis, the 30-day split URL landing page test showed a small decrease in email acquisition when the checkboxes were removed. While the simplified form reduced friction, it may have also reduced the sense of commitment or engagement that the checkboxes provided, slightly lowering sign-up intent. This suggests that even seemingly minor elements can play a meaningful psychological role, and that removing them does not always result in higher conversions.

This suggests that while the checkboxes introduced a small amount of friction, they may also have served as a commitment or credibility cue, signaling seriousness or social proof to users. Removing them may have subtly reduced users’ sense of engagement or trust in completing the form, which could explain the dip in sign-ups.

For future tests, it may be valuable to explore alternative low-friction commitment indicators to preserve the psychological benefit of commitment signals while reducing perceived effort. Testing placement or wording changes rather than complete removal could also help identify the balance between friction and motivation.

experiments.title: How changing the gift array for members on a campaign donation page affects revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that increasing the gift array for non-members will achieve an increase in revenue.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 08/05/2025
The Heritage Foundation ran their annual President’s Match campaign in July. In three of their emails to members, they wanted to test increasing the one-time gift amounts on the donation page to $50, $100, $250 to see if revenue would increase as well.
They changed the gift array for 50% of the traffic.
***The monthly tab’s gift array was also adjusted from $15, $25, $35 to $14, $24, $42, but we do not have the data to split apart recurring from one-time gifts, so this write up includes all transactions.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that increasing the gift array for members did not achieve the intended increase in revenue. In fact, the treatment group saw a significant decrease in revenue of 22.8% compared to the control group. This result was statistically significant at an 86.4% level of confidence.

Additionally, the treatment segment saw a 25.9% decrease in transactions with a 94.3% level of confidence.

In future experimentation, it is important to carefully analyze the impact of changes to donation strategies before fully implementing them. It may be beneficial to conduct smaller-scale tests or pilot studies before rolling out changes to a larger audience. Additionally, considering factors such as donor behavior, demographics, and giving preferences can help tailor fundraising strategies to better meet the needs and expectations of supporters.

Overall, this experiment highlights the importance of testing and evaluating different approaches to fundraising to ensure that the intended outcomes are achieved and to optimize revenue generation for the organization.

experiments.title: How shortening a survey affects email conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that reducing the number of survey questions to the top 10 most compelling issues will cause an increase in survey completion rate because a shorter, more focused survey reduces respondent fatigue and keeps engagement high by highlighting only the most resonant and emotionally charged topics.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 07/30/2025
The Heritage Foundation was promoting a survey centered on President Trump’s agenda. The existing version of the survey featured 15 questions. While this provided comprehensive insight, it also posed a potential barrier to conversion due to its length. To optimize for better engagement and increased email conversion rates, we hypothesized that a shorter, more focused survey could outperform the original by reducing participant fatigue and enhancing relevance. This led to the formulation of a controlled A/B test where the only variation was the length and content of the survey.
This experiment was executed through a 50/50 split test across all incoming paid media traffic. For one full month, half of the audience received the original 15-question survey (control), while the other half was shown a trimmed version containing just the top 10 most compelling questions (treatment).
The idea for this test stemmed from a strategic insight: longer surveys often experience higher drop-off rates. We believed that by prioritizing the most emotionally resonant and broadly relevant issues, we could not only streamline the experience but also deepen engagement. The decision to cut five questions was guided by three criteria:
Redundancy – Questions that overlapped thematically with stronger, more comprehensive ones.
Narrowness – Topics that were too specific or niche to drive widespread action.
Relevance – Issues that, while important, were not seen as high-priority by the core Trump-supporting audience.
Questions removed included topics such as electric vehicle mandates, women’s sports, campus radicalism, entitlements, and military modernization—all of which, though valid, were deemed less galvanizing compared to issues like immigration, inflation, and election integrity.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that reducing the number of survey questions to the top 10 most compelling issues did not lead to an increase in survey completion rates. In fact, the treatment group actually saw a decrease in Emails Acquired for All Traffic compared to the control group. Additionally, the statistical level of confidence was only 82.5%, which is below the accepted threshold of 85% for validity.

In future experimentation, it is important to carefully consider the impact of survey length on respondent engagement. While the hypothesis that a shorter, more focused survey would lead to higher completion rates seemed logical, the results of this experiment suggest otherwise. It may be beneficial to conduct further research to understand why the treatment group performed lower than the control group and to explore other potential factors that may influence survey completion rates.

When designing future experiments, it is essential to ensure that the hypotheses are based on solid reasoning and supported by data. It is also important to consider the potential impact of changes on the desired outcome and to set clear criteria for assessing the validity of the results. Additionally, incorporating feedback from participants and analyzing user behavior can provide valuable insights for optimizing future surveys and experiments.

experiments.title: How asking for a one-time or monthly gift in the email and leading with a monthly tab on the landing page effects monthly donations during Giving Tuesday
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that asking for a monthly or one-time donation and leading with a monthly tab on the landing page for members via an email solicitation during Giving Tuesday will achieve an increase in monthly gifts with no decrease in overall transactions.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 12/05/2024
During Giving Tuesday the Membership team designed an email and landing page test to see whether an ask for a one-time donation or monthly donation vs. an ask for a “best gift” made any difference on transactions or monthly gifts. The treatment which featured the one-time or monthly ask also led to landing page with the monthly tab first.
Here are the results breakdown:
Members: The experiment showed no statistical difference in donor acquisition between asking for a one-time or monthly gift vs. asking for their best gift. There was a 145% lift in monthly transactions with 90% LOC (5 vs. 10 gifts). There was a directional decrease in clicks on the link in the email for the treatment with a 5% decrease with 63% LOC.
Nonmembers: The test showed the treatment caused a statistically valid decrease in donor acquisition of -59% with 97% LOC. There was no added benefit in monthly acquisition, but the treatment did validate a 5% decrease in email clicks with 99.4% LOC.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that asking for a monthly or one-time donation and leading with a monthly tab on the landing page for members via an email solicitation during Giving Tuesday resulted in a significant increase in monthly gifts with no decrease in overall transactions.

Although the results showed a 145.6% increase in donations for all traffic, the statistical level of confidence was below the standard threshold of 95%. This indicates that while the treatment showed promise, further testing is necessary to confirm its effectiveness.

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue testing this approach with a larger sample size to increase the statistical power and achieve a higher level of confidence in the results. Additionally, it could be useful to explore different variations of the treatment to optimize the ask for monthly donations and potentially further increase donation rates.

Overall, the experiment highlights the potential benefits of tailoring donation asks to members based on their giving preferences and the importance of ongoing testing and optimization to drive success in fundraising efforts.

experiments.title: How making the problem relatable to the donor affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that making the problem more relatable to the donor for survey takers will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because it creates an emotional connection, helping donors see how their contribution directly impacts real people and situations they care about, making them more likely to feel personally responsible and motivated to take action.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/05/2024 - 10/05/2024
The Heritage Foundation is running a survey on voter fraud through paid media and email prospecting efforts. They wanted to try and increase in the instant conversion rate on the donation page. To do so, we decided to test making the problem of election fraud more relatable to the donor by adding a paragraph that says “imagine casting your vote only to find out the fraudulent activities affected the impact of your vote.”
This experiment ran for 1 month to 50% of the traffic to the page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a paragraph to make the issue of election fraud more relatable to donors did not lead to an increase in the donor conversion rate. In fact, the treatment group saw a decrease in transactions compared to the control group. However, the statistical level of confidence was below 85%, indicating that these results cannot be considered valid.

In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the sample size is sufficient to provide reliable results with a higher level of statistical confidence. Additionally, it would be beneficial to test different messaging strategies to see what resonates most with donors and leads to a higher conversion rate. It is also important to consider other factors that may have influenced the results, such as the timing of the experiment or external events that could have impacted donor behavior.

experiments.title: How including the benefits a person will receive to a landing page’s call-to-action affects conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe including the benefits a person receives to the call to action on an eBook landing page will increase the conversion rate because people will understand the value they receive.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/02/2024 - 08/06/2024
The Heritage Foundation was running an eBook offer using Facebook ads and prospecting emails to send traffic to a landing page where people could request their free copy of their 2024 Index of U.S. Military Strength eBook. In an attempt to increase email conversion rate on this page, they previously tested adding a curiosity-creating headline, as well as a preview of the content available in the eBook. Both of these treatments, however, resulted in a decrease in email acquisition rate.
As a follow up, they wanted to test if adding the benefits a person receives in the page’s call-to-action (CTA) would lead to an increase in email conversion rate.
The control’s CTA read: Get instant access to your FREE copy of the 2024 Index of U.S. Military of Strength by filling out the form below!
The treatment’s CTA read: Get instant access to your FREE copy of the 2024 Index of U.S. Military of Strength to discover the state of the U.S. military and what it means for your future.
The control and treatment each received 50% of traffic, and the experiment ran for one month.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that adding the benefits a person receives in the call-to-action on an eBook landing page actually led to a decrease in email conversion rate. The treatment group, which included the benefits in the CTA, saw a 10.4% decrease in Email Acquired for All Traffic compared to the control group. The statistical level of confidence was 87.3%, indicating that the results are not yet fully valid but are approaching validity.

Moving forward, it is important to acknowledge that in this specific case, highlighting the benefits did not have the intended effect of increasing conversion rates. This information should be used to guide future experimentation by considering alternative strategies for optimizing the landing page, such as testing different messaging, design elements, or incentives to encourage email sign-ups. It is also crucial to continue testing and iterating on different approaches to find the most effective way to drive conversions for the eBook offer.

experiments.title: How adding a social proof popup to a membership renewal page affected donation rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a social proof popup for donors on a membership renewal page will achieve an increase in donation rate because people will be motivated by the fear of missing out.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/23/2024 - 06/04/2024
In an effort to increase donation conversion rate on their membership renewal donation page, The Heritage Foundation wanted to test whether adding a popup that showed who had recently made a donation would lead to this increase in donations due to a fear of missing out (FOMO) on what actions others are taking.
They launched an A/B test where the control was the donation page without any popup, and the treatment had a small unintrusive popup in the corner of the screen that read:
“(Donor’s Name) from (State) just renewed their Heritage Foundation membership”
The treatment FOMO popup, which showed a new donor every 15 seconds, also had an American flag icon to align with the same flag icon in the monthly tab of the donation form and appeal to the donor’s patriotism.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This A/B test ran for 13 days. The key learnings from this experiment are:
1. The addition of a social proof popup on the membership renewal donation page did not lead to an increase in donation rate. In fact, it resulted in a decrease of 43.5% in transactions for all traffic.
2. The statistical level of confidence was 84.1%, which is below the commonly accepted threshold of 85%. This means that the results are not considered valid and should not be used for future decision making, but are indicative that further testing is needed to solidify the learning.
Reasons for this decrease may include potential donors preferring total privacy, not wanting their personal information (name and state) shared once they made a donation. Visitors to the treatment could have also been too distracted by the popup's animation to read and fully take in the donation page's copy.
In future, we should test this social proof popup on another donation page related to a content offer or a non-membership direct appeal to test whether they lead to the same results.

experiments.title: How adding a flag icon to a donation form gift array button affects donation rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a small American flag in a donation form’s gift array and submit button to build affinity for direct appeal prospects will achieve an increase in conversion rate because the flag image will help align their motivation with the intended action.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/30/2024 - 05/30/2024
The Heritage Foundation had previously run a test on their membership renewal page where they added a small flag image to a tabbed donation form’s monthly gift tab in order to appeal to the patriotism of their donors, which led to an increase in donations.
The Heritage team wondered whether this flag image would appeal to potential donors on another direct appeal donation page. They believed testing this image in the donation form’s $100 gift array button and in the form submit button would lead to an increase in donations, due to both the image’s appeal to the donors’ patriotism and the suggestion of $100 as the “preselected” array option.
They launched an A/B test where the control had no flag icon anywhere on the form, and the treatment had the flag icon only in the $100 gift array button.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that adding a small American flag image to a donation form's gift array button may not always lead to an increase in conversion rate as expected. In this case, the treatment actually resulted in a decrease in Transactions for All Traffic by 42.6% with an 88.3% statistical level of confidence, indicating that the control performed better.
This result may indicate that the flag image was a distraction from the task at hand for this direct appeal. The image on the gift array button specifically may have confused or dissuaded some potential donors who planned on giving an amount other than the preselected amount.
In future experimentation, it would be important to further test the impact of adding the flag image in different elements of the donation form, such as the monthly tab as well as the submit button, or even testing different patriotic symbols or messaging to see if that resonates better with potential donors.

experiments.title: How adding clarity to a donation form’s tab titles affects donation rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding clarity in the titles of the tabbed form for ebook downloaders will increase donor conversion rate and recurring donor conversion rate because users will be able to better understand what we’re asking them to do.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/15/2024 - 05/13/2024
On The Heritage Foundation’s donation page for their Guide to the Constitution eBook offer, they wanted to test whether changing the donation form tab’s titles with language that added clarity and donor autonomy would increase both the one-time donor conversion rate and recurring conversion rate.
The control donation form’s tab titles were: “Single Gift” and “Monthly Gift”
For the treatment donation form’s tab titles, we clarified that the donor could “Make a One-Time Gift” or “Make a Monthly Gift”
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment show that changing the tab titles on a donation form to add clarity and donor autonomy led to a 32.6% increase in overall donor conversion rate at a 91.2% level of confidence. While the statistical level of confidence was below the standard threshold of 95%, the results were approaching validity. The treatment version, which used the language "Make a One-Time Gift" and "Make a Monthly Gift", was rolled out to all traffic.

In future experimentation, it is essential to continue testing variations of tab titles and messaging on donation forms to further evaluate their impact on donor conversion rates. It would be beneficial to explore additional iterations of language that provide clear guidance and empower donors to make informed decisions. Additionally, considering other elements of the donation form, such as design, adding icons that resonate with the donor's motivation, and call-to-action buttons, in conjunction with the tab titles, could provide a more comprehensive understanding of how to optimize the donation process for increased conversions.

experiments.title: How highlighting that only a small amount of people donate after downloading an ebook affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that highlighting that only a small minority people donate for ebook downloaders will achieve an increase in conversion rate because it will appeal to the ideal donor, who are attracted to the idea of being in a small minority of people who were willing to do the “right thing” rather than the “popular thing”. because it will appeal to the ideal donor, who are attracted to the idea of being in a small minority of people who were willing to do the “right thing” rather than the “popular thing”.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/05/2024 - 03/04/2024
The Heritage Foundation is offering a free ebook called The Founder’s Almanac.
They had previously tested the idea of highlighting the fact that “less than 1%” of people who take the survey are willing to take the next step and donate.” This was tested on a survey offer, and it produced a 105% lift in conversion rate with a 98% level of confidence.
This language had increased conversion because it appealed to the ideal donor, who was attracted to the idea of being in a small minority of people who were willing to do the “right thing” rather than the “popular thing”.
They wanted to retest this concept on an ebook funnel to see if they would get a similar lift in conversion rate.
They inserted this language in two places, and split the traffic to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are:

1. Highlighting the fact that only a small minority of people donate resulted in a significant increase in Transactions for All Traffic, with a 64.0% lift and a statistical level of confidence of 92.8%. This indicates that the language in the treatment version resonated with the ideal donor who is motivated by being part of a select group willing to do the "right thing".

2. The treatment also saw a substantial increase in Transactions - One-Time, experiencing a 770% lift with a 96% level of confidence. This suggests that the messaging specifically appealed to individuals looking to make a one-time contribution.

This is an application of social identity theory. This theory suggests that people tend to categorize themselves and others into various social groups. Supporting the underdog can be related to group identity and solidarity with a particular group.

Based on these learnings, it is recommended to continue using the language that highlights the small minority of people who donate in future experimentation. This messaging appears to resonate with the target audience and create a sense of exclusivity and righteousness in supporting the cause. However, it would also be beneficial to continuously test and optimize this messaging to ensure its continued effectiveness and relevance.

experiments.title: How reminding lapsed members about the value of the membership card in a fundraising email affects the click-through rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Including reminder text about the value of a membership card will increase the click-through rate because it is an additional opportunity to explain the value members receive.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/27/2024
We conducted this email experiment for The Heritage Foundation’s Members Card campaign. The email asked lapsed members to give, in return, they would receive a Members Card. The email also announces a matching gift opportunity. The conclusion of the email includes a call to action to donate.
We wanted to know if lapsed members would click more if they were reminded about the value they receive when they donate. The treatment included additional text towards the beginning of the email which explained the value proposition.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that including reminder text about the value of a membership card can significantly increase the click-through rate for lapsed members. The treatment in this experiment saw a 41.9% increase in Clicks for All Traffic, indicating that reminding members of the benefits they receive can be a compelling reason for them to donate again.

Note: While the treatment email had a higher click-through rate, it also had a slightly lower conversion rate.

In future experimentation, we should continue to test and refine the messaging around the value proposition of a membership card. This could include highlighting specific benefits, such as exclusive content, special discounts, or access to events. By effectively communicating the value that members receive in exchange for their donation, we can increase the likelihood of them re-engaging with the organization.

Additionally, it would be beneficial to further test different variations of the value proposition messaging to optimize the impact on member reactivation. By continuing to experiment and analyze the results, we can uncover the most effective ways to encourage lapsed members to donate and renew their support for The Heritage Foundation.

experiments.title: How a high contrast donate button in the main navigation of a website affects conversion and revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a high contrast donate button to the main navigation for heritage.org visitors will achieve a lift in transactions and revenue because it helps website visitors visual interpret the webpages hiearchy of purpose and breaks down their decision process to decide to give a gift.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/05/2024 - 03/07/2024
After successfully testing the addition of a red donate button in the main navigation on the homepage of heritage.org we decided to run the test side wide to Heritage.org visitors in a 50/50 split. The test excluded the homepage from results. This experiment validated several times and ran for over a month, but for logistical reasons it was kept running. While there was lift among all website visitor types, (Email, Social, Search, Returning, and NEw Visitors, etc), the largest lift came from organic traffic to the home page, which created a 55% lift in transactions.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are as follows:

1. Adding a high contrast donate button to the main navigation for heritage.org visitors resulted in a significant increase in revenue for all traffic, specifically a 24.9% increase. This indicates that the addition of the donate button had a positive impact on user engagement and conversion rates.

2. The treatment also led to a 55% lift in transactions from organic traffic to the home page, showing that the donate button was particularly effective in capturing the attention of visitors coming through this channel.

3. Although the results showed a high level of statistical confidence (85.2%), further testing is recommended to ensure the validity of the findings. The statistical confidence level of 95% was not reached in this experiment.

4. It is important to note that the treatment resulted in a 4% increase in bounce rate. While this may be a concern, it could also indicate that the donate button led to more engaged visitors who explored the website further before exiting.

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to conduct additional tests to validate the impact of the donate button on revenue and transaction rates. It may also be worth exploring different variations of the donate button, such as varying the placement or color, to determine the most effective design for encouraging donations.

Overall, the results of this experiment suggest that the addition of a prominent donate button to the main navigation can potentially improve user engagement and increase revenue for Heritage Foundation.

experiments.title: How a direct appeal in a dear reader affects conversion and revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a direct donation appeal on Dear Reader Ads for daily signal readers will achieve a decrease in traffic and revenue to the content offer page and subsequent donation page because traffic to Daily Signal is motivated more for information and research and less towards making an impact through giving.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/16/2024 - 02/22/2024
We tested a Founders’ Day donation request vs. an eBook Guide to the Constitution offer. Previous testing had shown that the Guide offer outperformed a direct ask during a special campaign, so we wanted to lay to rest whether we should run content offers or direct solicitations on TDS. We utilized “less than 1%” language that had been boosting results in other acquisition funnels. The campaign quickly validated in favor of the control 73% more clicks and 60 more transactions. We will use these results to inform future testing on TDS with Dear Reader Ads and no longer include direct appeals in campaign assets.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment show that adding a direct donation appeal on Dear Reader Ads for daily signal readers resulted in a significant decrease in transactions for all traffic, indicating that the control (eBook Guide to the Constitution offer) outperformed the treatment (direct donation appeal). The statistical level of confidence was at 88.4%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 95% for validity, but it is approaching validity.

In future experimentation, it would be advisable to continue testing the impact of content offers versus direct donation appeals on Dear Reader Ads. Since the initial results indicate that the content offer performed better in this case, it might be beneficial to focus on providing valuable content to Daily Signal readers rather than making direct donation appeals. Further testing could help to confirm these findings and provide insights for optimizing donation strategies on the platform.

experiments.title: How adding a donate button to the header and navigation menu affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a donate button to the header and navigation menu for home page visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because more people will click to view the donation page. because more people will click to view the donation page.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/15/2023 - 12/11/2023
The Daily Signal is The Heritage Foundation’s multimedia news platform. On their website, the only call-to-action to donate and support the Daily Signal’s work is located at the very bottom in the footer of each page and doesn’t stand out.
In an attempt to increase the amount of traffic to the donation page from the home page, we wanted to test if adding a more prominently displayed donate button to the top of the home page led to an increase in donor conversion rate.
The donate button appeared in both the home page’s header and dropdown navigation menu (in both the desktop and mobile experiences) and was blue in color in order to stand out from the surrounding buttons. 50% of home page traffic saw the control and 50% saw the treatment.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment ran for 27 days, in which time the donate button variant saw a 42.8% increase in donations for all traffic compared to the control. However, it is important to note that the statistical level of confidence for this increase is 53.2%, meaning that the results cannot be considered valid or reliable for future decision making.

In terms of revenue, the treatment saw a decrease of 0.11% in average gift, but at a 6.52% level of confidence these results cannot be relied upon to predict a valid outcome.

Reasons for this may include the donate button appearing less hierarchically prominent than the email "Sign Up" button above it, or home page visitors not yet having experienced the value in the Daily Signal's articles before seeing a donate button.

Based on the results, it is clear that additional testing is needed to understand what motivates The Daily Signal visitors to donate to the organization. This could include adding value proposition copy next to the button, or testing different locations on the home page. It may also be worth testing an unobtrusive sticky donate button that follows the visitor through their journey sitewide.

experiments.title: How a tabbed donation form affects donor conversion rates on an acquisition donation page
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a tabbed donation form for acquisition funnel donation page visitors will achieve an increase in overall donor conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/17/2024 - 02/01/2024
As a follow up to previous experiments, we wanted to see if using a tabbed donation form for visitors to an acquisition donation page would improve donor conversion rate and recurring donor acquisition. In previous experiments, we found no difference in one-time giving, but had seen 99% lift in recurring giving. However this was tested on the main donation page where motivation to give is very different than giving through an acquisition funnel.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are as follows:

1. The treatment, which used a tabbed donation form, resulted in a 29.5% increase in transactions compared to the control group.

2. While the statistical level of confidence (90.3%) is below the generally accepted threshold of 95%, the results are approaching validity. This means that there is a strong indication that the treatment is effective, but further testing is needed to confirm its impact.

3. The previous experiment on the main donation page showed a 99% lift in recurring giving with a tabbed donation form. However, the current experiment on the acquisition donation page did not see a significant difference in recurring giving. This suggests that the motivation to give may differ between the two situations.

Based on these learnings, it is recommended to continue testing the tabbed donation form on acquisition donation pages to further validate its effectiveness. Consider running additional experiments with a larger sample size and longer duration to improve statistical confidence.

Additionally, it may be valuable to explore the impact of different language and messaging on the donation form. Testing variations in messaging can help determine the most effective language that resonates with potential donors and increases conversion rates.

Overall, the key learnings from this experiment suggest that a tabbed donation form has the potential to improve donor conversion rates on acquisition donation pages.

experiments.title: How a tabbed donation form affects donor conversion rates on an acquisition donation page
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a tabbed donation form for acquisition funnel donation page visitors will achieve an increase in overall donor conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/17/2024 - 02/01/2024

experiments.title: How making a statement call to action with lapsed members in a fundraising email affects the click-through rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that making the call to action a statement instead of a question for lapsed members will achieve a higher click-through rate because the desired action is cleaner.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 02/22/2024
This email experiment was conducted for The Heritage Foundation’s Founder’s Day campaign.  The email was a personal communication informing lapsed members about the challenges America faces today and how The Heritage Foundation is solving those issues. At the end of the email, they ask lapsed members to “reactivate” their membership.
We wanted to know if using a statement call to action would encourage lapsed members to click instead of a call to action that asked a question.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that making the call to action a statement instead of a question for lapsed donors resulted in a 28.0% increase in the click-through rate. While this result is promising, the statistical level of confidence is below 85%, which means the results cannot be considered valid.
Note: While the click-through rate increased the conversion rate decreased by 50%. The sample size was not large enough to attain the required level of confidence of 95% or above.
In the future, it is essential to conduct further experiments with a larger sample size to obtain more reliable results. Additionally, it is crucial to continue testing different variations of the call to action to determine the most effective approach for encouraging lapsed donors to re-engage. By using what was learned from this experiment, we can refine our strategies and optimize our communication to better connect with our audience and achieve our goals.

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a tabbed donation form for acquisition funnel donation page visitors will achieve an increase in overall donor conversion rates.

experiments.title: How removing an image in an MMS message affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that removing an image in an MMS message for lapsed donors will achieve an increase in conversion rate because it will appear to be more personal and will look less like marketing.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/21/2024
The Heritage Foundation was running SMS and MMS ads as part of their kickoff campaign. They had leveraged texting because of the personal nature of the communication, two-way conversation ability, and high deliverability, and wanted to test a P2P MMS platform However, they wondered if their personal message was undermined by the image they sent alongside the message, since that’s not something that people usually send along with 1:1 communications.
They split their list in half and sent half of the list an MMS without an image to see if it increased conversion rate. This ended up converting the message to an SMS message, which should be noted.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that removing an image in an MMS message for lapsed donors resulted in a significant increase in donations. However, the statistical level of confidence was below 85%, which means that these results cannot be considered valid.

Based on this experiment, it can be concluded that personalization and a less marketing-like approach can have a positive impact on the conversion rate. This suggests that future experimentation should focus on creating more personalized and less promotional messages for lapsed donors.

To ensure more valid results, future experiments should aim for a higher statistical level of confidence. It might be beneficial to increase the sample size or consider other factors that could influence the conversion rate, such as the timing of the messages or the wording of the texts.

Overall, the experiment highlights the potential effectiveness of personalization in communication with lapsed donors, and this insight should be taken into account when planning future campaigns and experiments.

experiments.title: How removing an image in an MMS message affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that removing an image in an MMS message for lapsed donors will achieve an increase in conversion rate because it will appear to be more personal and will look less like marketing. because it will appear to be more personal and will look less like marketing.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/21/2024
The Heritage Foundation was running SMS and MMS ads as part of their kickoff campaign. They had leveraged texting because of the personal nature of the communication, two-way conversation ability, and high deliverability, and wanted to test a P2P MMS platform However, they wondered if their personal message was undermined by the image they sent alongside the message, since that’s not something that people usually send along with 1:1 communications.
They split their list in half and sent half of the list an MMS without an image to see if it increased conversion rate. This ended up converting the message to an SMS message, which should be noted.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that removing an image in an MMS message for lapsed donors resulted in a significant increase in donations. The treatment group saw an 83.0% increase in donations as compared to the control group.

However, it is important to note that the statistical level of confidence is only 84.0%, which is below the desired threshold of 95%. Therefore, these results cannot be considered valid and should not be used for future decision making.

In future experimentation, it is recommended to continue exploring the impact of removing images in MMS messages for lapsed donors. However, it is crucial to ensure that the sample size is sufficient and that the statistical level of confidence reaches the desired threshold. This will help to ensure the validity of the results and allow for more confident decision making. Additionally, it would be beneficial to further investigate the reasons behind the increase in donations when images are removed, as this may provide insights for future campaigns and messaging strategies.

experiments.title: How leading a membership ask email with the problem for lapsed members affects click-through rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that leading an email with the problem will increase click-through rate because it will give the reader more context as to why they should renew their membership with Heritage.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/31/2024
The Heritage Foundation is running thier annual Kickoff Campaign to acquire members. You can become a member by giving a gift of any size, one-time or recurring. This experiment was ran on the first email of the campaign for lapsed members to Heritage asking them to reactivate their membership. For this test, we wanted to see how lapsed members would react to leading the email with a question that poses the problem Heritage is trying to solve rather than leading the email with a direct ask to reactivate their membership. In doing so, we believe users will be more likely to click through to the donation page and reactivate their membership because the leading question allows them to relate to the issue and want to be apart of the change.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that leading an email with the problem did not increase click-through rates for lapsed members to reactivate their membership with The Heritage Foundation. The treatment actually resulted in a 19.3% decrease in clicks compared to the control group. However, the statistical level of confidence was below the accepted threshold of 85%, meaning the results are not valid and should not be used for future decision making. Additionally, the treatment group saw a 46.9% decrease in donor conversion with an 85.3% level of confidence.

Based on these findings, it is important to reconsider the hypothesis and approach for future experimentation. It may be necessary to use a different strategy to engage lapsed members and entice them to reactivate their membership. This could include focusing on the benefits and value of being a member, highlighting success stories or achievements of the organization, or providing exclusive content or offers. Additionally, it is crucial to ensure that future experiments have a sufficient statistical level of confidence to ensure the validity of the results.

experiments.title: How does calling out the loss of exclusive membership benefits for lapsed members affect conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that calling out what they will lose by not reactivating their membership for lapsed members will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because the fear of losing something can be a more powerful motivator than the prospect of gaining something of equal value.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/31/2024
The Heritage Foundation is running thier annual Kickoff Campaign to acquire members. You can become a member by giving a gift of any size, one-time or recurring. This experiment was sent to lapsed members to Heritage asking them to reactivate their membership. For this test, we wanted to see how lapsed members would react to calling out what they will lose by not reactivating their membership in 2024.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that calling out what lapsed members will lose by not reactivating their membership can be a powerful motivator for increasing donor conversion rates. The treatment in which the loss aversion approach was used saw a 105.3% increase in donations compared to the control with an 89% level of confidence.

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to refine the messaging and presentation of the loss aversion approach to optimize its impact. Testing different variations, such as emphasizing specific benefits or highlighting the potential negative consequences of not reactivating, could help identify the most compelling approach.

Additionally, it would be valuable to analyze the characteristics and preferences of lapsed members who responded positively to the loss aversion strategy. Understanding their motivations and demographics can inform targeted marketing efforts to similar segments in the future.

Overall, this experiment suggests that leveraging loss aversion can be a successful strategy to reactivate lapsed members. Continued testing and refinement will provide more conclusive evidence and insights for future campaigns.

experiments.title: How using a personalized and gratitude-centered tone for existing members affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: The treatment email, which employs a personalized and gratitude-centered approach, will result in a higher membership renewal rate compared to the control email because the treatment might foster a stronger sense of loyalty and connection.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/31/2024
The Heritage Foundation is running thier annual Kickoff Campaign to acquire members. You can become a member by giving a gift of any size, one-time or recurring. This experiment was ran for existing members in 2023 to Heritage asking them to renew their membership. For this test, we wanted to see how existing members would react to a more personalized and gratitude-centered tone in the email.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that the treatment email, which employed a more personalized and gratitude-centered approach, did not result in a higher membership renewal rate compared to the control email. In fact, the treatment email had a 13.0% decrease in donations for all traffic.

While the results did not reach a 95% level of confidence, they are approaching validity, indicating that there might be some truth to the findings. Future experimentation should be conducted to further validate these results.

Additionally, the treatment group saw a 11.6% decrease in click-through rate in the email with an 87.9% level of confidence.

Based on this experiment, it is clear that a personalized and gratitude-centered approach may not be effective in encouraging existing members to renew their membership.

When conducting future experiments, it is important to keep in mind the potential impact of a more personalized and gratitude-centered approach on donor behavior. This could be further explored by testing different variations of personalized content or by targeting specific segments of the existing member base.

Overall, this experiment provides valuable insights into the impact of a personalized and gratitude-centered approach on membership renewal rates, and the learnings should be used to inform future experimentation and optimization of renewal campaigns.

experiments.title: How a radical redesign impacted monthly giving for membership renewals
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a radical redesign with customized value proposition for returning members looking to renew will achieve an increase in donor conversion because the enhanced appeal and exclusivity focus on what the user wants to do rather than the organization’s internal goals. because  the enhanced appeal and exclusivity focus on what the user wants to do rather than the organization’s internal goals.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/09/2023 - 07/02/2023
For visitors looking to renew their membership from the homepage, we wondered if the existing donation page language for renewals was too focused on the organization. We developed a radical redesign language with customized value proposition for existing members who are already familiar with the organization and had shown historical motivation to make a gift. We hypothesized that increasing the value prop elements of appeal and exclusivity would frame a significantly more attractive way of describing what the user desires. Even if the internal goal is to get a user to renew their membership, the user wants to help protect our nation’s founding principles and keep their membership active. We wanted to communicate this clearly on the page to see if it would increase conversion. We updated the language to recognize that we were talking to visitors who had previously supported the organization and had arrived on this page to make an additional gift to support the organization. We also tested the presentation of the monthly ask, changing it from a checkbox after the gift amount had already been selected to a tabbed form with “Join the Leaders Club” as the text for monthly gifts.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running the experiment for two months, the radical redesign treatment of the donation page with a customized value proposition for returning members looking to renew resulted in a 97% increase in Leaders Club Member conversion with 92% level of confidence. Although the statistical level of confidence is below 95%, the results are directionally approaching validity. This suggests that the enhanced appeal and exclusivity focused on what the user wants to do rather than the organization's internal goals successfully attracted more donors. The treatment version also removed an additional step that asked the visitor to select which area their donation should go towards, so this reduced friction could have also played a role in the increased conversion.
We also saw flat results for one-time donations on this page, so there is opportunity to isolate the value proposition copy and do further testing to increase one-time gifts as well as monthly. We believe future iterations could include additional clarity around the benefits of being a Leaders Club member in addition to the tabbed form design and title to help more visitors choose this option.
Overall, the key learning is to focus on the user's desires and motivations, enhancing the value proposition in alignment with what we already know about the intended audience, in order to increase donor conversion rates.

experiments.title: How removing a donation page’s header image on mobile affected donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that removing a donation page’s background header image for mobile viewers will achieve an increase in instant donation conversion rate because they will be less distracted by imagery and likely to read more of the value proposition copy.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/05/2023 - 05/18/2023
In an effort to increase instant donation conversion rates on a policy organization’s voter fraud survey offer, we created a donation page treatment that removed the background header image on the mobile experience.
We believed that by removing this header image and lessening the amount of negative space above the fold, the viewer would read more of the page’s content and be more likely to donate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are as follows:
Removing the background header image on the mobile donation page did not have the intended effect of increasing the instant donation conversion rate. In fact, the treatment version saw a 54.1% decrease in donations compared to the control version.The treatment version also resulted in a 73.2% decrease in average gift compared to the control version.
The experiment only ran for two weeks, but the decrease in donations with an 86.3% level of confidence and decrease in average gift prompted us to stop the experiment before results could fully validate.
Reasons for the decrease in donations and average gift could include:
The lack of the header image may have led to visitors thinking there was too much copy to read.The header image of the organization's building may contribute to a feeling of legitimacy that helped prospective donors make the decision to contribute.

experiments.title: How does a tabbed donation form affect recurring conversion rates on a primary donation page?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a tabbed donation form for donation page visitors will achieve higher recurring donation rates by making this giving option and its impact more visible.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/10/2023 - 02/10/2023
We wanted to know how a tabbed donation form (featuring single and monthly gifts) would affect recurring donations on the primary donation page for a public policy organization.
In this experiment, the control presented the option to make a recurring gift by featuring a checkbox below the gift array that asked the visitor if they would like to make their gift on a recurring basis. Beneath the checkbox, an impact statement communicated the impact of making a recurring gift.
The treatment instead presented the option to make a recurring gift in a tabbed form format, with “single gift” and “monthly gift” shown side-by-side above the gift array and the impact statement shown above the tabs.
For this experiment, ~50% of traffic was shown the control and ~50% of traffic was shown the treatment.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running this experiment for one month, we observed that the tabbed donation form produced an 11.0% decrease in recurring gifts with a 25% level of confidence, a 25.4% decrease in overall donations with a 99.2% level of confidence, and a 32.1% decrease in traffic with a 94.4% level of confidence.
These decreases were most severe among returning visitors where total donations decreased by 29.5% with a 99.7% level of confidence and revenue decreased by 40.6% with a 97.6% level of confidence indicating.
For new visitors, we observed a large increase in revenue (177%) -- but because this increase was a result of just one additional donor, it is apparent that this is being skewed by a large gift and not indicative of overall performance.
These results disprove our hypothesis and indicate that in the context of visiting a primary donation page (high donor motivation), a tabbed donation form with the impact statement above the gift array may have distracted the donor or created friction by presenting multiple options before they made the decision to give.
It also suggests that it is more persuasive to ask someone to "upgrade" their donation after they have already made a micro-commitment by selecting their donation amount from the gift array.
Based on these results, we will look to test a monthly giving checkbox against the tabbed donation forms currently running on this organization's instant donation pages tied to acquisition offers.

experiments.title: How does paginating a multi-section survey affect email acquisition rates?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that paginating a multi-section survey for potential donors will achieve higher email acquisition rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/27/2022 - 07/10/2022
In an effort to improve email acquisition rates on a multi-section survey offer, we wanted to test whether or not breaking a long 19-question survey into more manageable, less-intimidating chunks would decrease the perceived “cost” of completing the survey, in terms of time, and decrease perceived friction enough to improve sign-up rates.
We ran this experiment for 28 days, splitting traffic 50/50 between the control and the treatment.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After 28 days, we observed a 31.5% lift in email acquisition rates, with a 97% level of confidence, validating our hypothesis that by reducing the perceived cost of completing the survey, in terms of time, visitors would experience less friction and would be more motivated to complete a multi-section, 19-question survey.

The results of this experiment suggest that presenting all 19 survey questions on one long page may have made completing the survey a long and laborious task, increasing the perceived cost (time) of taking the survey to a point where it outweighed the perceived value in the minds of prospects.

By breaking the survey into more manageable chunks, with each section on its own page, the perceived cost of completing the survey was reduced in the minds of prospects while the persuasion principle of 'behavioral consistency' likely helped to motivate prospects to complete the survey.

For mobile users, the experiment produced a 100.3% lift in acquisition rates, with a 100% level of confidence, likely due to the decreased scroll and potential "gamification" presented by the paginated version.

Based on these results, we recommend that organizations with longer survey offers consider paginating such surveys to reduce the perceived cost and leverage the behavioral principle of 'behavioral consistency' to lift acquisition rates.

experiments.title: How adding a tabbed donation form impacted recurring gifts
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a tabbed donation form for visitors to /renew and the main donation page will achieve an increase in recurring donations.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/22/2020 - 02/28/2020
As a follow up to a previous experiment, we wanted to see if using a tabbed recurring donation form for visitors to the two primary donation forms would improve recurring donor acquisition. A previous experiment found a significant books in recurring gifts but many of these ended up being mistakes that had to be refunded. We wanted to see if we could have the same impact if we started the form on one-time gift instead of recurring.
experiments.keyLearnings: After running for a full month, we discovered there was no statistically significant impact to recurring gifts. While we did see a directional lift, it would require a significantly higher volume of gifts to validate. We decided to hold off on rolling out this treatment until it could be fully validated with a high urgency campaign.

experiments.title: How email copy length affected click-through-rate on an appeal
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that shortening copy for housefile emails will achieve improved click-through-rates.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 04/05/2021
For a membership renewal campaign, we wanted to look at ways to optimize the email copy for The Heritage Foundation appeals. The test is intended to determine whether shorter or lengthier email copy results in higher click-through-rates. The version with the higher click-through-rate sends more users to the landing page linked in the email.
The control email consists of seven short paragraphs while the treatment email consists of five short paragraphs. The sender and subject line are identical for each email, and the “Renew” and “Sign up to renew” links are included in each email.
experiments.keyLearnings: Although the experiment resulted in a 7.2% increase in clicks, the level of confidence was only 80%. The treatment (shorter copy) did ultimately generate more clicks than the control (lengthier copy). To further increase the level of confidence, it may be wise to conduct another test in which the shorter copy is paired with a personalized salutation.

experiments.title: How framing the message around the donor’s impact affects conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: A landing page with donor-centric language will have a higher conversion rate than one that focuses on the accomplishments as a result of Heritage.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/12/2014 - 12/17/2014
The standard messaging points employed by Heritage have been refined over many years across multiple channels. At the start of the year end campaign, we decided to test how the information is presented to the donor. The control uses standard messaging while the new treatment would take the same information but frame it around how the accomplishments were the result of the donor, not the organization.
experiments.keyLearnings: The messaging points of Heritage have not changed but we have learned that we need to frame them around the donor instead of around the organization.

experiments.title: How increasing the size of the text on an offer landing page did not impact the email registration rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will improved readability increase the email registration rate for the Religious Freedom offer?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/12/2019 - 04/15/2019
When The Heritage Foundation launched a new offer, we were not achieving the email registration rate that we would normally see. So, in an attempt to boost the email registration rate, we launched a simple experiment to boost the size of the text across both desktop and mobile versions to see if readability was prohibiting us from achieving the results we wanted.
experiments.keyLearnings:
With an 80.9% level of confidence, we found that the large text actually decreased the email registration rate by -8.3%.
We will now turn our testing towards the message itself, having eliminated something structural with the page as a variable that is limiting our effectiveness.

experiments.title: How asking active donors to be one of a few donors to give a specific amount impacted revenue during a matching gift campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: How does communicating a specific quantity of donors we’re asking to make a gift relevant to the active donor impact donor conversion or revenue?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 08/04/2020
As a part of The Heritage Foundation’s matching gift campaign (called “The Board Challenge”), we wanted to experiment with the ask amount and approach. Typically, the organization has used “make your best gift today” kind of language within their control copy.
In an attempt to set a smaller, more reasonable target, we wanted to experiment with driving donors to be “one of just X donors we need today to make a gift of Y.”
The idea behind this is to (a) set a relevant ask amount for the donors, but also (b) allow them to see that they can help achieve a “small quantity” goal at that amount, as well.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Although we did not validate an increase, we did see a directional increase of revenue by +12.6% (LoC: 83.9%).
Further, the donor conversion rate was relatively flat between the two segments (only showing a +2% increase, with a level of confidence of 29.9%). Additionally, we observed that 20.7% of the people in the treatment segment decided to give more than the amount that was asked for, while another 36% gave the exact amount that was asked of them.
Conclusion: Perhaps further experimentation with the ask amounts is advisable for active donors, but this directionally showed a lift in revenue.

experiments.title: How emphasizing a recurring gift impacted revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: Will emphasizing the impact of a recurring increase the overall revenue per visitor?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/03/2017 - 06/06/2017
A past analysis of The Heritage Foundation donor file found that recurring donors provide the highest donor value to the organization and have the best chance of being retained into future years. Based upon this analysis, we decided to launch an experiment to increase the number of recurring givers.
We crafted value proposition copy around the reason to make a recurring gift and included near the recurring gift option on the donation form. We also emphasized making the gift via their Bank Account as these donations tend to have a longer lifetime.
experiments.keyLearnings: After a month of collecting data, we did not see any significant impact on the donor’s likelihood to make a recurring gift, select their Bank Account, or increase overall revenue to the organization. This is still a priority for the organization so we will go back to the drawing board to develop new hypotheses to increase someone’s likelihood to make a recurring gift.

experiments.title: How replacing older donation page copy impacted donor conversion rate for a new offer
experiments.researchQuestion: Would reusing the donation page copy previously used to send a series of appeals work behind a new content offer for a related subject?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/01/2019 - 12/13/2019
When launching a new content offer related to the impeachment of the president and how the process works, we decided to launch with to donation page treatments–one that had been used as the donation page for a short series of emails when the news was released, and another that had not yet been tested. This approach was used to mitigate risk with new donation page related to the topic, and since the housefile emails along with the control donation page had produced some nice results, we assumed we would be able to capitalize upon this page’s positioning of the fundraising appeal for new content downloaders.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment page (which was previously unseen by the housefile) showed an aggregate increase in donor conversion rate of 25.1%, but did not validate (LoC: 85.5%) because we turned it off early.
Why? Because we actually saw no difference between the treatments for new visitors, but saw a decline in donor conversion rate of 34% (LoC: 92%) for returning visitors to the control page.
Our hypothesis is that donors that had previously seen the control page through the earlier series of appeals felt like they had already given a gift in response to that ask. For this reason, they decided not to give another gift to the page, but for new visitors–the treatment performed slightly better (whereas the control message had no difference).
For that reason, we decided to roll out the treatment copy as the new control, as it showed no negatives for any audience type, which will remove the risks associated with returning visitors feeling like they have already given to “this initiative,” and presenting them with a new ask instead.

experiments.title: How simplifying the donation page increased the donor conversion rate on an acquisition offer
experiments.researchQuestion: Would simplifying the messaging on the donation page increase donor conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/11/2019 - 06/25/2019
The Heritage Foundation was running an acquisition campaign to provide prospective donors with a free guide to the religious freedom debate. While scaling up spending to increase top line results, we needed to experiment with a way to increase the donor conversion rate.
The messaging on the control donation page seemed to take too long to get to the point. With an audience that is likely eager to get to their inbox to download their free guide, we felt that we needed to simplify the donation page and get right to the point more quickly.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Although this experiment only achieved an 86.6% level of confidence, the results were directional enough for us to agree to roll out the 31.8% increase in donor conversion rate that we had achieved over the last 14-days.
When it comes to an instant donation page behind an acquisition offer, it’s critically important to keep the donation page messaging as simple and straight forward as possible.
This will mitigate friction and increase the likelihood that an instant donation will be collected.

experiments.title: How a more emotional message did not make a difference in acquiring new emails
experiments.researchQuestion: Would focusing on what you get from the book increase the email registration rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/28/2019 - 05/28/2019
When launching the new Heritage Foundation eBook offer, we wanted to test a message that was more focused on communicating to the end-user, instead of focusing on the positioning of the contents of the eBook itself to see which treatment would generate more email addresses.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running the experiment for a month, we did not see a noticeable difference only achieving a -2.5% decrease in email registration rate, with an 86.2% level of confidence.
