It would appear that at least with this offer, it will not matter if we position the contents of the eBook itself or what you will get from the content as a reader as it pertains to the email registration rate.

experiments.title: How a Daily Signal core fundraising message impacted donor conversion rate for the Dear Reader ads
experiments.researchQuestion: Does a Daily Signal support call to action acquire more donors (or generate more revenue) than the existing message to activate a membership?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/25/2019 - 03/31/2019
In an attempt to move away from the existing control message (focusing upon “partnering with President Trump” by “activating your Heritage membership”), we wanted to experiment with a more TDS-focused message about why The Daily Signal exists, and how we can only continue with the support of readers.
experiments.keyLearnings:
With 84.6 level of confidence, we substantiated a -29.5% decrease in donor conversion rate. We also saw a -14.8% decrease in revenue (70.2% level of confidence) alongside these results.
We did, however, see a +20% increase in “Dear Reader” ad clicks (with a 95% level of confidence).
We decided to stop this treatment due to the compounding impact of decreased revenue and donor conversion rates.
When digging into the data, we found some interesting pieces of information as it pertains to the type of visitor (new visitor vs. returning visitor).
Here are some observations for new visitors and comparative impact for the treatments within this experiment:
The control was 138% better at converting new visitors.
The control generated a 119% higher revenue/visitor value from new visitors.
The treatment was 36% more effective at converting returning visitors (topping a 31% donor conversion rate).
The treatment generated a 22% higher average gift from returning visitors.
The treatment generated a 67% higher revenue/visitor value from returning visitors.
It would appear that the control message is most effective at converting first time visitors, while the treatment message was most effective at converting returning visitors.
Our recommendation would be to look to roll both treatments out based upon the number of times a visitor has arrived at a page. Specifically, if the the reader has already converted through a “Dear Reader” ad in the past, it might be advisable to immediately swap the ads they see to a message more similar to the treatment.

experiments.title: How Facebook ads impacted a campaign’s non-member conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: Will adding a 3rd channel of touches increase donor conversion rate for activating non-members for the first time?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 02/12/2019
During The Heritage Foundation’s Kickoff Campaign, we wanted to run Facebook ads designed to remind members to activate their membership to see if it would boost response rates by adding a 3rd channel (digital ads) along side regularly scheduled email and direct mail messages.
experiments.keyLearnings:
With a 99.9% level of confidence, the non-member segment that received Facebook ads in the 3rd channel of communication saw an online response rate increase of 20.3%.
Other metrics impacted are as follows:
Average gift: +13.1% increaseTotal Revenue: +36.09% increase
During the campaign, the total cost to run these ads to non-members generated a 2.03 ROI against the increase in revenue generated from the "with ads" segment. Simply put, 100% of the ad expense to increase responses from this audience was covered and generated an equal amount to the budget spent in additional revenue free and clear of any expense.
Final Conclusion:Where possible, adding a 3rd channel of communication to heed the campaign's call to action for non-members should be considered.

experiments.title: How a long-form, donor-centered appeal decreased donor conversion rates on the primary donate page
experiments.researchQuestion: Does a donor centered message help improve donor conversion rate (or revenue) on the primary donation form for Heritage?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/08/2019 - 03/13/2019
We wanted to test a donor-centered message on the primary donation page, as every treatment historically has been focused on an organizationally centered series of value propositions.
experiments.keyLearnings:
With a 94.7% level of confidence, we validated that the treatment decreased the donor conversion rate by -37.8%.
Although the donor conversion rate decreased in the treatment segment, because the average gift in the treatment jumped pretty substantially (+61.3%) compared to the control, the revenue difference between the segments was only -0.6% (with a 50.86% LoC).
Our hypothesis is that the copy length led to the decrease in donor conversion rate, but we were encouraged by the increase in average gift from the treatment, so we will now develop and launch a short-form version of a donor-centered message to experiment against the short-form control message.

experiments.title: How recurring gift language impacted donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will including value proposition language specific to The Leaders Club on the main donation form increase Leaders Club Acquisition? And how will it impact overall donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/29/2018 - 06/05/2018
The Heritage Foundation was looking for ways to increase donor acquisition for their recurring giving club, The Leaders Club. As part of a series of experiments, we tested out a few different hypotheses to impact this metric. The first hypothesis we had was that simply explaining the recurring gift option and the potential benefits would increase the perceived value of the offer enough to increase acquisition for the club. We decided to test this hypothesis on the main donation form.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running for 2 months, we did not reach the desired level of confidence to definitively identify how this experiment impacted both the likelihood to make a gift and the likelihood to make a recurring gift. However, the impact on the likelihood to make a gift was approaching significance (93% LoC) with a 13% decrease by having the Leaders Club language on the page. The impact on the chances to make a recurring gift was a long way from statistical significance (65% LoC).
Given the likelihood that the additional value proposition was hurting conversion and that there was no definitive proof it was helping recurring gift acquisition, we decided to end the experiment and look for alternative options to increase recurring gifts.

experiments.title: How increasing the visibility of the donation button impacts giving
experiments.researchQuestion: Will making the donation button easier to find increase the overall number of gifts received?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/05/2017 - 07/21/2017
This test, after running for nearly two months, we did not reach a 95% level of statistical validity. While the big red button seemed to increase giving, there was no significant change to the number of people that were clicking on the button. With this being the case, it does not seem reasonable to say that the bigger button motivated more people to give.
experiments.keyLearnings: Based upon donor feedback, The Heritage Foundation wanted to find a way to make the donation button on the Heritage.org website easier to find. In doing this, we wanted to measure what kind of impact it would have on donor giving.

experiments.title: How a radical redesign impacted the Membership campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: Which treatment will result in the greatest lift to donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/31/2017 - 06/30/2017
Neither of the treatments reached statistical validity. After further analysis, a big takeaway from this experiment can be found in the experiment setup itself. With a radical redesign, the goal is to test a variety of significant factors in order to achieve a bigger lift. However, when we do that, we are unable to identify the specific element that caused the lift.
With this test, we tested multiple factors but they were relatively minor to the conversion process. By testing the headline, background, and a button, there was no cohesive hypothesis that could be isolated and learned from in the future.
For the future, we need to focus on isolating individual factors on a page or making significant modifications to the elements of a landing page that we know influence conversion (copy, donation form, etc).
experiments.keyLearnings: During the Heritage membership campaign, we wanted to test out a radical version of the existing Join Heritage donation page. The new version utilized the size of the Heritage member base as a way to increase credibility of the headline. It also removed the majority of the design elements to try to make the content better flow together. A third version was prepared that also added a “Donate” button to the top that would allow people to jump straight to the form.

experiments.title: How targeting an appeal to high “D” DISC profiles impact response rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will targeting a high “D” style email and landing page at the high “D” constituents increase overall response rate
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/20/2017 - 07/28/2017
For the first email in the 2017 Heritage Board Challenge, we wanted to use the DISC profiles that we were able to append to subscriber and donor records. We created the first email and landing page for the campaign without worrying about the DISC profile of the writing. Once we had the approved copy, we then rewrote the same appeal in the style of the segment that the copy did not address. In this case, it was a high “D”.
We then split the constituents based upon their primary DISC profile and their donor status. We also made sure the split was evenly divided along highest previous giving levels so that one would not be “better” donors than the other.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The high “D” style email and landing page decreased the overall response rate across all profiles by 21% at a 87% level of confidence. When we focused in on just the high “D” segment, we saw a more pronounced decrease. While it did not reach a statistical level of confidence, it would indicate that this particular appeal was not able to better speak to high “D” profiles.
While this test was not successful, it is just the first step in validating the approach. There could be many reasons why the appeal did not work (such as selecting the wrong aspect of the appeal, i.e. the Board match). We will want to continue to validate this approach to reach a high level of confidence on its impact.
As a note, the control was an “Sc” email and an “Ic” landing page. The treatment was a “Ds” email and a “Di” landing page.

experiments.title: How addressing High-I subscriber in their style of communication impacts conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will speaking to subscribers in their preferred DISC style increase donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/28/2017 - 08/11/2017
During The Heritage Foundation’s 2017 Board Match campaign, we decided to test the style of communication to see if we could better align the messaging style with the subscribers’ known DISC scores. Using the data that we appended from the CrystalKnows website, we segmented the subscriber list by both donor status and primary disc style. We then took the Control version of the email and landing page (written in the I style) and recreated it in the style of a high C.
The goal of this experiment was to see if we could increase the giving of a high C subscriber. However, this write up is based upon the trends we observed when analyzing the high I subscribers of this same test.
experiments.keyLearnings: This experiment had no significant impact on the high “C” subscriber base which was the target of the experiment. However, after reviewing the data, we discovered that the high “C” landing page and email adversely impacted the giving of the high “I” subscriber base. While we did not hit the 95% level of confidence we strive for, it was quickly approaching statistical validity.

experiments.title: How 3rd-party evidential quotes impact conversion on the primary donation page
experiments.researchQuestion: Will 3rd-party evidential quotes improve donor conversion on the primary donation page?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/15/2017 - 01/03/2018
In experiments that were run several years back, we found that added 3rd-party quotes from well-known figures would boost conversion rate in fundraising campaigns. However, over the years, this is something that had fallen off of the primary donation page. With Heritage making the news headlines and receiving accolades from significant figures, we decided to re-test this strategy.
experiments.keyLearnings: After running for nearly 2 months, we did not see any statistically significant difference between the two treatments. As a result, we have shut off the experiment and continue forward with the control version.

experiments.title: How the specifics of a plan influenced conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will adding specifics about a strategic plan increase likelihood to convert?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/25/2017 - 11/25/2017
During Heritage’s Annual Member Survey, after a subscriber had filled out their survey, we presented them with a donation page. On this page, when making the solicitation, we were talking about the national budget blueprint that Heritage had created. At the end of the page, the call-to-action was to get the plan in as many lawmaker’s hands as possible.
However, we wondered if adding more specifics about the plan would help sell the importance of it. We created at treatment that did just that.
experiments.keyLearnings: This experiment did not reach a statistical level of validity to make any claims about whether the increased details helped or hurt the results. It is something that would need to be tested more in depth at a later date.

experiments.title: How a concise value proposition affects conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a simplified renewal page improve conversion over a campaign specific page?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/20/2016 - 01/29/2016
For the Heritage 2016 membership campaign, we created a campaign-specific landing page that laid out the various reasons that a donor should renew their membership in 2016. We identified not only the benefits to the donor but also the specific challenges that Heritage was going to be addressing in the coming year.
We discovered, from earlier testing in this campaign, that the benefits did not truly impact the conversion rate on the page for existing donors. With this in mind, we developed a hypothesis that the existing members may not actually need the entire value proposition we had laid out on the page given their knowledge of the organization. If this hypothesis was true, the added copy would actually be acting as a point of friction instead of adding additional value.  To validate this hypothesis, we tested the campaign-specific page against the standard renewal page that had been refined over time.
experiments.keyLearnings:
We found that the more concise renewal page was able to increase the donor conversion by 5.9% without negatively impacting the average gift. The existing donors already had enough of an understanding of Heritage’s unique value proposition that the longer page produced more friction than value in the donors’ minds.
As a side note, we were careful to monitored the average gift of both treatments in this experiment. The clarity of the value proposition can directly influence the generosity of the donor. Since we were removing much of the copy, we wanted to make sure it didn’t have any negative impact on that aspect of the giving experience.

experiments.title: How membership benefits affects conversion for new members
experiments.researchQuestion: How does membership benefits impact the conversion rate on the potential new member landing page?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/08/2016 - 01/20/2016
As part of their January membership campaign, The Heritage Foundation wanted to determine what factors were the biggest motivators for new members. Every member receives a handful of benefits such as newsletters, a membership card, and important updates. We wanted to find out if, by describing these benefits on the page, we could increase the perceived value of the membership.  We decided to create a treatment that identified and explained each of these benefits in detail to potential new members. Once finished, this treatment ended up doubling the length of the page with the added copy.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The membership benefits increased the conversion rate on the page by 20.2%. We had concerns that the added copy would increase the friction on the page more than it would increase the perceived value of the membership. This experiment proved this was not the case.
Interestingly, we performed this same experiment for existing members and it showed no statistical impact when we added the benefits. In that situation, the existing members didn’t need to be reminded about the benefits they received since the got them on a regular basis.

experiments.title: How cognitive tension and the incentive of “free instant access” affects conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will the promise of “instant access” increase conversion when tested against a call to action of “get the facts”?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/10/2015 - 11/24/2015
The Heritage Foundation’s team of research scholars assembles an Index of Military Strength each year to assess the readiness of America’s military and the strength of existing global threats. The team at Heritage promoted this on Facebook to acquire new email addresses. Their existing landing page made the offer to “get the facts”, which had previously been a compelling incentive. They developed a treatment that leveraged proven keywords “get instant access”.
This was coupled with a new headline. Instead of aligning the offer to “get the facts”, the treatment headline asked a question: “Is the US Military ready to defend our country?” This was designed to draw the reader into the copy and introduce tension that could be answered by activating the offer.
The only variables tested on this treatment were the headline and call-to-action.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment increased conversion by more than 20%. This tells us two things:
1. The use of cognitive tension can be a useful element to increase conversion from prospects and visitors.
2. The concept of “instant free access” can increase incentive to convert, if it is combined with an offer that is desirable to our prospects and visitors.

experiments.title: How the right message affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Which messaging approach will produce the greatest number of donors?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/03/2015 - 08/14/2015
As part of a capital campaign to raise funds for a new building, The Heritage Foundation wanted to be sure they were utilizing the most powerful value proposition available to them. Historically, fundraising messages had always been about what Heritage does each day to research and promote conservative values in Washington. This building campaign offered an opportunity to try a different tactic that might be even more appealing to donors.
We created a treatment that promoted the benefits of the new building. This approach allowed us to talk about more tangible aspects such as proximity to the Capital and having a larger foot print.  We then launched the campaign and split traffic between the two options.
experiments.keyLearnings:
By talking about the specific benefits of the building, we were able to increase donor conversion by 21.2%. This is what is known as product-level value proposition. The control talks about The Heritage Foundation at a high level but this specific appeal had tangible elements that were appealing to the donors. The already understand the primary value proposition of Heritage so the treatment just reinforced this specific need.
By taking this approach, we were able to validate the messaging approach before the final days of the campaign. This allowed us to find the ideal message before the bulk of the gifts would come in.

experiments.title: How design and copy placement affects conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Which design will drive the most donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/23/2015 - 06/01/2015
As part of an appeal for their Reclaim America campaign, The Heritage Foundation wanted to implement a landing page that used the same look and feel as the campaign’s microsite.  This landing page was heavier on imagery than is traditionally used in their appeals so we wanted to ensure the design would be as effective as the standard design style.
We created an experiment using a design from a previous campaign (with minor variations) as the control and the new microsite design as the treatment.  The copy and quotes used were identical between the two pages.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The new microsite design created a 14% increase to donor conversion on the page. After reflecting upon the page further, this may be attributable to both the page design and the placement of the quotes from well-known public figures.  In the control, the quotes are placed in a sidebar while in the treatment, they are in-line with the copy.
For this specific campaign, we rolled out the new design for the remainder of the campaign.  We will want to run future experiments with the placement of 3rd party quotes to validate their potential impact.

experiments.title: How live chat on a donation page affects revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: How does live chat affect donation conversion rate and average gift amount?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/05/2015 - 02/04/2015
The Heritage Foundation tested a Live Chat feature on their donation page as a way to boost engagement with the potential donors. The audience was split between existing members and nonmembers in order to monitor how it affected each audience.  The results below are for nonmembers only.
experiments.keyLearnings: The segment targeted at existing donors showed no significant difference between the segment of visitors seeing live chat and those not seeing it.  For new donors, the live chat dropped the conversion rate slightly but was able to increase the average gift by 21.6%.  While it is not statistically significant, this test directionally indicates that live chat can increase the average gift.  Further testing would be necessary to validate this hypothesis.

experiments.title: How a soft call-to-action in the email affects response rate
experiments.researchQuestion: A softer call to action will drive more traffic which will, in turn, garner more gifts.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/18/2014 - 12/19/2014
In the second email of Heritage’s year end campaign, they wanted to find our which messaging stance appeals most to donors and drives the most donations.  They tried three variations: a direct ask, soft call-to-action around the organization, and a soft call-to-action focused on the donor.
experiments.keyLearnings: The emails with softer calls-to-action were able to increase the number of clicks more than 91%, however, the traffic was not as motivated to give when compared to those who were asked to make a gift within the email.   As a result, the email making a direct ask was able to convert 50% more donors than the other treatments.

experiments.title: How increasing the clarity of the value proposition affects conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Which of the three value propositions, backed by evidentials, will increase both average gift and conversion rate the greatest amount?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/30/2014 - 07/20/2014
Despite many experiments, the control version of the main Heritage Foundation donation form had consistently maintained the highest conversion rate. In an effort to influence both conversion rate and average gift, we developed and tested multiple value propositions and backed them with evidentials.
experiments.keyLearnings: The research aspect of Heritage’s efforts had the biggest impact on conversion rate. The main donation form will use this language moving forward and this aspect of Heritage’s mission will be emphasized in future campaigns when communicating with donors.

experiments.title: How Using Heritage Member Survey Insights to Shape Year-End Messaging Affects Donor Conversion Rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using Heritage member survey insights to shape more urgent, action-oriented year-end messaging in the sitewide sticky bar will increase donor conversion rates because it reflects supporters’ strongest motivations—fear of wasting this moment and a desire to lock in permanent wins through accountability—making the appeal feel more relevant, concrete, and compelling at the point of decision.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/22/2025 - 01/05/2026
This experiment took place during The Heritage Foundation’s calendar year-end fundraising campaign, when urgency, traffic volume, and donor intent were at their highest. It coincided with the release of new qualitative research from Heritage’s own audience, which showed deep anxiety about conservatives “blowing it” by failing to lock in recent political gains. Year-end provided a natural deadline, but the research made clear that what truly motivated this audience was not just time pressure—it was fear of losing momentum and allowing courts, bureaucrats, and future elections to reverse progress.
The sticky bar was tested sitewide on Heritage.org, ensuring persistent visibility across the full donor journey rather than limiting the message to a single landing page.
We wanted to run this experiment because the control message leaned heavily on a standard year-end frame—“give before midnight on December 31”—without fully addressing the deeper emotional drivers revealed in the research. Survey respondents repeatedly expressed fear that conservative leadership would waste a once-in-a-generation window and fail to codify wins. The treatment was designed to test whether explicitly framing a gift as a way to lock in victories, dismantle the deep state, and hold policymakers accountable would better align with those anxieties.
Finally, the sticky bar format itself made this a low-risk, high-impact test. It allowed us to introduce sharper, more militant language drawn directly from donor sentiment without rebuilding core pages mid-campaign. In essence, this experiment tested whether shifting from abstract urgency to permanence, accountability, and action would increase engagement during the most critical fundraising moment of the year.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The main takeaway from this experiment is that there was no meaningful difference between the control and treatment in overall transactions or sticky bar–driven clicks. Conversion rate remained flat at 2.4% across both variants, and while the treatment showed slight directional movement in sticky bar clicks, the difference was not statistically meaningful and did not translate into increased giving. Due to the small number of sticky bar–initiated conversions and low confidence levels, the results are not considered valid for drawing performance conclusions.
Moving forward, this test suggests that changing sticky bar messaging alone is unlikely to materially impact donor behavior during year-end, when most conversions are driven by higher-intent paths like email and direct visits. We will use this learning to deprioritize sticky bars as a primary lever for conversion optimization and instead apply survey-informed messaging insights to higher-impact surfaces—such as homepage heroes, landing pages, and email—where message framing has a greater opportunity to influence donor decisions and reach statistical validity.

experiments.title: How Using Heritage Member Survey Insights to Reframe Homepage Hero Messaging Affects Donor Conversion Rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that reframing the homepage hero message using Heritage member survey insights to emphasize permanence, preventing reversal, and turning wins into law will increase donor conversion rates because it aligns the first on-site message donors see with their strongest motivations—fear of lost momentum and a desire for durable, enforceable outcomes—making the case for giving feel more concrete and urgent.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/22/2025 - 01/05/2026
This experiment was ran during The Heritage Foundation’s calendar year-end fundraising campaign, when homepage traffic and donor intent were elevated across all channels. At the same time, Heritage had just gathered extensive qualitative feedback from its members revealing deep anxiety about recent conservative wins being reversed by courts, bureaucrats, and future elections. Year-end urgency was already present, but the research suggested donors were especially motivated by the idea of permanence—turning temporary victories into lasting law before political momentum fades.
The test was conducted in the homepage hero block on Heritage.org, specifically within the primary text block layered over the homepage hero image. This placement ensured maximum visibility, as the homepage serves as the front door for email, direct, and organic traffic during year-end. Because this block is often the first message visitors see, it plays a critical role in framing both Heritage’s mission and the purpose of giving during high-traffic moments.
The control homepage message emphasized broad values (“Stand for Conservative Values”) and a generic year-end match framing, which aligned with traditional nonprofit appeals but did not reflect the sharper emotional drivers uncovered in the member survey. Survey respondents expressed frustration with symbolic wins and fear that the Left would undo progress unless reforms were codified quickly and aggressively.
This experiment was designed to test whether shifting from abstract values to concrete outcomes—explicitly naming permanence, lawmaking, and dismantling the deep state—would make the homepage appeal feel more urgent, specific, and aligned with donor expectations. The treatment directly translated member language and priorities into the hero message, positioning a year-end gift as a tool to prevent reversal and secure long-term change.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The main takeaway from this experiment is that changing the homepage hero messaging did not meaningfully affect overall conversion rates or revenue in a statistically valid way. Conversion rate held steady at 2.4% across control and treatment, and while the treatment showed a lower CTA click rate, none of these differences reached statistical significance. Although the treatment produced a higher average gift and higher revenue per visitor directionally, the experiment did not reach the required sample size or confidence threshold, so these results cannot be treated as conclusive.
Moving forward, this test reinforces that homepage hero copy changes alone are unlikely to drive measurable performance shifts during high-traffic year-end periods, when donor behavior is heavily influenced by prior intent from email and direct channels. We will use this learning to treat homepage hero messaging as a brand- and framing-level lever rather than a primary conversion lever, and continue applying survey-informed language in contexts where it can be paired with clearer calls to action, tighter message-to-donation-page alignment, and tests that can reach validity more efficiently.

experiments.title: How Elevating Matching Gift Messaging on the Donation Page Affects Donor Conversion Rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believed that highlighting the matching gift higher on the donation page would increase donor conversion rates because it would immediately reinforce urgency and perceived impact at the moment of decision, encouraging donors to complete their gift while the match was available.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/31/2025 - 01/05/2026
This experiment ran during The Heritage Foundation’s year-end fundraising campaign, when matching gifts were a core component of the overall appeal strategy. At year-end, donors expect urgency and leverage, and the presence of a large match was already baked into campaign messaging. The question was not whether to use the match, but how prominently it should appear once a donor reached the donation page.
At this point in the funnel, visitors had already demonstrated strong intent. The experiment tested whether elevating the match higher on the page—immediately after the headline—would increase conversions by reinforcing urgency and perceived impact, or whether it would be redundant for donors already primed to give.
The test was conducted on the donation page, specifically in the sub-headline area directly beneath the main headline. All donation page visitors were randomly assigned to either the control experience (no sub-headline; match mentioned later in the page copy) or the treatment experience, where the match was surfaced immediately at the top of the page.
Previous tests suggested that donors arriving at the donation page already have high intent, raising an important question: does additional urgency messaging at this stage help—or does it create friction? Because matches are one of the strongest traditional fundraising levers, we wanted to test whether bringing the match forward earlier would reinforce commitment and improve conversion, or whether donors would respond better to a calmer, more streamlined donation experience.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment did not produce a statistically valid result, but the directional outcome suggests that bringing the match higher on the donation page did not improve performance. The treatment showed a lower conversion rate than the control (-8.9% relative difference), while revenue per visitor was essentially flat. Although average gift size was slightly higher in the treatment, this did not offset the decline in conversions.
These results suggest that for high-intent donors, surfacing the match earlier may be unnecessary—or even distracting—once they have already decided to give.
Moving forward, we will avoid prioritizing prominent match messaging at the very top of the donation page and instead continue placing match details further down the page, where they can reinforce value without interrupting the donation flow. This test supports a broader pattern across year-end experiments: at the point of conversion, clarity and simplicity outperform added urgency.
Match messaging remains a powerful lever earlier in the funnel—emails, landing pages, homepage messaging—but on the donation page itself, the focus should be on reducing friction and making it easy for motivated donors to complete their gift.

experiments.title: How adding clarity of the gift type to the submit button affects transactions (Guide to the Constitution)
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding clarity for the gift type on the submit button for donors will achieve an increase in transactions because the donor more clearly understands the commitment of the gift.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/20/2025 - 09/04/2025
This experiment aimed to increase completed transactions on the Guide to the Constitution donation form by adding clarity about the selected gift type (one-time vs. monthly) to the submit button. The existing version of the form used a generic “Make My Gift” button, which provided no clear indication of the commitment donors were making when completing their gift. We hypothesized that this lack of clarity could create hesitation or uncertainty at the final step of the giving process.
To address this, we created an A/B test with two versions of the submit button. The control version used the existing generic button, while the treatment version explicitly included the gift type within the button text, such as “Make My Monthly Gift” or “Make My One-Time Gift” depending on the donor’s selection. This approach was designed to reinforce what the donor had chosen earlier in the process and reduce any last-minute confusion before completing the transaction.
The test ran for a little over two weeks, with traffic randomly and evenly allocated between the two page versions to ensure comparable audiences and sessions.The primary metric for the test was completed transactions, with secondary metrics including transactions monthly, one-time and overall conversion rate from page views to completed gifts. By clarifying the commitment on the button itself, we expected to see an increase in donor confidence and a corresponding lift in transactions.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key findings of this experiment are the treatment that added gift type clarity to the submit button resulted in a -28.4% decrease in transactions compared to the control, with a confidence level of only 48%. This is well below the typical threshold for statistical significance, indicating that the observed difference is likely due to random variation rather than a true effect. Rather than improving clarity and increasing conversions, the added text on the button may have created friction or uncertainty for donors at the final step.

These results suggest that clarifying gift type at the point of submission is not an effective strategy for improving conversions and may even discourage donors from completing their gift. Future efforts to improve clarity should focus on earlier points in the giving flow—such as during gift selection or form completion—rather than on the submit button itself.

experiments.title: How including a button in an email appeal affects click-through rate for recurring donors
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a button to an email for recurring donors will achieve an increase in click-through rate because it provides a clear, visually distinct call-to-action that reduces friction, draws the reader’s eye, and makes it easier for people to take the intended next step.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 08/26/2025
The Heritage Foundation is running its annual Leader’s Club campaign. The second email of the campaign offered a free ebook called “Flags of Our Fathers.” Within this email, they wanted to test how changing the raw URL to a button would affect the click-through rate to the landing page. This experiment ran on one email, and this segment included donors to The Heritage Foundation.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a button to an email for recurring donors did not lead to an increase in click-through rate as hypothesized. In fact, the treatment group saw a 13.6% decrease in clicks compared to the control group. The statistical analysis showed a 63.4% level of confidence, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 95% for results to be considered valid.

In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the sample size is large enough to provide reliable results and that any conclusions drawn are statistically significant. Additionally, it is crucial to carefully consider the design and implementation of changes such as adding a button, as they may not always have the desired effect. It is also recommended to conduct further testing and analysis to understand the reasons behind the unexpected outcome and to inform future decision-making. Experimentation is a valuable tool for learning and refining strategies, and it is important to approach it with a curious and open mindset to adapt and improve based on the findings.

experiments.title: How changing the gift array for non-members on a campaign donation page affects revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that increasing the gift array for non-members will achieve an increase in revenue.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 08/05/2025
The Heritage Foundation ran their annual President’s Match campaign in July. In three of their emails to non-members, they wanted to test increasing the one-time gift amounts on the donation page to $50, $100, $250 to see if revenue would increase as well.
They changed the gift array for 50% of the traffic.
***The monthly tab’s gift array was also adjusted from $15, $25, $35 to $14, $24, $42, but we do not have the data to split apart recurring from one-time gifts, so this write up includes all transactions.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that increasing the gift array for non-members from $25, $50, $100 to $ $50, $100, $250 resulted in a 1.4% increase in revenue for all traffic. However, the statistical level of confidence is only 51.6%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 95% for results to be considered valid.

Additionally, we saw a 16.8% decrease in transactions on the treatment donation page with a 64.8% level of confidence.

Therefore, while the treatment version did show a slight increase in revenue, the lack of statistical significance means that these results should not be used for future decision-making. It's important to conduct further experiments with larger sample sizes or different variations to validate the impact of changing the gift array on donation revenue.

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue testing different gift amounts and messaging to see if there are ways to further optimize donation revenue. Additionally, ensuring that experiments have a sufficient sample size and statistical significance is crucial in order to draw reliable conclusions and make data-driven decisions.

experiments.title: How including a button in an appeal email affects donor conversion rates for non-members
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a visually prominent, action-oriented button that says “Double My Gift” will increase donor conversion rate because it draws more attention than a plain-text hyperlink, creates a clearer and more compelling call to action, and reduces friction—especially for mobile users—by making the next step more obvious and easier to complete.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 07/31/2025
The Heritage Foundation conducted this experiment as part of their President’s Challenge matching gift campaign. This campaign was designed to drive donor engagement by offering a limited-time opportunity to double the impact of a donor’s gift through a matching challenge. The email in question was the first send in the campaign sequence, making it a high-leverage moment for influencing donor behavior and maximizing initial response rates.
Given the high stakes of the campaign’s early momentum and the importance of clearly communicating the match opportunity, the Heritage Foundation sought to test whether different call-to-action (CTA) formats would affect click-through and conversion rates.
The test was deployed via email, targeting the Heritage Foundation’s non-member list.
Both versions of the email—control and treatment—were linked to the same donation landing page but differed in their CTA presentation.
The experiment was driven by a hypothesis rooted in behavioral and conversion rate optimization principles: a visually prominent, action-oriented button might outperform a plain-text hyperlink in driving user action.
Key motivations included:
Improving clarity and visibility of the CTA: While the raw URL in the control version is technically functional, it lacks the immediate visual weight and action language of a button. The team suspected that this might cause the CTA to be overlooked or feel less urgent.
Reducing cognitive friction: A clear, concise button like “Double My Gift” directly mirrors the donor’s intent and may simplify decision-making.
Encouraging mobile-friendly design: Buttons often perform better on mobile devices where touch interaction is key, and plain-text links can be harder to tap.
This test is a good example of using micro-optimizations in email fundraising design to explore how seemingly small changes can have an outsized effect on user behavior and conversion rates.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that using a visually prominent, action-oriented button with a clear call to action like “Double My Gift” can potentially increase donor conversion rates compared to a plain-text hyperlink. While the treatment did show a 0.6% increase in donations, the statistical level of confidence was only 1.3%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 85% for significance.

In future experimentation, it is important to consider the potential impact of small design changes such as CTA formats on user behavior and conversion rates. However, it is crucial to ensure that the statistical significance of the results is high enough to draw valid conclusions. Therefore, when conducting similar tests in the future, it is recommended to continue testing different CTA formats while ensuring a larger sample size to achieve a higher level of statistical confidence before making any definitive decisions based on the results. Additionally, considering other factors such as audience segmentation, messaging variation, and testing on different platforms can further enhance the reliability and applicability of the experiment's findings.

experiments.title: How defaulting the monthly tab on an acquisition offer as apart of a high urgency campaign affects recurring conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that defaulting to the monthly tab will achieve an increase in recurring conversion rates because it reduces friction by aligning with the donor’s default behavior, subtly encourages sustained giving through visual anchoring, and reframes the donation as an ongoing commitment rather than a one-time gift.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/14/2025 - 07/19/2025
The Heritage Foundation is running their annual President’s Match Challenge campaign to members and non-members of Heritage. In this experiment, they wanted to test how defaulting to the monthly tab on the acquisition donation page of an ebook offer as apart of the campaign would affect the rate of recurring giving.
50% of traffic was randomly assigned to each variation (Control vs Treatment). The primary goal was to determine whether defaulting the donation format to monthly giving (rather than one-time giving) would influence the recurring conversion rate.
The idea to run this test stemmed from two core insights:
Behavioral Economics Research:
People tend to go with the default option presented to them. When monthly giving is presented as the default tab, it subtly frames that behavior as the norm or recommended path.
Organizational Goal: Boost Recurring Revenue:
Like many nonprofits, Heritage is invested in growing their base of recurring donors. Monthly gifts provide more predictable, long-term revenue.
By making “Make a Monthly Gift” the default selection (as seen in the treatment version), the hypothesis was that donors would be more likely to choose that option simply because it required no extra clicks or decisions to get there.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are as follows:

1. Defaulting to the monthly tab on the donation page resulted in a 0.2% increase in Transactions - Monthly for All Traffic, indicating a potential positive impact on recurring conversion rates.

2. The increase in Revenue by 134% with a 100% level of confidence suggests that while the primary metric did not meet the desired level of significance, there was a significant improvement in the total amount of revenue generated.

3. The results should be cautiously interpreted due to the statistical level of confidence being below the recommended threshold of 85%, indicating that the findings may not be 100% reliable.

4. Future experimentation should consider adjusting the duration of the experiment or increasing the sample size to ensure more robust results.

5. Despite the inconclusive nature of the primary metric, the significant increase in Revenue implies that there may be value in further exploring the impact of defaulting to the monthly tab for donation offerings.

6. It is essential to continue testing this hypothesis in future experiments to validate the findings and determine the true impact on recurring conversion rates.

In conclusion, while the experiment did not provide a definitive answer regarding the impact of defaulting to the monthly tab on recurring conversion rates, the increase in Revenue suggests that there may be potential benefits to implementing this strategy. Further testing and optimization are recommended to gain a deeper understanding of the effects and to make more informed decisions in the future.

experiments.title: How increasing the gift array on a new donor acquisition page affects conversion rate and revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a higher gift array for new members will achieve an increase in revenue with no decrease in conversion rate because the differentials in the arrays is not significant.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/14/2025 - 06/16/2025
The Heritage Foundation had been testing on their new member acquisition page for years. At some point, the page had been altered to have a lower gift array ($25, $50, $100) than on other pages. They knew that their data showed that donors who started with a gift of $50 traditionally had higher retention rates. They wanted to test a higher gift array, with the caveat that they might reduce conversion rates overall as a tradeoff.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that implementing a higher gift array for new members led to a significant increase in revenue for all traffic by 331.0%. However, the statistical level of confidence was 0.0%, which means that these results cannot be considered valid.

In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the sample size is sufficient to provide accurate and reliable results. Additionally, it is crucial to conduct experiments for a longer duration to capture any potential fluctuations over time.

Based on the results of this experiment, it would be advisable to revisit the hypothesis and potentially retest the impact of a higher gift array on new member acquisition. It is essential to gather more robust data and ensure a higher level of statistical confidence before making any changes to the current strategy. Continuous testing and learning from experiments is crucial for making data-driven decisions and optimizing performance in the long term.

experiments.title: The Big 5 Personality Traits effect on email fundraising
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that writing a fundraising email for conservatives who are low in openness and high in conscientiousness will increase donor response.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 06/13/2025
During our sustainer invite campaign we tested the use of AI in writing fundraising copy for conservatives using 2 of the 5 Big Personality Traits. Copilot was asked to “Rewrite this fundraising email as if it was going to be sent to a conservative who is low in openness and high in conscientiousness according to the big 5 personality traits. ” The prompt did not include any ask to improve the fundraising appeal, which may have been the reason for the average results. As other testing has shown that prompting AI to rewrite an email to increase the likely-hood someone would donate has increased donations.
Here is the control:
Email 8a—few days until mid-campaign deadline
Sender: Virginia Haggerty
Subject: Deadline approaching/ Tony asked that I follow up with you
Date: 5/22/25
Dear <First Name>,
Tony asked me to reach out to let you know that the mid-campaign deadline for our Leaders Club membership drive is rapidly approaching.
When you activate/upgrade your Leaders Club membership today, your increased monthly gift will help protect America’s founding principles, increase prosperity for American families, and stop the leftist establishment’s sabotage of President Trump.
The sooner you act, the greater chance we have of defeating the radical Left and ushering in America’s Golden Age.
Activate/Upgrade your Leaders Club membership before the mid-campaign deadline using the secure link below.
URL
Thank you for your prompt attention to this matter. And thank you for helping save our country.
Best,
Virginia Haggerty
Assistant Director, Membership
P.S. Don’t miss the mid-campaign deadline. Activate/Upgrade your Leaders Club membership now to advance your principles and stop the radical Left’s sabotage of President Trump: URL
Here is the treatment:
Sender: Virginia Haggerty
Subject: Deadline approaching/ Tony asked that I follow up with you
Date: 5/22/25
Dear <First Name>,
Tony asked me to reach out to inform you that the mid-campaign deadline for our Leaders Club membership drive is rapidly approaching.
By activating/upgrading your Leaders Club membership today, your increased monthly gift will help safeguard America’s founding principles, enhance prosperity for American families, and counteract the leftist establishment’s efforts against President Trump.
Your prompt action is crucial. The sooner you act, the better our chances of defeating the radical Left and ensuring America’s continued success.
Please activate/upgrade your Leaders Club membership before the mid-campaign deadline using the secure link below:
[URL]
Thank you for your immediate attention to this matter. Your support is vital in preserving our country.
Best regards,
Virginia Haggerty
Assistant Director, Membership
P.S. Don’t miss the mid-campaign deadline. Activate/Upgrade your Leaders Club membership now to uphold your principles and stop the radical Left’s sabotage of President Trump: [URL]
experiments.keyLearnings:
Key Learnings:

1. The treatment, which was written for conservatives who are low in openness and high in conscientiousness, did not perform better than the control in terms of donation response rate.

2. The treatment saw a decrease in donations for all traffic, indicating that the messaging may not have resonated as effectively with the target audience.

3. The statistical level of confidence was below 85%, indicating that the results are not considered valid for future decision making.

How to Use What Was Learned in Future Experimentation:

1. It is essential to carefully consider the characteristics and preferences of the target audience when crafting fundraising messaging.

2. Future experiments should include a prompt for AI to focus on improving the fundraising appeal, as this has shown to increase donation response in previous tests.

3. Conduct further experiments targeting conservatives who are low in openness and high in conscientiousness, using different messaging strategies to determine what resonates best with this audience segment.

4. Ensure that experiments are conducted with a sufficient sample size to generate statistically significant results, allowing for accurate conclusions to be drawn.

experiments.title: How scarcity affects donor conversion rate during digital kick off campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that emphasizing scarcity in the email will increase the donor conversion rate because framing the opportunity as rare and time-sensitive leverages the psychological principle of urgency and exclusivity, motivating recipients to act quickly to avoid missing out.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 02/19/2025
The membership team tested language that focused on scarcity. Copy used words like “rare and fleeting” and “a unique chance” to evoke a feeling that the opportunity to donate would slip pass them if they didn’t act now.
Results varied between segments. Members saw an 8% decrease in donor conversion rate with a 40-50% LOC, but prospects saw a 16% lift in conversion rate with a 78% LOC.
Putting the results together the added copy did not seem to make difference. The test ran during a mid campaign deadline, which makes testing difficult because the donor intent to give is much higher during deadlines.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that emphasizing scarcity in the email did not have a significant impact on donor conversion rates. While the treatment did see a slight increase in donations for all traffic, the statistical level of confidence was below the threshold of 85%, making the results inconclusive and not valid for future decision making.

In future experimentation, it is important to consider the context in which the test is being conducted. In this case, the mid-campaign deadline likely affected donor behavior, making it difficult to isolate the impact of the scarcity messaging. It may be beneficial to run similar tests outside of peak donation periods to get a clearer understanding of the impact of scarcity messaging on donor conversion rates.

Additionally, it is important to continue testing different messaging strategies and elements to determine what resonates best with donors. While scarcity messaging may not have had a significant impact in this particular experiment, it is still valuable to explore other tactics that may help increase donor conversion rates.

Overall, the key takeaway is that while scarcity messaging did not show a significant impact in this experiment, continued testing and experimentation are essential to optimize messaging strategies for increased donor engagement and conversion rates.

experiments.title: How using a more provocative title for the subject of a survey impacts Facebook ad clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using an more provocative name for the Administrative State for survey Facebook ad viewers will achieve an increase in clicks on the ad because the term “Deep State” invokes stronger emotions. because the term “Deep State” invokes stronger emotions.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/19/2024 - 01/08/2025
The Heritage Foundation was running a “Stop the Administrative State” survey and promoting through Facebook ads. In an attempt to increase click rates on the ads, they wanted to test whether using the more provocative term “Deep State” in place of the more straightforward “Administrative State” survey title would achieve this effect because people would be more provoked to take action against the subject when referred to as a more negative term.
The control’s Facebook ad image and copy referred to the Administrative State, while the treatment’s ad image and copy referred to the Deep State, and both received 50% of traffic. In addition, the control led to survey landing page with “Administrative State” language, and the treatment led to a survey page with “Deep State” language for continuity.
This test ran for 3 weeks.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that using the term "Deep State" in place of "Administrative State" did not lead to an increase in clicks on the Facebook ad. In fact, the treatment group saw a slight 1.1% decrease in Clicks for All Traffic compared to the control group.

However, since the statistical level of confidence is only 15.9%, these results are not considered valid and should not be used for future decision making.

In addition to click rate, the treatment saw a valid 12.8% decrease in sign up rate on the landing page at a 97% level of confidence. This suggests that the term "Deep State" may have led to fewer people completing the survey.

Finally, the treatment saw a 8.5% decrease in donor acquisition rate on the post-survey landing page compared to the control, but at a 18.6% level of confidence, this result cannot be relied upon as valid.

In future experimentation, it is essential to carefully design the study with a larger sample size or run the experiment for a longer period of time to increase the statistical power. Additionally, it may be beneficial to test different variables or messaging strategies to determine what resonates best with the audience. Conducting thorough data analysis and ensuring statistical significance will help in making informed decisions based on experimental results.

experiments.title: How adding the gift amount in a donation form’s submit button affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding the donor’s gift amount to the submit button for Heritage membership campaign donors will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because the increase in clarity on the donation amount will lead more people to donate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/17/2024 - 11/12/2024
The Heritage Foundation has a page where people can make a gift in order to become a member of Heritage. In an attempt to increase donor conversion rate on the campaign’s donation page, they wanted to test if adding the donor’s selected gift amount to the donation form’s submit button would increase clarity and lead more people to feel secure in their donation.
The control’s submit button read: “MAKE MY GIFT”
The treatment’s submit button read: “MAKE MY GIFT OF $[amount]”
The control and treatment each received 50% of traffic from on-site buttons and popups.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding the donor's gift amount to the submit button for Heritage membership campaign donors did not lead to an increase in donor conversion rate. In fact, the treatment version with the gift amount displayed saw a 0.8% decrease in transactions compared to the control version, but at a 3.92% level of confidence, these results cannot be relied upon as valid.

In future experimentation, it would be valuable to continue testing different variations of the submit button language and design to see what impact they have on donor conversion rates. Additionally, it may be beneficial to run experiments for a longer duration and with more traffic to ensure reliable and conclusive results.

experiments.title: How lowering the gift array on the instant donation page of a quiz affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that decreasing the amounts on the gift array for people who have taken a quiz will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because the lower suggested gift amounts will make giving a gift feel more attainable to a larger amount of people.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/09/2024 - 10/15/2024
The Heritage Foundation has an offer where people can take a quiz to test their knowledge on Marxism and then are led to a donation page where they can make a gift to support the organization’s efforts to fight the Marxist movement in America today. In an attempt to increase the donor conversion rate on this page, they wanted to test whether lowering the gift array amount options would lower the barrier to donating and in turn increase the amount of donors.
The control’s gift array was $50, $100, and $250.
The treatment’s gift array was $25, $50, and $100.
The experiment ran for one month and the control and treatment each received %50 of traffic.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that decreasing the gift array amounts from $50, $100, and $250 in the control group to $25, $50, and $100 in the treatment group did not lead to an increase in donor conversion rate. In fact, the treatment group saw a 1.0% decrease in transactions for all traffic compared to the control group, but at a 3.6% level of confidence, this result is invalid and should not be used for future decision-making.

In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that a statistically significant level of confidence is achieved before drawing any conclusions or making changes based on the results. This can be helped by increasing traffic to the test page. Additionally, it may be beneficial to consider other factors that could influence donor conversion rates, such as the messaging, imagery, or targeting strategies used in the donation process. Conducting further experiments with different variables and analyzing the results with a higher level of confidence will help in making informed decisions to optimize donor conversion rates.

experiments.title: How “Fighting the Negative” language on a donation page effects end of year giving
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that framing the cta from a “fight the negative” perspective for prospects and members will achieve a lift in transactions.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/10/2024 - 12/16/2024
The team decided to test “Fighting the negative” copy against the more positive “Save the American Dream” copy. The treatment copy emphasized the impact of the radical Left’s bad policies and our need to not just overcome them but to utterly destroy and dismantle their grip on America. In the past because giving intent is high during end of year, it is difficult to get a valid experiment so the decision was made after week to end the test. The test showed a directional increase in transactions for the “Fighting the negative” version that did not mention fighting the “Administrative State” in the headline.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that the "Fighting the negative" copy without mentioning fighting the "Administrative State" in the headline led to a 16.4% increase in transactions for all traffic. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 52.0%, which means that these results cannot be considered valid for future decision making.

In future experimentation, we should aim for a higher statistical level of confidence, ideally above 85%, to ensure that the results are reliable and can be used to make informed decisions. Additionally, we could consider further testing different variations of the copy to see if there is a consistent increase in transactions with the "Fighting the negative" messaging. It is also important to continue monitoring and analyzing the results of future experiments to gather more data and insights that can drive more successful outcomes.

experiments.title: How a homepage takeover affects donations during a Giving Tuesday campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a homepage takeover will increase donor conversion rate by reducing friction for motivated traffic during the Giving Tuesday campaign.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/03/2024 - 12/04/2024
For The Heritage Foundation’s Giving Tuesday campaign, they wanted to test the impact of a homepage takeover that would direct visitors to their homepage on Giving Tuesday to the campaign’s donation page with a link to go to back to their intended destination.
We believed that redirecting visitors to the donation page would reduce friction for highly motivated traffic that came to give a special gift and in turn increase donor conversions and revenue.
This experiment ran for one day. Each variant received 50% of traffic from mostly organic, direct sources to The Heritage Foundation’s home page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that implementing a homepage takeover to direct visitors to a donation page on Giving Tuesday can potentially increase donor conversions. We saw a 19.6% increase in transactions for all traffic during the one-day experiment. However, with a statistical confidence level of 56.7%, these results are inconclusive and not suitable for future decision making.

These results may indicate that site visitors may not have been highly motivated to navigate to the Heritage homepage in order to give a gift for the Giving Tuesday campaign.

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to conduct a similar homepage takeover experiment over a longer period of time to gather more data and increase the statistical confidence level. This can be accomplished by running another test over the last few days of the year during the calendar year end campaign.

experiments.title: How referencing the new presidential administration in donation page copy affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that updating instant donation page copy to reference the new presidential administration for eBook downloaders will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because visitors will have increased context on the renewed opportunity for Heritage’s policy solutions to be implemented with the new administration.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/14/2024 - 11/25/2024
The Heritage Foundation was running a campaign where visitors could sign up to receive an eBook version of their Founders Almanac book. After signing up to receive the free eBook, visitors were redirected to a donation page where they could choose to give a gift.
In an effort to increase donor conversion rate on the donation page, and in response to the changing landscape of American policy following the presidential election, they wanted to test whether updated page copy referencing the incoming Trump administration and the work that Heritage can do because of it leads to this increase.
The control page contained copy that referenced the radical Left. The treatment page took the opportunity to take on a more hopeful tone of restoring the Founding Father’s vision that is more attainable with the newly elected administration.
The test ran for one week and each variant received 50% of traffic from Facebook ads and email prospecting.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that updating instant donation page copy to reference the new presidential administration for eBook downloaders resulted in an increase in Transactions by 18.6%. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 42.5%, which means that these results cannot be considered valid and should not be used for future decision making.

These results suggest that more timely copy referencing the new administration may not be as motivating a factor as focusing on the Left's destructive policies.

In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the sample size is sufficient to achieve a higher level of confidence in the results. Additionally, it may be beneficial to run the experiment for a longer period of time to account for any potential fluctuations or outliers. It is also essential to carefully analyze the data and results before drawing any conclusions or making decisions based on them.

experiments.title: How adding the gift amount in a donation form’s submit button affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding the donor’s gift amount to the submit button for high urgency campaign donors will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because the increase in clarity on the donation amount will lead more people to donate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/19/2024 - 08/05/2024
The Heritage Foundation was running their yearly high-urgency President’s Challenge campaign. In an attempt to increase donor conversion rate on the campaign’s donation page, they wanted to test if adding the donor’s selected gift amount to the donation form’s submit button would increase clarity and lead more people to feel secure in their donation.
The control’s submit button read: “MAKE MY GIFT”
The treatment’s submit button read: “MAKE MY GIFT OF $[amount]”
The control and treatment each received 50% of traffic from Facebook ads, prospecting and housefile emails, and on-site popups.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment was ultimately inconclusive. We learned that adding the donor's selected gift amount to the donation form's submit button may have a positive impact on donor conversion rate for high urgency campaigns. The treatment version, which included the gift amount in the submit button, saw a 1.2% increase in transactions for all traffic.

However, the statistical level of confidence for this result was only 12.5%, which is below the threshold of 95% for results to be considered valid. This means that we cannot confidently say that the increase in transactions was solely due to the change in the submit button language.

In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the sample size is large enough to yield statistically significant results. Additionally, it may be beneficial to test the impact of including the gift amount in the submit button across multiple campaigns and audience segments to see if the results are consistent.

Overall, while the initial findings are promising, further testing is needed to confirm the impact of this change on donor conversion rates.

experiments.title: How asking for a one-time or monthly gift in the email and leading with a monthly tab on the landing page effects donations during Giving Tuesday
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that asking for a monthly or one-time donation and leading with a monthly tab on the landing page for members via an email solicitation during Giving Tuesday will achieve an increase in monthly gifts with no decrease in overall transactions.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 12/05/2024
During Giving Tuesday the Membership team designed an email and landing page test to see whether an ask for a one-time donation or monthly donation vs. an ask for a “best gift” made any difference on transactions or monthly gifts. The treatment which featured the one-time or monthly ask also led to landing page with the monthly tab first.
Here are the results breakdown:
Members: The experiment showed no statistical difference in donor acquisition between asking for a one-time or monthly gift vs. asking for their best gift. There was a 145% lift in monthly transactions with 90% LOC (5 vs. 10 gifts). There was a directional decrease in clicks on the link in the email for the treatment with a 5% decrease with 63% LOC.
Nonmembers: The test showed the treatment caused a statistically valid decrease in donor acquisition of -59% with 97% LOC. There was no added benefit in monthly acquisition, but the treatment did validate a 5% decrease in email clicks with 99.4% LOC.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment include:

1. The treatment asking for a monthly or one-time donation and leading with a monthly tab on the landing page did not lead to an increase in monthly gifts for all traffic.

2. Despite seeing a decrease in donations for all traffic, the statistical level of confidence was only 8.3%, which is below the 85% threshold for results to be considered valid.

3. The experiment did not achieve the hypothesized increase in monthly gifts with no decrease in overall transactions among members during Giving Tuesday.

In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the sample size is sufficient to produce statistically significant results with a higher level of confidence. Additionally, it may be beneficial to revisit the hypothesis and consider potential adjustments to the treatment based on the insights gained from this experiment. Further testing with larger sample sizes and potentially refined treatment variations can help validate findings and inform decision-making for future fundraising campaigns.

experiments.title: How adding a flag icon to a donation form tab affects recurring donation rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a small American flag in a donation form’s monthly gift tab and submit button to build affinity for direct appeal prospects will achieve an increase in conversion rate because the flag image will help align their motivation with the intended action.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/30/2024 - 06/11/2024
The Heritage Foundation had previously run a test on their membership renewal page where they added a small flag image to a tabbed donation form’s monthly gift tab in order to appeal to the patriotism of their donors, which led to an increase in donations.
Wondering whether this concept would appeal to donors on another direct appeal donation page, they also tested this flag image in the donation form’s $100 gift array button and in the form submit button, which led to a decrease in donations at a near-valid level of confidence.
As a follow-up experiment on the direct appeal donation page, they wanted to test whether the flag icon in the monthly gift tab and the form submit button would lead to an increase in donations, namely recurring donations.
They launched an A/B test where the control had no flag icon anywhere on the form, and the treatment had the flag icon in the monthly gift tab and the form submit button.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a small American flag image to a donation form's monthly gift tab and submit button did not lead to a valid increase in donations, specifically recurring donations. In fact, the treatment group saw a 40.3% decrease in transactions for all traffic compared to the control group. However, it is important to note that the statistical confidence level of 78.8% is below the valid threshold of 95%, indicating that these results may not be reliable.

The treatment also resulted in just a 0.17% increase in recurring donations at a 68.3% level of confidence. With such a small sample size, these results cannot be relied upon to inform future action.

In future experimentation, it is crucial to ensure that the sample size is adequate to achieve a statistically valid result. Additionally, it is important to carefully consider the impact of visual elements such as flag icons on donor behavior. Other ideas for follow-up testing include testing a different icon than an American flag or testing this concept on a donation page following a content offer.

experiments.title: How adding the gift amount to a main donation page form submit button affects donation rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding the donor’s gift amount to the submit button for main donation page donors will achieve an increase in donation rate because increased clarity and donor autonomy will persuade donors to follow through with making their gift.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/01/2024 - 06/25/2024
In an effort to increase donation rate on their main donation page, The Heritage Foundation wondered whether adding the donor’s gift amount in the donation form submit button would increase clarity and donor autonomy and lead to this increase.
The control form submit button read “MAKE MY GIFT” while the treatment button read “MAKE MY GIFT OF $(amount)”.
Each variant in this A/B experiment received 50% of traffic which ran for 24 days.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that adding the donor's gift amount to the submit button on the main donation page did not lead to an increase in donation rate. In fact, the treatment group saw a decrease in transactions compared to the control group. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 3.0%, which is below the commonly accepted threshold of 85%. This means that the results are not valid and should not be used for future decision making.

Future experimentation should include running this test on a donation page that receives more traffic, such as a content offer.

Additionally, it may be beneficial to test different variations of the submit button text or other elements on the donation page to identify what truly resonates with donors and leads to an increase in donations.

experiments.title: How highlighting that only a small amount of people donate after requesting an ebook affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that highlighting that only a small minority people donate for ebook downloaders will achieve an increase in conversion rate because it will appeal to the ideal donor, who are attracted to the idea of being in a small minority of people who were willing to do the “right thing” rather than the “popular thing”.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/31/2024 - 06/25/2024
