The Heritage Foundation had been running a free ebook offer for “Guide to the Constitution” for several years. Due to how long this offer has been in the marketplace, we’ve seen ICR slowly decline.
Because of this, we wanted to test highlighting the fact that “less than 1%” of people who take download the ebook are willing to take the next step and donate.” They’ve ran this experiment for other offers and have seen valid lifts in ICR.
This language had increased conversion because it appealed to the ideal donor, who was attracted to the idea of being in a small minority of people who were willing to do the “right thing” rather than the “popular thing”.
They inserted this language in two places, and split the traffic to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that highlighting the fact that only a small minority of people donate for ebook downloaders did not lead to an increase in conversions. In fact, the treatment group saw a 32% decrease in transactions compared to the control group. However, since the statistical confidence level is only 66%, therefore these results cannot be considered valid and should not be used for future decision-making.

Moving forward, it is important to continue testing different messaging and strategies to determine what resonates best with potential donors. It is possible that highlighting the low donation rate may not have had the intended effect on the target audience. Future experiments could explore different ways to appeal to the ideal donor and emphasize the impact of their contribution, rather than focusing on the low donation rate. By continuing to test and iterate, The Heritage Foundation can refine its approach to maximize conversions and ultimately increase donations.

experiments.title: How adding social proof to an instant donation page through a FOMO slide out affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding social proof through a FOMO slideout for ebook downloaders will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because  seeing that others are actively engaging and donating will create a sense of urgency and validation, encouraging more people to contribute.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/05/2024 - 06/25/2024
The Heritage Foundation is running a free ebook offer through multiple paid advertising channels.
They had previously tested a FOMO slideout on their ‘Renew Membership’ donation page but did not see any valid results in terms of donor conversion. They wanted to test how this concept would perform on an acquisition donation page.
The FOMO slideout pulled the most recent donations from that donation page and slid out every 10 seconds showing a different donor’s first name and state saying they just donated to The Heritage Foundation.
This test was ran for 20 days and traffic was split 50/50 between the control and the treatment.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that adding a FOMO slideout for ebook downloaders did not lead to an increase in donor conversion rate. In fact, the treatment saw a slight decrease in Transactions for All Traffic, but with a statistical level of confidence of only 2.3%. This means that the results are not statistically significant and should not be used for future decision making.
In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the sample size is large enough to detect any significant differences between the control and treatment groups. It is also crucial to consider other factors that may have influenced the results, such as the timing of the experiment or the targeting of the paid advertising channels. Additionally, it may be beneficial to test the FOMO slideout on different types of pages or with different messaging to see if it has a different impact on donor conversion rates.

experiments.title: How a continuation headline affect donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a continuation headline for ebook downloaders will increase the donor conversion rate because we’re providing clarity on their next steps and sparking curiosity by capitalizing on the visitor’s heightened attention and interest.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/15/2024 - 05/30/2024
The Heritage Foundation had previously seen lifts in ICR when removing ‘thank you’ from the headline and instead, clarifying on what the users next steps are. Because of this, we wanted to test this concept on their Guide to the Constitution donation page. This instant donation page follows a free ebook download landing page.
Traffic was split 50/50 between the control and treatment.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that using a continuation headline for ebook downloaders may have a positive impact on donor conversion rates. The treatment version, which clarified the next steps for the user and prompted them to consider donating, saw a 2.2% increase in transactions for all traffic.

However, the statistical confidence level of 6.2% is below the generally accepted threshold of 85%, indicating that these results may not be reliable for future decision-making.

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to further test this concept with a larger sample size or a longer duration to determine if the trend towards increased donor conversions holds true. It is important to always prioritize statistically significant results with a high level of confidence when making decisions based on experimentation outcomes.

experiments.title: How adding clarity to a donation form’s tab titles affects donation rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding clarity in the titles of the tabbed form for survey takers will increase donor conversion rate and recurring donor conversion rate because users will be able to better understand what we’re asking them to do.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/02/2024 - 05/14/2024
On The Heritage Foundation’s donation page for their Voter Fraud Survey offer, they wanted to test whether changing the donation form tab’s titles with language that added clarity and donor autonomy would increase both the one-time donor conversion rate and recurring conversion rate.
The control donation form’s tab titles were: “Single Gift” and “Monthly Gift”
For the treatment donation form’s tab titles, we clarified that the donor could “Make a One-Time Gift” or “Make a Monthly Gift”
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that clarifying the language in the tab titles of a donation form can potentially lead to an increase in conversion rates, both for one-time donations and recurring donations. The treatment version, which used clearer language ("Make a One-Time Gift", "Make a Monthly Gift"), saw a slight 0.5% increase in Transactions for All Traffic compared to the control version.

However, the statistical level of confidence was only 2.4%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 95% to consider the results valid. Therefore, these results should not be used for future decision-making.

Interestingly, the same treatment did lead to an increase in email conversion rate on a donation page following an ebook offer. This suggests that the treatment tab titles may resonate more strongly with people downloading an ebook as opposed to people who have taken a survey.

In future experiments, it would be beneficial to continue testing the impact of language clarity in survey donation forms on conversion rates, ensuring that statistical significance is achieved to draw reliable conclusions. Additionally, exploring other factors such as the design, layout, and call-to-action language in donation forms could also be valuable for optimizing conversion rates.

experiments.title: How adding clarity to the titles of a tabbed donation form affects donor conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding clarity in the titles of the tabbed form for previous members will increase donor conversion rate and recurring donor conversion rate because users will be able to better understand what we’re asking them to do.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/08/2024 - 05/09/2024
The Heritage Foundation has a membership renewal page where previous members of the organization can renew their membership either through a monthly gift or a one-time gift.
They wanted to test adding clarity to the titles of the donation form to see if it would increase the recurring conversion rate and one-time donor conversion rate.
The control monthly tab is labeled “Join Leaders Club” and the one-time tab is labeled “Single Gift.” For the treatment, we clarified on the monthly tab that they could “Make a monthly donation” and for the one-time tab they could “Make a one-time donation.”
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that adding clarity to the titles of the donation form tabs did not have an effect on donor conversion rates for one-time or monthly giving. We saw that clarifying the monthly tab to "Make a monthly donation" led to an 11.8% increase in recurring transactions but with only a 19% level of confidence. Additionally, we saw a 5.4% lift in one-time giving, but with only a 13% level of confidence.

This means that the results of the experiment cannot be considered valid or reliable for future decision-making.

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue testing variations of the donation form titles to further optimize conversion rates. Additionally, it is essential to ensure that experiments are run for a longer duration to gather more data and increase the statistical confidence level of the results. By continuously testing and refining different elements of the donation process, the organization can work towards maximizing donor conversion rates and ultimately increasing support for its cause.

experiments.title: How a recurring donation interrupter affects recurring donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a donation interrupter to ask users to convert their one-time gift to a monthly gift for potential donors will achieve a higher recurring donor conversion rate because   they will be presented with the opportunity to know what impact a monthly gift can have.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/23/2024 - 05/09/2024
The Heritage Foundation has a page on their website where people can join the organization as a member by making a one-time or recurring donation, and they wanted to test how a donation pop-up interrupter would perform to increase the amount of recurring donors they get through new memberships.
On the donation page, users have the option to make a one-time or monthly gift, but if the user chooses one-time and makes a gift under $100, they would receive a pop-up after hitting “Make my gift” asking them to convert their gift to a $10 monthly donation.
The pop-up included value proposition as to what impact a monthly gift will have.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that implementing a donation interrupter to ask users to convert their one-time gift to a monthly gift had no effect on conversion rates for monthly or one-time giving. The treatment group saw a 32% decrease in Transactions - Monthly, but this decrease is only based on 5 recurring transactions and only had a 33% level of confidence. This means that the results cannot be considered statistically significant and should not be used for future decision-making.
Additionally, we saw a 15% lift in one-time giving but with only a 38% level of confidence.
The key takeaways are that the recurring interrupter will most likely not hurt one-time or monthly giving conversion rates, but we do not have enough data to know if it will produce a lift of any kind.
In future experimentation, it is essential to continue testing the effectiveness of the donation interrupter with a larger sample size and over a longer period to determine its true impact on recurring donor conversion rates. Additionally, considering other factors such as user experience, messaging, and timing of the interrupter could provide valuable insights for optimizing donation processes and increasing donor engagement.

experiments.title: How letting members know that any gift they give, no matter the size, are appreciated affects the click-through and conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Letting members know that any gift they give, no matter the size, is appreciated will increase the click-through and conversion rate because they will know their gift is helping support the mission
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 04/01/2024
We conducted this email experiment for The Heritage Foundation’s Members Card campaign. The email asked members to give, in return, they would receive a Members Card. The email also announces a matching gift opportunity. The conclusion of the email includes a call to action to donate.
We wanted to know if more members would click if they knew they would receive a Member’s Card no matter how much they gave. The treatment included the phrase, “Any amount you give is greatly appreciated” as part of the call to action.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are:

1. The treatment version, which included the phrase "Any amount you give is greatly appreciated", did not perform as well as the control version in terms of click-through rates. There was a 9.3% decrease in clicks for all traffic with a 44.1% confidence level, signaling that the control version was more effective at encouraging action. The conversion rate was also lower for the treatment email.

2. The statistical confidence level of 44.1% is below the recommended threshold of 85%, indicating that the results are not considered valid for future decision-making.

In future experimentation, it is important to:

1. Consider the messaging carefully and ensure that it aligns with the organization's goals and mission. While emphasizing that any amount is appreciated may seem like a positive way to encourage donations, it's crucial to test different messaging strategies to see what resonates best with the audience.

2. Continue to experiment with different messaging, calls to action, and incentives to understand what drives donor behavior. By testing various approaches, we can gather valuable insights to inform future campaigns and optimize results.

3. Increase the sample size or test over a longer period to improve the statistical confidence level of the results. This will help ensure that the findings are reliable and can be used to make informed decisions.

Overall, this experiment highlights the importance of testing different strategies and messaging to determine the most effective way to engage supporters and drive action. By learning from these results, we can continue to refine our approach and improve the effectiveness of our campaigns in the future.

1. The treatment version, which included the phrase "Any amount you give is greatly appreciated," did not perform as well as the control version in terms of click-through rates. There was a 4.2% decrease in Clicks for All Traffic and a slight increase in conversions with a 58.4% statistical level of confidence. 2. The hypothesis that letting non-members know that any gift they give, no matter the size, is appreciated will increase the click-through and conversion rate was not supported by the results of this experiment.

In future experimentation, it is essential to consider the impact of wording and messaging on donor behavior. It may be beneficial to conduct further tests with different variations of language to determine the most effective way to communicate the value of donations to non-members. experiments.title: How explaining the value of the membership card to non-members in a fundraising email affects the click-through rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Including reminder text about the value of a membership card will increase the open rate because it is an additional opportunity to explain the value non-members receive.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/27/2024
We conducted this email experiment for The Heritage Foundation’s Members Card campaign. The email asked non-members to give, in return, they would receive a Members Card. The email also announces a matching gift opportunity. The conclusion of the email includes a call to action to donate.
We wanted to know if non-members would click more if they were reminded about the value they receive when they donate. The treatment included additional text towards the beginning of the email which explained the value proposition.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that including reminder text about the value of a membership card can potentially increase the click-through rates for donation emails, as evidenced by the 0.6% increase in Clicks for All Traffic in the treatment group. However, the statistical significance level of 11.0% is below the standard threshold of 85%, indicating that these results may not be reliable for future decision-making.

Note: While the click-through rate increased in the treatment email, the conversion rate had a slightly lower conversion rate.

Moving forward, it is important to continue exploring the impact of including value proposition reminders in donation appeals, perhaps refining the messaging or placement within the email to maximize effectiveness. Additionally, it would be beneficial to conduct further experiments with larger sample sizes to validate the findings and make more informed decisions for future campaigns.

experiments.title: How reminding members about the value of the membership card in a fundraising email affects the click-through rate

experiments.summary:

experiments.title: How mentioning a match offer sooner in a fundraising email to lapsed members affects the click-through and conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Adding the match gift offer sooner in a fundraising email to lapsed members will increase the click-through rate because the incentive to give is given greater emphasis.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/28/2024
We conducted this email experiment for The Heritage Foundation’s Members Card campaign. The email asked lapsed members to give, in return, they would receive a Members Card. The email also announces a matching gift opportunity. The conclusion of the email includes a call to action to donate.
We wanted to know if lapsed members would click if the matching gift offer was placed earlier in the body of the email. The treatment included a one-sentence call-to-action that mentioned the matching gift opportunity after the second paragraph of the email.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding the matching gift offer sooner in a fundraising email to lapsed members did not increase the click-through rate. The treatment saw a decrease in clicks compared to the control group. However, since the statistical level of confidence is below 85%, these results cannot be considered valid and should not be used for future decision-making.

Note: The conversation rate of the treatment email also experienced a slight decrease.

In future experimentation, it may be beneficial to continue testing the placement of the matching gift offer within the email to determine the most effective position for maximizing click-through rates. Additionally, exploring other factors such as the messaging around the matching gift offer or the design of the email could also be valuable in optimizing fundraising efforts for lapsed members.

experiments.title: How mentioning a match offer sooner in a fundraising email to members affects the click-through and conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Adding the match gift offer sooner in a fundraising email to members will increase the click-through rate because the incentive to give is given greater emphasis.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/28/2024
We conducted this email experiment for The Heritage Foundation’s Members Card campaign. The email asked members to give, in return, they would receive a Members Card. The email also announces a matching gift opportunity. The conclusion of the email includes a call to action to donate.

In future experimentation, it is important to consider the placement of incentives like matching gift offers in fundraising emails carefully. experiments.title: How addressing one specific problem in a fundraising email affects the click-through rate for members
experiments.researchQuestion: Addressing a specific problem/issue will increase the click-through rate because members have a clearer understanding of the problem they will help solve.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/25/2024
We conducted this email experiment for The Heritage Foundation’s Members Card campaign. The email asked members to give and in return, they would receive a Members Card. The conclusion of the email includes a call to action to donate.
We wanted to know if describing one issue America faces instead of three issues would encourage members to click. The control mentioned three issues while the treatment focused on one. The issues were mentioned in the body of the email and in the call to action.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that addressing a specific problem/issue in an email campaign can potentially increase the click-through rate. In this case, the treatment focusing on one issue saw a 13.5% increase in clicks compared to the control that mentioned three issues. This indicates that providing a clear and specific understanding of the problem members will help solve can be more impactful in encouraging action.

Note: The donation conversion rate for the treatment email also experienced a slight increase.

However, it's important to note that the statistical level of confidence for this result is 57.4%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 85% for statistical significance. Therefore, these results should not be considered valid for future decision-making.

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue testing the impact of addressing specific issues versus multiple issues in email campaigns. By conducting further experiments with a larger sample size or different variations, we can gather more conclusive data on whether this approach effectively increases engagement and donations. Additionally, it may be helpful to explore other factors such as the framing of the issue, the language used, or the placement within the email to further optimize campaign effectiveness.

experiments.title: How adding match-giving language to the call to action affects the click-through rate for members
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe adding match-giving language to the calls to action will increase the click-through rate because it provides urgency and a feeling of greater impact.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/25/2024
We conducted this email experiment for The Heritage Foundation’s Members Card campaign.  The email asked members to give and in return, they would receive a Members Card. The email also announces a matching gift opportunity. The conclusion of the email includes a call to action to donate.
We wanted to know if members would click more if the matching gift offer was mentioned again in the call to action text. The control email described the matching gift opportunity once in the body of the email. The treatment mentioned the match in the body of the email and in the call to action.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding match-giving language to the calls to action can potentially increase the click-through rate. The treatment group, which included the matching gift offer in both the body of the email and the call to action, experienced an 11.3% increase in clicks compared to the control group.
Note: The treatment email also had a higher conversion rate.
However, it's important to note that the confidence level of the results was only 70.1%, falling below the 85% threshold typically required for statistical significance. As a result, these results should not be considered valid for future decision-making.
In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue exploring the impact of emphasizing matching gift opportunities in calls to action. This can help to determine if this strategy consistently drives higher engagement from recipients. Additionally, it is crucial to ensure results have a high level of statistical significance before drawing any definitive conclusions or making changes based on the findings.

experiments.title: How belonging to a like-minded community in a fundraising email affects the click-through rate for lapsed members
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe creating a sense of unity will increase the click-through rates because supporters want to belong and connect with other people who have the same values and passions.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/22/2024
We conducted this email experiment for The Heritage Foundation’s Members Card campaign.  The email asked lapsed members to give and in return, they would receive a Members Card. The conclusion of the email includes a call to action to donate.
We wanted to know if including text about being in a community of like-minded individuals would encourage lapsed members to click. Text was added to the body of the email and in the call to action.
The idea of unity is based on a principle from Robert Cialdini and the psychology of persuasion.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that the inclusion of text about being in a community of like-minded individuals did not have a significant impact on click-through rates for The Heritage Foundation's Members Card campaign. The treatment saw a decrease in clicks compared to the control, with a statistical confidence level below the threshold for validity.
Note: The donation conversion rate experienced a slight increase in the treatment email.
In future experimentation, it is important to consider alternative hypotheses and strategies for increasing engagement among lapsed members. This could involve testing different messaging approaches, incentives, or targeting tactics to better understand what resonates with supporters and drives them to take action. Additionally, it is crucial to ensure that experiments are designed with a sufficient sample size and statistical power to draw meaningful conclusions.

experiments.title: How making a statement call to action with members in a fundraising email affects the click-through rate

Ended On: 03/11/2024
This email experiment was conducted for The Heritage Foundation’s Founder’s Day campaign. The email was a personal communication informing members about the challenges America faces today and how The Heritage Foundation is solving those issues. At the end of the email, they ask members to “activate” their membership.
We wanted to know if using a statement call to action would encourage members to click instead of a call to action that asked a question.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that changing the call to action from a question to a statement did not lead to a higher click-through rate among members. The treatment saw a decrease in clicks and the number of conversions, although the results were not statistically significant.

In future experimentation, it is important to note that small changes in wording or formatting may not always have the desired impact on member engagement. It is essential to conduct thorough testing with a significant sample size and statistical confidence to draw valid conclusions. Additionally, it may be beneficial to explore other factors that could influence member behavior, such as the timing of the email, the content of the message, or the overall design of the communication.

Overall, the findings from this experiment should not be used to make decisions about future email campaigns, as the results do not provide a clear direction. Instead, it would be advisable to continue testing different strategies and elements to determine the most effective approach for encouraging members to take action.

Overall, it is crucial to rely on statistically significant results before implementing any changes based on an experiment.

experiments.title: How adding a side column ad in TDS articles affects transactions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a digital content offer in the sidebar of TDS article pages for TDS readers will achieve an increase in transactions and emails acquired because they are receiving relevant content that brings more value to their visit.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/26/2024 - 03/05/2024
We added an ad for our Founders Almanac eBook offer in the side column of TDS articles. Our hypothesis was that adding an additional digital offer alongside our Guide to the Constitution Dear Reader ads would boost conversions for email sign ups and donations. There was a lift in email sign-ups but a non-validated decrease in transactions and revenue.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are:

1. The addition of a digital content offer in the sidebar of TDS article pages led to a decrease in revenue for all traffic by 18.4%, which was not statistically significant at a 52.1% confidence level. This means that there was not enough evidence to conclude that the digital offer had a negative impact on revenue.

2. However, there was a significant increase in Almanac Button Clicks by 1% and Submit Founders' Almanac Form submissions by 5441%. These results suggest that TDS readers were engaging more with the digital content offer.

3. The results of this experiment should not be used for future decision-making due to the low level of statistical confidence (below 85%).

In future experimentation, it is important to conduct tests with a larger sample size and for a longer duration to ensure the results are statistically valid. Additionally, it would be beneficial to analyze user behavior and feedback to understand the impact of digital content offers on user experience and engagement. It is also crucial to continue testing different types of offers and placements to determine the most effective strategy for increasing email sign-ups and donations without negatively impacting revenue.

experiments.title: How adding a donate button to the header and navigation menu affects donor conversion rate

rows.name: Donate Button in Header and Navigation Menu

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a donate button to the header and navigation menu for home page visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because more people will click to view the donation page. because  more people will click to view the donation page.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/15/2023 - 12/11/2023
The Daily Signal is The Heritage Foundation’s multimedia news platform. On their website, the only call-to-action to donate and support the Daily Signal’s work is located at the very bottom in the footer of each page and doesn’t stand out.
In an attempt to increase the amount of traffic to the donation page from the home page, we wanted to test if adding a more prominently displayed donate button to the top of the home page led to an increase in donor conversion rate.
The donate button appeared in both the home page’s header and dropdown navigation menu (in both the desktop and mobile experiences) and was blue in color in order to stand out from the surrounding buttons. 50% of home page traffic saw the control and 50% saw the treatment.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment ran for 27 days, in which time the donate button variant saw a 42.8% increase in donations for all traffic compared to the control. However, it is important to note that the statistical level of confidence for this increase is 53.2%, meaning that the results cannot be considered valid or reliable for future decision making.
In terms of revenue, the treatment saw a decrease of 0.11% in average gift, but at a 6.52% level of confidence these results cannot be relied upon to predict a valid outcome.
Reasons for this may include the donate button appearing less hierarchically prominent than the email "Sign Up" button above it, or home page visitors not yet having experienced the value in the Daily Signal's articles before seeing a donate button.
Based on the results, it is clear that additional testing is needed to understand what motivates The Daily Signal visitors to donate to the organization. This could include adding value proposition copy next to the button, or testing different locations on the home page. It may also be worth testing an unobtrusive sticky donate button that follows the visitor through their journey sitewide.

experiments.title: How using different images for a content offer acquisition funnel affects email conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using an image of the ebook on the landing page for potential downloaders will achieve a higher email acquisition rate because users will perceive the ebook image as higher value.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/14/2023 - 01/16/2024
The Heritage Foundation is offering the “Guide to the Constitution” digital version as a free gift through paid Facebook ads, email prospecting and website traffic.
Heritage originally tested the concept of the laptop image vs the ebook image in a Dear Reader on daily signal. The results showed that click-through rate on the dear reader was 68.6% higher when using the ebook image vs no image at all. The laptop image produced a 30.1% decrease in clickthrough rate compared to the control.
The offer has always had the laptop image on the landing page, so they decided to test changing the image to the ebook to see if it would increase email acquisition rate on the landing page.
They believed it would because the ebook looks to have more value than just a picture of the offer through a laptop or phone screen.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that changing the image on the landing page from a laptop to an ebook did not result in a higher email acquisition rate. The treatment version actually saw a decrease in Transactions for All Traffic.

Although the results showed a decrease in Transactions, the statistical level of confidence was below 85%, which means that the results cannot be considered valid and should not be used for future decision making.

In future experimentation, it would be important to gather more data and increase the sample size in order to achieve a higher level of statistical confidence. It may also be worth considering other elements on the landing page, such as the layout, color scheme, or messaging, to determine if any of those factors have an impact on the email acquisition rate.

experiments.title: How using different images for a content offer acquisition funnel affects email conversion rate

experiments.title: How adding a “Support Honest Journalism” Sticky Button to TDS during EOY
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by adding a “Support Honest Journalism” sticky button for new visitors to The Daily Signal will achieve a lift in conversion rate and average gift because media consumers are hungry for trustworthy news and will support a media outlet who represents independent and reliable journalism.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/29/2024
We tested three different sticky buttons on The Daily Signal during EOY 2023. Two button’s were direct solicitations and one was a content offer for our Guide to the Constitution. The first button asked the visitor to “Support Honest Journalism” and the other asked them to “Donate”. Part of the experiment was to determine whether a more defined CTA like supporting honest journalism would create a lift in revenue over a tried and true content offer and a regular donate button. The donate button and Guide to the Constitution buttons resulted in a lower conversion rate and average gift, but the support honest journalism button saw a significant lift of lift in avg. gift and a slight lift in conversion rate. Looking at subsegments we identified that the primary lift came from New Visitors to the site which had an 18.77% increase in conversion rate, and a 98% increase in avg. gift. Returning visitors for the journalism button saw a net -58% drop in revenue.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are as follows:

1. A more defined call to action, such as "Support Honest Journalism," can result in a lift in both conversion rate and average gift.

2. The "Support Honest Journalism" button specifically resonated with new visitors to the site, leading to a significant increase in conversion rate and average gift.

3. However, returning visitors did not respond well to the "Support Honest Journalism" button, resulting in a net loss in revenue.

4. The overall increase in revenue for all traffic was substantial but did not reach a statistically significant level of confidence.

Based on these learnings, it is clear that the "Support Honest Journalism" button has potential as a strong call to action for new visitors. However, it is important to consider the impact on returning visitors, as the negative response from this segment had a significant impact on the overall revenue.

For future experimentation, it would be beneficial to further explore how to tailor the messaging and call to action for different visitor segments. Additionally, it is crucial to conduct experiments with a larger sample size to ensure statistical significance and validate the results before making any impactful decisions.

experiments.title: How does adding a more personalized introduction affect email revenue on Giving Tuesday?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a personalized introduction with rhetorical questions framed from personal experience relating to the project or topic being discussed for our email housefile will achieve a lift in revenue and an increased conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 12/15/2023
This test was run to our email housefile on Giving Tuesday 11/28/23 and was our first morning email. We tested a personalized introduction that featured rhetorical questions framed from the donors perspective and text specifically calling out bureaucrats and unlected agencies vs. a control without those additions.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The results recorded here reflect cumulative results for members (168K emailed) and nonmembers (790K emailed). Below are results for subsegments.
Member: treatment created a 58% increase in revenue with a 62.3% level of confidence
 	Increased conversion rate by 13.7% with a 64.1% level of confidence
 	Nonmembers: treatment created a 8% increase in revenue with a 52.3% level of confidence
 	Increase conversion rate by 24.8% with a 58.1% level of confidence
 	LC: Treatment created a 8.2% increase in revenue with a 52.2% level of confidence
 	No statistical difference between conversion rates
 	Lapsed Members: Treatment crated a -61.1% decrease in revenue with a 71.8% level of confidence
 	Decreased conversion rate by -26% with a 33.6% level of confidence

experiments.title: How using a personalized and gratitude-centered tone for nonmembers affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: The treatment email, which employs a personalized and gratitude-centered approach, will result in a higher membership renewal rate compared to the control email because the treatment might foster a stronger sense of loyalty and connection.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/31/2024
The Heritage Foundation is running thier annual Kickoff Campaign to acquire members. You can become a member by giving a gift of any size, one-time or recurring. This experiment was ran for nonmembers to Heritage asking them to activate their membership. For this test, we wanted to see how nonmembers would react to a more personalized and gratitude-centered tone in the email.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that the personalized and gratitude-centered approach used in the treatment email did not result in a higher membership renewal rate compared to the control email. In fact, the treatment email saw a decrease in donations.

Based on this, it is important to recognize that not all alternative approaches will yield better results. In this case, the treatment email might have been perceived differently by the recipients, leading to a decrease in donations. It is also worth noting that the statistical level of confidence is below 85%, which means the results are not valid.

In future experimentation, it is crucial to conduct further testing with a larger sample size and higher statistical confidence. This will help ensure more accurate and reliable results. Additionally, it might be beneficial to explore other approaches and messaging strategies that resonate well with the target audience.

Overall, this experiment emphasizes the importance of testing different strategies, analyzing the results, and using the learnings to inform future experimentation. It highlights the need for ongoing testing and optimization to effectively engage and retain members.

experiments.title: How a simplified message that focuses only on impact in the final email of a high urgency campaign affects conversions for members
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that simplifying the message and only focusing on the impact in the final email of the campaign will produce a higher conversion rate because the treatment email presents a more straightforward and concise message, which can be easier for recipients to understand quickly.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 02/06/2024
The Heritage Foundation is running thier annual Kickoff Campaign to acquire members. You can become a member by giving a gift of any size, one-time or recurring. This experiment was ran for members to Heritage asking them to renew their membership. For this test, we wanted to see how a concise message that only focuses on their impact in the final email of the campaign would affect conversion rates. We believe a clear and simple call-to-action may lead to a better response as it reduces cognitive load for the reader.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that the treatment email, which focused solely on the impact of The Heritage Foundation, did not have a significant impact on conversion rates compared to the control email.

The treatment email saw a .35% decrease in clicks for all traffic, but the statistical level of confidence is only 3.4%. This means that the results cannot be considered valid and should not be used for future decision making.

Although there was no difference in clicks, we did see an 8.9% decrease in donor conversion rate with a 77.4% level of confidence. This tells us that using concise copy for the members-only segment does not motivate them as much to renew their membership when compared to other segments like lapsed donors and non-members.

experiments.title: How does calling out an incomplete membership with a suggested ask amount for non-members affect membership conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that highlighting in the email that their membership is incomplete and giving them a suggested gift amount  for nonmembers will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because it adds clarity to the ask which can cause Potential members appreciate knowing what is expected of them and how much they are expected to contribute.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/31/2024
The Heritage Foundation is running thier annual Kickoff Campaign to acquire members. You can become a member by giving a gift of any size, one-time or recurring. This experiment was ran for non-members to Heritage asking them to activate their membership. For this test, we wanted to see how non-members would react to calling out in the top of the email that their membership is incomplete and providing them with a suggested gift amount to activate it. The callout also included the deadline of January 31st.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that highlighting in the email that their membership is incomplete and suggesting a gift amount for non-members can potentially increase donor conversion rate. The treatment group showed a 12.8% increase in donations compared to the control group but only with a 60.6% level of confidence. Additionally, the treatment group saw a 21% decrease in clicks on the email with a 99% level of confidence.
Because the statistical level of confidence of 60.6% is below the recommended threshold of 85%, this means that the results cannot be considered valid and should not be used for future decision making.
In future experimentation, it is important to increase the sample size or conduct the experiment for a longer duration to achieve a higher level of statistical significance. This will provide more reliable results and help make informed decisions about the effectiveness of different strategies.
Additionally, it would be beneficial to test different variations or combinations of strategies in order to identify the most effective approach for increasing donor conversion rate. This could include testing different types of messaging, callouts, suggested gift amounts, or deadlines.
Overall, the key learning from this experiment is that highlighting membership incompleteness and providing a suggested gift amount may be a promising strategy for increasing donor conversion rate. However, further testing is needed to validate these findings and make informed decisions.

experiments.title: How using a personalized and gratitude-centered tone for lapsed members affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: The treatment email, which employs a personalized and gratitude-centered approach, will result in a higher membership reactivation rate compared to the control email because the treatment might foster a stronger sense of loyalty and connection.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/31/2024
The Heritage Foundation is running thier annual Kickoff Campaign to acquire members. You can become a member by giving a gift of any size, one-time or recurring. This experiment was ran for lapsed members to Heritage asking them to reactivate their membership. For this test, we wanted to see how lapsed members would react to a more personalized and gratitude-centered tone in the email.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are as follows:

1. The treatment email, which employed a personalized and gratitude-centered approach, did not result in a higher membership reactivation rate compared to the control email.

2. The treatment email saw a 7.5% decrease in donations for all traffic.

3. The statistical level of confidence for this experiment was 21.6%, indicating that the results cannot be considered valid.

4. The hypothesis that the treatment email would foster a stronger sense of loyalty and connection was not supported by the data.

Based on these learnings, it is clear that the personalized and gratitude-centered approach used in the treatment email did not have the intended effect on lapsed members. However, since the results are not valid, it is not recommended to make any decisions based on this experiment.

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to conduct a similar test with a larger sample size to increase the statistical significance of the results. Additionally, conducting user research or surveying lapsed members to gather insights on their motivations and preferences could help inform future experiments and improve the effectiveness of reactivation campaigns. It is important to continue testing different approaches to find the most effective strategy for reconnecting with lapsed members and increasing reactivation rates.

experiments.title: How clearly stating the problem and positioning the organization as the solution affects conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe the treatment with its emotional appeal, direct questions, and emphasis on the threat to democracy, will lead to a higher donor conversion rate because it’s focusing on the problem and how Heritage is the solution.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/21/2023 - 01/16/2024
The Heritage Foundation is running a survey through paid acquisition channels to get the public’s opinion on voter fraud. Although the offer receives a lot of traffic and receives names for very cheap, they wanted to test on the instant donation page to see if we can decrease our cost per donor and increase our conversion rate.
They believe that the combination of emotional appeal, direct questions, and emphasis on the threat to democracy, along with positioning Heritage as the solution, will make the treatment more compelling and result in a higher donor conversion rate compared to the control page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that the treatment, which aimed to increase the donor conversion rate by using emotional appeal, direct questions, and emphasizing the threat to democracy, did not lead to a higher conversion rate compared to the control page.

The treatment saw a decrease of 22.1% in transactions for all traffic. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 75.0%, which means the results are not valid and should not be used for future decision making.

Based on this, it is clear that the treatment did not have the intended impact and may not resonate with the target audience. In future experimentation, it would be important to explore other strategies or messaging that can effectively increase the conversion rate.

It is also important to note that the experiment ran for 26 days, which is a significant duration. This indicates that the results are reliable in terms of the data collected. However, as the statistical level of confidence is below 85%, it is necessary to conduct further experiments to validate the findings and make informed decisions.

experiments.title: How using different images for a content offer acquisition funnel affects email conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using an image of the ebook on the landing page for potential downloaders will achieve a higher email acquisition rate because users will perceive the ebook image as higher value.

It may also be worth considering other elements on the landing page, such as the layout, color scheme, or messaging, to determine if any of those factors have an impact on the email acquisition rate.

experiments.title: How adding a donate button to the header and navigation menu affects donor conversion rate

rows.name: Donate Button in Header and Navigation Menu

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a donate button to the header and navigation menu for home page visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because more people will click to view the donation page. because more people will click to view the donation page.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/15/2023 - 12/11/2023
The Daily Signal is The Heritage Foundation’s multimedia news platform. On their website, the only call-to-action to donate and support the Daily Signal’s work is located at the very bottom in the footer of each page and doesn’t stand out.
In an attempt to increase the amount of traffic to the donation page from the home page, we wanted to test if adding a more prominently displayed donate button to the top of the home page led to an increase in donor conversion rate.
The donate button appeared in both the home page’s header and dropdown navigation menu (in both the desktop and mobile experiences) and was blue in color in order to stand out from the surrounding buttons.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment ran for 27 days, in which time the donate button variant saw a 42.8% increase in donations for all traffic compared to the control. However, it is important to note that the statistical level of confidence for this increase is 53.2%, meaning that the results cannot be considered valid or reliable for future decision making.

In terms of revenue, the treatment saw a decrease of 0.11% in average gift, but at a 6.52% level of confidence these results cannot be relied upon to predict a valid outcome.

Reasons for this may include the donate button appearing less hierarchically prominent than the email "Sign Up" button above it, or home page visitors not yet having experienced the value in the Daily Signal's articles before seeing a donate button.

Based on the results, it is clear that additional testing is needed to understand what motivates The Daily Signal visitors to donate to the organization. This could include adding value proposition copy next to the button, or testing different locations on the home page. It may also be worth testing an unobtrusive sticky donate button that follows the visitor through their journey sitewide.

experiments.title: How a tabbed donation form affects donor conversion rates on a primary donation page
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a tabbed donation form for main donation page visitors will achieve an increase in overall donor conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/27/2023 - 12/12/2023
We previously tested how a tabbed donation form affected recurring donations on a public policy organization’s main donation page. In this experiment, the tabbed donation form produced an 11% decrease in recurring gifts (with a 25% level of confidence) and a 25.4% decrease in overall donations (94.4% level of confidence), which disproved our hypothesis that a tabbed donation form would achieve higher recurring donation rates than a monthly giving checkbox by making the monthly giving option and its impact more visible.
In an effort to learn whether a tabbed donation form on this organization’s main donation page would lead to an increase in donor conversion rate, we retested this idea. The control in this experiment presents a monthly giving option as a checkbox below the gift array, while the treatment has two tabs for either a Single Gift or Monthly Gift with a value proposition callout box above the monthly gift tab. 50% of traffic saw the control and 50% saw the treatment.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running this experiment for 16 days, we observed that the tabbed donation form on the main donation page led to an insignificant 2.2% increase in overall donor conversion rates, but its 11.3% level of confidence is not high enough to consider these results valid. Therefore, we cannot confidently conclude that the tabbed donation form is more effective in increasing conversions, but we also cannot conclude that it is less effective.
Additionally, the treatment saw a 99.5% increase in recurring donations, but at an invalid 58.4% level of confidence.
These results, along with data of tabbed donation forms leading to an increase in donations in the organization's acquisition offers funnels, may indicate that people visiting their main donation page may have high donor motivation and be less influenced or even more distracted by the tabbed design of the form. The results also may have been impacted by an additional variable—the control not having the value proposition callout box found in the treatment's experience.

experiments.title: How a larger impact number affects donor conversion on the main donation page
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that showing a larger impact number for main donation page visitors will achieve an increase in conversion rate because it will increase their perception of Heritage’s impact.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/18/2023 - 11/06/2023
The Heritage Foundation had long used their 500k member base as a leg of their value proposition to show their broad support. However, their marketing team’s outreach initiatives meant that they actually reached a lot more people each year than they had members.
Donors responded very favorably to this “larger” metric, but they wanted to see if that proved out in increased fundraising results.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that by showing a larger impact number for main donation page visitors, the conversion rate can be increased. This is supported by the fact that the treatment version, which presented the larger number of 10 million members, advocates, and concerned Americans reached per day, resulted in a 7.7% increase in transactions for all traffic.

However, it is important to note that the statistical level of confidence for this result is only 34.4%, which is below the recommended threshold of 85%. Therefore, the results cannot be considered valid and should not be used for future decision making.

Based on this, it is recommended to conduct further experiments to validate the findings and determine the true impact of showing a larger number on the conversion rate. The future experimentation should involve a larger sample size and longer duration to ensure more reliable results. Additionally, it may be beneficial to test variations in the language used to present the impact number to see if different phrasing or positioning can further improve the conversion rate.

experiments.title: How a recurring tab impacted conversions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by adding a monthly option for web donors will achieve higher conversion rate because there is additional give options and clarity in the type of gift.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/10/2024 - 01/26/2024
Pathway to Victory is the broadcast ministry of First Dallas.
PTV’s offers are primarily various books and premiums with donations. Our January emphasis was to increase the number of recurring donors. With this in mind, we decided to try adding a recurring tab to normal offers to see if we could passively acquire recurring donors by just having the option available.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Note: treatment generated 0 recurring gifts
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a monthly option for web donors did not achieve a higher conversion rate. In fact, the treatment group saw a decrease in transactions compared to the control group. However, it is important to note that the statistical level of confidence is below 85%, meaning the results are not considered valid and should not be used for future decision-making.
Based on this, it is necessary to conduct further experiments with a larger sample size or different variations to determine the effectiveness of adding a recurring tab for web donors. It is also important to consider other factors that may have influenced the results, such as the timing of the experiment or the specific offer being presented.
Future experimentation should focus on gathering more data and conducting rigorous A/B testing to determine the impact of different variables on conversion rates. It may also be beneficial to analyze other aspects of the donor journey, such as the effectiveness of different messaging or marketing channels, to gain a deeper understanding of donor behavior and preferences. Additionally, seeking feedback from donors and analyzing their motivations and decision-making processes can provide valuable insights for future experiments.

experiments.title: How adding donor autonomy to a headline impacted conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding specificity and autonomy in the headline will increase instant conversion rate because people will be more likely to read the rest of the page when they are offered autonomy to help something as opposed to a command to join something.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/09/2023 - 06/09/2023
When looking to improve donor conversion on an instant donation page, we wondered if the headline to “join the fight to eliminate election fraud” was too vague, too much mental work, and too commanding of a call to action. We hypothesized the headline needed additional specificity, clarity and autonomy to drive donor engagement. We developed a treatment that phrased the next step as an option or a choice rather than a direct instruction. We included “Here’s how” to drive curiosity from the reader and keep the communication in motion and guide the reader’s attention to continue down the page. We split traffic between the control “Your answers have been submitted! Now join the fight to eliminate election fraud in the U.S.” and the treatment “Your answers have been submitted–Now you can help eliminate voter fraud in the U.S. Here’s how:”
experiments.keyLearnings:
The A/B experiment aimed to test whether changing the headline's language to "you can help eliminate voter fraud, here's how" would increase the instant conversion rate compared to the control headline. Upon review, the treatment resulted in a 75% increase in donations for all traffic. However, the level of confidence was 75%, and the total number of donations evaluated was below 50, which means the results cannot be considered valid for future decision-making.
Because we did not reach the needed sample size during the experiment's duration we will launch the next iteration of this test that will maintain the headline change in the treatment while also including additional focus in the body copy on what specific solutions to voter fraud issues that the donor’s gift would directly support.

experiments.title: How using support-focused language impacted gifts on an instant donate page
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using language that focuses on how a donor can directly support an issue on a donation page will achieve an increase in revenue because people are more motivated to give if they feel that their individual support is needed and important.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/09/2023 - 04/30/2023
In an effort to improve the instant donation rate of a survey offer, we tested how a more conversational tone and donor-focused language would affect donations.
The original donation page focused on what policy reforms the organization was promoting to impact fiscal reform. It also mentioned that a donor’s gift would help introduce over 50 specific policy recommendations that would affect the donor. However, we saw an opportunity to focus more on “you” language and putting the donor’s support at the center of the proposed solution. We believed that prospective donors would be more motivated to give if they felt that their support was integral to solving the problems outlined in the survey.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that using language that focuses on how a donor can directly support an issue on a donation page may not be effective in increasing donations. The treatment saw a very slight decrease in donations compared to the control, although the results are not statistically valid. The treatment saw a larger 55% decrease in average gift.

Reasons for this decrease could be:
the treatment's removal of a bulleted list in favor of only paragraphs, which can cause people to move on from without reading
 	the treatment's removal of specific policy recommendations could have led the viewer to not understand what actual, tangible solutions their donation would support

experiments.title: How an urgent message presented as a sticky bar impacted donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that highlighting relevancy and urgency for instant donation page visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion because we increased the motivation to give by explaining in a relevant way why they should give their money to accomplish something specific to an area they care about.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/21/2023 - 04/23/2023
On a post-survey instant donation page, we wondered if we could increase the relevance and urgency of the donation page ask by calling the reader’s attention through a sticky bar at the top of the page. The first treatment was a general urgency around the need to fight against inflation due to a recent worsening of the problem. The second treatment took a more specific approach by highlighting current events in the newscycle that increased the need for action in this policy area. We split traffic 3 ways and ran the test for one month.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The experiment aimed to increase donor conversion by highlighting relevance and urgency on the instant donation page through a sticky bar. The results showed an 11.6% increase in donations for all traffic, but with a low statistical level of confidence of 36.2%, indicating that the results cannot be considered valid. However, the experiment did show significant increases in donations for Paid Social visitors, Male visitors, 45-54 visitors, and visitors over the age of 55.

The key learnings from this experiment are that highlighting relevance and urgency can increase donations, but there is a need for further experimentation to validate the results before implementing changes. Additionally, it is important to segment visitors based on demographics and traffic source to understand which groups respond positively to the treatment. Future experimentation could focus on refining the message and delivery of the relevance and urgency to boost donations across all visitor segments.

experiments.title: How reducing friction by shortening survey length impacted email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that reducing the length of a survey will increase email acquisition rates by reducing friction and capitalizing on the cognitive momentum created by the ad that sends traffic to the landing page.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/06/2023 - 03/17/2023
When reviewing results of a survey offer funnel, we observed a solid click-through rate, a low cost per click, and a fair instant conversion rate indicating that this is an offer and an issue people care to engage with. However, we identified lower-than-average email acquisition rates as an opportunity to improve overall results. After reviewing this campaign, we believe that the length of the survey was introducing significant friction and causing visitors to abandon. In all, the survey contains 20 questions. In a previous experiment, we were able to produce a sizable lift in survey completions by paginating the survey, rather than presenting all 20 questions on one page. However, over time, email acquisition was still struggling for this offer and we believe that its length was causing the cognitive momentum in landing page visitors to fizzle out. To test this theory, we built a treatment that truncated the survey to ~5 questions all presented on one page to reduce friction/cost of completing survey in effort to improve email acquisition. We split traffic 50/50 between the two versions and ran the experiment for one month.
experiments.keyLearnings: After running the experiment for one month we saw a 0.86% increase in conversion rate with 12.4% level of confidence. However, we learned that not all friction is bad friction because we saw a significant decrease in Donations and Revenue, with a 100% and 98% level of confidence, respectively. This suggests that reducing the length of the survey may have a negative impact on the willingness of visitors to donate or contribute financially. When reviewing downstream effects on donations, the treatment produced a 90% decrease in gifts with 99% level of confidence. We believe this happened because the more time that was invested in the survey, the more willing the visitor was to convert on the instant donation page. This means the control version with a lengthier survey was actually producing more qualified traffic to the donation page. Because the results were overall flat at the landing page level, we will need to do additional testing to improve email acquisition rate while still maintaining the quality of the visitor at the donation page level. In future testing, we could add additional elements to the process to let visitors know how many steps there are to complete the survey and display their progress along the way so they have more clarity around how long it will take them to finish the survey and submit their responses. It is important to note that we saw a significant increase in email acquisition rates for Housefile Email visitors, suggesting that the treatment may have a positive impact on visitors who are already familiar with our organization. Future experimentation should focus on finding a balance between reducing friction and maintaining the willingness of visitors to donate or contribute financially. We can also consider targeting Housefile Email visitors with a truncated survey to capitalize on their familiarity with our organization and potentially increase email acquisition rates.

experiments.title: How revamping the value proposition presented in the headline and body copy with specific, solution-focused language affects donation rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that improving the value proposition on an instant donation page will increase donation rates by more effectively communicating the impact of a potential donor’s gift and how it will be achieved.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/03/2023 - 02/06/2023
In an effort to improve conversion rates on an instant donation page for a public policy client, we wanted to know how stregthening the value proposition by adding a headline and solution-focused body copy would impact potential donors’ motivation to give.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running this experiment for 30 days, we observed a 14.3% increase in donations (30.7% level of confidence) and a 13.2% increase in overall revenue (62% level of confidence), however, we did not receive a large enough sample size of donations to reach statistically valid conclusions about our results. That being said, because the treatment did not have a negative effect on donations or revenue, we have recommended rolling out the treatment to all donation page traffic.

It is significant to note that among visitors obtained through paid social media (the primary acquisition source for this offer), we observed a 121.5% increase in donations with a 91.0% level of confidence.

This leads us to believe that the conclusion of a value prop-driven headline and body copy that is solution-focused and specific about why a donation is needed, what it will accomplish, and how increases the motivation for potential donors to give. Based on these results we will look for other opportunities to improve the value proposition on donation pages in an effort to increase donation rates.

experiments.title: How does more solution-focused copy on an instant donation page affect donation rates?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that solution-focused copy for instant donation page visitors will achieve higher donation rates because it provides a potential outcome for the donor.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/04/2023 - 02/06/2023
We recently ran an experiment testing how agitating the problem on an instant donation page for an acquisition offer about Big Tech accountability would affect donation rates and found that the treatment did not outperform the control as hypothesized. So we wanted to follow up this test with a new treatment that instead focused on the solutions the donor’s gift will achieve.
For this experiment, we focused exclusively on solutions and gave specific examples of some of the initiatives that the donor’s gift would help support. This was set up as an A/B test with ~50% of traffic seeing the control and ~50% of traffic seeing the treatment.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running this experiment for 30 days, we observed an 18.2% increase in donations—but a small sample size prevented us from validating these results, yielding only a 32.5% level of confidence. When looking at the results, we observed that the treatment only produced one additional donation while also producing a 25% decrease in revenue with a 72.4% level of confidence.
The low level of confidence coupled with the decrease in donations prevents us from drawing reliable conclusions about this experiment and we will therefore look for another opportunity to test this concept to see if we can get a valid learning.

experiments.title: How adding urgency with a sticky bar affects donation rates during a high urgency campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including a sticky bar that conveys urgency during a fundraising campaign will increase donation rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/21/2022 - 11/04/2022
During a special high-urgency campaign for a public policy client, we wanted to know how adding a sticky bar with a high-urgency message from the director of the project and including his image would affect donation rates.
The treatment including the sticky bar was run during the entirety of the campaign and was shown to 50% of prospective donors who clicked through to the donation page
experiments.keyLearnings: After running this experiment for just over 30 days, we observed a 5.3% decline in donation rates ... but with only a 49.4% level of confidence. Because we did not receive a large enough sample size to validate these results, it is not possible to draw reliable inferences about this experiment. However, we can hypothesize that the sticky bar either created friction by attracting the viewer's eyes back up the page, rather than progressing down the copy to the donation form. It could also be possible that the particular language chosen for this sticky bar "this can't wait" was a bit too forceful and created resentment in viewers. But in either case, we cannot know for sure.

experiments.title: How increasing the relevance of the problem to donors affected donation rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that positioning the problem as something that would directly impact the donor’s life will increase donation rates on an instant donation page because it would increase the urgency and relevancy of the problem.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/26/2022 - 09/30/2022
In an effort to improve donation rates, we wanted to know how “making the problem closer” than the donor might think would affect donation rates. We believed that by positioning the problem as something that could directly impact the day-to-day life of the donor (instead of speaking in generalities), we would be able to increase donation rates because donors would sense the problem as more urgent and relevant and thus boost their desire to support efforts that confront the issue. We split traffic between the control and the treatment 50/50.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running this experiment for one month, we observed a 13.4% DECREASE in donation rates with only a 41% level of confidence. Because of our low level of confidence in these results, we are unable to validate any learning from this experiment.

However, with significant decreases in donation rates among some key demographics, it may be that further agitating the problem at this stage of the funnel may come across as redundant or even as fearmongering, potentially harming conversion rates.

Because we still desire to see an increase in donation rates related to this offer, we believe it may be more effective to focus the headline and the body copy on the solution to maintain momentum as potential donors engage with this offer while decreasing friction and even apathy by moving from the problem (already expressed in social media ads and the landing page) to a solution and inviting the donor to "take the next step".

experiments.title: How changing the landing page design affects revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: Which landing page design produces the most donor conversions and revenue?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/09/2015 - 06/23/2015
The Heritage Foundation had been using the same landing page design for their search engine marketing (SEM) donor campaigns for the past three years. During those three years, we had run multiple experiments on the main organizational donation page which had gradually evolved into something that looked completely different.
We were in the process of moving the SEM page to the same domain as the main donation form and realized that it would be a good time to validate that the old design was still effective for the SEM audience segment.  We created an experiment by duplicating the page content but displaying it on the two different page designs.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The new design treatment caused an 85.1% drop in revenue by decreasing conversion rate by 67.6% and decreasing average gift by 54.1%. 

The simplified design used in the treatment was developed for a page with minimal copy. When we added the long value proposition text that had been developed for this specific landing page, it became difficult to read.

Landing pages must be designed so that they are easily scannable by the visitor. In this situation, the sub-headlines didn't stand out enough and the lack of page border made the page seem longer than it was.  All of these elements caused enough friction in the mind of the donor to have a negative impact on both conversion rate and average gift.

experiments.title: How adding social proof affects ebook offer conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding social proof by calling out the amount of people who have downloaded the ebook within the last month will increase conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/22/2022 - 07/15/2022
The Heritage Foundation was promoting their critical race theory ebook content offer. We wanted to test whether adding a visual element of social proof—that the ebook was downloaded more than 2,500 times in the last month—on the offer landing page would show that the resource was popular and inspire more people to download the ebook.
We added a grey box under the image of the book with the copy “This e-book was downloaded over 2,500 times last month!”
The experiment was set up as a 50/50 split test where 50% of traffic would see the control and 50% would see the treatment.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Overall, the social proof treatment decreased conversion rate by 2.2%, but these results did not validate. This experiment is also affected by the change in headline. A more clear learning could be achieved by isolating the two treatment edits, but we can still hypothesize a learning from the result.

Why didn't the treatment produce an increase in conversion rate?
The social proof box of text might have been distracting from the value proposition copy to the left or from the form itself.
 	Social proof might have dissuaded some from wanting to "follow the herd" and join in on what lots of other people have done.
New visitors had a 5.8% decrease in email acquisition with a 98% level of confidence.
 	Internal Links visitors had a 5.5% decrease in email acquisition with a 99% level of confidence.
 	Returning visitors had a 17.9% increase in email acquisition with a 100% level of confidence.
 	Paid Social visitors had a 39.4% decrease in email acquisition with an 86% level of confidence.
 	Housefile Email visitors had a 16.2% increase in email acquisition with a 100% level of confidence.
 	65+ visitors had an 11.8% decrease in email acquisition with a 98% level of confidence.
 	55-64 visitors had a 10.7% decrease in email acquisition with an 88% level of confidence.
 	18-24 visitors had a 42.6% increase in email acquisition with a 100% level of confidence.
 	25-34 visitors had a 17.2% increase in email acquisition with a 98% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How does removing intro language affect pledge participation for mobile visitors?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that removing intro language for mobile users will achieve higher sign-up rates.
experiments.summary:
