Timeframe: 04/22/2022 - 07/24/2022
In an effort to increase sign-up rates among mobile users who have been sent a pledge by The Heritage Foundation, we decided to test removing the intro language above the pledge to reduce friction by eliminating scroll and hopefully prompt more mobile users to complete the pledge and provide their email address.
We wondered if the introduction language was causing users to bounce because they had to scroll to see the pledge
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running this experiment for 90 days, we observed a slight 3.2% lift in sign-up rates, with a 74.3 level of confidence.

Despite being unable to validate our hypothesis, we were able to glean some interesting learnings from this experiment.

First, it seems that removing the intro language did not have a significant effect on sign-up rates, even though the intro language requires a user to scroll before seeing the pledge. This may be because the reduced scroll in the treatment is offset by a motivation to act caused by the problem-agitating into copy found in the control.

Second, as we drill down into the data we see that while the treatment (with intro language removed) did produce a modest lift in sign-up rates, it also produced a significant 27.9% decrease in donations (94% level of confidence) ... indicating that the inclusion of intro language that creates urgency around the issue at hand provides significant motivation for people to act downstream by making a donation.

One final metric of note is that removing the intro language did produce a 58.2% increase in email acquisition (100% level of confidence) for housefile email visitors, indicating that people already familiar with Heritage are more aware of the problem and thus require less motivation to complete the pledge.

Based on these learnings, it does not appear that there was a significant benefit to removing the intro language for mobile users in this offer. And because it produced a sizable decrease in instant donation rates, with only a slight lift in email acquisition, we can deduce that the benefits of including intro language outweigh the impact of reducing scroll to reach the pledge sooner on mobile devices.

experiments.title: How emailing for a well-known sender in a high urgency campaign impacted email engagement from the housefile
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that emails from a well-known sender during a high urgency campaign for housefile subscribers will achieve an increase in opens and clicks.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 04/05/2021
During a high urgency campaign, we wanted to test multiple senders to assess how the housefile responded to emails from different team members. Because they receive a weekly cultivation email from the Vice President of Development, we thought we might see higher engagement on emails sent from him during the campaign rather than a member of the Membership Programs team since they are familiar with hearing from him regularly.
experiments.keyLearnings: We did not have a large enough sample size to validate the test. We saw a slight decrease in the open rate on the treatment email but only with 46% level of confidence. Going forward, we will want to also measure the click rate and donation conversion rate for each sender to see if there is any significant difference in response.

experiments.title: How a problem-specific subject line impacted email engagement in a high urgency campaign

rows.name: Treatment - The Left is relentless. So are you.

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding mystery to the subject line for the housefile in a high urgency campaign will achieve an increase in the open rate.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 04/05/2021
During a high urgency campaign, most of the subject lines were in reference to membership and were relevant to the campaign. We wanted to find out if a problem-oriented subject line that held some mystery about the contents of the message would increase open and click rates for the email. We split the file 50/50 and send the control subject line “About your membership” vs. “The Left is relentless. So are you.” both from the same sender.
experiments.keyLearnings: We did not validate the experiment since we only reached a 16% level of confidence that there was a .24% increase in the open rate on the treatment. Since there was no significant change observed, we will have to test this concept again on future emails or try other methods to improve the email engagement rate during a high urgency campaign with frequent emails. We want to find out if the housefile is more responsive to membership-specific messages concerning their status or if they are more intrigued by the problem we are facing and need their help to solve.

experiments.title: How changing the email sender affects a time-sensitive membership renewal campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that changing the email sender for the housefile email list will achieve an increase in open rate.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 04/05/2021
For an annual membership campaign, we wanted to look at ways to optimize the email envelope for The Heritage Foundation appeals. We tested the open rate for emails from two different senders. The control email included Maggy Smith – a representative from Membership Programs – as the sender, and the treatment email included Kay C. James – President of The Heritage Foundation – as the sender. The subject line and body copy was the same for each email.
experiments.keyLearnings: Although there was a 1.6% increase in traffic, the experiment failed to validate at a 79.9% level of confidence. Users may be more inclined to open an email from the president of the company than a representative of Membership Programs; however, the data does not show a significant impact. This idea would need to be re-tested in the future.

experiments.title: How interrupting the donation process with a value proposition question affects conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a slide-out question shown on the donation form improve conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/25/2015 - 11/18/2015
Based upon the results of a previous experiment, we had learned that if we solicit a donor’s opinion while they are making their gift, it can actually improve conversion rate. Our previous test was visually similar to a gift designation question that is often seen on other organizations’ donation form but we framed in a donor-centric manner.
Our concern with this tactic is that by placing it inline with the rest of the donation form, it was adding an element of friction that may have adversely impacted a segment of the visitors that didn’t need the added value reinforcement. We decided to use the same question but move it into a window that slid out as the donor scrolled down the page. The hypothesis was that this would remove it from the donation form which would reduce the visual friction but would still have the same impact that was seen with the original test.
experiments.keyLearnings: We saw no statistically significant difference between the form with the slide out question and our standard form that didn't ask the question at all. It would appear that the lift achieved with our previous test was, in part, due to how the question was positioned to the donor. By making it part of the donation form, it became a question that the donor had to answer before moving forward. This value that it reinforced the in donor's mind outweighed the added friction associated with the questions.

experiments.title: How changing the sender name in a membership email affects the open rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a different sender name for the housefile audience will achieve a higher open rate .
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 04/05/2021
In an effort to increase overall email engagement during a two-week high urgency annual campaign, we wanted to test different sender names to measure open and click response. We wanted to test the effect a different sender name would have on the open rate of an email. The control email was from “Maggy” while the treatment was from “Andrew.” The audience is accustomed to receiving membership emails from “Maggy” and weekly cultivation emails from “Andrew.”
experiments.keyLearnings: Though there was a .74% decrease in traffic this experiment does not validate because only a 47.3% confidence level was achieved. Further testing and a larger sample size would be needed in order to validate these results. We recommend sending from another person in the organization to see if that affects the open rate since the housefile is accustomed to seeing emails from both "Maggy" and "Andrew."

experiments.title: How changing the sender name affects the open rate of an email
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that changing the sender name for the housefile audience will achieve a greater open rate .
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 04/05/2021
In an effort to increase overall email engagement during a two-week high urgency annual campaign, we wanted to test sender names to measure open and click response. We tested two different names from the organization in order to see how each name would affect the open rate of an email. We used “Maggy Smith” for the control email and “Andrew McIndoe” for the treatment. “Andrew” sends a weekly cultivation email while “Maggy” is the usual sender for membership card appeals.
experiments.keyLearnings: Though we saw a 1% increase in traffic, this test did not validate because it resulted in a confidence level of 66.8%. However, a retest with a larger sample size is recommended in order to validate results. There was also no statistical difference in the click thru rates for each email. We recommend potentially using another person's name to see if it would significantly increase open rates since the housefile is used to seeing both "Andrew" and "Maggy" in their inbox.

experiments.title: How a subject line test impacted open rates on a high urgency campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that creating a new subject line for returning donors will achieve an increase in open rate.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 04/01/2021
In an effort to increase overall email engagement during a two-week high urgency annual campaign, we wanted to test various subject lines to measure open and click response. We developed a treatment that used more active rather than passive language. The control subject line asked the question “Are you ready?” while the treatment asked “Are you coming?” while the body copy remained the same for both versions.
experiments.keyLearnings: We did not see any valid results from the experiment and only reached 50.2% level of confidence so this concept would need to be re-tested in the future. Looking at the body copy for the treatment, we would want to also test aligning the body copy with the subject line to make sure the original question is addressed or expounded upon to help the message flow more congruently.

experiments.title: How a more humanized subject line affects open rate on an email
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a humanized subject line for a housefile audience will achieve an increase in open rates .
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 04/05/2021
In an effort to increase overall email engagement during a two-week high urgency annual campaign, we wanted to test various subject lines to measure open and click response. We wanted to test the effect of a more humanized subject line vs. a standard subject line in an email. The control subject line read “Membership question” and the treatment read “I hope you will be joining us.”
experiments.keyLearnings: We saw a .69% increase in traffic on the treatment but this test did not validate because it resulted in a confidence level of 49.2%. This would require retesting and tested with a prospecting email instead of a housefile email in order to test a larger sample size. We also recommend testing a more humanized subject line again using a first name in the subject line instead of "you" if testing the housefile email again.

experiments.title: How changing the sender name affected the open rate on an email appeal
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that changing the sender for housefile email recipients will achieve increased open rates.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 04/05/2021
In an effort to increase overall email engagement, we wanted to test different email senders to measure open rate. We wondered if users would be more inclined to open an email from one sender than another. The control sender was “Maggy Smith” and the treatment sender was “Andrew P. McIndoe.” Maggy works in Membership Programs and Andrew is the Vice President of Development. Each email contains the same subject line and body copy.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The experiment resulted in a -0.59 decrease in traffic and a 39.0% level of confidence. Users engaged less with Andrew P. McIndoe than they did with Maggy Smith. A possible reason is users are more familiar with Maggy Smith, as they've heard from her before about membership relations. Also, an email from a representative of Membership Programs may be more engaging than an email from the Vice President of Development, assuming the user is familiar with each.

The next experiment could be to change the subject line to something more specific, such as "Renew your membership today" rather than "I haven't heard from you." The user may be more inclined to open the email because of a needed action, regardless of the sender.

experiments.title: How asking lapsed donors to be one of a few donors to give a specific amount impacted donor conversion rate during a matching gift campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: How does communicating a specific quantity of donors we’re asking to make a gift relevant to the lapsed donor impact donor conversion or revenue?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 08/04/2020
As a part of The Heritage Foundation’s matching gift campaign (called “The Board Challenge”), we wanted to experiment with the ask amount and approach. Typically, the organization has used “make your best gift today” kind of language within their control copy.
In an attempt to set a smaller, more reasonable target, we wanted to experiment with driving lapsed donors to be “one of just X donors we need today to make a gift of Y.”
The idea behind this is to (a) set a relevant ask amount for the donors, but also (b) allow them to see that they can help achieve a “small quantity” goal at that amount, as well.
experiments.keyLearnings:
We did not validate an increase, we did see we did observe a +10.8% increase in lapsed donor conversion rate — albeit at only a 58% level of confidence.
Further, the revenue was relatively flat between the two segments (only showing a +7.2% increase, with a level of confidence of 60.9%). It was promising, however, that we also observed that 29% of the people in the treatment segment decided to give more than the amount that was asked for, while another 41% gave the exact amount that was asked of them.
Conclusion: Perhaps further experimentation with the ask amounts is advisable for lapsed donors, but this directionally showed a lift in revenue.

experiments.title: How positioning the printed guide impacted donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion:
Would using the appeal of the ability to take notes in a physical book improve donor conversion rate?
5/16/2019 Note: As it stands currently, there is only a 1% difference in Stickybar clickthrough rate, but the donor conversion rate for the “take notes in a printed guide” message is proving to increase donor conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 04/23/2019
While continuing experimentation around what sticky bar message generates the most donors, we wanted to test the donation page headline (from the control) against a new message. We assumed that one motivation to receive the printed guide might be simply because the person like the benefits of a physical book. So, we played with the message to lean into that assumption.

experiments.title: How Target ROAS settings in Facebook didn’t impact the email registration rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Would “Target ROAS” improve results for the offer in terms of leads generated, donors acquired and/or dollars raised?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/12/2019 - 05/10/2019
When The Heritage Foundation launched a new ebook offer, we wanted to test the viability of increased ROI and donor conversion rate efficiency by installing a competitive “Target ROAS” campaign to split target audience traffic.
experiments.keyLearnings:
With only a 19.8% level of confidence, we were only able to notice a 1.6% increase in email registration rate.
Although the campaign served only about 1/3 the number of impressions and percentage-wise was about even with the “Lowest Cost” campaign type, we saw costs continue to scale with donation results flatline. For that reason, we decided to turn off this treatment to the traffic split and focus on creating the volume we needed at this time.
We may reconsider adding a “Target ROAS” element to this campaign in the future, but for now it appears that the best approach is to let the algorithm create results from the “lowest cost” setting.

experiments.title: How text reminders impacted campaign giving
experiments.researchQuestion: Will cultivating donors via text during a moneybomb campaign increase overall conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/17/2018 - 04/24/2018
For the 2018 Tax Day Moneybomb, we decided to test out a next texting technology to see if reminding donors to give on the day of a big campaign would increase overall conversion. Since we did not have a significant volume of cell phone numbers, we had to append the numbers to donor records. We began the campaign with a cultivation text the day before the deadline and then a reminder to give on the day of the deadline.
experiments.keyLearnings: When we compared those donors that were sent a text with those that could have received a text, we see that there was no statistically significant difference between the two segments. When we also factor in the time it took to set up the texting, the complaints received from members, and relatively low potential impact, this does not seem like a tactic that would be worth repeating for future campaigns.

experiments.title: How running Facebook ads to Membership Card campaign targeting impacted donor conversion rates from non-members
experiments.researchQuestion: Does adding a third channel in Facebook advertising (alongside email and direct mail channels) improve response rates or revenue?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/01/2019 - 03/31/2019
We wanted to continue experimentation with running Facebook ads alongside focused campaign messaging used this time to promote their Membership Card campaign.
experiments.keyLearnings:
With only a 63.2% level of confidence, we were unable to validate this experiment.
Directionally, we did note a -7.8% decrease in non-member donor conversion rate.
Overall revenue decreased by -11.9% within this treatment, as well.
Our hypothesis is that the non-member segment splits may have more to do with the likelihood that a donor will be reactivated with the third channel of outreach (online advertising).
Note: Offline gifts were not included as a part of this assessment. This included online transactions only, and was run through the first half of the final day of the campaign. Additional updates will be forthcoming.

experiments.title: How Facebook ads impacted a campaign’s lapsed member conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: Will adding a 3rd channel of touches increase donor conversion rate for reactivating lapsed members?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 02/12/2019
During The Heritage Foundation’s Kickoff Campaign, we wanted to run Facebook ads designed to remind lapsed members to renew their annual membership to see if it would boost response rates by adding a 3rd channel (digital ads) along side regularly scheduled email and direct mail messages.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment did not validate for this donor segment, but directionally, we see alignment with the non-member and member segments that both validated towards increasing segment response rates when serving ads.
It’s worth noting that similarly to the non-member experiment we ran, the total cost to run these ads to lapsed members generated a 4.2 ROI against the increase in revenue generated from the “with ads” segment. Simply put, 100% of the ad expense to increase responses from this audience was covered and generated an amount equal to slightly more than 3x the spend in additional revenue free and clear of any expense.
Final Conclusion:Further experimentation is required with future campaigns for the lapsed segment, but directionally these results align with active and non-member segments seeing an increase in response rates with ROI free and clear of the incremental expenses associated with adding the 3rd messaging channel of Facebook ads.

experiments.title: How the button text hurt email acquisition rate on a new offer
experiments.researchQuestion: Can something other than “get” in the button text improve email acquisition rates?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/11/2019 - 04/12/2019
When launching a new ebook offer, The Heritage Foundation wanted to test their way into improving email acquisition rates. For a long time, we’ve always used the term “get” in the titles and buttons within offer pages like this, so we decided to run an additional experiment to validate whether or not this old positioning statement still worked best.
experiments.keyLearnings:
With a 64.6% level of confidence, we saw a -18.6% decrease in email acquisition rate.
Although the experiment didn’t yet validate, we decided to turn off the experiment due to the directional validation that the word “get” does indeed still perform best.
Continued experimentation with the offer should be made to improve email registration rates, but it would appear that the term “get” in the button is not where the testing should be.

experiments.title: How did the use of an exit popup increase donor conversion rates from The Daily Signal visitors?
experiments.researchQuestion:
We had two questions:
How did the use of an exit popup increase donor conversion rates?
Which message would convert the most donors (stand with either (a) the president, or (b) Heritage)?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/02/2019
On The Daily Signal’s year-end fundraising page in 2018, we installed an exit popup with two messages, where one was focused on “Stand with the President” while the other was “Stand with Heritage”.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Although the experiment did not validate, we gleaned some insight from the experiment.
Exit Popup Tactic Observations:
It’s also worth noting that the landing page secured a total of 27 transactions without firing the “exit popup”, and that the “exit popup” regardless of messaging variant secured an additional 42 gifts combined between the two message types. That means that the exit popup seemingly secured an additional 55% more gifts from donation page viewers who were attempting to leave the page. Subsequently, the revenue from the “exit popup audience” drove an incremental 58% in additional revenue to the audience that did not see the popup.
This supports the idea that the exit popup does indeed work to secure additional transactions and revenue.
Messaging Observations:
Although the “Stand with Trump” message actually had a 14% higher click rate on the affirmative button (40 / 350 = 11.4% CTR) compared to the “Stand with Heritage” message (which was 38 / 391 = 9.7% CTR).
However, we actually saw that the “Stand with Heritage” message drove an increase of 8.4% for donor conversions (5.9% vs. 5.4%).
Finally, The “Stand with Heritage” also had a 12.6% higher average gift value ($45.81 vs. $40.67) compared to the “Stand with Trump” message.
Final Conclusion:
With only 21% of the required sample size collected during this experiment, we propose continuing to test which messages work best to drive donor conversions, but that it would certainly appear that installing an exit popup on donation pages can have a profound impact as a tactic to convert donors and drive incremental revenue that otherwise would leave the donation page without interruption.

experiments.title: How an exit popup did not impact donor conversion rates during a campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: Would adding an exit popup to the Kickoff campaign landing page improve donor conversion rates?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/01/2019 - 02/25/2019
During the 2019 Kickoff Campaign for The Heritage Foundation, we tried experimenting with a more emotional appeal for the visitor to activate/renew their membership, but did not see any noticeable lift in the donor conversion rate. So, we wanted to test something more tactical by adding an exit popup to intercept the visitor before they left to ask them to reconsider.
experiments.keyLearnings:
With only a 77% level of confidence, we only saw a negligible difference between the two treatments (-3%) in instant donor conversion rate.
Although the popup was displayed 1,847 times on the treatment, and 756 (or 41% of those shown the popup) of those people clicked on the button that read: “Yes, I will stand with Heritage!” — it somehow showed a decrease in instant donor conversion rates.
In digging into the data specifically, we noticed the following, as well:
Revenue/Visitor: +1.3% (68.9% LoC)
Avg. Gift: +3.79%
Even with those observations, it appears that the exit popup overall had a very negligible impact during the course of the campaign. The visitor motivation to give a gift on these pages is largely programmed via the emails (or ads) designed to drive them there. That said, experimentation with increasing motivation is likely not going to work, and tactical elements (like an exit popup) may not be effective to drive a noticeable split between the treatment and control during high conversion campaigns like this.
There are other tactical elements to experiment with during campaigns like this, but perhaps the most effective experimentation is likely going to be running tests with the motivation (which is driven from the emails / ads used to drive page traffic).

experiments.title: How the sender did not impact donor conversion rates for prospecting emails
experiments.researchQuestion: Does the sender of the Kickoff Campaign messaging impact donor conversion rates at all?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/28/2019 - 01/31/2019
With the senders in the 2018 Kickoff Campaign messaging no longer with the organization, we wanted to identify who should be the primary sender for consideration on the 2019 Kickoff Campaign messages through the email prospecting channel.
experiments.keyLearnings:
With regards to the Kickoff Campaign in 2019, we found that through the email prospecting channel, that the sender from Heritage played no role in donor conversion rates. As shown, we see a -1.3% difference between the two senders, but the level of confidence is only 7.52% — nowhere near the 95% validation that we’re looking for.
This was the case with the other performance metrics we would subsequently be interested in, as well, which were effected as follows:
Email Clickthrough Rate: +2.9% for Maggy (over Andrew), with a 41.9% LoC.
Email Open Rate: +0.5% for Maggy (over Andrew), with a 48.9% LoC.
Any additional experimentation was turned off, and Andrew was used for the remainder of the campaign sends within the timeline.
The key takeaway from this experiment is that the Heritage Foundation’s file is so accustomed to these cyclical campaigns, and that messaging for the campaign is very transactional in nature, which focuses upon the primary motivation of: “Renewing” or “Activating” your membership for 2019, that the sender, at least in the email prospecting channel, is not important.

experiments.title: How a more emotional appeal impacts donor conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a more emotionally charged message about partnering with the president convert donors at a higher rate than an organizationally-focused message?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/24/2019 - 01/31/2019
As a part of the annual Heritage Foundation’s Kickoff campaign, we wanted to experiment with a more emotional message to stand as a partner with the president that has worked well in converting new donors from The Daily Signal against the more organizationally-focused, core control message that has previously been used.
experiments.keyLearnings:
As with previous experiments with the landing page language within The Heritage Foundation’s annual “Kickoff Campaign”, we were unable to substantiate a lift over the control language.
The key here is that the motivation is so high for members to go online and renew their membership that emotionally charged language cannot further lift their intent to renew, which is being programmed within the email language used to drive them online to give.
We ended this experiment early so that we could move on to experiment with tactics on the donation page and capitalize upon this unique high-traffic, high-donation period of time by identifying and/or capitalizing upon new opportunities.

experiments.title: How an additional opt-in impacted email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: Will adding an additional opt-in option for have any impact on email acquisition?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/07/2018 - 05/03/2018
The Heritage Foundation did an analysis and discovered that subscribers that were not cultivated with their daily newsletter, The Morning Bell, were far less likely to convert to become donors. However, we had also found that The Morning Bell by itself was not an appealing enough offer to collected subscribers for a reasonable costs. With these two facts in mind, we decided to launch an experiment to see if we could acquire Morning Bell subscribers through other offers.
We decided to add a checkbox on the acquisition form for one of their eBook that would allow people to get both the eBook and the Morning Bell newsletter. We wanted to test this before rolling it out to measure the impact this checkbox would have on acquisition.
experiments.keyLearnings: After running this experiment for over a month, we did not see any statistically significant impact on email acquisition. Given the additional friction this checkbox could have caused, this is actually the ideal outcome since we are now able to acquire higher potential subscribers with no impact to conversion rate or cost.

experiments.title: How a targeted serial impacted conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will aligning the messaging of the serial ask around the individual campaign increase overall conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/13/2018 - 04/23/2018
For the 2018 Tax Day Moneybomb, we wanted to see if we could increase the number of gifts made to Heritage Action as part of the serial ask. In past testing, we found that a more relevant message was able to increase those giving to the serial ask. We decided to see if crafting this serial ask around the individual campaign would have increased results.
experiments.keyLearnings: Unfortunately, we did not get enough donors to the primary ask for this campaign to be able to to validate a serial ask message. Since the samples for this treatment is based upon those that make an initial gift, we were not able to get a large enough sample size to validate the experiment.

experiments.title: How shortening the funnel on the Dear Reader impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will including a link to the donation page in the mid-article teaser increase overall donations?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/27/2018
The Daily Signal has been running an inline donation ask for over a year now. Through a series of iterations, we had found that moving the article to the end of the article was the ideal positioning. However, we had always kept a mid-article teaser that would link to the bottom of the article where the ask was found. It would hint about the need to take action. Given the high volume of traffic that clicked on the link, we decided to test if sending people directly to the donation page would increase our overall donor conversion. Our hypothesis was that, even if we sent a far lower motivated traffic, the overall increase would net out an increase in donors.
experiments.keyLearnings: Including a link and a direct call to action in the mid-article teaser was able to triple the amount of traffic getting to the donation page but it had no statistically significant impact on donor conversion. It turns out that despite the significant increase in traffic, the visitors had such a low motivation that it was unable to move the overall donor conversion.

experiments.title: How clarity of navigation buttons impacted donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will shortening the Renew Membership link text in the navigation impact donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/04/2016 - 02/06/2017
Prior to the launch of the new Heritage homepage, we wanted to test out if the new proposed navigation would have an impact on donor conversion. The navigation was trying to limit the number of words for each link to just one. As a result “Renew Membership” would be shortened to just “Membership.”
Our concern was that this change may decrease the motivation of the people clicking on the link which would, in turn, decrease the total donor conversion from the homepage. We set up a test to validate this hypothesis.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Shortening the navigation link to just one word did not have any significant impact on the overall donor conversion of visitor coming from the home page. We did see a 53% increase in traffic from the shortened link but since these were less motivated visitors, the additional traffic did not impact the conversion in either direction.
The good news is that it does not appear that changing the navigation will significantly impact overall donor conversion.

experiments.title: How the gift array options impacted Leaders Club memberships
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a lower gift array of $3, $10, & $15 increase the conversion rate and overall revenue to the Leaders Club program?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/21/2016 - 11/10/2016
For a recent Leaders Club campaign, we wanted to test the standard gift array of $5, $15, & $25 to see if we could lower the mental friction associated with the high monthly ask. Our hypothesis was that if we can reduce the initial ask amount, we could get more donors which would lift the overall revenue.
experiments.keyLearnings: The lower gift array did not have any statistically significant impact on a donor’s likelihood to join the Leaders Club or their gift amount once they joined. It is recommended that we maintain the current control of $5, $10, and $15 for future campaigns.

experiments.title: How the style of donation form impacted conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a traditional form design increase donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/15/2017 - 03/28/2017
The Heritage Foundation underwent a website redesign for their primary website, Heritage.org. In order to maintain visual congruence, we updated the design of the donation forms to match the look and feel of the new site. Part of that new design incorporated a more modern style of form fields and buttons.
There was concern that this new style for form fields may cause confusion for older Heritage donor base. We decided to test these form elements against the more commonly used designs.
experiments.keyLearnings: Based upon these results, the design of the form fields did not have any significant impact on donor conversion. It would seem that the more modern style of form fields were clear enough that they did not cause any friction or anxiety.

experiments.title: How visually highlighting security impacted donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will visually highlighting the security of the credit card section reduce anxiety enough to increase donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/11/2017 - 02/08/2017
Based upon experiment results from similar organizations, we decided to launch an experiment across the primary donation forms for Heritage to see if visually reinforcing the security of the credit card form was able to lift donor conversion. The hypothesis was that by visually separating the form from the rest of the page, we could reduce the anxiety associate with entering a credit card online.
experiments.keyLearnings: This change did not seem to have any significant impact on the donor’s likelihood to make a gift. It may be possible to increase the visibility and the language around the security but, given this experiment result, it does not seem like it would be significant lift even with those changes. Given the current schedule of experiments, we feel that we would be better spent testing new technologies and radical changes than iterating on this concept.

experiments.title: How the primary donation value proposition is impacted by relevance
experiments.researchQuestion: Which value proposition will produce the greatest donor conversion for Daily Signal readers?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/14/2016 - 12/15/2016
Our last test on The Daily Signal found that a value proposition that talked about the quality of journalism produced by TDS was the highest performing with regards to donor conversion. To continue that line of testing, we wanted to incorporate the relevant news of Donald Trump’s election as president and Heritage’s role in that transition.
With that in mind, we rolled out the Journalism value proposition as the winner and created a new treatment around the Donald Trump transition plan.
experiments.keyLearnings: This test did not produce a significant lift for either treatment. Our hypothesis is that these results are less about the value proposition itself and more about the tactic used to deliver the language. Including this snippet of text at the bottom of each article results in it not getting seen by a large proportion of the audience and it may not be standing out. Subsequent tests will focus on the tactic used to deliver the message.

experiments.title: How a homepage donation ask impacted donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will adding value proposition and additional calls to action to the homepage increase overall donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/10/2017 - 03/16/2017
At the launch of the new Heritage.org redesign, we wanted to find a way to add emphasis to the Donate and Renew buttons that were commonly found in the navigation. The belief was that the navigation would be too hard to find which would result in a reduction in donors coming from the homepage.
To alleviate this potential drop in traffic, we created a treatment that overlaid a donation ask with value proposition copy on the primary banner. The goal was to create an easier to find pathway for visitors to give a gift.
experiments.keyLearnings: The additional callout and buttons were able to drive 20% more traffic to the various donation pages. However, the increased traffic did not statistically increase the overall donor conversion on those pages.

experiments.title: How addressing High-C subscriber in their style of communication impacts conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will speaking to subscribers in their preferred DISC style increase donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/28/2017 - 08/11/2017
In a follow up experiment during the 2017 Board Match, we wanted to test writing an appeal email with associated landing page in the style of a high “C” DISC profile.
Using the data that we appended from the CrystalKnows website, we segmented the subscriber list by both donor status and primary disc style. We then took the Control version of the email and landing page (written in the I style) and recreated it in the style of a high C.
experiments.keyLearnings: The high-C treatment showed no significant difference in conversion rates between the control and treatment. Interestingly, the treatment which was written in the high-C format, decreased the conversion rate for the high-I group by 56%. It had no significant impact on the other groups.

experiments.title: How a phone number field on a donation form affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: How will adding a phone number field impact donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/26/2017 - 10/31/2017
The Heritage Foundation had started using phone numbers to reach out and thank their donors for their gifts. Historically, the phone number was something that only came from direct mail donors. In order to increase the number of donors that had an available phone number on file, we wanted to see if adding a phone number field to the donation form would have an impact on donations.
experiments.keyLearnings: In this situation, the insignificant result was exactly what we were hoping for. This proves that there is no significant impact to conversion by adding a phone number field and making it optional. Since it doesn’t impact conversion and provides us with additional data that will be helpful downstream, this has since been rolled out to all donation forms.

experiments.title: How the membership offer impacted donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a membership offer increase donor conversion on those that just subscribed to The Agenda?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/25/2017 - 11/22/2017
In a recent meta-analysis, we discovered that when it comes to new donor acquisition, the membership offer generally outperforms a straight donation ask. We had recently seen success promoting a subscription to The Agenda but it was using a direct ask on the confirmation page. We decided to see if a membership offer would be right for this audience too.
experiments.keyLearnings: After running for nearly 2 months, we did not see any significant level of improvement with the membership offer. While there was a slight improvement in performance, the conversion rate and traffic levels were too low to statistically validate. This speaks to the low donor motivation of this audience and the need to shift efforts to boosting traffic by optimizing the earlier steps in the funnel.

experiments.title: How a Facebook headline affects direct-ask donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will the headline ‘Donate now’ or ‘Become a member’ drive more donor conversions on a Facebook ad?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/24/2017 - 12/12/2017
The Heritage Foundation noticed an opportunity on Facebook to retarget visitors who viewed their online donation pages. They created to direct ask Facebook ads that invited anyone who visited the page but did not complete the form to return and support the Heritage Foundation. In the first ad, they simply asked all visitors (including members who visited the page) to ‘Donate now.’ In the second ad, the asked all non-member visitors to join the Heritage Foundation by ‘Becoming a member today.’ They launched a test to find out which approach would be more effective at converting donors.
experiments.keyLearnings: They found that that the results were inconclusive. Due to a low overall donor conversion rate, The Heritage Foundation decided to pause this campaign and assess the results. The first ad did receive 1 donation, but this was not enough to validate this approach over the other. Using the urgency of the calendar year, They decided to launch and run a similar test that is directly tied to their CYE theme.

experiments.title: How the order of a donation ask impact donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: What type of offer will lead to the greatest return in terms of email subscribers and donations acquired?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/14/2017 - 09/05/2017
In a previous test, we had found that an inline donation ask, was able to increase the number of donations that were received from readers of Heritage.org articles. However, despite the percentage increase, the total number of donations was still not very high.
We had the hypothesis that this audience might not yet be ready for a donation ask. Instead, we wanted to see we could get significantly more email addresses that would outweigh the immediate value received from the donations. We would set up the email acquisition offer to also have an instant donation ask for immediate conversion and we also know that we can convert 1% to 5% of all emails acquired downstream. Based upon these factors, we wanted to see if offering an email acquisition offer before the donation ask would achieve better long-term results.
experiments.keyLearnings: We found that there was no statistically significant difference in donations between the two treatments. However, during this same period, the email offer also collected nearly 1,000 new subscribers for the house email file. Given the long term conversion that is likely to happen with these new subscribers, this makes the email acquisition offer a clear winner.

experiments.title: How membership benefits impact instant donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will membership benefits increase donor conversion for signers of the petition?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/30/2016 - 05/03/2016
For the instant donor conversion page that followed the petition against nominating a Supreme Court justice, we wanted to determine if membership benefits would be a strong incentive that could increase the overall donor conversion.
experiments.keyLearnings: After running for two months, we did not find that membership benefits had any significant impact on donor conversion. Our hypothesis is that this is the result of a low motivated audience; not that the offer did not work. We have found the petition signers do not make the best donor prospects given the low barrier to entry onto the file.

experiments.title: How a membership offer impacted instant donor conversion on a premium donation page
experiments.researchQuestion: Will removing membership language on an instant donor conversion page impact conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/05/2016 - 06/10/2016
On the instant donor conversion page for the email acquisition offer “Guide to the Constitution,” we wanted to test whether the membership language was creating significant enough friction to the process to be negatively impacting conversion. We decided to test removing this language to see what kind of impact it would have on the results.
experiments.keyLearnings: After running for one month, we did not see a significant difference from removing the membership benefits language. In this situation, it is our hypothesis that the value added by the membership was equal to the friction associated with the additional copy. In future tests, we should try testing a shorter version of the membership information to see if it has the same impact.

experiments.title: How a button to receive the “incentive” impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will “accepting the challenge” button increase donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/18/2016 - 07/28/2016
During the 2016 Heritage Board Challenge moneybomb, we wanted to test a tactic that we had observed on several large ecommerce sites. In these sites, the visitors had to click a button to receive an incentive. Our belief was that this action increased the perceived value of the offer that they were receiving which would positively impact conversion.
During the Board Challenge, the incentive is the match from the board. We created a button above the donation form that was a way for the visitor to “Accept the challenge.” Once pressed, it told them their gift would be doubled.
experiments.keyLearnings: We did not see a statistically significant impact from the button after running for a full week. This would indicate that it did not increased the perceived value of the matching incentive as we hypothesized that it would.

experiments.title: How a donation ask based upon the content of the article impact donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will aligning the content of the ask to the content of the article increase donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/27/2017 - 09/11/2017
The “Dear Reader” ask had become a significant source of revenue for The Heritage Foundation. Over a span of a year, we had tested a variety of aspects include positioning, aligning with year end, and individual value props. However, with the diversity of content on The Daily Signal, we had the hypothesis that if we aligned the content of the ask to the content of the article, we could increase the relevance of the ask which would impact donor conversion. We decided to test this out by writing 8 different value propositions that would be shown based upon the article the visitor was reading.
experiments.keyLearnings: After over a month of collecting data, we did not see any statistically significant impact come from targeting the content of the ask to the content of the article. This mirrors the findings that we had seen with similar tests run on the Heritage.org homepage when we tried to align the message to popular topics in the news. In those instances, the topical asks either had negative or no impact on donor conversion when compared to the standard organizational value proposition.

experiments.title: How the addition of Apple Pay impacted donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will the addition of Apple Pay decrease the friction associated with the donation process enough to increase donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/24/2017 - 04/18/2017
The Heritage Foundation is consistently looking for new ways to make the donation process both easier and better for their members. The Apple Pay option had recently gone mainstream with its incorporation into both iPhones and Mac web browsers. This one-click giving option made it significantly easier for visitors that had the service set up; for those that did not, they would not have seen any difference between the forms.
However, before rolling out the new feature, we wanted to measure its impact on the donor audience. Since this new feature was only applicable to Safari users on a Mac, we targeted the experiment to that audience.
experiments.keyLearnings: The new Apple Pay feature did not have any statistically significant impact on donor conversion or revenue. Our hypothesis is that this is likely due to the slow adoption of Apple Pay for Mac and Apple users. The portion of users that will be implementing Apple Pay will only be increasing in coming month so we decided to roll out the new form for visitors moving forward.

experiments.title: How a new landing page design impacted donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: How will the new redesigned page and donation form impact donations?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/17/2016 - 11/10/2016
The Heritage.org website is in the process of a redesign. As part of this transition, it was suggested that the existing donation page design be migrated to the new format. This would include the transition to a new look and feel for the donation form itself. Before making this change, we wanted to measure what kind of impact it would have on the performance of the donation pages.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running for the length of a money bomb campaign, we determined that the new design had no statistically significant impact on the performance of the campaign. While this is reassuring, it has led to two more questions:
How does the donation form design specifically impact conversion? We want to isolate this factor to ensure the more modern design is not having a negative impact on the older donor base.
Once the new site launches, will this new design actually increase conversion by maintaining visual congruence.
Both of these questions will be tested in upcoming campaigns.

experiments.title: How the function of the form elements affects conversion (and the “most beautiful form in the world”)
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a most condensed donation form that utilized more modern labeling techniques increase overall donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/03/2016 - 09/12/2016
In an effort to continue to iterate on the general donation form used throughout the Heritage properties, we developed a test that would incorporate the modern web technique of adaptive placeholders. The goal of this technique is to visually condense the form by using placeholder labels inside of the text fields but to then move those same placeholders to the top of the field once a visitor focuses on it so the purpose of the field is clear. This is a technique that was taken from some of the larger eCommerce sites like Hotwire.com.
experiments.keyLearnings: The new form with adaptive placeholders did not produce any statistically significant results. While the form was more condensed, it looks like it was not a significant enough change to increase donor conversion.

experiments.title: How the general donation page affects member conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Does a general donation page perform better than a campaign specific page for new members?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/21/2016 - 02/03/2016
For the Heritage 2016 January membership campaign, we created a campaign specific landing page that introduced all of the benefits of Heritage membership and made a pitch to join to kick off 2016. We had the hypothesis that we were overcomplicating the process with additional language that was unnecessary. Since the membership offer was relatively simple, we decided to test the campaign specific page against the general donation page made a clear and concise pitch for membership.
experiments.keyLearnings: Neither of the pages produced a significant improvement in conversion over the other treatment. Our hypothesis is that by the time this test was launched, the end of the campaign was near enough that the campaign deadline became a significant motivating factor. With the added motivation, the reduced friction became just as important as the added value the additional copy provided. This test would need to be repeated earlier in a campaign to validate this hypothesis.

experiments.title: How low visitor motivation affected value proposition arguments
experiments.researchQuestion: Which value proposition will lead to the highest number of gifts and revenue?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/06/2015 - 07/03/2015
Heritage was running an email acquisition campaign using a petition against the government’s Common Core initiative. As part of the process, signers to the petition would be sent to a donation page following their signature.  The standard practice for these types of campaigns was to create a landing page thanking them for their signature and asking them to make a gift to Heritage based upon the high-level value proposition of Heritage.
We had a hypothesis that if we could focus the language on the page on how Heritage is specifically impacting this topic (Common Core), it would lead to a better conversion rate.  As a result, we created an experiment to test this hypothesis.
experiments.keyLearnings: By the time the campaign was ended, we did not have enough conversions to be able to reach a statistically significant conclusion.  In this situation, there was a low motivation to give a gift for visitors coming to the page. As a result, neither of the value propositions tested were able to significantly impact the overall conversion of the page.

experiments.title: How speaking to donor passion points affects donations
experiments.researchQuestion: A call to action derived from Hillsdale’s financial independence from the federal government will get increased donors and average gifts when compared to a message involving their work teaching the principles of freedom.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/23/2014 - 12/25/2014
As a way to thank their donors, Hillsdale produced a video close to Christmas. In addition to the stewardship aspect of the video, it offered a view of the Hillsdale campus and student body that donors rarely get to experience. To get more out of this video, we developed a landing page to send out to the full email file and included a contextualized donation form below.  This donation form offered an opportunity to test different aspects of Hillsdale’s value proposition to see what messages resonated best with their donors.
experiments.keyLearnings: Given the nature of the passive ask and the inability to test the entire audience, we were unable to produce conclusive results. Further testing with more direct asks will be necessary to gain a better understanding how the donors view each of the elements of the value proposition.

experiments.title: How the minimum premium gift amount impacted revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: Which price point for the premium will result in the greatest lift to revenue?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/14/2016 - 07/26/2016
In one of the more successful donor acquisition campaigns for Heritage, we were offering a copy of The Guide to the Constitution for a gift of $100 or more. To see if we could improve the revenue made from the campaign, we created a treatment that offered the same premium for a minimum of $125 gift. We targeted the offer at some rented lists.
experiments.keyLearnings: We saw a 20% decrease to conversion rate and a 25% increase to average gift as a result of the higher minimum gift amount. This meant slightly more revenue (not statistically significant) but since this campaign was targeted at primarily donor acquisition, it did not make sense to make slightly more revenue while losing 20% of new donors. As a result, we then moved on to testing a $75 minimum gift to see how that would impact revenue and donor acquisition.

experiments.title: How an expanded value proposition impacted member renewal
experiments.researchQuestion: Which value proposition will result in the best conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/24/2016 - 04/26/2016
For the member renewal campaign, we wanted to see if we could add some relevance to the appeal by talking about the upcoming challenges. We tested this expanded value proposition against the original control that had worked in the previous campaign.
experiments.keyLearnings: There was no statistically significant difference in donor conversion or average gift. This would seem to imply that the two different value propositions were not different enough to have much of an impact on a donor’s likelihood to give.

experiments.title: How taking advantage of high visitor motivation affects follow on donations
experiments.researchQuestion: Will moving friction to the front of the petition process increase the likelihood to donate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/05/2016 - 04/27/2016
The Heritage SCOTUS petition had been running for a while and was acquiring email addresses for a incredibly low gross cost. However, we were getting very few instant conversions in the follow on ask.
We noticed that the email acquisition page was acquiring emails at a 60%+ conversion rate so we decided to try a test to take advantage of the high motivation. We changed the email acquisition page to require the visitors’ address fields in addition to just name and email. We knew this would decrease the initial conversion rate, however, we could then use this information on the tail end of the process to pre-fill the donation form. Our hypothesis was that if we reduced the friction on the second step, it would increase donor conversion which was ultimately what we were wanting.
experiments.keyLearnings: While we did not achieve significance before the campaign was shut off, we did notice that the front-loaded friction was able to increase donor conversion by 60%. The campaign still did not receive a high enough donor conversion to make it worth continuing but it is a good learning to utilize moving forward. When we see a high-converting email acquisition page, the decrease in email acquisition may be worth the subsequent increase on the donor acquisition page.

experiments.title: How membership benefit language affects the conversion of existing members
experiments.researchQuestion: How will listing membership benefits on the page impact the likelihood of an existing member renewing their membership?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/08/2016 - 01/20/2016
During the January membership campaign, we had the hypothesis that the associated benefits may not serve as much of an incentive to existing members. We decided to create two versions of the existing member page, one with a handful of benefits listed out and another without. We then split the existing member traffic between the two pages and monitored conversion.
experiments.keyLearnings:
We were not able to produce a statistically significant result in either direction. It would seem that a reminder of their benefits does not have any impact on the likelihood of an existing donor to renew their membership.
Interestingly, we performed this same experiment for new members and it produced a 20% increase when we added the benefits. The benefits would appear to have a higher perceived value with those that have not yet experienced them.

experiments.title: How the load time of a page affects conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a faster loading page increase donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/15/2016 - 01/20/2016
We noticed a decrease in the landing page conversion in the last Heritage year end campaign. One of the hypotheses for the cause was that the load time of the new server infrastructure may have been adversely impacting the conversion.  To test this hypothesis, we created a flat version of the same landing page that did not require any database calls, had a reduced amount of styles and code, and excluded several elements for tracking users.  The net result of these changes was a 47% decrease in the load time of the page (3.23 ms vs. 1.70 ms).
We then tested these two pages against each other.
experiments.keyLearnings: We did not reach any statistically significant results with this experiment. Directionally, it would seem the faster page gave a minor boost to conversion but the initial load time of 3.23 milliseconds may already have been fast enough that it was not as impactful to the conversion process as we initially envisioned it would be.

experiments.title: How seasonal imagery affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will seasonal background imagery impact the conversion rate on the page during the holiday season?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/16/2015 - 12/28/2015
We had a hypothesis that if we could subtly incorporate seasonal imagery into Heritage’s calendar year end campaign, we would be able to encourage the spirit of giving that comes with the holiday season. We created two landing page treatments, one of which incorporated a bokeh image (blurred Christmas lights).
experiments.keyLearnings: The experiment produced no significant impact on conversion rate or average gift. It would appear that either the image was too subtle and didn’t remind visitors of the Christmas season or the imagery played such a small part in the likelihood to convert that a change to this area didn’t make much of a difference.

experiments.title: How an alternate call-to-action, positioned at the right time, affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: How will an alternate call-to-action impact the visitor’s likelihood to give a gift?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/28/2015 - 10/28/2015
With an upcoming radio membership campaign, we wanted to find a way to get more out of the traffic being driven to the landing page. We had the idea of offering a free subscription to the Morning Bell when people showed the intent to leave the page.  The hypothesis is that this would not interfere with the giving process but would still allow us to get capture visitor information for those who decided not to give.
We decided to test this hypothesis to ensure that A) we did not impact donor conversion and B) the offer was strong enough that people would give their email address.
experiments.keyLearnings: We knew that by offering the opportunity for a visitor to give their email address would acquire more emails than not even giving that option. What we learned in this experiment was that there was no significant impact on donor conversion by having the secondary call to action.  We subsequently rolled out the exit intent offer to 100% of the traffic.

experiments.title: How the landing page design affects conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Which design will allow for the greatest number of gifts and revenue?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/24/2015 - 09/25/2015
A recent experiment was done to bring older landing pages up to the latest levels of optimization. However, what this experiment proved is that a design that works for one page may have the adverse reaction on another.
Based upon these results, we decided to revalidate the main Heritage donation page to ensure that we were using a design that was achieving the best possible results for us.
experiments.keyLearnings: After 90 days, this test had not yet reached statistical significance so we decided to turn it off in order to move to the next experiment. However, based upon a level of confidence above 85%, we can classify this as “approaching validity.” This result reinforces that the current simple design of the main donation page is effective with the current amount of copy.

experiments.title: How the landing page imagery affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Does the use of imagery help boost donor conversion on the page?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/16/2015 - 08/31/2015
In a previous experiment, we had validated the messaging approach to use for a money bomb campaign. Because it involved a physical building, we felt this campaign would lend itself better to images to help convey the message. This is a different approach than we normally take with money bombs so we wanted to be sure to validate the impact these images had on the overall campaign performance
experiments.keyLearnings: The addition of the images to the page created no significant lift to either donor conversion or average gift. They made the page more visually appealing but this didn’t impact the donors’ likelihood to give a gift.

experiments.title: How the minimum gift amount affects donation form results
experiments.researchQuestion: Which minimum gift amount will result in the greatest amount of revenue and donors?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/07/2015 - 06/24/2015
For the Heritage Foundation, the minimum level for membership technically begins at $25. However, the renewal page for their members has a minimum donation amount of $2 which is often utilized by visitors.  In an effort to lift the average gift, we decided to implement a new page that would make the minimum gift amount $25 to match the membership levels.  When doing this, we want to be sure to monitor both donor conversion and overall revenue to ensure this new $25 minimum didn’t significantly impact the number of donors acquired.
experiments.keyLearnings: Both revenue per visitor and conversion rate of the two different pages were nearly identical and neither achieved statistical validity. We learned from this test that, with regards to membership renewal, the minimum gift did not factor into most donors’ decision to give.

experiments.title: How the placement of elements to add urgency affects conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Which placement of the countdown clock and thermometer of the donation goal will have the greatest impact on donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/03/2015 - 06/04/2015
Previous experiments had taught us the positive impact increasing urgency and progress toward a goal can have an a campaign. As part of a moneybomb campaign, The Heritage Foundation wanted to determine if the placement of the visual elements designed to promote these two factors would impact the donor conversion.
For the campaign, we designed two landing pages: one with a thermometer at the top and a countdown clock at the bottom and another with these two visual elements flipped.
experiments.keyLearnings: Regardless of whether the countdown clock or thermometer was displayed at the top of the page, the pages had nearly identical conversion rate. From past experiments, we know both of these elements will boost campaign performance. What we learned here is that their placement is less important than simply being included in the page.

experiments.title: Understanding the perceived value of video to email recipients
experiments.researchQuestion: Will using text instead of a video will allow donors to focus on the call-to-action which will increase the response rate of the campaign?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/28/2014 - 12/30/2014
With no significant difference between click rates and nearly identical response rates (both are 0.1%), the effort put into the video may not be necessary this late in a year end campaign.
experiments.keyLearnings: Video is often used as a way to increase visitor engagement on donation landing pages. However, it was our hypothesis that the videos actual distracted from the ultimate goal, giving. As a result, we created an experiment to test a video against a text version of the same message.

experiments.title: How taking a softer call to action affects donations
experiments.researchQuestion: A softer call-to-action within the email will drive more traffic to the website which will, in turn, convert a greater number of donors.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/26/2014 - 12/27/2014
As it was getting closer to the end of the year, Heritage wanted to test a messaging approach with their fifth email in their year end campaign. Toward the end of urgency-based marketing campaigns, it is common practice to make a direct ask in the email.  The question was how early can that direct ask be made and have it still be effective.
experiments.keyLearnings: The softer language in the email increased the number of visitors by 41% but it actually converted fewer total donors in the test.  At this stage in the campaign, there does not seem to be any impact to response rate by utilizing a softer call to action.

experiments.title: How a shift in copy tone affects clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: An email talking about how Heritage impacts the constituents’ family life will drive more clicks than one talking about how they stand for the constituents’ principles.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/23/2014 - 12/24/2014
In the fourth email in the Heritage year end campaign, we tested what messaging point (principles or family) would drive the most traffic to the landing page.
experiments.keyLearnings: While the two treatments spoke to different prospect level value propositions, the copy did not seem to be different enough to see any significant impact on click throughs or donations.

experiments.title: How a soft call-to-action affects conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: A softer call to action to “Stand with Heritage” will increase the click through rate which will increase the number of donors that convert on the page
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/21/2014 - 12/22/2014
On the third email in Heritage’s year end campaign, we ran a test asking constituents to either “Stand with Heritage” or “Make a Donation.”
experiments.keyLearnings: Even though the softer call-to-action drove 436% more visitors to the landing page, the traffic was not motivated enough to have a significant number of those visitors convert to donors.  On offers like this in the future, we may consider special messaging or landing page to help continue them down the donor funnel.

experiments.title: How removing Challenge Gift language affects conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will not including a challenge gift with the campaign have minimal impact on the fundraising campaign?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/26/2014 - 12/28/2014
As part of their year end campaign, the Heritage Foundation secured multiple gifts from major donors to serve as challenge gifts to their members. It has always been said that Challenge Gifts can increase conversion rate and average gifts but we have never known how much the increase is. We decided to test this well-known tactic to determine how much impact a Challenge Gift can have on a fundraising campaign.
experiments.keyLearnings: There was a slight increase in conversion rate for those that received the version with language about the challenge gift but it did not reach significance with sample size or lift. This would suggest that the challenge gift may not have the level of impact that best practices tells us it has. However, this test would need to be run with a larger segment to validate that conclusion.

experiments.title: How simplifying the email design affects clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: A simplified email will have a more personal feel to it which will prompt a greater number of click throughs.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/20/2014 - 12/22/2014
The design of the email did not have any significant impact on the supporter’s likelihood of clicking on the email. It also did not have any significant impact on the donors’ propensity to give. Moving forward we will utilize the existing email templates to save both cost and time.
experiments.keyLearnings: The standard format for DTS emails is a designed layout with images and a color scheme that matches the website. In an effort to increase the number of visitors to the calendar year end campaign, we created an experiment to test the standard design against a simplified email.

experiments.title: How we ensured a smooth transition from Kimbia to Stripe
experiments.researchQuestion: Will the new Stripe form have any impact on the donation conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/29/2016 - 02/08/2016
When The Heritage Foundation decided to move their payment processing and form generation from Sage/Kimbia to Stripe, we wanted to have a way to do a “soft-launch” that would ensure a successful roll out. Specifically, we wanted to be able to test the form to ensure there were no unintended bugs and that the form did not possess some unseen factors that would have hurt the donor conversion rate.
We decided to do a split-test between three different donation pages (the main donate page, the renewal page, and the Join Heritage page). We tested these pages using a Kimbia form and a Stripe form and split traffic equally between them.
experiments.keyLearnings: The experiment resulted in no significant impact in either direction. In this situation, that is actually what we were hoping for. We wanted to ensure that Stripe didn’t negatively impact the conversion rate. While a lift would been nice, since the forms were nearly identical, we didn’t expect that to actually happen.
